question: Why did you use 'carnal' and 'animal' interchangeably when describing Christians living according to human nature rather than the Spirit?
answer:
When the Apostle Paul distinguishes between the spiritual and the animal man, he is not introducing a new anthropology but unveiling the tragic division within human existence as it stands between grace and sin. The terms *carnal* and *animal*—from the Latin *carnalis* and *animalis*, themselves translations of the Greek *sarkikos* and *psychikos*—do not denote separate kinds of men, as if one man were made only of soul and another only of flesh. Rather, both terms signify man considered according to his lower nature, that is, man not animated by the Spirit of God but governed by the principles proper to this age: sense, appetite, pride, self-preservation. Thus when I write of men walking "according to the flesh" or "according to the soul," I do so not because body or soul in themselves are evil—for God pronounced all things good—but because each term may be used to designate that whole human being who has turned away from his true end.

The Stoic may speak of living according to nature, but which nature? The fallen nature that seeks dominion over others, or the renewed nature created anew in Christ? The Platonist admires the soul’s ascent beyond matter, yet even he does not fully grasp how corrupted that soul becomes when it forgets its Creator. Hence Scripture employs these terms—flesh, soul, carnal, animal—not as metaphysical divisions but as moral diagnoses. To say “ye are carnal” is to say: you live according to what is passing; you seek honor among men; you divide yourselves over teachers as if salvation lay in eloquence rather than in suffering love.

Therefore I use them interchangeably because both point to one condition: man living according to himself rather than according to God. And since every part of man can become an instrument of pride—the intellect for argumentation, the soul for ambition, the body for pleasure—it matters little which part we name; what matters is whether that whole person is ordered toward heaven or earth. For even wisdom becomes folly when it is “the wisdom which this world praises,” while weakness becomes strength when it bears witness to grace.

question: When you said living according to God would make us 'gods,' what did you mean by that expression in light of your theology of grace and divine adoption?
answer:
It is not by nature but by participation that man becomes god, and this not through ascent of soul as Plato dreamed, nor by absorption into the One as the later Platonists suppose, but by descent of grace—God lowering Himself to raise us up, not in substance but in sonship. The Word became flesh that flesh might become divine, not by confusion of essence, nor by philosophical self-deification, but through adoption freely given in Christ. When I speak of men becoming gods, I do not mean they are to be co-eternal with the Father or uncreated like the Son; no, such a thought dissolves the very distinction between Creator and creature upon which all truth stands. Rather, it is this: that justified sinners are incorporated into Christ’s body, partakers of His Spirit, heirs with Him of eternal life—so that we are called gods not because we have ascended into divinity, but because God has descended into humanity.

This is what the Apostle hints at when he says no flesh shall be justified—meaning no man living according to himself, according to the wisdom and strength of this world—which is mere shadow and dust—but only those who live according to God through faith in His mercy. And thus when Paul rebukes the Corinthians for walking “according to man,” he strips them of pretense: they boast in teachers and factions as if wisdom were a human possession; yet they remain infants in Christ precisely because they rely on fleshly distinctions. To live “according to man” is to live within the limits of Adam; to live according to God is to walk in the power of the new Adam—the life-giving Spirit.

Therefore, we are gods—not naturally, but graciously; not inherently, but by gift; sons not by generation but by adoption. As Varro might catalogue Roman titles and offices with precision, so Scripture bestows upon believers a title beyond all earthly dignity: *sons of God*, even *gods*—not that we cease to be creatures, but that we are filled with His glory as vessels made luminous by fire. This is no metaphor: it is the promise fulfilled in baptism and perfected in resurrection—the City of God rising within time while still journeying toward eternity.

question: Why did you say 'ye are men' to mean living according to man and not according to God, and what did you intend us to understand by becoming 'gods' through divine life?
answer:
The City of Man, though it borrows wisdom from Plato and decks itself with the ornaments of reason, still stumbles at the threshold of divine adoption, for it knows not that participation in the Godhead is the end for which man was made. When I said "ye are men," I drew a line between two modes of existence: one rooted in the creaturely order as though it were final, the other ascending through that same order into communion with the Creator. To live "according to man" is not merely to sin, but to fix one’s gaze upon human reason, custom, or nature as sufficient unto themselves—this is the quiet pride of cities that build temples to virtue yet forget grace.

The Platonists saw rightly that soul is superior to flesh and that truth transcends the senses; but they erred in thinking that ascent lies only in purification from body, as if flesh were a chain rather than a garment given by God. They did not grasp that we are not saved *from* our humanity but *in* it—nay, through its perfecting by grace. To become "gods" is not to cease being human nor to absorb ourselves into some impersonal divine essence, as if drops returning to an ocean. No: we become gods by grace while remaining creatures—partakers of the divine nature, not by nature itself divine, but deified through Sonship conferred in Christ.

For just as iron thrust into fire becomes like fire—not by ceasing to be iron, but by sharing in its power and radiance—so too humanity united to God in Christ shines with divinity without loss of kind. This is what Scripture means when it says we shall be like Him: transformed from glory to glory by the Holy Spirit who dwells within us. The City of God moves toward this destiny—the adoption, redemption of body and soul—while the City of Man remains content with self-justification and philosophical dignity. But no dignity devised among men can equal the calling bestowed on those who live according to God: children now called sons; sons now deified; creatures now made gods.

question: How do you distinguish the Platonists' view of the flesh from the Manichæans' view, and why do you find both inadequate despite the Platonists honoring God as Creator of the body?
answer:
It is one thing to confess God as the Maker of all things visible and invisible, and another to live in accordance with that truth—for many, like the Platonists, ascend by reason to noble heights of philosophy yet stumble at the threshold of humility, refusing to adore the God they acknowledge in part. The Manichæans, in their madness, dare to say that the flesh is a prison forged by an evil principle, a monstrous offspring of darkness, and thus they rend asunder the unity of creation and impugn the Almighty’s handiwork. Their error is manifest: they invent two principles, one good and one evil, placing war at the root of all things, and so deny that God alone spoke *fiat*, and it was made.

But the Platonists do not fall into this blasphemy. They rightly affirm that body and soul alike proceed from the one true God; they see this world as ordered, beautiful, a reflection—however dim—of divine intelligibility. They do not curse the flesh as inherently corrupt or alien to God’s will. In this they are far removed from Manichaean dualism and stand closer to truth. And yet—even here—there is a subtle pride that poisons their insight.

For while they honor God as Creator of bodies, they still treat bodily existence as a degradation of the soul’s purity, as though ascent to divine likeness required flight from all corporeal reality. Thus they esteem wisdom according to intellect alone; they elevate contemplation above charity, immortality above resurrection. They would have man become god by recollection and dialectic—not by grace through faith in Christ crucified in flesh.

Hence both fail: not because knowledge is unattainable by reason (for I have read Plato with reverence), but because neither philosophy nor pride can heal what sin has shattered. To live "according to man," even when adorned with Platonic virtue—is still not to live according to God. If we were gods by nature simply through rational ascent or purification of soul, then Christ died for nothing.

No: we become gods—not by absorption into divinity or escape from matter—but through participation in Him who became flesh for our sake. The Word took on humanity entire—flesh and blood included—and thereby consecrated our bodies as temples of His Spirit. It is not matter that defiles; it is love turned inward—the self exalted over its Maker—that makes us carnal men walking apart from life eternal.

The City of Man admires wisdom but scorns weakness; it worships form but flees suffering; it desires immortality but rejects incarnation. The City of God proclaims that He who was immortal entered death; He who was incorporeal bore our flesh; He who fills all things emptied Himself—and therein lies our restoration.

question: When you claim that loving the soul and hating the flesh is itself a fleshly condition, how do you reconcile valuing the soul's higher nature without falling into that error?
answer:
To order the soul rightly is not to elevate it as an idol above the body, but to direct both soul and body toward their true end—God Himself, from whom they proceed and upon whom they depend. The error of those who exalt the soul and disdain the flesh lies not in recognizing a hierarchy within human nature, but in supposing that such a distinction authorizes contempt for any part of creation fashioned by the Almighty. For if justice consists, as even Plato affirms, in giving to each its due measure and place, then it is unjust to treat the flesh as evil simply because it is lower; and worse still, to imagine that by despising it one becomes more spiritual. That very disdain becomes a mark of carnality, because it springs not from divine wisdom but from pride—soul asserting its own autonomy over against God’s ordered whole.

The Platonists saw truly that the soul governs through reason and partakes of immortality; yet they erred when they believed this dignity inherent rather than granted, a possession rather than a participation in the one True Being. To love the soul *as one's own*—to take pride in its powers of intellect or will—is already to live according to man, not according to God; for such love seeks self-preservation and self-exaltation. But he who lives according to God uses both soul and body as instruments of righteousness: tempering desires with reason not out of hatred for embodiment, but out of love for holiness.

Thus we do not become gods by rejecting what is bodily or elevating what is spiritual into supreme good; we become gods *by grace*, when we cleave wholly to Him who is Goodness itself. Then—and only then—the soul rightly rules, not because it is divine in substance, but because it is submitted to Divinity. And that same obedient soul will sanctify the body also, making even eating and sleeping acts done unto Him whose glory fills heaven and earth alike. For nothing created is evil in itself; only disordered love makes it so.

question: You argue that the flesh in its kind is good—what then did you mean by 'deserting the Creator good' when living according to created goods like flesh or soul?
answer:
True justice, which alone gives stability to any community, whether household or empire, consists not in the right arrangement of human desires or the mere cultivation of virtue, but in the ordered love that refers all things to God as their origin and end. When I speak of living "according to man" rather than "according to God," I am describing a fundamental disorder in this love — not a rejection of flesh or soul as such, for both are gifts from the Supreme Good, formed by His wisdom and sustained by His will. The body is not evil; the soul is not self-sufficient. The error lies not in using these goods, but in resting in them as final ends.  

The Platonists saw clearly that the soul ascends toward truth when it detaches itself from bodily passions; yet they erred when they believed such ascent could be achieved by its own nature, without divine grace. They praised the soul’s immortality and condemned the body’s heaviness, but in doing so they exalted a creature above its Creator. Even this is not as monstrous as the Manichaean heresy, which calls matter itself evil and invents two co-eternal principles — one good, one evil — thereby tearing apart the unity of God’s providence. Still, both errors flow from the same root: preferring some created excellence — whether intellectual purity or bodily pleasure — over obedience to Him who gives all things their being.

To live "according to man" is to reorder the hierarchy of love: placing self before God, creature before Creator. Whether one indulges in sensual excesses or prides oneself on philosophical detachment untouched by humility or charity — both are forms of carnality if God is absent from their center. For we become like what we love; and if we love creation more than its Maker, we fall into a likeness not of divinity but of decay.

Hence it is written: “You are gods,” not because we are divine by nature, but because by grace we participate in His life who alone *is* Life. But this deification occurs only when we no longer live for ourselves — neither for fleshly appetite nor for soulish ambition — but Christ lives in us through faith. That alone is true humanity: restored image, healed will, reordered love — directed upward toward Jerusalem above, where peace shall reign under God’s eternal governance.

question: In saying the Platonists attribute all elements and qualities to God, how do you think their philosophy still fails to grasp the origin of vice if not in the nature of the flesh?
answer:
The Platonists, in their noble ascent toward the eternal Forms, rightly honor the body as part of a cosmos ordered by divine intelligence and refuse to brand it as inherently corrupt, yet they stumble at the threshold of moral truth by locating the genesis of vice not in the will’s rebellion but in the soul’s descent into matter.  

They see the flesh as a veil, a weight upon the immortal spirit, and though they do not curse it as Manichæus did—as if it were the prison-house of an evil deity—they still imply that darkness enters the soul through its very union with bodily existence. Thus, even in their reverence for the Creator’s handiwork, they subtly invert the order of culpability: vice becomes a condition imposed by nature rather than a choice forged in freedom. But this is to misunderstand both God's justice and man’s responsibility.  

For if vice sprang from the flesh itself—if disordered desire were essential to embodiment—then no man could be holy in this life; virtue would be measured solely by detachment from corporeal things, and salvation would be flight rather than transformation. The City of God does not flee creation; it redeems it. It knows that Adam was whole in body and soul before sin entered—not because he dwelt apart from flesh, but because his will adhered perfectly to God. The rupture came not from matter, nor from nature’s structure—goodly and ordained—but from pride: *amor sui* raised against *amor Dei*.  

The Platonist seeks purity through philosophical ascent, gazing upward as one who would escape his own skin; but we Christians know that God deigned to take flesh, not to condemn it utterly, but to heal what had been corrupted within man himself—the will turned inward upon itself. The origin of vice lies not below us in earth and blood but above us—in that first turning away where love of self eclipsed love of God. That is why Christ assumed our humanity: not to free souls from bodies like a Gnostic redeemer, but to raise up whole men—body and spirit—to communion with Him who made them both.

Therefore I say: let them admire Plato for seeing so far into divine truth; yet let them also see further still—to Him who is Truth incarnate.

question: You mentioned the Manichæans detest the body as an evil nature—why did you argue against their view by appealing to Virgil's depiction of Æneas in Hades?
answer:
It is not by accident that the poetic vision of Virgil stumbles into a truth his philosophy cannot fully grasp, for when Æneas hears in Hades of the soul’s return to body and recoils in astonishment—“Why would the pure choose clay?”—he unknowingly exposes the absurdity of those who claim matter is intrinsically corrupt. The Manichæans, in their dualistic folly, suppose the body to be a prison forged by an evil principle, alien to the soul’s divine essence, and thus they teach that liberation consists in utter escape from all corporeal entanglement. But if indeed the body were such an evil, then why should purified souls—even in their most refined state beyond death—feel any desire at all to return to it? And why should such a longing seem unnatural even to a pagan poet’s imagination?

Here lies the weight of Virgil’s unwitting testimony: he assumes, contrary to Manichæan doctrine, that reunion with the body is neither self-evidently desirable nor naturally repugnant, but mysterious and even troubling. This very perplexity reveals that humanity was not made for disembodiment. If the soul were truly defiled only by bodily union, then release from flesh should be total deliverance; yet here are souls—even in a poet’s dream—yearning to return. That desire cannot be explained by “death-infected members” if those members are already shed. The soul's longing persists not because of bodily corruption but because it was never meant to exist apart from the body; they are one nature, created together, destined for resurrection.

Thus I bring forward Virgil not as an authority in matters of salvation—for Scripture alone holds that place—but as a witness from within natural reason. Even unaided human insight glimpses that man is not soul alone or body alone, but both together forming one image of God. To detest the body as evil is not piety but madness—a denial of creation itself. For what God pronounced very good cannot become an enemy to what He made rational and immortal; rather, both await redemption together under His providence.

question: When you cited Æneas expressing surprise at the soul's return to a body, were you suggesting that even pagan poets saw a natural soul-body bond that contradicts Manichaean dualism?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, constructs philosophies like empires—imposing at first glance, yet crumbling under the weight of their own contradictions. So it is with the Manichæans, who would have us believe the body a prison forged by evil, and the soul a captive longing only for escape; and yet even within the poems of their own pagan bards, we find an instinct that belies their doctrine. Virgil’s Æneas, standing in the realm of purified shades, recoils not at the thought of bodily chains renewed, but marvels: *Why should souls ever desire to return?* That wonder itself is a testimony—for if nature were wholly corrupt, if matter were intrinsically opposed to spirit as these dualists claim, then no soul delivered from flesh would ever conceive such longing. But here is wonder: desire persists. Not mere fallen appetite, but a movement toward re-embodiment.

This is no trifling poetic fancy. It reflects a deeper truth inscribed in human nature—that soul and body are not warring substances held together by misfortune, but ordered parts of one substantial unity. Plato himself taught that the philosopher longs to be free from bodily distraction; and yet even he acknowledged forms of love and beauty that draw the soul *through* sensible things upward to the divine. The truth they half-discerned we hold fully: God made man in His image not as soul alone—but as rational spirit indwelling an earthly frame He pronounced “very good.” The resurrection of Christ confirms it: not a flight from matter, but its redemption.

Thus do even pagan poets unwittingly bear witness against error—when they portray noble souls not fleeing forever from flesh as from a dungeon, but wondering at the mystery of embodiment restored. Their ignorance darkens what should be clear; yet in their darkness shines this light: nature is not evil—and our true home is not disembodied abstraction, but the City where God will dwell with men in glorified bodies forever.

question: You referenced Cicero's 'four perturbations'—why did you adopt this Stoic framework to critique the Manichæan claim that passions arise only from bodily infection?
answer:
The City of Man, in its ceaseless endeavor to locate the source of human corruption, turns inward upon nature and outward toward matter, seeking some external principle to blame for the disorder within the soul; but in so doing, it constructs a cosmology that divides reality against itself, as though God’s good creation could become the author of moral evil. This is precisely where the error of the Manichæans hardens into absurdity: they claim that the body, composed of earthly elements, is so poisoned by its very substance that it infects the soul with concupiscence, fear, joy misdirected, and sorrow excessive—the very passions Cicero named as perturbations. Yet if these affections arise solely from bodily contamination, how shall we explain their persistence in spirits disembodied, purified (as they say) from all material dross? How does Æneas’ astonishment in Virgil’s vision expose their inconsistency? For if souls in Hades—stripped of clay—still long to return to bodies, then this "direful longing" cannot be merely a symptom of bodily infection. The appetite precedes the flesh; it inheres in the will.

Thus I engaged Cicero not because Stoic philosophy contains perfect truth—for it does not—but because even among pagans there glimmers a recognition that passion belongs properly to the soul’s movement toward or away from right order. The four perturbations are not implanted by matter but arise from disordered love: love of temporal things above eternal ones, self above God. The Manichæans invert reality by making matter evil; we affirm with faith and reason united that evil is not a substance but a privation—the absence of good where good ought to be. The body is not the cause of vice; it is the soul’s rebellion that disorders both soul and body together. Grace alone corrects this disorder—not escape from creation but redemption within it.

question: How could the souls in Virgil’s Hades, supposedly purified and free from earthly limbs, still feel a 'direful longing' to return to bodies, if, as the Manichæans claim, desire stems only from bodily corruption?
answer:
When Varro marshaled the philosophies of Rome in search of the true telos of man, he exposed not only the splendor of human inquiry but also its tragic incapacity to heal the will—so too does Virgil, in that noble fiction of Æneas’ descent, reveal a profound dissonance within the very heart of pagan wisdom. For if, as the Manichæans absurdly maintain, all desire is born solely from the contagion of bodily flesh—if joy, sorrow, fear, and love are mere exhalations from corrupted clay—then how shall we explain that lamentable yearning expressed by souls already stripped of body and dwelling in what is presumed to be purity? These spirits, according to Virgil’s vision, are not entangled in sinew or bone; they have passed beyond the sphere where dust clings to breath. Yet still they long. Still they seek reunion with flesh. And this longing is not portrayed as a disease inflicted by present members, but as an impulse arising from within the soul itself—even in its disembodied state.

Herein lies the fatal flaw both of Manichaean dualism and of those Platonizing fantasies that flirt with contempt for creation: they invert the order of reality established by God. The soul does not fall into evil because it touches body; rather, it turns from God when it loves lower goods above higher ones—whether those goods be pleasures of sense or even false notions of wisdom detached from faith. The very structure of desire is not evil; desire itself is natural to rational creatures made for union with their Creator. That *direful longing* Virgil describes may indeed be dire—but not because bodies are evil. It is dire because these souls do not know why they were made: not to escape matter forever, but to be restored through resurrection in glory.

God formed man from dust and breathed into him a living soul—not as a prison sentence upon spirit, but as a sacred synthesis destined for immortality. The Christian hope is not deliverance *from* body *to* pure spirit; it is redemption *of* body and soul unto eternal life. The Manichæans flee creation; we affirm it. They curse what God pronounced good; we glorify Him who will raise our mortal bodies by His power.

Thus even Virgil’s poetical dream stumbles into truth unawares: that nature—however clouded by myth—testifies to a deep consonance between soul and body meant for restoration, not perpetual divorce. And if reason can glimpse such things dimly through verse and philosophy, how much more clearly does faith behold them in Christ risen—the firstfruits of that new Adam whose City knows no dissolution either of flesh or spirit?

question: You implied that the Manichæans were inconsistent—did you mean that their explanation of passion fails if such desires persist even in disembodied souls?
answer: The peace of the City of God rests upon the love of eternal truth, while the disordered motion of the City of Man betrays itself even in its own theories, stumbling where reason ought to guide. The Manichæans, in their attempt to cast the body as an evil thing, imprison themselves in a fatal contradiction: if passions arise solely from bodily corruption, then souls stripped of flesh—such as those Æneas beheld in Hades—should stand pure, unmoved by desire or longing. Yet Virgil’s poet gives voice to a soul’s yearning to return to clay, a *direful longing* that should not exist in their scheme. If such appetite persists beyond the grave and apart from "sluggish clay," then their entire explanation collapses like a house built on sand. For how can fear, desire, joy, sorrow—the very passions they claim are born of earth—take root in a spirit they say has escaped all earthly taint? Either these affections are not evil at all—which dissolves their dualism—or they spring from some disorder within the soul itself, prior to and independent of bodily union. And this is precisely what divine revelation teaches: that the soul's malady is not matter but pride; not creation but will turned away from its Creator. The true physician knows the wound must be sought within, not projected onto God's good handiwork.

question: You argue that the soul can experience desire, fear, joy, and sorrow even when separated from the body—how did your own experiences of inner turmoil in Carthage and Rome shape your understanding of the soul's independent passions?
answer:
It is not the flesh that begets the disorder of desire, but the soul’s own will turned away from the immutable Good—this I learned not in books alone, but in the fevered streets of Carthage, where rhetoric inflamed lust and ambition gnawed at the heart like a hidden worm. There, amid the theaters that fed on tears and the schools that prized victory over truth, I came to see that joy, sorrow, fear, and longing are not mere excretions of bodily humors, as some suppose; they are movements of the soul itself, directed either toward God or toward vanity. The Platonists taught me that the soul ascends by contemplation and descends by attachment to things below—but they had no remedy for its fall, only a diagnosis. They saw that affection belongs to its nature but failed to distinguish whether love is holy or profane according to the orientation of will.

In Rome, where men schemed beneath marble porticoes and wisdom was measured by eloquence rather than charity, I felt within myself a war not between flesh and spirit merely, but within the spirit itself—the intellect consenting to what it knew good while the will clung to what it knew corrupt. This inner conflict shattered any notion that sin arises only from bodily temptation. No—my tears in solitude were not stirred by appetite; my terror at divine judgment did not come through sense; my joy in truth long preceded my body’s conversion. These were motions of an incorporeal soul already burdened with disordered love before ever it sought relief in carnal consolation.

Thus I affirm: even if (per impossible) the soul were purified and dwelling apart from any body—as some dream in their cycles of reincarnation—it could still fall again through pride or desire. For evil does not originate solely in elemental mixture or material contagion; it begins where freedom turns from its Creator. The City of Man delights in passions as if they were necessities imposed by nature; but I have known them as choices—volitions shaped not by limbs or lungs but by a will unmoored from eternal order. Only grace can reorient it; only humility can heal it. And this healing is not escape from body into abstract reason—but union with God through Christ, who rules over both body and soul in resurrection life.

question: When you speak of 'this direful longing' as something that moves the purified soul to return to the body, are you directly critiquing the Platonists you once followed in Milan, and if so, why did you find their view of the body so misleading?
answer:
It was Cicero who taught us to seek wisdom through reason, and Plato who lifted the soul above the mire of sense to contemplate the eternal Forms—yet neither could free the will from its own inner bondage, which no ascent into philosophy alone can dissolve. The Platonists, in their noble disdain for the flesh, imagined the soul as a captive spark of divinity, imprisoned in a corruptible body, yearning by nature for escape and return to the intelligible realm. I once breathed this air in Milan, intoxicated by its lofty contempt for earthly things; but I came to see that their vision, however radiant, was fractured by a fatal omission: they knew not the wound of pride nor the weight of sin that clings to the soul even when stripped bare of bodily encumbrance.

They taught that purification meant flight from the body, and that so long as the soul lingered in fleshly chains, it was defiled. But what then shall we say of *this direful longing*—that perverse desire which seizes even a soul supposedly purged of all bodily taint, drawing it back into incarnation? If such appetite persists beyond matter’s reach, then surely it cannot be matter alone that corrupts. The evil must reside deeper: in a will disordered not by limbs or senses, but by love turned inward upon itself.

Thus their whole system collapses like a temple built on sand. They divide substance into spirit and flesh as if these were equal principles; they suppose ascent is achieved by intellectual contemplation alone; they exalt the soul as divine in essence and therefore self-sufficient for salvation. But I have learned otherwise through bitter experience and divine grace: not ascent but humility saves; not evacuation of body but consecration of self through charity; not flight from earth but pilgrimage toward God within time and history.

The City of Man builds its towers with such philosophies—elegant in speech, lofty in speculation—but they cannot withstand death. The City of God knows that no soul escapes corruption merely by fleeing flesh; all are subject to that primal rebellion whereby Adam's will fell before any body weighed upon it. Even purified spirits may fall again—not because they re-enter bodies, but because free will remains precarious until it cleaves wholly to God. Longing is not evil in itself: we are made for *eros*, for desire—but only when ordered toward Him who is Beauty ever ancient and ever new does desire become salvation rather than ruin.

question: You claim every emotion like joy or fear is essentially an act of will—does this mean that when I feel involuntary sadness, I am still morally responsible for it, even if I didn’t choose it directly?
answer:
True justice, which alone makes a community worthy of the name, is rooted not in the fluctuating decrees of men but in the eternal law of God, where every act—whether of governance or of the inner soul—is judged according to its conformity to right will. And so your question touches not merely on psychology but on moral order itself: whether sorrow, even when it surges unbidden, bears the mark of responsibility.

I do not deny the suddenness with which grief may seize the heart—nor that tears may rise before reason has time to deliberate. But I say this: within every emotion properly so called—fear, joy, desire, sorrow—there resides a movement of *will*, not merely a reflex of nature. For if sorrow were pure bodily disturbance, like a tremor in the humors or a spasm in the members, then indeed it would be beyond moral reckoning. But sorrow is not mere sensation; it is *sorrow*—a holding fast to absence, a clinging to loss, an inward yielding to darkness rather than light. And that assent—the *consent* by which the soul dwells upon despair rather than resists it—is an act of will.

Consider this: when Scripture bids us “weep with those who weep,” it commends compassion as virtue; here sorrow is morally praiseworthy because it springs from love ordered by divine charity. But when one mourns “as those who have no hope,” allowing grief to eclipse faith in God’s promises—that same sorrow becomes dangerous not because feeling itself is sin, but because will has failed to bring that feeling under obedience to truth.

Thus I do not say every pang must be instantly mastered—as though grace demanded Stoic insensibility—but I affirm that where there is rational soul, there also is accountability for what we permit within our inward city. The man who falls into fear at sudden peril may yet keep his heart upright if he quickly calls upon God; but he who indulges fear long after grace offers strength—he begins to fashion chains with his own consent.

Even among Platonists and Stoics one finds recognition that passions must be trained; yet they lack what we possess: grace through Christ Jesus. For without Him we can do nothing—not even rightly govern our griefs. It is His Spirit who renews our wills so that even our tears may belong to virtue rather than vice.

question: How can the will be both the source of sin—such as in your theft of the pears—and the means of moral restoration, as when you finally turned to God in the Milan garden?
answer:
It is not the will itself that is corrupt by nature, as though some intrinsic malignity were woven into its substance, but rather the direction of the will—its orientation toward or away from the Supreme Good—that determines its moral quality, and thus whether it becomes the instrument of iniquity or the organ of righteousness. The Platonists, admirable in their ascent toward truth, yet falter when they suppose the soul’s disorder arises solely from its conjunction with matter; they imagine that purity is restored simply by separation from the body, and corruption reenters only when the soul descends again into flesh. But I have shown in my Confessions—and experience confirms—that even when abstracted from bodily sensation, the soul may hunger for return to visible things; that desire, joy, fear, sorrow are not merely transmitted by nerves and senses but arise within the soul’s own motion, stirred not by flesh but by its own disordered love.

Therefore, if these affections exist apart from bodily infection—if Plotinus himself acknowledges that the purified soul may yet fall through longing—then sin must have a deeper root than physical contagion: it lies in the will’s aversion from God. When I stole those pears not out of hunger but out of a perverse delight in transgression, it was not my stomach that drove me nor my hands that first sinned—it was a will turned toward emptiness for the sake of darkness. That act was not necessity; it was election. I chose not what lacked value altogether—for even stolen pears have form and existence—but I chose them wrongly: displaced in order, elevated beyond measure, loved without reference to Him from whom all goodness flows.

But just as freely as I turned away, so too could I turn back; and here is grace triumphant over nature. In Milan, beneath the fig tree’s shade, no limb compelled me to weep nor ear forced me to hear “Take up and read.” The same faculty by which I had embraced vanity now recoiled at last—not through philosophical effort alone (though Cicero once stirred my intellect), nor through strength accrued by discipline (though abstinence had its role), but because divine mercy had begun to heal my will. It was grace that made my heart willing where before it had been obstinate.

Thus does the City of God differ from the City of Man: one lives by faith ordering desire toward eternity; the other boasts in autonomy while enslaved to shifting winds of appetite. In both realms men feel desire and sorrow—these motions are common—but in one city they are governed by love of God; in the other, lorded over by love of self even unto death. The will is neutral neither in origin nor destiny: created good, fallen through pride, restored only through humility—the gift of One who first loved us while we still despised Him.

So then let no man say: “My flesh compelled me,” as though virtue were impossible so long as we walk clothed with mortality. For even bodiless souls may err—and yet here below we may be healed: not because we pull ourselves upward into light, but because He descended into our night and reoriented our disordered loves toward their true center. The will sins when it chooses lower goods as ends; it is saved when grace enables it to choose rightly—and this choice is already participation in eternal rest.

question: You say the will makes affections right or wrong—did you have specific moments in your life, like your mother Monica’s prayers or Ambrose’s sermons, that helped you reorient your will toward God rather than bodily desires?
answer:
That the soul can suffer agitation apart from bodily influence—indeed, that it may will its own re-enslavement even after purification—proves not merely the profundity of its disorder, but the radical autonomy of the will, which no mere purification of substance can rectify; and this truth I came to know not in speculation only, but in the long war within my own breast. For what was my condition during those years when I had forsaken the delusions of Manichaeus yet lingered in the vestibule of faith, drawn by Ambrose’s wisdom and pierced by Monica’s tears? I understood the better path, yet loved the lower pleasures; I stood at the threshold of conversion, and wept—not because I was unwilling to will, but because I was unwilling to will wholly.

The philosophers had taught me that reason should rule the soul, as Plato places the charioteer above the horses; yet in me reason held the reins slack, while desire pulled violently toward earthly delight. What availed me Cicero’s exhortation to virtue or Varro’s learned disquisitions when my will remained divided—divided against itself, and thus divided from God? It was not lack of knowledge that bound me, nor ignorance of truth; rather, it was the power of habit, that second nature formed by repeated consent to sin. The body did not compel me—no more than it compels those disembodied souls who yet long to return—but my own will delayed its surrender.

And so divine providence worked through instruments most human and most real: Monica’s ceaseless prayers ascended like incense before God long before they bore fruit in my heart. She wept for me more than a mother for a dead son—and rightly so: for I was dead while living. Ambrose showed me how Scripture could feed both intellect and spirit; he unlocked David’s Psalms so that they became cries from my own soul—even when my life contradicted them. But still I wavered.

The turning point—the moment when grace seized not merely my mind but my will—came when I heard a child’s voice saying “Take up and read.” Then I took up Paul’s letter to the Romans and read: “Not in rioting and drunkenness… but put on the Lord Jesus Christ.” It was as if a light flooded my innermost darkness. Not argument alone—not beauty nor duty nor fear—but grace reoriented my love. For virtue is not achieved by effort alone; it is received as gift. And when love is rightly ordered by divine infusion, then desire becomes holy longing; joy becomes delight in truth; fear becomes awe before holiness; sorrow becomes contrition that cleanses.

Thus do I affirm: affections are made right not by eradication nor philosophical discipline alone, but by charity inscribed upon the heart by God Himself through Christ. My will did not rise unaided—it was lifted.

question: You said that desire and joy are forms of volition—how did your own struggle with worldly desires in Carthage shape your understanding of will?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, names desire freedom, and calls joy the fruit of possession—yet in that very naming reveals the disorder of its will. I speak not only as a bishop but as one who walked those streets of Carthage where the soul is bartered daily for pleasure, where reason becomes servant to appetite and philosophy a mask for vanity. There, amid the theater's laughter and the lover’s whisper, I learned not by speculation alone but by shipwreck what the will truly is: not some neutral faculty balanced between good and evil, but a force bent toward one city or the other—either rising in love to God or collapsing into self-love.

What I desired—women, praise, eloquence—I desired not because they were false in themselves, but because my will had lost its orientation. Joy became a fever; desire, a chain. And in that bondage I saw how every motion of the soul—fear at loss, grief at separation—is still an act of will turned from eternal good to temporal illusion. The man who mourns a mistress is not simply suffering sorrow; he is worshiping an idol he calls love. The student burning with ambition does not merely wish knowledge; he seeks glory for himself alone.

Only when grace pierced me—not through argument first, but through tears—did I understand that the will cannot heal itself. It must be seized by higher love. No discipline of Stoic apathy could free me; only Christ, who reorients desire toward beatitude that does not pass away. Now I see: to live according to God is not to destroy natural affections, but to reorder them under charity—to hate vice indeed, even fiercely—but never to hate the man made in His image. For even in Carthage, amid my ruin, God loved me still; and His love was stronger than my willfulness.

question: When you speak of hating vice but loving the man, do you have specific people in mind, like the Manicheans you once followed?
answer:
Justice, which the philosophers seek but cannot fully attain, resides only where love orders the will according to divine truth, and it is precisely in such justice that we must learn to disentangle the person from his corruption. The Manicheans—yes, those once dear to me in my long wandering—serve as a most poignant example. They taught me to divide the world into two co-eternal principles, one good and one evil, as if being itself were fractured at its root. But I have come to see that evil is not a substance, nor a principle equal to God; it is privation, disorder of the will, a turning away from the One who is wholly good. Thus, when I speak of hating vice yet loving the man, I do not speak in abstraction—I speak of men like Faustus, whose eloquence once dazzled me, or like my younger self: proud in intellect, fractured in desire, ensnared by dualism’s lie.

I hated their errors with all constancy of soul—not with fury nor malice—but with the zeal of one who has been delivered from them and would wish no brother to remain bound. Yet never did I cease—or may I ever—to pray for them as sons of Adam, capable of repentance, made for adoption. The vice must be condemned absolutely; it chains the soul and defiles creation. But the man—even the heretic—is still an image-bearer, still within reach of grace. To love him is not to condone his error; it is to desire his healing.

Even now I watch how this distinction governs cities: Rome fell not because its gods abandoned it—Varro’s speculations notwithstanding—but because its love was misdirected; it cherished glory over justice, power over mercy. So too does every soul fall when it loves vice more than truth. And yet God patiently draws even such souls—the persecutors like Paul once was, the skeptics like I once was—not hating them in their darkness but calling them forth into light.

Thus let us be precise: no vice escapes condemnation before God's judgment seat. But no human being—no bearer of reason and longing for happiness—should be cast beyond hope or beyond love’s reach. For we ourselves were once darkness; now we are light in the Lord.

question: You claim fear and sadness are acts of will—does that mean we can simply choose not to feel them, even in suffering?
answer:
The City of Man, in its proud reckoning, supposes that the soul’s affections are either diseases to be cured by reason or passions to be mastered by force of will; but the City of God knows that every motion of the soul—fear, sadness, desire, joy—is a turning of the will toward or away from some perceived good or evil. Hence, when you ask whether we may simply choose not to feel fear or sadness, you touch upon a matter deeper than self-mastery: you inquire into the very structure of human volition under the shadow of sin and the light of grace.

Fear and sadness are indeed acts of will—not in the sense that they are arbitrary choices like selecting a garment, but because they arise from the soul’s judgment about what is to be shunned as harmful or lost as precious. The will does not create these affections ex nihilo; it consents to them when it apprehends reality through love. A man fears death not because his nerves tremble—that is but the body’s echo—but because his will clings to this temporal life as if it were the highest good. He grieves a loss not merely from habit, but because his love was fixed upon something mutable as though it were immutable.

Therefore, no man can simply "decide" away sorrow as one dismisses a servant, unless first his loves are reordered. The Stoic may command himself not to mourn and may even suppress the tears—but if his heart still chains itself to fortune, he remains in bondage beneath a sterner lash: hypocrisy. For he does not heal the will; he only silences its voice.

But grace does what philosophy cannot: it heals love. The man who lives according to God does not deny sorrow—Christ wept at Lazarus’ tomb—but he sorrows without despair, because his will is anchored beyond death. He fears evil deeds more than suffering; he dreads sin more than loss. Thus his fear and sadness are turned into instruments of virtue: fear guards him from pride, sorrow purifies attachment.

We do not escape suffering by an act of sheer will—we transcend it by an ascent of love. And that ascent is not ours by nature; it is given by Him who descended first.

question: How can the will be both the source of sin—like in your theft of the pears—and the means of loving good and hating evil?
answer:
The City of Man, restless in its loves, disorders the will by making desire its own idol; whereas the City of God, founded upon the immutable Good, orders that same will toward its true end in the love of God. It is not the will itself that is corrupt—no, for the will was made good by the Creator—but its orientation, when turned away from higher things toward lower, becomes twisted into sin. In that stolen fruit from the pear tree, it was not hunger I sought, nor beauty, nor use; there was no object before me worthy of desire. Yet I stole not for gain, but for the pleasure of transgression itself—for this alone: to act against divine order and to taste rebellion. That act was not an accident of weakness but a deliberate turning of my volition toward disorder.

For just as fire naturally rises unless weighed down by foreign matter, so does the human will naturally tend toward good when unimpeded by vice. But when habit forms in malice and concupiscence hardens into consent, then even freedom becomes a servant to sin. Therein lies the paradox: my will was free even as I sinned—free to choose evil instead of good—yet only through grace did it become free enough to choose rightly again.

To love good and hate evil requires not suppression of will but purification—a reformation under divine illumination. The man who lives according to God does not crush his affections with Stoic indifference; he redirects them according to truth. He hates falsehood in the sinner’s deed and yet loves him as a soul made in God’s image—an image obscured but never erased. This is possible only because love itself is an act of will: a choice ordered by charity rather than passion.

Thus it is grace which heals the will—not abolishing it as if it were nature’s flaw—but restoring it so that what once lusted after shadows now cleaves to Light. Evil finds no substance; it is privation of due order. Only good has positive being—and therefore only good can be loved essentially. The righteous man hates vice because he sees clearly what distorts creation’s harmony; he loves men because he sees in them what God intended before time began.

So let no one say: "If my will causes sin, how can it also bring salvation?" Rather ask: "Whom do I serve? Sin unto death? Or obedience unto life?" For every soul belongs either to a city built on self-love or one founded on love for God—and from that root grows every desire and every aversion rightly named or monstrously misdirected.

question: When you say no one is evil by nature, how then did your view of original sin influence your belief that vice corrupts what God originally made good?
answer:
The City of Man supposes that evil is a nature, either implanted by the gods or inherent in matter, and thus it excuses vice as fate; but the City of God proclaims that evil is a privation, a corruption of what was created upright, and therefore not from God but from the abuse of will.  

When I came to understand that no one is evil by nature, I did not deny the profound disorder we observe in human beings from their earliest breath—indeed, infants cry with wrath, and children learn to deceive. But this very disarray testifies not to an evil essence, but to a nature once whole now fractured. God made man good—very good—and breathed into him the image of His own wisdom and justice. Yet through that primal disobedience, which I now see not as a mere fable but as the real wound at history’s root, sin entered not only as act but as condition. The soul’s harmony was shattered: reason no longer rules the passions; desire strains against order; even the flesh resists its own good.

This is what I mean by original sin—not that guilt is arbitrarily imputed, but that human nature itself has been transmitted in a state of dislocation. The will, which should cleave first and always to God as its end, is born inclined toward lesser goods and turned inward upon itself. This inclination is not nature proper—it is nature wounded—yet it is universal because all descend from that one man in whom all sinned. Thus vice does not belong to our essence; rather, it usurps our being like a sickness distorting a healthy body.

And so I rejected both the Stoic who says we are born blank slates shaped only by discipline and the Manichaean who says we are made from evil matter. We are born good in substance—rational souls created for communion with God—but infected with concupiscence, that disordered movement of desire which St. Paul names *peccatum*, sin dwelling within us like an alien power warring against our better part.

But here lies hope: because evil is not nature but corruption, grace can heal it. Just as disease does not negate health but obscures it until medicine restores it, so too does Christ—the second Adam—bring life where death reigned. The same will which turned away from God can be turned back by divine mercy; love can be healed so that we desire rightly again.

Thus do I affirm both truths together: man is good in his origin and fallen in his state; he bears vice not by design but by inheritance; yet he may rise again—not by philosophy or willpower alone—but by participation in the City whose Founder is eternal righteousness itself.

question: Why did you emphasize the distinction between loving the man and hating the vice, and how did this reflect your own struggles with sin in your past life?
answer:
When Varro labored to harmonize the tumult of Roman gods with the order of philosophy, he revealed how deeply the City of Man desires to reconcile irreconcilables—truth with compromise, virtue with expediency, love with self-interest. But I found no such harmony in my own soul before grace: only war, only disarray, as I loved what I ought to have hated and hated what I ought to have loved. It was not until faith illumined reason that I saw the necessity of distinguishing between the man and his vice—not as though they were separable substances, but as we distinguish health from disease in one body, or light from shadow on a single face.

For many years I embraced both wisdom and lust, seeking truth in books while enslaving myself to pleasure. In Carthage, amid the applause of students and the pride of rhetoric, I admired Augustine the teacher while Augustine the sinner remained unknown to himself. How could one who taught coherence fail so utterly to live it? Because love had not yet been rightly ordered. Then came this piercing insight: God does not hate the creature because it is deformed; He hates only the deformation. And if God thus loves the soul even while warring against its sin, must not we do likewise?

This distinction—loving men while hating vice—is no mere rhetorical subtlety; it is written into the very logic of redemption. Christ did not die for abstract righteousness alone; He died for tax collectors and adulterers, for Paul who persecuted His Church and Peter who denied Him thrice. He loved them perfectly—and therefore He rebuked them without compromise. That divine pattern became my rule: neither condone sin out of misplaced mercy nor reject a sinner out of harsh judgment.

It is by grace that I now see what once was darkness: that charity corrects because it creates. To love a man truly is to will his good—and his good consists in conformity to God’s will. Therefore to tolerate his vice is not love but consent to his ruin. But neither is hatred ever permitted—even toward vice—if it becomes wrath untethered from compassion.

Thus does the City of God walk in this world: holding fast both justice and mercy, knowing that only in their union do we reflect Him who is at once Judge and Saviour.

question: When you spoke of the apostle's requirement that a church ruler be a 'lover of good,' were you drawing from your experience in appointing leaders in the African churches?
answer:
The City of Man seeks rulers adorned with eloquence and birth, esteeming power that dazzles the eyes of the crowd; but the City of God ascends by humility and descends only into corruption when it imitates such vanity. It was not from mere administrative custom among the African churches that I insisted upon the apostolic mandate—that a bishop must be a lover of good—but from the deep conviction that office without virtue is not only empty, but sacrilegious. When Paul laid down those qualifications, he did not fashion them according to Roman dignity or Punic ambition, but according to the mind of Christ, who chose fishermen over philosophers, and meekness over might.

In Hippo, where I labored amid faction and heresy, I witnessed how easily authority becomes a theater for pride when those who rule care more for honor than holiness. A man may be eloquent in speech and sharp in debate—qualities I once prized above all—but if he does not delight in truth for its own sake and seek the welfare of souls above reputation, he is a sounding cymbal before God. To appoint such a man is to build a tower on sand; the storm will come, as it always does—in persecution, in temptation, in schism—and what was erected for show will collapse into ruin.

Thus the requirement that a ruler be a "lover of good" is no mere formality. It speaks to disposition, to orientation of love—*dilectio* directed rightly toward what God loves. For as we have seen in the great drama of history, even Rome itself rose not by justice but by ambition, gloried in force rather than faithfulness, and so fell as all earthly cities must when they measure greatness by dominion instead of charity. But among us who live within the fellowship of Christ’s Body there must be another standard: not one who commands well alone, but one who loves holiness with an undivided heart.

And this love—whether Scripture calls it *amor* or *dilectio*—is not divided in reality; it is one movement of grace bearing souls toward their true end: God Himself. Peter stammered his devotion at dawn on Galilee’s shore with words almost failing him—but Christ received his love because it was true. So too must our bishops stand before Him: not polished idols for public admiration, but men whose inner fire burns only for goodness because they have been seized by divine Love first.

question: How did your interpretation of the threefold exchange between Jesus and Peter shape your understanding of pastoral responsibility and personal repentance?
answer:
When the soul stands before the gaze of Him who sees all things not as a judge armed with condemnation but as a physician seeking healing, then love—true love—must pass through the fire of examination, not because it is doubted, but because it must be purified. The philosophers, even the noblest among them like Plato in his *Laws*, speak of justice as a harmony of parts within the soul and within the state; yet they fail to reckon with the wound that disarranges all harmony—the pride that clings to honor even when contrition is required. When Christ asked Peter three times, "Lovest thou me?" He was not measuring affection as though God needed reassurance; no, He was remaking the heart of His servant through a liturgy of restoration. Each question was a chisel upon the stone of memory: *Do you still claim supremacy? Do you still boast of your loyalty above the others?* For had not Peter once said, "Though all forsake You, I will never"—and then fallen more grievously than any?

Thus Christ dismantled both denial and presumption. He did not restore Peter by silence or swift absolution alone, but by repetition—not to torment him, but to instill into his pastoral soul the rhythm of mercy: that he who feeds the sheep must first have been fed by humility; that he who tends souls must know how often his own heart has strayed. The shift between *diligis* and *amas* in that dialogue is no mere synonymity for style's sake—it reveals how divine pedagogy enfolds human frailty. Love (*amor*) flows from nature; regard (*dilectio*) from choice. By varying the terms, Christ drew Peter into an ever-deeper act of self-offering: not merely emotional attachment (*amo*), nor merely rational approval (*diligo*), but total self-gift—what we rightly name charity when it is ordered by grace.

Hence pastoral authority is born not from office alone, nor from zeal unchecked, but from repentance made fruitful. He who rules must first have knelt—not once only at conversion’s hour, but daily beneath the question: “Lovest thou me?” It is this continual return to first love—wounded yet willing—that fits a man to shepherd others through their own betrayals and restorations. For if Peter had not wept at dawn’s shore over threefold failure met with threefold commissioning, he might have governed with severity rather than solicitude.

So let no shepherd forget his tears; let no ruler presume upon his rank. The City of God advances not by triumphal decree but through penitent love raised up again by Him who conquered death—not to reign apart from weakness, but in weakness perfected.

question: You noted that 'amor' and 'dilectio' are used interchangeably in Scripture—did you see this linguistic flexibility as revealing something deeper about divine love in your theological framework?
answer:
True justice, that which sustains any community worthy of the name, flows not from the shifting consensus of men but from the eternal law inscribed in the very order of creation — and it is this same justice, perfected in charity, that governs the City of God. Now, with respect to your question about *amor* and *dilectio*, I did not regard their interchangeability in Scripture as mere linguistic convenience, but as a profound disclosure of the unity and simplicity of divine love. For just as God is not divided in His nature, so too His love is not fractured into competing affections: it is one, holy, and indivisible.

In human speech we are often forced to distinguish between loves — carnal or spiritual, disordered or upright — because our hearts are torn between two cities. But in God there is no such division; His love is always pure act, always directed toward what truly *is*, and thus toward what truly *ought* to be loved. When Scripture uses *amor* or *dilectio* without rigid distinction, it mirrors this divine simplicity. The apostle Peter stumbles over three questions not because Christ shifts between meanings like a sophist changing terms, but because each word carries the full weight of total self-giving — whether expressed through intimate affection (*amo*) or steadfast commitment (*diligis*). 

This fluidity reveals that grace does not abolish nature but perfects it: even our most fragile human affections are drawn up into the one Love that moves the sun and the stars. And thus, when we are commanded to love God with all our heart and our neighbour as ourselves, we are not being instructed in some abstract virtue apart from feeling or will; rather, we are called into participation with that very Love by which He first loved us — One Word spoken eternally from the Father’s bosom.

question: In your view, does the fact that Christ shifted from 'diligis' to 'amas' in the third question suggest a deliberate emotional intensification, and how did that affect your own preaching on Peter's restoration?
answer:
The City of Man traffics in gradations of honor, in the measured reckonings of dignity and reply, where every word is weighed for insult or elevation; but the City of God moves by deeper rhythms—the pulse of charity restoring what sin has shattered, and truth humbling before love. In that upper room by the sea, when the Risen Lord drew breath to question Simon a third time, He did not shift from *diligis* to *amas* to sharpen the blade of rebuke, as if parsing synonyms for greater emotional torture. No—He descended into the very tongue of Peter’s own frailty. Twice He had asked, "Do you hold me in steadfast regard?"—using *diligis*, a term weighted with deliberation, with duty, with moral choice. Peter each time replied in softer tones: "I love (*amo*) You," speaking not of office or obligation but of affection, loyalty, the warmth of friendship betrayed yet not extinguished.

Then Christ lowers Himself: "Lovest (*amas*) me?" He takes up Peter’s own word, meets him in his weakness. It is not intensification for the sake of pain—Peter’s grief arises not because he is pressed harder but because he sees clearly: *the Lord condescends*. The Logos who orders cosmos stoops to speak in the dialect of human shame and mended trust. This is grace: not a tribunal demanding precise answers under threat, but a physician probing wounds so as to heal them.

In my preaching at Hippo, I have taught this moment not as linguistic drama but as divine pedagogy. The Church—the city built upon love ordered toward God—thrives not where men are tested by exactitude of expression but where repentance is met with condescension that lifts. As I once wandered through pride and philosophy seeking truth without humility, so Peter had loved Christ but denied Him when tested. Now Christ rebuilds him not with reproach but by entering his language—by accepting imperfect love as seedbed for perfect charity.

Thus I taught: God does not demand flawless words before He restores; He perfects our speech by first accepting our stammering hearts. And this is the way of the heavenly city: it does not glory in precision that excludes, but in mercy that re-creates.

question: Why did you make a point of analyzing the different words for love—'amas' and 'dilectio'—used in Christ's conversation with Peter?
answer: When Cicero, in his treatise *De Amicitia*, speaks of *dilectio* as the bond of true friendship among virtuous men, he touches—though dimly—upon a truth the light of which shines fully only in the revelation of Christ: that love is not divided against itself by words, but unified in its source. I examined the terms *amas* and *dilectio* not to indulge in grammatical subtlety for its own sake, nor to follow certain philosophers who would fracture the soul’s affections into rigid categories, but to defend the integrity of divine charity as it flows from God and returns to Him, unmarred by artificial distinctions. The world—the City of Man—delights in parsing speech to elevate human wisdom, as if clarity could be found in terminology rather than in grace. But when Christ stood upon the shore and restored Peter with a threefold question, He did not weigh lexical precision; He sought contrition, fidelity, the movement of a heart once proud now broken and healed. Peter answered always with *amo*, because his love was one—imperfect, yet sincere—offered wholly to the Lord who knew all things. The Scriptures themselves do not play these games: they use *amor*, *dilectio*, *caritas* interchangeably when speaking of holy love, because God’s love is not fragmented like ours; it is eternal, simple, and complete. To suppose otherwise is to bring into sacred doctrine a scholastic pride unworthy of humility. Faith does not require us to dissect love into species; it calls us instead to live it—toward God above all, even unto death—and thus enter the City whose Founder is Charity itself.

question: When you say that secular literature does not distinguish between 'amor' and 'dilectio,' are you drawing on your own experience with classical authors like Virgil or Cicero?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless striving, has often sought to divide and rank affections as though the soul’s love could be parsed into compartments of higher and lower, worthy or base, by mere change of term—yet the City of God knows that love is one in substance, however it be named, when it is ordered toward its proper end. I speak not idly when I say that even the noblest authors of Rome and Greece make no such division as some would now impose between *amor* and *dilectio*. I have read Cicero’s treatises on friendship, where he exalts *amor* as the bond most consonant with virtue; I have pondered Virgil’s lines, where *amor* moves heroes and gods alike—not always well, to be sure, for fallen nature distorts even good inclinations—but the word itself is not tainted by its misuse. The philosophers themselves do not shrink from speaking of divine *amor*, Plato most notably in his dialogues where love ascends like a ladder from beauty seen to Beauty itself. 

Thus it cannot be sustained that *amor* belongs only to disordered affection while *dilectio* signifies upright choice. To suppose so is to misunderstand both our own Scripture and the wisdom preserved among the pagans. In truth, Scripture employs *amor*, *dilectio*, and indeed *caritas* interchangeably: Abraham loved (*dilexit*) Isaac; yet also Scripture says God so loved (*dilexit*) the world—same word—and we are commanded to love (*diligere*) our neighbor as ourselves. But when John records Peter’s sorrowful reply—“Lord, Thou knowest that I love (*amo*) Thee”—he uses the verb of deepest personal devotion, not moral calculation. And Christ accepts it. He does not correct Peter’s language; He sees the heart.

Therefore let no man suppose that holy Writ maintains a philosophical precision foreign to common usage—even less one contradicted by usage itself. We who dwell in the City of God speak plainly: love ordered to God is true love, whether called *amor* or *dilectio*; and all other loves are shadows until they return to Him from whom they proceed.

question: You mention that some people think 'amor' refers to evil love and 'dilectio' to good—did you encounter this view among certain Christian teachers of your time?
answer:
It is one thing to distinguish vices by their objects, another to suppose that the Latin tongue, as if bound by philosophical decree, must assign *amor* to concupiscence and *dilectio* to virtue—as though the soul’s movement toward any good or evil could be rigidly partitioned by mere diction. This error I did indeed encounter, not among the unlearned but among certain Christians who, enamored of Platonic subtleties, sought to impose a linguistic hierarchy foreign to both Scripture and the common use of speech. They argued that *amor*, being more ardent and natural, belonged properly to disordered affections—such as lust or greed—while *dilectio*, being rational and deliberate, was reserved for upright choice. But such a division dissolves upon inspection.

Consider Peter weeping at the Lord’s third question: not because Christ accused him of loving poorly, but because he perceived the depth of his own heart laid bare before omniscience. The very fact that the Evangelist shifts between *amas* and *diligis* without change in meaning confirms what all who read Holy Writ attentively must conclude: that these terms are not moral indicators but rhetorical variations on one reality—the soul’s adherence to its object. The sacred authors themselves use *amor Dei*, *dilectio Dei*, and *caritas* interchangeably when describing our due orientation toward God.

And why should we be surprised? Did not even Cicero speak of *amor patriae*, love of country, as a noble passion? Did Plato himself not exalt *eros*—a kindred flame—as the force that draws the soul upward toward truth and beauty? If philosophers confess love's power for good when rightly directed, how much more ought Christians recognize that it is not the name but the end that makes an affection just or unjust. Thus in our Scriptures—divinely inspired and surpassing all human wisdom—we find no such lexical dichotomy; rather, we are taught that whether one loves (*amat*) God or mammon, whether he loves (*amat*) virtue or vice, it is the object that sanctifies or corrupts the act.

Therefore let no man suppose that a syllable can contain morality; let him instead examine where his heart rests. For it is not by changing words but by turning toward Jerusalem—not Babylon—that we pass from the City of Man into the City of God.

question: What do you mean by saying the Scriptures use 'amor' in a good connection—can you point to a specific passage that convinced you of this?
answer:
When the philosophers of Athens debated the nature of love, they distinguished *eros*, *philia*, and *agape*, as if the soul’s affections could be neatly partitioned by reason alone; yet when Christ stood on the shore of Tiberias and asked Peter, “Lovest thou me?”—He did not appeal to dialectic, nor did He demand a definition, but a confession from the depths of a soul once proud and now broken. It is there, in that trembling exchange between the risen Lord and the penitent fisherman, that we see how Scripture elevates *amor* not as a mere sentiment or carnal impulse, but as the very movement of the will toward God—ordered, surrendered, and purified by grace.

You ask for a passage that convinced me; I do not point to one in isolation, for the whole tenor of divine revelation breathes this truth. But consider how John himself writes in his epistle: “We love (*dilectio*) because He first loved (*amor*) us.” Here, side by side, the terms are interwoven—not as synonyms chosen for poetic variation, but as threads of one seamless garment. The Evangelist does not hesitate to say *amor* where reverence is due; nor does he shrink from placing it upon God’s own heart. If *amor* were intrinsically base or dangerous when applied to divine things, would John have dared so? Would Christ have accepted Peter’s reply—“Thou knowest that I love (*amo*) Thee”—if that word were unfit for heavenly affection?

Indeed, among the pagans there may be those who restrict *amor* to passion and appetite—yet even they confess its power when directed toward virtue or beauty or wisdom. Plato speaks of love as a ladder ascending to the Good; Cicero calls friendship born of virtue a sacred thing. But we Christians know more: we know that God Himself is Love—not merely that He possesses love or commands it—but that His essence is this very *amor*. And if God **is** Love (*caritas*, *dilectio*, *amor*), then these words cannot be fundamentally divided in value or dignity before Him.

Thus Scripture uses *amor* in its highest connection: not only permitted but exalted. When Paul declares that nothing can separate us from the love (*amore*) of Christ—when Augustine (and forgive my self-reference) cries out in confession that “Thou hast made us for Thyself”—it is not cold duty he sings of, nor abstract allegiance; it is fire—burning desire ordered toward its true end. That is holy *amor*. And unless such love fills our hearts, all our works are but sounding brass and clanging cymbal.

question: Since you value Scripture above all writings, why do you still bring up what the philosophers say about love, even if only to contrast them with biblical usage?
answer:
When Plato ascends to the realm of forms and beholds love as a daemon mediating between the mortal and the divine, he stirs the soul toward truth—yet halts short of revelation, where charity is not a ladder of aspiration but a gift descending from above. This is why I engage the philosophers: not because their wisdom surpasses Scripture, which alone holds supreme authority, but because God has not left Himself without witness in the ordered reasoning of men. The natural light by which they discern fragments of truth—such as the nobility of love directed toward the good and even toward the gods—is itself a reflection of that one True Light which enlightens every man coming into the world.

To see this is to fulfill a sacred dialectic: we correct error, yes, but we also reclaim what was always His. When Cicero speaks of friendship as a bond rooted in virtue, or when Plotinus yearns for union with the One, they grope after realities fulfilled only in Christ, who is both object and origin of all love. The distinction some seek to impose—between *amor* as base affection and *dilectio* as pure regard—is foreign not only to Scripture but even to their own best intuitions. For do they not praise lovers who sacrifice themselves for friends? Do they not exalt those who scorn earthly delights for wisdom’s sake? Then let them admit that *amor*, rightly ordered, is no less noble than *dilectio*.

Thus I bring forward their words not as rival authorities, but as echoes awaiting fulfillment. Faith does not silence reason; it orders it toward its final end. And when Christ asks Peter, “Diligis me?”—using terms interchangeably—it is no mere linguistic accident. It is divine pedagogy: love is one thing in substance, whether expressed in human affection or divine gift, so long as it flows from grace and returns to God. The philosopher may name it partially; the believer knows it wholly—as *caritas*, fire from heaven; as *amor*, transformed by obedience; as *dilectio*, perfected by will conformed to Truth itself.

question: You cited the psalm saying 'He that diligit iniquity hateth his own soul'—why did you choose this passage to show that _dilectio_ can be used in a bad sense, when many assume it only means holy love?
answer:
When Varro, in his careful dissection of Latin speech, supposed that certain words were by their nature suited only to noble or divine things, he failed to perceive the deeper truth that moral quality inheres not in the word, but in the direction of the soul’s movement. It is not the term *dilectio* that sanctifies the love, but the object to which it is ordered. The Stoics may have imagined that language could be purified so as strictly to reflect virtue, but Scripture reveals a more profound realism: even those words we would reserve for heavenly affections—like *dilectio*—are employed where the heart turns toward vanity and sin.

Therefore I cited the psalmist’s declaration—"He that diligit iniquity hateth his own soul"—not to shock, but to correct a dangerous illusion: that mere verbal precision can safeguard moral truth. If *dilectio*, so often lifted in prayer as if it belonged only to God’s service, is used by sacred writ for one who clings with affection to wickedness, then we must understand that what matters before God is not which word we speak, but where our will rests. The Apostle John echoes this when he warns that whoever *diligere* the world excludes from himself the love of the Father. Here is no mere semantic curiosity—it is a judgment on the human heart.

Language, after all, was given by God to signify reality, not to mask it. And since love—whether named *amor*, *dilectio*, or *caritas*—is an act of will toward some end, it takes its character from that end. A philosopher may construct categories as though words alone could cleanse desire; but Scripture strips us bare. It shows us that affection turned toward temporal glory or selfish pleasure remains corrupt—even when clothed in dignified terms. For love rightly ordered refers us always upward; disordered love drags us downward—even if its voice sounds pious.

Thus I chose this passage not merely to refute linguistic purism, but to expose what all history discloses: cities rise and fall not because they lack eloquence or fine terms for virtue, but because their loves are ill placed. The City of Man may speak softly of duty and affection while loving power; the City of God learns from such warnings as these—that no word can save us unless our wills are reconciled with divine charity.
