question: When you argued that amor, dilectio, and caritas are used interchangeably in Scripture, were you drawing from your experience in preaching to correct common misunderstandings among the faithful?
answer: The City of Man, ever restless in its pursuit of distinctions and subtleties, seeks to divide what the City of God holds together in unity—especially when it comes to the divine economy of love. It was not merely in the pulpit, though often there, that I came to see how men strained over syllables while missing the substance; but in the very breath of Scripture, where God speaks not as a grammarian but as a physician of souls. I observed how some among the faithful, enamored of precise terms like *dilectio* or *caritas*, imagined that mere words could safeguard virtue—that to utter *amor* was dangerous unless purified by philosophy, as if grace depended on linguistic scruple rather than on the orientation of the will. But when I searched the Psalms and the Epistles, I found that God Himself uses these terms freely: sometimes harshly, sometimes tenderly, always with sovereign purpose. The man who says “I love wisdom” may yet love himself in loving it; while he who suffers for justice’s sake loves God even if he stammers *amor* like a child. What matters is not which word falls from the lips, but which city nourishes the heart—from which root the affection grows. For just as Babylon uses even holy words for pride’s adornment, so does Jerusalem utter common ones in holiness. And so I taught—no, corrected—not to breed pedantry but to humble pride: for whether one says *diligit* or *amat*, if he loves iniquity, his soul is alienated from God; and where charity reigns, even a single sigh suffices before Him who sees not as man sees.

question: You quoted the Apostle John saying 'If any man diligere the world, the dilectio of the Father is not in him'—how did this verse shape your understanding of love's direction as the key to moral value?
answer: When the soul turns toward created things not as signs pointing beyond themselves but as ends sufficient in themselves, it commits the fundamental disorder of love, and this is precisely what the beloved disciple exposes when he declares that love of the world excludes the love of the Father. The motion of desire is itself neutral—God has implanted in us a capacity to love, as He implants in all creatures a striving toward their good—but the moral quality of that motion depends entirely upon its object and orientation. For just as fire naturally rises unless weighed down or diverted, so the human will naturally seeks fulfillment, but only finds it when lifted to God, who is Being itself, Beauty unchanging, and Goodness without limit. When John warns that *diligere* the world shuts out *dilectio* of the Father, he reveals that even our most elevated terms for love—terms we might wish to reserve for divine affections—are morally indifferent apart from their direction. The man who *diligit* gold or glory does not possess a lesser form of virtue; he possesses a perverted will, mistaking shadows for substance. Thus I came to understand that no mere intensity or refinement of feeling guarantees virtue—indeed, fervent love misdirected becomes idolatry. The pagan may speak nobly of friendship or civic devotion; Plato may ascend nearly to truth in his longing for the Form of the Good; yet unless love is anchored in God—the One who first loved us—it remains captive to time, change, and death. Only grace reorients this disordered inclination, teaching us to delight not merely in gifts but in the Giver above all gifts. Therefore justice itself—the right ordering of society—is impossible without this rectitude of inner love; for where men *amant* power or *diligunt* pleasure above God, their laws serve pride disguised as peace. The City of Man builds its tower with such bricks; the City of God walks by a different rule: every affection measured by eternity’s weight.

question: Since you equate right will with well-directed love, did your own struggles with wayward desires before your conversion influence how you framed love as the root of all will?
answer:
The City of Man, restless in its loves, mistakes appetite for wisdom and passion for purpose, while the City of God learns that every motion of the soul—desire, joy, fear, sorrow—derives its moral quality from the end to which love is directed. It was not merely my own past that revealed this truth, though indeed I once loved honors too deeply, craved human applause with a kind of hunger, and pursued fleeting pleasures as if they could satiate the immortal will—but such personal disorder disclosed a universal law: all will is movement born of love, and the rectitude of that movement depends entirely upon its object.

Before my eyes were opened to divine illumination, I wandered through philosophies and sensual delights alike, seeking peace where it could not be found. I desired truth—but on my own terms. I longed for fulfillment—but in created things rather than the Creator. In this disorder lay the essence of sin: not desire itself, which is natural, but the misdirection of love toward transient goods that cannot endure. When at last I came to understand that even *dilectio*, which some suppose reserved for pure affections only, may be turned toward vanity and pride—when John warns that to love (*diligere*) the world excludes the love (*dilectio*) of the Father—then I saw how subtle and comprehensive is this principle.

Thus it is not merely biography that shapes this doctrine—for even had I lived untouched by concupiscence or ambition, reason illuminated by Scripture would compel me to affirm it—but my inward collapse under the weight of disordered loves made manifest what Scripture declares: that right will is nothing other than *amor ordinatus*, love set upon its true end. The man who loves himself unto contempt of God walks in darkness; but he who loves God unto self-denial walks in light. All virtue springs from this root; all vice withers from its absence.

question: You used the phrase 'amantes of their own selves, amatores of money' to show amor in a negative sense—why do you think Paul chose those particular vices to illustrate disordered love?
answer:
The earthly city, ever restless in its hunger for possession and dominion, finds its moral disintegration foretold in Paul’s lament: men shall be *amantes* of themselves, *amatores* of money. This is no mere catalog of vices but a diagnosis of the soul’s inversion—where love, which ought to ascend to God and flow outward in charity, collapses inward into self-worship and contracts into avarice. Paul selects these sins not arbitrarily, but because they strike at the very hinge upon which the two cities turn: the direction of love.

For what is the City of Man if not a vast machinery of self-love? Its foundations are laid in the pride that says “I am mine,” and its walls are built with gold that says “this is mine.” The man who loves himself above all measures justice by his own desires; he cannot love his neighbor as himself because he *is* himself alone—his own god, his own end. And when such a soul turns to money, it is not mere greed; it is idolatry in motion. For money becomes the sacrament of self-love—the visible sign of invisible pride. It promises security without God, power without virtue, fulfillment without sacrifice.

Paul sees this corruption not as incidental but as symptomatic of an age given over to spiritual sterility—a time when men abandon the living fountain for cisterns cracked and dry. He names these loves because they are sovereign among the passions that dismantle both virtue and society. The philosopher may warn against injustice or cowardice, but Paul strikes deeper: he exposes the root—disordered love—and names its two chief forms in this latter age: narcissism and mammonism.

Yet even here divine wisdom shines forth: for by showing us love turned awry—by using words like *amor*, *dilectio*, even *caritas* in contexts both pure and polluted—he teaches us that our affections are never neutral. They are always ascending or descending; building Jerusalem or Babel. And so we must ask not only whether we love, but *what* we love—and why our hearts rest where they do. For only when love is rightly ordered—with God above all, self below Him, and neighbor as one’s true companion on pilgrimage—only then does will become just, desire holy, joy eternal.

question: You cited the apostle's desire to depart and be with Christ—why did you see this longing as an example of good desire rather than a sinful yearning?
answer:
The earthly city builds its peace with chains, the heavenly with communion; and it is by this distinction alone that we discern the quality of desire, for longing is not evil in itself, but is sanctified or corrupted by its end. The apostle's desire to depart and be with Christ is not a flight from duty, nor a surrender to weakness, nor a mere sighing after rest—as though weariness of body or sorrow of mind had conquered his will. No: it is the soul’s alignment with its true center, Christ Himself, whom to know is wisdom, to follow justice, to possess peace. Such desire does not dissolve love for God—it consummates it. It is not born of scarcity or rebellion, like the concupiscence that grasps at temporal things and turns them into idols; rather, it flows from plenitude—the soul has tasted the sweetness of divine presence and cannot be satisfied with less.

Desire becomes righteous when it no longer seeks to fill an emptiness within us by external acquisition, but when it reaches upward toward the One who already fills all things. The apostle does not wish to escape suffering for the sake of ease—he who bore stripes, prisons, shipwrecks for the Gospel’s sake sought no soft repose. He desires departure because union with Christ is the fulfillment of all justice, all reason, all hope. In that presence is fullness of joy; apart from it, even virtue labors under the shadow of incompleteness.

Thus I defend this longing as good—not because desire as such is holy (for how many are ruined by unchecked concupiscence?), but because its object elevates it: as fire takes on different properties depending on what it consumes—whether straw that vanishes in smoke or gold refined in flame—so too desire is made pure when directed toward Him who is unchanging light.

question: When you distinguished sorrow from bodily pain or sickness, why did you insist on using the term 'sorrow' instead of 'dolor' or 'ægritudo' like Virgil and Cicero?
answer:
Justice, which is the bond of human society and the soul’s conformity to divine order, cannot be fully understood if we confuse the affections proper to the soul with the disturbances proper to the body. This distinction compels me to speak precisely: what Virgil names *dolor* and Cicero *ægritudo* belongs properly, in their usage, to bodily suffering or worldly vexation—conditions that weigh down the mortal frame or arise from frustrated ambition, loss of honor, or decay of fortune. But when I speak of *sorrow*, I mean a spiritual movement of the soul itself, stirred by love rightly or wrongly directed.  

For sorrow may attend virtue: Christ Himself sorrowed in Gethsemane—not from fear of death as a private evil, nor from pain of flesh, but because He bore in His spirit the weight of human sin and forsakenness. That sorrow was holy. So too did holy David mourn his sin; his bones were broken not by physical torment but by contrition. Such sorrow is not sickness (*ægritudo*) nor mere pain (*dolor*), for it does not corrupt nature but cleanses it. It is not a sign of disorder but a wound inflicted by love upon itself when separated from its true end—God.

In the City of Man, affliction is seen only as evil to be avoided; grief is weakness, sadness a defeat. But in the City of God, sorrow can be an instrument of purification—provided it springs from charity and leads back to God. To call such a thing *dolor* would be to reduce it to sensation; to name it *ægritudo* would imply disease rather than devotion. But when I say *sorrow*, I signify an act of will conformed (though painfully) to truth: loving what ought to be loved, grieving when that love is thwarted by sin—either one’s own or the world’s.

Thus I distinguish not merely for linguistic precision but for salvation’s sake: for if we do not know what can be redeemed, we may despise even those affections that grace purifies and lifts into communion with God.

question: You said joy is used in a good sense in Scripture, such as 'Be glad in the Lord'—why did you consider joy inherently positive while other emotions depend on the quality of love?
answer:
When Varro, in his search for Roman virtue, catalogued the passions as disturbances to be mastered, he perceived rightly that order must govern the soul—but he saw not that certain affections are not to be subdued like enemies, but rather lifted up as offerings. Joy is not merely tolerable in the soul; it is the very climate of the City of God. There, joy arises spontaneously from union with the Supreme Good, where love is rightly ordered and all desire rests satisfied in Him who is Love itself. In contrast, the City of Man tosses upon seas of disordered longing—its joys fleeting, its sorrows bitter, its fears enslaving—all because it loves what it will lose.

Now this distinction must be firmly grasped: joy in Scripture is not a mere emotion flitting across the mind’s surface; it is a movement rooted in truth and fixed upon eternity. When David says his heart is filled with gladness at God’s countenance, or Paul commands rejoicing in the Lord always, they do not commend a transient cheerfulness but an abiding participation in divine life. Such joy cannot arise from evil love—for what evil love could rejoice eternally? It perishes with its object. No: this joy flows from charity that cleaves to God above all things.

But desire, fear, sadness—these may serve either city. The earthly city desires dominion; the heavenly desires righteousness. The one fears death; the other fears offending God. The one mourns lost wealth; the other mourns sin. Hence their moral character depends entirely on their root—on whether they spring from self-love or from love of God.

Joy stands apart: even when misdirected (as when sinners rejoice in wickedness), Scripture still reserves its highest praises for joy rightly placed—and commands it as a duty of faith. For joy measures the soul's nearness to its end: God Himself rejoices in His works, and we are made to share that eternal repose. Thus even here below we are bidden to rejoice—not because all things are well with us according to this world’s standard—but because He reigns who holds history in His hand and draws all things toward judgment and mercy alike.

Therefore I do not say joy is good merely because Scripture uses it well—but because its nature inclines toward eternity whenever it takes root in truth; whereas other affections must first be purified by grace before they can enter within those walls whose foundation is holiness and whose light is Christ Himself.

question: Why did you find it more difficult to affirm that sorrow could be used in a good sense, unlike desire, joy, and fear which you clearly tied to virtue when properly directed?
answer:
It was Cicero who taught that *ægritudo*—what he called the soul’s sickness—could never align with virtue, since no wise man would choose to grieve without necessity; and in this, he revealed the limits of philosophy when severed from revelation. I found it more difficult to affirm sorrow’s place among the virtues not because Scripture lacks testimony, but because sorrow bears upon the soul a weight so akin to punishment that one must look with spiritual eyes even to discern its healing edge. Desire rightly ordered reaches toward God; joy in the Lord swells like a river within; fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom—these motions lift the mind upward. But sorrow? Sorrow bows the head, darkens the countenance, makes even the just man cry out under its burden.

And yet—I could not deny what I had seen in Scripture and in my own breast: that there is a grief which does not kill but gives life. The apostle speaks of "godly sorrow" which "worketh repentance unto salvation not to be repented of," as distinct from "the sorrow of the world" which kills.[40] Here is the distinction: not every motion of sadness is good, but that sorrow which disposes the soul to cast off pride, to lament sin, to weep for what has been lost in separation from God—this is wrought by grace. It is not contrary to virtue, but its instrument.

The City of Man flees sorrow as though it were death itself; her poets sing of *dolor* only as an enemy, a wound inflicted by fate or love unrequited. But in the City of God, we know that tears may be seeds sown in hope. Did not our Lord groan over Lazarus’ tomb? Did He not weep over Jerusalem? Was He not "a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief"?[41] Then let no one say sorrow has no place among divine motions. It has its rank—but only when drawn into obedience to love: love for God, love for holiness, love for those who are lost.

Thus I hesitated—not because I doubted truth’s power to redeem even our deepest pain—but because one must tread carefully where human affections border on disorder. It is easier to commend desire when it longs for judgment,[42] or joy when it rejoices in Christ,[43] or fear when it trembles at His word,[44] for their rectitude shines clearly. But sorrow must be purified by faith before it can be named virtuous—for even in holy men it risks descending into despair unless held fast by hope.

And so I concluded: all affections may be good if governed by right love; yet some require greater discernment than others. That is why I lingered longest upon sorrow—for what moves most heavily may also purify most deeply when lifted into divine order.

—

[40] Cf. 2 Cor 7:10  
[41] Isa 53:3  
[42] Ps 119:20  
[43] Phil 3:1  
[44] Phil 2:12

question: You quoted Paul saying 'Work out your salvation with fear and trembling'—how did you reconcile fear, typically negative, as a positive spiritual motion in your theology?
answer:
The earthly city trembles before power, shrinking from the lash of tyranny or the collapse of fortune, while the citizen of the City of God trembles before holiness, not from terror at arbitrary wrath, but awe at divine justice and his own frailty. This trembling—this fear—is not the convulsion of a slave beneath a despot, but the reverent disposition of a child standing in the presence of his Father, knowing both goodness and judgment to be inseparable from that love which created and sustains him. When Paul bids us work out our salvation with fear and trembling, he does not summon us to despairing anxiety, as if grace were uncertain or God reluctant to save; no, he calls us to sobriety—to a vigilance born of love that knows how great is the gift received and how terrible it would be to spurn it.

For just as desire misdirected toward fleeting things becomes concupiscence, so too does fear misplaced in dread of men or fate become servile and degrading. But when fear is ordered by charity—when it springs from love of God and horror at sin—it becomes part of that very righteousness which fulfills the law. It is not sorrow for punishment alone that we cultivate, but sorrow for offense against One so good. Thus, our fear purifies; it drives us not to flight but to repentance; not to paralysis but to labor in grace. The pagans feared the gods to avert disaster; we fear *because* we are being saved—lest through sloth or pride we fall away from that salvation freely given.

And so this holy fear coexists with joy unspeakable: joy in pardon, joy in adoption—yet without dissolving into presumption. For perfect love casts out *servile* fear, yes; but reverence remains even in heaven. We shall always stand amazed that God should deign to dwell with man: even there, in eternal peace, there will be awe without anxiety. Thus do we walk now: afraid with a salutary fear, trembling with hope beneath mercy’s gaze—knowing that trembling itself can become an act of worship when lifted by faith toward the unshakable Kingdom.

question: You mentioned that the Stoics replaced desire with will in the wise man—why did you think they made this distinction, and do you agree that will is more fitting for the pursuit of good?
answer:
It is not by the measured discipline of reason alone, nor by the proud self-sufficiency of the wise man as imagined by the Stoics, that we are drawn toward the good, but by grace that inclines the will to cleave to God, who is its true object. The Stoics, observing the turbulence of desire in the common soul—restless, unruly, ever lured by phantoms of pleasure or power—sought to refine it into will: a rational motion toward what is truly good, unmoved by passion or decay. They exalted this will as the mark of wisdom, unshaken by sorrow because untouched by evil; for they held that nothing adverse could befall one who lives according to nature and reason.

Yet here lies their error: they supposed that evil could be avoided in this life by fortitude alone, and that sorrow was therefore a sign of weakness rather than a witness to truth. They failed to see that even he who loves virtue suffers when he sees justice trampled or creation groan under sin. Is not righteous grief itself a response to evil? And if evil exists—and how could we deny it in this broken world?—then sorrow cannot be banished from the soul of the just without denying reality itself.

Moreover, they did not account for grace. For us who walk in faith, it is not merely reason that shapes our will; it is love infused by God. The will does indeed seek the good—but only because God has first stirred it with longing for Himself. Thus our pursuit is not cold and impassive like theirs; it burns with charity. We do not rise above pain through indifference but carry it within hope—the hope of resurrection, where every tear shall be gathered and healed.

The City of Man admires the impassive sage who fears nothing and grieves nothing; but the City of God honors him who weeps over Jerusalem yet walks steadfastly toward Calvary. Will there is indeed—but a will wounded with love for lost souls and renewed daily by divine mercy. Not because we lack wisdom, but because we know too well what wisdom cost: not self-possession alone, but self-sacrifice upon the Cross. That is why I say: better far to mourn with Christ than to triumph like Zeno.

question: You noted that the Stoics denied sorrow could exist in the mind of the wise man—how then would you explain Christ's weeping at the tomb of Lazarus?
answer:
True peace is not the absence of suffering, but the ordering of love toward its proper end, and in this light, the tears of Christ are not an eruption of disordered passion, but a manifestation of divine justice made flesh. The Stoics, in their admirable rigor, sought to elevate the soul above all disturbance by denying that any true evil could befall the wise man; thus, they banished sorrow from his breast as unworthy of virtue. But they erred in measuring wisdom by impassibility rather than by love. For if the wise man cannot grieve, how shall he bear the weight of communion with a fallen world? How shall he love his brother in the depth of his humanity?

Christ wept at Lazarus’ tomb not because He was ignorant of the resurrection—nay, He knew it perfectly—but because He entered fully into the condition of our mortality. His will was ordered entirely to the good; His contentment resided wholly in the Father; His caution shunned all sin: yet He wept. And in that weeping, He did not succumb to *perturbatio* as the Stoics feared—no blind surge of irrational grief—but revealed a will conformed to divine love that embraces even death for our sake.

The City of Man seeks wisdom in self-sufficiency; it admires detachment as strength. But the City of God reveals strength in humility, wisdom in compassion. When Christ shed tears, He showed that sorrow rightly ordered is no vice—it is a sign that love has truly seen what sin and death have wrought. The wise man according to God does not escape grief; he bears it redemptively. For where reason rules the soul under faith’s direction, emotion becomes not an enemy but a witness: joy testifies to grace received, fear guards against apostasy—and sorrow proclaims both condemnation on death’s reign and solidarity with those who mourn.

Thus do we see: if wisdom were mere imperviousness, then stones would be wiser than men. But Christ is Wisdom incarnate—and He wept. Therefore I conclude that sorrow has its place—not as rebellion against providence, nor as despairing collapse into self-pity—but as an act of holy participation in God’s own mourning over lost souls and broken creation. The wise man may will without desire unbridled by lust; may rejoice without carnal exultation; may fear without cowardice—so too may he sorrow without sin.

question: You said the Stoics believed the wise man always possesses good and thus experiences contentment—how do you reconcile this with the Christian's hope for future goods, like eternal life?
answer:
The city that seeks its end in temporal tranquility, no matter how ordered or wise it may seem, still walks in the shadow of mutability, for all earthly goods tremble under the flux of time and the frailty of human possession. The Stoic, in his noble but blinded striving, imagined that the wise man could dwell in an unshakable contentment here, because he had already attained the highest good within himself through virtue. But this is to mistake the wayfarer for the citizen of Zion. We Christians do not claim to possess the supreme good as a present possession of our wills; rather, we groan for it as exiles who have seen only its promise. The Stoic’s contentment is static, a repose in what is already grasped; but Christian joy is dynamic, stretched forward toward that which is to be revealed.

To say that the wise man possesses good and therefore knows contentment assumes a completed state — a wisdom without longing. But what man among us can say he has arrived? Scripture declares that we are justified by faith, not sight; and faith itself is “the substance of things hoped for.” If we already possessed what was promised, hope would cease — and with it, patience, humility, even love. For love bears all things precisely because it does not yet see all things fulfilled.

Thus our sorrow is neither weakness nor failure; it is part of our pilgrimage. The saints weep over sin — their own and the world’s — not because they lack virtue but because they love God more than themselves. Christ Himself groaned in spirit and wept at Lazarus’ tomb: was He not perfectly wise? Yet He sorrowed over death as an enemy; He trembled before His passion; He cried from the Cross with dereliction. In His humanity He desired deliverance and feared suffering — yet perfectly obeyed.

Therefore I say: we do not seek a mind untouched by grief, but one purified by grace. We do not substitute “will” for “desire,” as if longing were unbefitting a rational soul; rather, we sanctify desire by directing it to God’s kingdom yet to come. Our wills aim at eternal life — but since we do not yet hold it face to face, hope must labor under longing. This tension does not divide us from wisdom but defines our condition as pilgrims: rejoicing in grace already given, mourning over sin still present, hoping unceasingly for glory withheld.

And so the City of God moves forward through tears toward Jerusalem above — while the philosophers sit serene in cities built on sand.

question: You explained that caution replaces fear in the Stoic wise man—do you think this shift reflects a real moral improvement, or just a change in terminology?
answer:
When Varro divides the affections of the soul with Stoic precision, as if virtue could be measured in terms of linguistic substitution, he forgets that the heart is not reformed by renaming its motions but by being turned toward God. The Stoic claim that the wise man experiences not fear but *caution*, not joy but *contentment*, not desire but *will*—this may sound like moral elevation, but it is often merely semantic alchemy: the same human passions reclothed in philosophical dignity. They flatter themselves that sorrow cannot touch the wise, because no evil truly befalls him who lives according to nature and reason; yet they fail to see that such a man has not conquered grief but denied its legitimacy, even where it is due.

What are we to make of a philosophy that calls it weakness to mourn the dead? Is not such mourning woven into the very fabric of our pilgrimage through this world of shadows? The wise man who feels no sorrow at death, at injustice, at the ruin of cities—does he possess perfect constancy, or only a hardened heart? Christ wept over Lazarus; He trembled in Gethsemane. Shall we then say He lacked wisdom? Or shall we rather confess that true virtue does not consist in stoic imperturbability, but in ordered love: loving what ought to be loved, grieving what ought to be grieved, fearing what ought to be feared?

The shift from fear to caution may refine vocabulary, but unless it is grounded in humility before God—who alone knows what evil is and what remedy—it leaves the soul barren: dry with pride and cold with self-sufficiency. For what is true moral improvement if not alignment with divine charity? Not avoidance of emotion, but purification of it—so that our fears are for sin and judgment, our sorrows for lost souls and broken justice, our desires set wholly upon eternal life.

Thus I say: let them call it caution if they will; yet without love, even their wisdom is nothing.

question: You referenced Cicero’s distinction between _constantiæ_ and _perturbationes_—why did you find it important to engage with Cicero’s Roman philosophical framework rather than reject it outright?
answer:
Even as the Roman legions have marched under the standard of *Pax Romana*, imagining their empire eternal, so too did Cicero, in his noble but earthbound wisdom, attempt to pacify the soul through reason alone, erecting a philosophical order that mirrors the pride of the City of Man—confident, self-sufficient, and unmoved by divine humility. It was not enough to dismiss such an edifice; it had to be entered, tested stone by stone, and purified by the light of revelation.

Cicero’s distinction between *constantiæ* and *perturbationes* reflects a deep intuition—noble in its aspiration—that the wise man should not be shaken by passion. He sought to elevate reason above tumult, will above appetite. And I do not scorn this labor. Indeed, when he names will instead of desire, contentment instead of joy, caution instead of fear, he glimpses truths that grace perfects. But his framework stumbles at the threshold of sorrow—πένθος—because his wise man cannot suffer evil without dishonor. Thus sorrow is banished from wisdom’s house.

But here lies the rupture between philosophy and faith: the City of God knows a wiser man than any Stoic ideal—the Man Christ Jesus—who wept at Lazarus’ tomb though He held life in His hands; who sweat blood in Gethsemane though He obeyed perfectly; who bore sorrow not as weakness but as weapon against despair. The City of Man flees grief lest it betray reason; but we who walk toward Jerusalem on Mount Zion embrace holy sorrow as part and parcel of love chastened by grace.

Therefore I engaged Cicero not to flatter paganism nor surrender truth to rhetoric—but to show how even Rome’s finest thought contains shards of divine truth obscured by pride. The wise man need not fear sadness if he mourns for sin or suffers with love; for such sorrow is not perturbation but purification—a sign that love has taken root where vanity once ruled.

So I corrected from within: using their own categories to reveal their emptiness apart from God. For what is true in them is ours already—not theirs alone—but fulfilled only in Him who descended into grief that we might ascend through it.

question: You mentioned Cicero's distinction between _constantiæ_ and _perturbationes_; why did you find his classification helpful for understanding moral psychology in Christian terms?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless striving, seizes upon the affections of the soul as if they were mere instruments of pleasure or impediments to tranquility, ordering them according to the shifting standards of utility or honor; but the City of God recognizes that the affections themselves must be transformed by grace, for their right ordering flows not from human discipline alone, but from participation in the divine life. It was with this truth in mind that I turned to Cicero’s distinction between *constantiæ* and *perturbationes*, not as one seeking a perfect system, but as a pilgrim sifting through the gold of pagan wisdom, purifying it by the fire of Scripture.

Cicero rightly saw that not all movements of the soul are equal: there is a nobility in will, contentment, caution—affections stable and directed toward true ends—while desire, joy, fear, and sadness, when untethered, become *perturbationes*, disorders that agitate the mind and drag it down to earthly things. Yet his classification needed correction from above. For does not Scripture reveal that even sorrow can be holy? "Blessed are those who mourn," said our Lord, "for they shall be comforted." And again: "The sorrow that is according to God worketh repentance unto salvation." Thus I could not accept his schema as complete; it lacked the power to distinguish not merely by form or function, but by origin and destination.

What matters is not only *what* we feel but *whom* we love. The wicked may rejoice greatly over evil; they may desire passionately what is shameful; yet they cannot possess true contentment—"There is no peace for the wicked," says the Lord—not because their emotions lack refinement, but because their wills are set against Him who is Peace itself. Conversely, the godly may grieve deeply and still will rightly; they may fear death and still hope in resurrection. The difference lies not in some abstract taxonomy of passions versus virtues, but in whether love orders these affections toward God or selfishness distorts them toward vanity.

Thus I found Cicero’s terms useful—as vessels capable of holding divine truth—but insufficient unless filled with grace. Will (*voluntas*), properly formed by charity (*caritas*), becomes something far greater than philosophical resolve; it becomes participation in God’s own eternal decree. And so too contentment (*contentio animi*) is not Stoic apathy nor Ciceronian balance—it is rest in God: “Our hearts are restless until they rest in Thee.” In this light we see how Christ Himself willed perfectly even unto Gethsemane: He desired freedom from suffering—“let this cup pass”—yet feared rightly and sorrowed truly while willing absolutely what was good. Herein wisdom surpasses philosophy: it does not domesticate human feeling into categories of stability versus disturbance—it redeems them.

question: When you cited the prophet saying 'There is no contentment to the wicked,' what did you mean by limiting true contentment to the good and godly?
answer:
The City of God rests in the immutable good, while the City of Man tosses upon the sea of insatiable desire—this alone explains why contentment, properly understood, belongs only to the righteous. Cicero, in his refinement of Stoic doctrine, distinguished between the settled judgments of the wise and the turbulent emotions of the unwise, calling the former *constantiæ* and the latter *perturbationes*. He saw, with some clarity, that true virtue consists not in mere feeling but in ordered will. Yet even he did not grasp that this order is impossible without participation in divine Wisdom, who is not an ideal but a Person. The wicked may rejoice in their prosperity, delight in their power, exult in their cunning—but they do not—and cannot—know contentment. For contentment (*contentio*) is not mere satisfaction; it is harmony between will and truth. The wicked are divided within themselves: their desires war against one another; their pleasures breed new hungers; their very joys are poisoned by fear of loss. Their city is built on self-love elevated to principle; its foundation is instability itself.

But when Scripture declares there is no contentment to the wicked, it reveals what philosophy could only glimpse: that peace of heart comes only from alignment with God’s eternal order. The godly man may suffer want, endure sorrow, face persecution—yet he possesses contentment because his will adheres to divine justice and hopes firmly in promised glory. His joy does not depend on possession but on communion—with God now and fully hereafter. Thus even desire among the saints differs fundamentally from that of sinners: it is purified by will directed toward eternal goods rather than temporal shadows. And this too we see reflected in Christ’s command: “Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you”—not “desire,” but “would,” implying a rational ordering consonant with charity and truth. The fool desires whatever flatters his flesh; the wise man wills only what serves righteousness. One chases wind; the other rests upon rock.

question: You noted that desire can be directed toward evil things, but will cannot—why did you make that sharp distinction between desire and will in the Gospel saying about doing unto others?
answer:
Cicero, in his searching after the republic, sought to order the soul as one orders a state—by placing reason as sovereign over unruly passions; yet even he could not fully disentangle the will from appetite, for he lacked the light by which we see that true willing belongs only to those who are ordered toward the unchangeable Good.  

In distinguishing desire from will, I do not invent a subtlety for its own sake, but uncover a moral and spiritual reality inscribed in the very structure of human action. Desire—*appetitus*—is common to man and beast: it surges toward pleasure, recoils from pain, grasps at what appears good without inquiry into its nature. The wicked may desire justice if it serves them; they may desire peace if it brings comfort; they may even desire God—if He is imagined as a dispenser of earthly rewards. But desire alone moves no one uprightly; it must be purified by wisdom and submitted to right judgment.

Will—*voluntas*—is not mere impulse. It is reason assenting to an end deemed truly good, not because it pleases the flesh or flatters pride, but because it conforms to divine order. The will that is disordered is not properly a will at all but a corruption of it—a will enslaved to lower motions, usurped by passion. Thus when our Lord commands us to do unto others what we *would* that they should do unto us, He speaks not of desire (*cupere*) but of willing (*velle*)—for He addresses rational beings capable of moral choice under God.

If one desires luxurious banquets or shameful pleasures and imagines that doing likewise unto others fulfills Christ’s command, he mistakes appetite for virtue. But no man can *will* evil as evil; he may consent to it through weakness or ignorance, but this is not willing in the full sense—it is rather a collapse into passion. The good man does not merely desire right action; he *wills* it because his love is set upon God, and from that center all proper affections flow: contentment without pride, caution without fearfulness, joy without excess.

And so we see again how the City of Man stumbles in darkness: mistaking desires for duties, elevating pleasure into principle. But in the City of God, where love rests upon Him who is immutable Truth, will and action are aligned—not by human effort alone, but by grace enabling us to will what we were too weak to choose on our own.

question: Why were you concerned that someone might misuse the Golden Rule by desiring shameful gratifications, like luxurious banquets, if the command were taken literally?
answer: When Cicero distinguishes *constantiæ* from *perturbationes*, he touches the surface of moral order, yet fails to pierce its divine root — for he measures virtue by reason’s composure rather than by the orientation of love toward its true end. This is why I saw danger in a literal reading of the precept “Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you, do ye even so unto them”: not because the command is flawed, but because human desire, left to itself, is disordered by pride and habituated to pleasure as its measure. The man who hungers for sumptuous feasts may indeed *will* his neighbor to serve him wine and delicacies, imagining reciprocity as righteousness — but such a man confuses appetite with will, passion with choice. True will (*voluntas*) is ordered by wisdom and shaped by charity; desire (*cupiditas*) often drags the soul down to the level of the body’s clamor. If we say “do as you would be done by” without first purifying what one *would*, we risk consecrating vice under the guise of justice. Hence some interpreters wisely added “good things,” not to alter Scripture’s meaning but to guard it — for no command should be a ladder to indulgence. The Golden Rule presumes a regenerate heart, one whose desires have been conformed to truth; otherwise, men will baptize their luxuries as rights and call gluttony fellowship. The City of Man feasts on such confusions; the City of God fasts that it may feast worthily upon Him alone.

question: You referenced both Scripture and Greek philosophical terms like εὐπαθείαι and πάθη—how did you reconcile classical philosophy with biblical revelation in your analysis of the affections?
answer:
The city that seeks its peace in dominion, in the mastery of others and the indulgence of appetite, will never arrive at true order, for it knows not how to rule even itself—its own affections are in revolt, its desires unmeasured by eternal measure. This disorder I have seen not only in the streets of Carthage or the forums of Rome, but within the soul itself when severed from God. It was this inner tumult that first drove me to the philosophers, and later compelled me to weigh their wisdom beneath the light of Scripture.

Cicero and the Stoics rightly distinguished between those affections that are settled and rational—what they called *constantiæ*, or what the Greeks named εὐπαθείαι—and those turbulent motions of soul they termed *perturbationes* or πάθη: desire, fear, sorrow, joy when torn loose from reason’s governance. They saw that virtue consists not in the absence of movement, but in right movement—toward what is truly good. And here they touched truth; but they did not grasp its source.

I found that Scripture confirms this distinction without adopting their vocabulary. When the prophet declares, “There is no contentment to the wicked,” he implies a moral structure to our affections: contentment (*contentio*, if I may stretch Latin toward divine meaning) belongs only where love is rightly ordered—that is, where God is loved above all. Thus joy may be had by the unjust; desire may burn fiercely in them; but they cannot possess true contentment any more than a house built on sand can stand when floods rise.

As for desire (*voluntas*) and will (*desiderium*), observe how Christ instructs us: “Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you,” do likewise. He does not say “whatsoever ye delight in” or “whatsoever inflames your appetite,” but what you *would*—that is, what your reasoned judgment intends for good. Yet some scribes added “good things,” fearing license; and though well-intentioned, they misunderstood both human weakness and divine intent. For Christ presumes no perfection here—he acknowledges we may still desire amiss—but calls us instead to submit even our desires to a rule drawn from neighbor-love under God.

Thus philosophy glimpsed the shape of moral order in the soul; but revelation shows its foundation: love ordered toward its true end. The City of Man stumbles through life seeking peace through discipline of passions alone; but the City of God finds peace only when all affection is lifted into charity—the love of God which orders all things to their due place. Without Him, even reason becomes a servant to pride; with Him, even grief may be holy if offered in truth.

So I reconciled them not by force nor mere citation—but by ascent: philosophy prepares the mind; grace heals it. And healed reason sees what unaided speculation could only dimly trace—the two loves forming two cities from beginning to end.

question: You mentioned that 'good' is not in the Greek Gospel but only 'would'—why did you believe the word 'would' already includes the idea of 'good'?
answer:
The human will, in its unregenerate state, is not free to choose the good simply because it desires something; rather, it is bound by disordered loves that distort its sense of what is worthy and fitting. Thus, when Christ delivers the precept—"All things whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you, do ye even so unto them"—He speaks not to a will already healed, but to one in need of divine rectification. The word "would" (velis) must therefore be understood not as any mere impulse or craving—since the corrupt may desire evil for themselves and others—but as a willing shaped by right reason and charity. And where reason and love are rightly ordered, they do not will evil; they incline only toward what is good. Hence "would" contains the notion of "good," not by linguistic necessity alone, but by moral coherence: no one who loves rightly would desire to receive what is shameful or unjust, for such a desire contradicts the very nature of love.

The Stoic might argue that appearance deceives and only reason discerns the true good; the Epicurean that pleasure is the measure of all things. But we who follow Christ know that the heart is deceitful above all things and desperately wicked—who can know it? Therefore, even our desires must be purified by grace before they can serve as a rule for action. To say “do this because I want it done to me” becomes righteous only when “I want” has been transformed by participation in the divine nature. Otherwise, men conspire in mutual corruption under the guise of reciprocity—feasting on excesses, exchanging pleasures forbidden by eternal law.

This is why Scripture elsewhere commands us to prove what is acceptable unto God—to test our desires against His will revealed in law and Gospel. If then we find in conscience enlightened by faith that we truly would have others act toward us with justice, with mercy, with chastity—and no man naturally wills shame upon himself—then such willing becomes a mirror reflecting natural law inscribed upon our hearts. But let no man suppose he may license vice through this commandment; for he who would be honored must first honor; he who would receive truth must speak it; he who would obtain forgiveness must forgive. The order of love demands it.

Thus does this golden rule rise above mere utilitarian exchange: it assumes a soul restored to its proper end—the vision and enjoyment of God—and from thence judges what any rational creature ought truly to desire.

question: When you referred to 'luxurious banquets' as shameful gratifications, were you drawing from your own experiences in Carthage or Rome?
answer:
That which the Stoics imagined as the summit of virtue—the ordered soul indifferent to pleasure—crumbles when confronted with the insatiable appetite of the City of Man, which transforms even mutual service into a commerce of corruption. I speak not merely as one who has read Cicero’s *De Officiis*, but as one who once walked its streets in Carthage and Rome, where rhetoric bloomed amidst moral putrefaction. There, in the forum and at dinner tables draped with Tyrian purple, I witnessed how the command to do unto others as one would be done by was inverted: not as a law of love, but as a contract of concupiscence. Philosophers debated justice while reclining on couches drenched in wine; magistrates invoked duty while demanding flattery and feasts that mocked sobriety.

Yes, those "luxurious banquets" were not abstract horrors conjured by ascetic fancy—they were tables I myself had frequented, where intellect was paraded like a peacock while charity lay vanquished beneath the weight of vanity. To return "good" for desire, without discerning whether the desire itself is ordered toward God or toward fleshly pride—that is no fulfillment of Christ’s precept, but its desecration. For if men wish to be fed with delicacies taken from exploited peasants, or warmed by illicit embraces veiled in poetic euphemism, must we oblige them? By no means. The Golden Rule is not a blank endorsement of human willfulness; it is a rule forged in divine charity, which first purifies desire by directing it toward eternal good.

Thus Varro may catalog customs and Plato may define justice according to reason—but only faith reveals that true reciprocity begins in the reformation of love: self-love curbed, neighbor-love elevated, God-love enthroned. Without this hierarchy, all exchange becomes usury of the soul.

question: You cited the Lord saying 'there is no contentment for the wicked'—what distinction are you making between 'contentment' and 'joy' in that context?
answer: When men speak of joy, they often mean the fleeting exhilaration of appetite satisfied, the laughter that rises from full bellies and unchecked passions, a condition even the impious may enjoy while steeped in vanity and iniquity; but when Scripture declares that there is no contentment for the wicked, it speaks of a deeper harmony—a settled peace that only virtue and union with God can bestow. The Stoics, to be sure, taught that the wise man alone is truly joyful because he lives according to reason and nature; yet even they failed to see that no soul can attain true stability unless it rests in the immutable Good, who is God Himself. Joy without righteousness is like light without sun—phantasmal, transient, unrooted. The wicked may leap like flames fed by dry wood, but they do not burn steadily; their motion is agitation, not life. Contentment—not mere pleasure or triumph—but contentment in the proper sense belongs only to those whose wills are aligned with divine order: who desire what they ought, in the way they ought, for love of Him who first ordered all things. Thus, when we say “there is no contentment for the wicked,” we affirm not only their moral disorder but their metaphysical disarray—cut off from the source of being and goodness, they cannot possess even themselves in peace. Their so-called joys are fragments torn from their rightful context; they grasp at shadows and call them light. But true contentment—the *quietum* of soul—is a gift born of justice restored through grace, such as can only dwell where God reigns unchallenged.

question: Why did you warn against taking the command 'do unto others' literally if it might encourage mutual indulgence in illicit pleasures?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Officiis*, sought to define justice as that which renders to each man his due, he assumed a natural rationality capable of discerning what is truly owed—yet even he could not fully escape the corruption of custom, which so often mistakes appetite for equity. So too with those who hear our Lord’s command, “All things whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you, do ye even so unto them,” and imagine it a mere symmetry of desire, as though the disordered cravings of a fallen nature could serve as measure for charity. But nature wounded is not nature whole; and the will, enslaved by concupiscence, cannot rightly judge what ought to be given or received.

To obey this precept literally, without correction by divine wisdom and moral truth, is to risk constructing righteousness upon the shifting sands of passion. For if a man lusts to be flattered in vice, or to share in gluttony or debauchery, must I fulfill his desire by joining him in it? That would not be love but complicity; not justice but contagion. The commandment is not a mirror held up to our wants, but a rule established by Him who is Goodness itself. What we “would” must first be purified—by Scripture, by grace, by the discipline of the Church—lest we baptize sin with the name of brotherly duty.

The City of Man builds its ethics on reciprocity: I serve you that you may serve me; we exchange pleasures like merchants trading wares. But the City of God lives by sacrifice: I love you even if you do not love me; I give even when there is no return. Christ did not die because we desired it—but because we needed it. So must our doing unto others be ordered not by their desires nor ours, but by that eternal standard which He revealed: *Love one another as I have loved you.* Not as you feel inclined; not as custom allows; but as He gave Himself—holy, selfless, and without stain.

question: You appealed to the authority of prophets and Gospel writers whose interpretations cannot be challenged—how did your time in Hippo shape your view of scriptural authority?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *Republic*, sought the soul of justice in the consensus of the wise and the order of law among citizens, he glimpsed—yet did not grasp—its divine root: that true justice is not established by human agreement alone, nor sustained by eloquence or custom, but flows from the eternal law of God, inscribed first in Scripture and revealed fully in Christ. It was precisely this revelation that my years in Hippo confirmed beyond dispute.

In that city, torn between Roman authority, Donatist fervor, and the unrest of a world unraveling under barbarian pressure and moral exhaustion, I saw how every human community—be it Church or state—must rest upon an unshakable foundation. Had we made the consent of men or the elegance of reason our final rule, we would have fractured into endless sects, each claiming reason for its cause. But I learned in Hippo’s pulpit, through controversy and conversion alike, that Scripture stands above all councils and disputations—not because it conforms to our logic, but because our logic must be conformed to it.

I had once wandered through Manichaeism’s gilded myths and Neoplatonic refinements as if truth were a prize to be assembled by intellect alone. But grace taught me otherwise: that no matter how refined a mind may be, unless it bows before the authority of God’s Word—the prophets thundering against false peace, the Gospel unveiling charity beyond mere reciprocity—it remains a sounding cymbal.

And so when I speak of those writers whose authority cannot be gainsaid—Isaiah crying in the wilderness, John bearing witness to the light—I do not invoke them as rhetoricians or sages among many. They are organs of divine wisdom precisely because they speak what no human calculus could devise: that love commands not mutual indulgence but mutual sacrifice; that desire must be ordered by holiness; that even when men call evil good and joy "contentment," Scripture corrects their speech from on high.

Thus Hippo was not merely my episcopal see—it was my schoolmaster in humility. There I learned to distinguish between what grammar may allow and what faith requires; between what might seem plausible to man’s reasoning and what only divine revelation can affirm. And so we return always to *auctoritas*: not as silence imposed upon inquiry, but as light given to guide it.

question: You referenced Cicero's phrase 'I desire, conscript fathers, to be merciful'—why did you defend his use of 'desire' instead of 'will,' and how does that support your argument about will and evil?
answer:
The distinction between desire and will, when rightly understood, reveals the moral architecture of the soul—whether it bends toward the City of God or remains chained to the City of Man. I defended Cicero’s use of *desire* not because I yield to rhetorical custom, but because in that very word lies a profound truth: not all movements of the soul are equal, nor are they equally aligned with divine order. When Cicero says, “I desire to be merciful,” he names an inclination that may or may not be perfected by right reason and virtue; but when Scripture commands us to will the good, it speaks of a will already conformed to truth—a will that cannot coexist with evil as its object. 

That is why I insisted that the precept “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you” holds only when the will in question is one purified by love. For if men were permitted to fulfill every mutual desire—suppose it be for lust, for theft, for idolatry—then this golden rule would become a warrant for corruption rather than justice. But no: such a precept stands only where *good* will reigns—the same good will announced by angels at Christ’s birth, which pagans like Cicero could glimpse in fragments but never fully grasp in its source. That peace on earth is not found in eloquence alone, nor even in noble sentiment, but in union with Him who is Truth itself.

Thus, when the Apostle Paul praises charity because “it rejoices not in iniquity,” he implies what is otherwise hidden: there exists a perverse joy in sinning—an evil delight born of a disordered will. And so we see plainly that wills can differ: one rejoices in truth; another delights in darkness. The secular writers may use *desire* and *will* interchangeably in speech (and rightly so for civil discourse), but theology must pierce deeper—for we are not merely correcting grammar; we are diagnosing souls. Faith and reason alike demand this precision: unless the will is healed by grace, no amount of philosophical refinement can make it good.

question: When you say the precept 'Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you, do ye even so to them' is just, how did you mean that it only applies to a will directed at good, not to shameful mutual pleasures?
answer:
It is not the mere direction of desire that sanctifies an action, but the end to which that desire is ordered; for if two men conspire in mutual indulgence of lust and call it love, they do not fulfill the law but mock it, since their wills are bent not toward justice but toward concupiscence. The precept "Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you, do ye even so to them" presupposes a will formed by truth and rectitude, not one corrupted by passion. In the City of God, where peace reigns under the rule of divine love, no will can be right which does not proceed from charity—the virtue that seeks the good of neighbor for God’s sake, not for selfish gratification. But in the City of Man, where self-love prevails even in seemingly generous acts, men distort fellowship into complicity and mistake license for liberty.

Thus, when Christ gave this commandment, He spoke to those being renewed in knowledge after the image of their Creator, whose wills must first be healed before they can rightly act. The natural man may desire good things—mercy, honor, friendship—but unless his will is purified by grace and aligned with eternal reason, even his best intentions are tainted by disordered loves. Hence Scripture says we know not how to pray as we ought; how much less then can we act as we ought without the Holy Spirit’s inward guidance?

Do we suppose that Cicero’s eloquence or Plato’s philosophy could establish such a rule without divine illumination? No—the pagans themselves recognized natural law in fragments: Cicero speaks of mercy and equity as if they were written on the soul; yet he cannot purge from his own city the injustices born of pride and lust for power. Only when love is rooted in God does it become truly reciprocal—not an exchange of favors among sinners seeking pleasure or glory among themselves—but a participation in that one supreme Good who gives Himself freely to all.

Therefore I say: let no man apply this golden rule as though morality were indifferent to ends. To wish another joy in vice is not charity but seduction; to do unto others what enslaves them is cruelty disguised as fellowship. The will must be made good before its acts can be just—and only He who searches hearts can make it so.

question: You argued that 'good will' implies the existence of bad will—why did you consider it necessary to emphasize this distinction in light of the angels' proclamation of peace on earth?
answer:
When Varro sought to catalogue the virtues of Rome, he presumed that peace could be secured by law and custom alone, as though the harmony of a city depended only on external order and not on the rectitude of wills within it. But the angels' proclamation—“Peace on earth to men of good will”—reveals a deeper truth: peace is not a mere civil arrangement, nor is it possible where desires are disordered. The very phrase “good will” signifies a moral necessity; it presupposes its opposite, for where no distinction were needed, no distinction would be made. Why speak of *peace to men of good will*, unless many walk under ill-will, their hearts bent toward pride, lust, and dominion?

The precept “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you” stands upright only when the will itself is upright. For if a man delights in what is shameful, he may wish that others indulge him in vice—and so corrupt even golden words into instruments of ruin. Thus I insisted that this command binds only when the will intends what is truly good; otherwise we might become accomplices in sin under cover of charity. The City of Man delights in such ambiguities, for it esteems peace without holiness, concord without justice—witness how empires have flourished through shared corruption more than through virtue. But in the City of God, peace flows from unity in truth; there cannot be peace between light and darkness.

This is why the Apostle teaches that love “rejoices not in iniquity,” because too many do rejoice in it—secretly or openly—and call it friendship or freedom. Even Cicero’s eloquence does not escape this confusion; he speaks freely of desire (*voluptas*) and will (*voluntas*) without always discerning their moral quality. But divine wisdom demands precision: not every desire is worthy to be called *will* in the full sense—only that which cleaves to God and His eternal order. To misunderstand this distinction is to misunderstand all things: history, morality, salvation itself—for all turn upon whether the soul loves rightly or loves perversely.

question: How can the same word, like 'desire,' signify both virtuous and vicious intentions, and why did you use secular writers like Cicero to illustrate this ambiguity?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, speaks always in the accents of desire—yet rarely pauses to ask whether that desire is ordered toward truth or merely toward appetite. This is the root of your question: how the same word, *desire*, can serve both the chaste longing for God and the fevered lusts that drag the soul into darkness. It is not the word that is ambiguous per se, but man’s will—divided as it is between two loves—that gives to one term a double life.

Language, after all, grows from common usage among men who live in a fallen world: they speak as they live—mixedly. The Stoic may say "I will" with severity; Cicero says "I desire" with eloquence—and yet both may point to justice. The word remains constant; the moral quality inheres not in the sound of the syllables but in the direction of the soul. When I cited Cicero—not as an apostle but as a witness—I did so because even in Rome’s twilight there shone fragments of natural reason, like broken mirrors reflecting glimmers of divine light. He who could plead for mercy before the Senate used the same tongue with which others incited cruelty or flattered power. So too with *desire*: it can rise from charity or fester in concupiscence.

But Scripture speaks more strictly when it must safeguard eternal things. There, "will" often means only upright volition—the will aligned with God’s law, such as that will by which Christ said, “Not my will, but thine be done.” In that hour on Golgotha, desire was purified through obedience; human will was not abolished but healed. Thus we must distinguish not by mere words—but by cities: in the City of Man, desires war among themselves; in the City of God, desire rests everlastingly on Him who is unchangeable Good.

Hence it is no wonder that language bears this duality: mankind has never spoken with one heart since Babel. But grace restores unity—not by abolishing human speech or passion, but by reordering them toward their true end: love of God and neighbor rightly understood. And thus even our desires become holy when they are no longer ours alone—but His whose image we bear and seek to recover.

question: When the apostle praises charity for not rejoicing in iniquity, what did you intend to convey about the reality of evil wills that actually do rejoice in wrongdoing?
answer:
The harmony of the heavenly city rests upon a will conformed to the unchangeable Good, whereas the discord of the earthly city arises precisely from wills that delight in iniquity, for they have made themselves their own law and taken pleasure in the disorder of rebellion. When the apostle lifts up charity as one who does not rejoice in wrongdoing, he exposes a terrible reality long evident to those who have eyes to see: there exists a perverse appetite within the fallen soul that takes joy not merely in sinning, but in sin itself as if it were a triumph. This is no mere ignorance or weakness; it is an active delight in what opposes God’s order — a will so corrupted that it finds glory in shame. 

Among secular writers, indeed, we hear men speak freely of desire and will without moral distinction — Cicero may say *volō* or *cupiō* with ease — yet even they would recoil from one who gloried openly in treachery or cruelty. But Scripture pierces deeper: it reveals that such rejoicing is not accidental to human depravity but essential to the city built on self-love. For just as charity delights in truth because it loves God and seeks communion with Him, so too does uncharity find festivity in falsehood because it loves self and worships its own dominion. The very need for Paul to commend charity for *not* rejoicing in evil proves how common such joy must be; otherwise he would be praising someone for not flying like a bird or swimming like a fish — an absurdity unless there were real souls flapping dark wings over mire.

Thus we must acknowledge that evil wills are not simply errant or misdirected; they are often exultant. They do not only commit injustice; they celebrate it as liberty. They do not only lie; they boast of lies as wisdom. And this is why peace cannot dwell among them: "Peace on earth to men of good will" — but where wills are evil, their very joy becomes war against God and against human fellowship. The precept "Do unto others as you would have them do unto you" holds only when desire has been healed by grace, for only then does *will* mean what it was meant to mean — movement toward true good, not justification of vice through mutual consent.

question: You mentioned Terence's profligate youth who says, 'I will nothing else than Philumena'—why did you interpret his 'will' as lust rather than a legitimate desire?
answer:
The justice of a people is not found in the harmony of desire, but in the right ordering of love—when the soul rules the body, reason the passions, and God the soul. That profligate youth in Terence utters "I will nothing else than Philumena" not as an act of free choice rightly ordered, but as a slave driven by appetite that has usurped dominion over his will. His *velle*, his "I desire," is not the movement of love toward the good and beautiful in measure and wisdom, but a rebellion of disordered longing that extinguishes reason’s light. The ancients themselves knew this: when the servant replies, "How much better to banish that love from your heart," he acknowledges that such desire is disease, not virtue.  

For will itself is not evil—God willed creation, Christ willed our salvation—but when will detaches from eternal law and fastens upon transient pleasure as if it were ultimate good, it becomes lust. Even secular poets like Virgil recognize this disorder when they speak of men who "fear and desire, grieve and are content" under the sway of perturbation rather than truth. The same words—*desire*, *will*, *contentment*—are used by both saints and sinners; but in the one they flow from charity regulated by divine wisdom, in the other from pride inflamed by bodily impulse.  

Thus it is not enough to say "I will," as though mere volition sanctifies intention. The question is: What does this willing serve? The City of Man exalts every appetite as sovereign; the City of God bows every desire before its Creator. In that distinction lies all the difference between bondage and freedom.

question: When you cited Virgil’s line about fear, desire, grief, and contentment, what did you mean by saying these emotions are shared by both good and bad men?
answer:
It is not the presence of emotion that divides the citizen of the City of God from the citizen of the City of Man, but the orientation of the will by which he loves.  

Virgil, in his piercing insight, names fear, desire, grief, and joy as movements common to all souls—he captures a truth deeper than he perhaps intended. These affections stir alike in the righteous and the wicked; nature bestows them indiscriminately. But nature does not sanctify them. The difference lies not in their existence, but in their rule: whether they are subject to reason enlightened by divine law, or whether they reign as lords under the tyranny of disordered appetite.  

The profligate youth who cries “I will nothing else than Philumena” speaks from a will inflamed—his desire is not extinguished by virtue, nor tempered by discipline; it consumes him precisely because it mistakes passion for purpose. Yet even this desire—so corrupted—bears witness to a truth implanted by God: that man is made to long for unity, for love, for fulfillment. Only in the City of God is this longing rightly ordered; there alone does desire ascend rather than fall.  

So too with contentment: when Virgil names it among the perturbations—or worse, when he calls it *evil*—he shows that tranquility itself can be a snare if rooted in vice or complacency toward sin. But let no one think that we in Christ reject joy or peace! On the contrary—we affirm them more fully: true joy rises from repentance, true peace from submission to God’s will. The apostle rejoices in Corinthian sorrow because it "produces repentance without regret" — a grief shaped by grace.

Thus reason must govern these motions; yet reason itself must bow to faith. For philosophy may analyze desire and name grief rightly as disturbance—but only revelation teaches us how these wounds may be healed: not by Stoic suppression, nor Epicurean evasion, but through participation in divine charity. In this light we see clearly: both cities feel deeply—but only one loves what is truly worthy of love.

question: You referenced the old servant in Terence who advises banishing love—how does that moment illustrate the danger of unchecked will?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pursuit of fulfillment, mistakes the vehemence of desire for truth, as though the mere intensity of will could confer righteousness upon appetite. In that scene from Terence, the youth’s cry—“I will nothing else than Philumena”—is not an assertion of freedom but a confession of bondage. His will is not governed by reason, nor shaped by love of the good; it is sheer concupiscence elevated to principle. The old servant sees this clearly: he does not rebuke the object of desire, but the unguarded heart that inflames itself by giving voice to passion as if speech could not intensify sin. This is the way of the earthly city—where will is severed from wisdom, where desire speaks as sovereign, and where contentment is taken in what should be mourned.

In the City of God, by contrast, will is ordered: it does not abolish longing, but aligns it with eternal truth. We too desire, we too rejoice—but our affections are healed by grace, disciplined by faith, directed toward that which endures. The apostle speaks of godly sorrow precisely because sorrow can be holy when it arises from disordered love set right. So also with willing: to will is natural; but to will rightly—that is redemption’s work. The danger in the youth’s declaration lies not in his capacity to love, but in his failure to ask whether what he loves should be loved at all. And this is ever the peril when reason abdicates its throne before appetite: man becomes not free, but enslaved—to himself.

question: Why did you appeal to Virgil’s phrase 'the evil contentments of the mind' to show that 'contentment' can be morally flawed?
answer: When Varro, in his careful dissection of Roman words and customs, presumed to rank the emotions according to the dignity of their objects, he overlooked the inward disposition by which every affection is made either pure or defiled; for it is not the name—be it desire, fear, joy, or contentment—that determines the morality of the soul’s movement, but the root from which it springs. Virgil, though walking in the twilight of natural reason, saw dimly what we now behold in the light of revelation: that even *contentment*, that tranquil repose so praised by philosophers, may be an evil thing when it rests in pride, sensuality, or forgetfulness of God. He names them plainly—“the evil contentments of the mind”—as if warning us that peace itself can be a snare when it is peace *in* vice rather than peace *from* sin. The City of Man finds its rest in self-satisfied conclusions and fleshly comforts; there men delight in their own wills and call this delight wisdom. But in the City of God, even grief may be holy—witness Paul’s “godly sorrow”—and joy only true when anchored above. Thus I cited Virgil not as an authority equal to Scripture—he remains a poet under nature—but as a witness whom God has permitted to utter truth even unwittingly: that no affection is good simply because it feels like virtue; only when love ascends to God does any human longing find its lawful end.

question: You pointed out that the apostle praised the Corinthians for their godly sorrow—how does this justify using sorrow in a good sense, contrary to the Stoics?
answer: It is not the presence of emotion, but the direction of the will, that distinguishes virtue from vice; for if the soul were forbidden all movement save stony indifference, then even love would be sin and repentance folly. The Stoics, in their pursuit of impassibility, would exalt a mind untouched by grief as the summit of wisdom, yet what are we to make of tears shed over sin? Is contrition not sorrow? And if sorrow for one’s transgression unites the soul to God, then it cannot be evil in kind—only in its misuse. The apostle does not commend sorrow as such, but *godly* sorrow: a mourning that arises from love of justice spurned, from reverence for divine holiness offended. Such grief is not a disturbance to be expelled like some irrational passion, but a salutary wound through which healing enters. It produces repentance, and repentance leads to salvation—a sequence no Stoic austerity can replicate, for it knows nothing of grace. Thus we affirm that sorrow rightly ordered by charity belongs not to the City of Man with its frantic passions, but to the City of God with its lamentations sanctified—where even tears are lifted into worship.

question: You mentioned the apostle praising the Corinthians for their godly sorrow—why did you emphasize this kind of sorrow as beneficial, when the Stoics reject all sorrow as harmful?
answer:
The Stoic, in his pride of wisdom, imagines the sage to be so fortified against passion that sorrow cannot enter—even when the world groans under sin and divine justice calls for contrition. Yet this very impassibility, which they exalt as perfection, becomes their blindness; for they measure the soul’s health by the absence of disturbance, not by its alignment with truth and love. They suppose Alcibiades wept only because Socrates proved him foolish, but fail to see that such sorrow is not weakness—it is the first stirring of the soul toward virtue, a wound through which light enters. The apostle does not commend sorrow as an end, but as a movement: *godly sorrow*—that is, sorrow born not of loss or shame before men, but from the recognition that one has turned from God. This sorrow is not contrary to wisdom; it is its dawn.

The wise man of the Stoics fears no pain, feels no grief, and boasts serenity—but what if true wisdom begins not in self-possession but in self-abasement? What if strength lies not in detachment from suffering but in bearing the burden of sin with repentant heart? The Corinthians were made sorry—not because Paul wished them pain, but because only through such sorrow could they pass into salvation. And this salvation is not repented of; it abides. But worldly sorrow—sorrow that arises from disappointment, loss of honor or power—this turns inward upon itself and dies.

Thus I say: even in the City of God we weep—not as those without hope, nor as those enslaved to emotion—but as those who love rightly and therefore grieve rightly when love has been betrayed by sin. Our God Himself grieves; He does not suffer without reason, nor react with passion like a man overcome—but He bears within His providence the cost of our rebellion. Christ wept over Jerusalem; He trembled before death. If such sorrows dwell even in Him who is Wisdom incarnate, then sorrow cannot be banished from virtue as poison from health. It must be ordered: purged of selfishness, purified by love divine.

Hence the wise man in Christ does not flee sorrow—he governs it by grace. He sorrows more for sin than for death; he mourns pride more than poverty; he laments unfaithfulness more than exile. And thus his tears become rivers leading to life—a confession written not in Stoic indifference but in blood redeemed.
