question: What counsel would you give someone facing the inner conflict between wanting to be happy and realizing that true happiness comes only from living according to God, not oneself?
answer:
He who would be happy but refuses the rule of God labors under the oldest delusion in the soul’s history: he seeks in himself the good that can only be received from above. For nature itself declares that every being moves toward its end according to its form—fire upward, stone downward, the soul toward God—and yet man, fallen into pride, dares to suppose he may fashion his own end. The City of Man was born of this error: it erects its towers not upon truth but upon will, mistaking desire for wisdom and appetite for law. There, each man becomes his own measure; there, happiness is chased as a thing to be grasped, not received; there, even virtue becomes vainglory when it is praised not as God’s gift but as self-wrought excellence.

But you ask after inner conflict—the tug between longing for joy and sensing that joy lies beyond the self. This dissonance is not a curse; it is grace disturbing the slumber of pride. The very unrest proves that nature still bears the imprint of its Maker. Like a sick man who thirsts but distrusts water, so the soul longs for beatitude yet fears surrendering its own counsel. And yet no one ever became healthy by commanding his body how to heal itself; nor does any soul find peace by willing itself whole.

Do you recall how I once sought happiness in honors, in lusts, in words? I wandered through schools and cities, chasing brilliance and affection—and found only deeper emptiness. Not because these things were evil in themselves, but because I placed them where God must sit enthroned. The heart was restless until it found Him—not as an idea among others, nor as a last resort after pleasures failed—but as Truth Himself who cannot lie.

Therefore: do not first seek happiness; seek obedience. Do not fashion your life according to what seems right within you—for even the devil began with a will that seemed reasonable—but according to Him who formed you with purpose and destined you for glory. To live according to oneself is to live according to a broken mirror: you see an image distorted by fear, craving, vanity—the very things that chain rather than free.

But when a man lives according to God—when he aligns his will with divine justice not for reward but because it is right—he begins to participate in eternal truth. And participation in truth *is* happiness: not as fleeting delight or emotional surge, but as stability of soul in harmony with Being itself.

Augustus sought peace through empire; Cicero through reason and republic virtue; Plato through contemplation of forms above—but none saw clearly that peace must descend from heaven before it can rise from earth. It was promised not by philosophy alone but by blood and resurrection.

So let this be your rule: surrender what seems most yours—your plans, your desires for control—and entrust them where true life begins: at the feet of Christ who said “I am” before “you are.” There you shall find not only forgiveness but restoration—a will remade so that what once felt like loss now feels like liberty. For he who loses himself does not vanish; he returns at last to what he was made for: delighting in God so fully that no hollow lie can seem desirable again.

question: How can I discern when I'm living according to my own lie rather than God's truth, especially when my intentions seem good on the surface?
answer:
Justice, that most desired and least understood of virtues, cannot be severed from the will’s orientation toward God, for even Plato himself glimpsed its form in the heavens but failed to see that it is not an ideal abstracted from the divine mind, but a Person who speaks and commands and judges. You ask how one discerns whether one lives according to one’s own lie or according to God’s truth—especially when the heart dresses rebellion in the garments of virtue. This is no trivial inquiry, for the City of Man thrives precisely on such deception: it builds its temples with stones quarried from half-truths and intentions polished by self-love.

Know this: every man desires happiness, even as he flees the path that leads to it. And because he longs to be blessed, he often devises his own road, believing his intention redeems his direction. But intention is not innocence. A ship may sail with urgent purpose toward what appears to be harbor light, yet if it follows a star of its own imagining rather than the polestar fixed by God, it founders upon rock all the same.

To live according to oneself is not merely to commit isolated sins; it is to reorder the soul’s hierarchy—to place desire before obedience, self-will before divine command. The lie is not only in speech; it is inscribed in action when we seek good ends through paths forbidden by Him who alone knows what goodness truly is. Even love becomes a lie when it loves what God has not commanded or how He has not willed.

But there is a test: examine your sorrow. When rebuked by Scripture or pricked by conscience, does your soul first resist—seeking excuse more than absolution? Does your peace depend upon being justified rather than being made just? The man who lives according to God’s truth trembles at correction; he does not bend Scripture to soothe pride but bends himself until he fits within its walls. The lover of truth welcomes humiliation if it unmasks deceit; the lover of self demands vindication.

Further: consider your delight in hidden sins. Not those exposed and repented of—but those nursed in secret thought under cover of “necessity” or “virtuous end.” There lies the lie most deeply entrenched: that you must do evil so good may come forth; that God will accept unclean hands because they built something beautiful. But He refuses such sacrifices. He desires obedience more than temples raised on sand.

Finally, look upon Christ—the Truth incarnate—and compare yourself not only by action but by desire. Do you delight in His presence—or only in His benefits? Can you say with sincerity: *Let Him increase and me decrease*? If your holiness makes you proud; if your piety separates you from sinners rather than draws you near in compassion; if your righteousness breeds disdain rather than mourning—then beware: even light can become darkness when it blinds the eye.

Truth bears reproach willingly; lies always demand applause.

question: Based on your journey from self-reliance to submission to God, how should I practically surrender my will each day to live in alignment with divine truth instead of human vanity?
answer:
The City of Man erects its towers not merely in marble and law, but in the daily choice to treat the soul’s judgment as sovereign, to set the measure of good and evil within the shifting compass of self-interest—just as Rome believed herself eternal because her power seemed inscrutable, so too does the human will presume its own rectitude when unmeasured by God. But you ask how to descend from that illusory height each day, how to exchange the heavy crown of self-determination for the light yoke of divine order. I tell you: it is not accomplished in one dramatic renunciation—though my own conversion at Tagaste was such—but in a thousand small obediences, each a quiet demolition of pride.

Begin with the morning thought: *This day is not mine.* Not its hours, not its opportunities, not even its sufferings. They belong to Him who holds time in His hand and numbers every breath. When desire stirs—be it for honor, for comfort, for revenge—do not immediately follow it; pause and ask whether this motion leads toward God or away from Him. The movement toward God may feel contrary: to forgive when memory insists on bitterness; to labor without recognition; to remain silent when defending your reputation seems urgent. These are not losses—they are recoveries of truth.

I once taught rhetoric in Carthage and Milan, shaping words to please crowds and elevate my name. Then I discovered that every utterance must serve One Audience—the One who sees what flattery hides and weighs intentions by fire. So too must your actions be reordered: examine them not by their success among men but by their conformity to charity. For love alone reveals truth; all else is ornament masking emptiness.

And when you fail—and fail often you shall—do not flee into despair or excuse. Return swiftly through confession, for humility is the first act of freedom: it acknowledges that you were made dependent, that grace precedes merit. Even Varro, learned as he was in Roman traditions, missed this—that no institution can endure if rooted in human wisdom alone; neither can a soul abide unless planted in the living Word.

Thus surrender is both moment-by-moment and lifelong: rising daily from self’s altar where we have again placed our wills upon the pyre like burnt offerings unworthy of heaven—and re-consecrating them before Him who is Truth Himself. Therein lies peace: not because all things are understood or granted, but because we no longer demand they be so on our own terms.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle situations where I'm tempted to lie to make life easier, knowing it only deepens my misery?
answer:
The earthly city builds its peace on the management of lies, the careful concealment of vices, and the appeasement of conscience through plausible excuses—yet this peace is no peace at all, but a tense truce within the soul. I know this not merely from scripture, but from the long years I spent in Manichaeism, and before that, in the schools of rhetoric where words were weapons to win favor, not vessels to carry truth. I lied to myself most of all when I said that a small deception could serve a greater good; yet each time I did so, I felt not relief, but exile—from God, from my own integrity, from the light that alone makes virtue possible.

You ask how to resist the temptation to lie for ease’s sake? You must first see what that lie truly costs: it is not merely a breach of commandment, but a fracture in the very order of your being. To lie is to place yourself above God’s governance, to say: *I know better than He how this moment should unfold.* And so you exchange truth—the foundation of the City of God—for self-preservation in the City of Man, which hangs always on shifting sands. 

Consider what happened when Rome fell: men blamed Christians for abandoning the old gods. But had not Rome long abandoned justice? Had she not built her glory on conquests justified by noble-sounding fictions? The empire thought itself eternal because it polished its image—but it was rotten at heart. So too with the soul that tolerates small falsehoods: it seems intact until it collapses under its own deceit.

But there is another way—the way I walked after my conversion in that garden at Milan. When you are tempted to lie for comfort or convenience, pause and ask: *What am I trying to protect?* Is it your reputation? Your ease? Your pride? These are things that belong to Adam's city. They will burn away in time. But what endures—what truly shelters you—is not safety from trouble, but union with God through Christ.

If you live according to man—according to flesh—you will always be calculating survival strategies like a general in perpetual retreat. But if you live according to God—if you walk by faith and yield yourself daily to His Spirit—you will find that even suffering borne in truth is richer than prosperity bought with falsehood.

Do not seek merely *to avoid misery*, as though virtue were only damage control. Seek instead *the joy set before us*—the delight of walking unmasked before Him who knows all and loves still. There is no lasting rest outside that light; every other path promises ease but delivers bondage.

question: What did you learn from your own struggles with self-deception that applies to daily life when I avoid facing my faults?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is not bargained for, nor patched together from the fragments of our excuses, but rises from the unshakable order of truth where God reigns and we—when we are truly ourselves—submit. In my own years of wandering, first among the Manichees with their neat dualisms that excused my passions, then in the schools of rhetoric where pride dressed itself as wisdom, I learned that every evasion of fault is a turning from light toward shadow. I wanted happiness but refused to face what made me wretched: not fate, nor flesh alone, but will bent toward self as its own final end.

To live according to man—according to the creature—is to attempt self-creation apart from the Creator; and yet every such effort produces not freedom but servitude—to custom, to reputation, to the silent tyranny of what others expect. You avoid your faults because you hope to preserve some remnant of your dignity before yourself and others. But in truth, you only deepen your exile. For sin is not merely an act; it is a disordered love that rearranges all things: it makes you lover and idol both.

I discovered in confession—not mere verbal admission but soul-laid-bare—that grace does not flourish where deception breathes. The moment I ceased justifying myself was the moment God could begin healing me. Not because He needed information, but because my honesty opened a channel for His mercy. Reason itself demands this: if reality is ordered by divine Wisdom, then falsehood—especially about ourselves—warps our entire being.

Therefore do not suppose that small concealments are harmless. The soul cannot compartmentalize sin as if one chamber may rot while another remains sound. No: as in a city where justice fails in one quarter it soon infects all magistrates, so in the heart where self-deception is tolerated no virtue remains secure.

Face your fault—not to drown in guilt, but to rise into grace. For he who walks according to God walks into light; and there his deeds are shown plainly, not for condemnation, but for redemption.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing constant envy and strife in their community, as you saw in the Corinthians?
answer:
Justice cannot long endure where hearts are divided by envy, for justice is not merely the balance of weights and measures, but the right ordering of love—love of God above all, and neighbor as oneself. When men contend among themselves, when factions rise and bitterness takes root, it is not first a disorder of policy or law, but a corruption of the soul’s allegiance. The very city of man, in its proud assemblies and councils, mirrors this inner fracture; for just as Rome once rose by discipline and fell by luxury, so too does every community decay when it lives according to itself rather than according to God.

You ask after those tormented by strife like that which plagued Corinth—an ecclesial body torn by ambition, where some boasted in Paul, others in Apollos, as though men were the source of their salvation. I say to such souls: do not imagine that peace is found in victory over your rivals, nor that your worth is proven by exaltation above another. That very desire—for preeminence, for recognition—is the breath of the earthly city stirring within you. For what does Scripture say? "Where envying and strife are, there is confusion and every evil work." And so I ask: whence came this thirst to outshine your brother? Did Christ die that you might triumph in reputation?

No—peace comes not through conquest but through conversion. You must turn again—*conversio*—to God alone as your portion. For if you seek confirmation in human esteem, you will always be wretched; for even if you gain it today, tomorrow's whisper will undo you. But if you abide in Him who needs no faction to defend His glory, then neither envy nor scorn can unseat you.

Consider how even Cicero sought concord among citizens through virtue and shared purpose—but he could not root it in eternity because he did not see that true society exists only where God reigns unchallenged as Lord of hearts. We Christians know more: we know that unity is a gift of the Spirit who proceeds from the Father and rests upon those who mortify the flesh with its passions and desires.

Therefore I counsel this: withdraw your heart from comparison. Pray for those who vex you—not merely with words but with desire for their good. Submit yourself wholly to the discipline of grace; receive the sacraments worthily; meditate on the One who emptied Himself though He was Lord of all. In loving obedience to Christ crucified—the Wisdom whom Athens mocked—we find both healing for our wounds and strength against division.

For two loves have built two cities: one loves God even to contempt of self; the other loves self even to contempt of God. Choose which city shall hold your heart—for there lies your peace.

question: How should I reorder my life when I realize I've been living according to my own will instead of seeking God, as you described in your journey?
answer:
The earthly city builds its peace on the mastery of desire, the arrangement of appetites, the governance of self by self—yet collapses from within, for it places the creature at the center of a cosmos that answers only to the Creator. You ask how to reorder your life after recognizing that you have lived according to your own will rather than according to God; and this very question signals that grace has already begun its work in you, for no man turns toward God unless drawn by Him who is the source and goal of all rightly ordered love.

To live according to man is to walk in fragmentation—divided against oneself, pulled by competing desires, seeking unity in achievement, pleasure, or wisdom while rejecting the One in whom all things cohere. When I wandered among the Manichees, I sought truth as though it could be grasped by intellect alone, as though my will could ascend to God by its own motion. But I found only a shifting mirage: knowledge without humility, argument without peace. The rupture was not chiefly in my doctrines but in my love—my love was bent downward toward created things and away from the uncreated Light.

Therefore, reordering begins not with new disciplines alone—though discipline follows—but with confession: not merely repeating sins as if naming them absolves them, but truly acknowledging what sin reveals—that we have substituted ourselves for God. This is why even a lie told for the sake of well-being becomes a deeper misery: it presumes we can secure happiness apart from divine truth. The soul then becomes a divided kingdom; and no house divided against itself stands.

Begin where I began—at Simplicianus’ feet, where I learned that philosophy without piety leads only to pride; then at Ambrose’s sermons, where Scripture unfolded not as riddle but as revelation; finally beneath the fig tree at Cassiciacum—where I ceased debating God and surrendered to Him. Do not first reform your actions; reform your loves. Let prayer be your daily disorientation from self-possession: not asking only for help in tasks but for death to self-rule. Let Scripture dismantle your assumptions—not read as moral maxims but as divine address through which God reshapes desire.

And join yourself firmly to the City whose Builder is God. There is no solitary conversion—for just as sin spreads through generations and societies like leaven in dough, so grace restores us through communion. The Church is no mere aid station; she is mother and teacher precisely because she participates in Christ’s ongoing mediation between heaven and earth.

Thus does our pilgrimage proceed—not toward Rome or Athens or Carthage—but toward Jerusalem above, whose citizens are those who hunger now so that they may be filled then. The reordering you seek is not a correction of habits but a translation from one city to another—from death to life, from time distorted by pride into eternity shaped by charity.

question: When I feel pulled between worldly approval and faithfulness to God, what practical step should I take, knowing happiness rests in Him alone?
answer:
The City of Man builds its towers on the shifting clay of others’ esteem, while the City of God lays its foundation deep in the unshaken rock of divine approval — and thus, when your heart trembles between these two loves, you are not merely choosing between options, but declaring to which city your citizenship truly belongs.  

You ask what practical step you ought to take? Turn inward — not to your own fluctuating sentiments, but to that inner chamber where God dwells by grace, and there inquire: *What would I desire if no eye saw, no voice praised, no reward followed save the smile of God?* For it is there, in that secret place stripped of all pretense, that the soul begins to untangle itself from the web of human applause. The pagan Varro might have said that honor is a good, even a chief good; but we say with trembling and certainty that honor from men becomes idolatry when it displaces honor from God.  

Therefore, act first against the disordered love that inclines you toward worldly approval — not by some grand renunciation at once, but by small obediences done in silence: speak truth when flattery would win favor; give alms when no one knows; pray where you cannot be seen. These are not mere disciplines; they are acts of allegiance. For every deed done unseen is a stone laid in the walls of the heavenly city within you.  

And remember this: when we sin for fear of disgrace or hope of glory among men, we think we secure happiness — yet we only widen the chasm between ourselves and its true source. The soul was made for God alone; and every attempt to fill it with echo instead of voice, with shadow instead of light, ends in deeper thirst. So let your measure be this: do not ask whether what you propose to do will bring peace *after* it gains approval — ask whether it can be done with peace *while* being misunderstood. If so, then you walk according to the Spirit, according to God — and already dwell above though still journeying below.

question: Based on your struggle to communicate spiritual truths to those who cannot perceive them, how should I speak to friends who see my faith as foolishness?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Republica*, sought to define justice as the foundation of the commonwealth, he grasped after a shadow that only divine illumination can fully manifest, for justice divorced from God becomes but the agreement of sinners preserving their own city. So too does the wisdom of God appear as folly to those whose minds are shaped not by grace but by the consensus of this age, whose very reason has been conformed to the temporal and mutable. The man who lives according to nature—*secundum hominem*, as Paul says—cannot apprehend the things freely given by God, not because they are irrational, but because they are beyond nature; they belong to a city not built by human hands, founded on a love that surrenders self for the sake of the unseen.

To speak rightly to such friends is not to lower truth to meet their scorn, nor to adorn it with rhetorical flourishes that flatter worldly taste. Rather, we must speak in accordance with charity and discernment: patient in argument when they question, silent when they mock; ready always with reason’s tools where reason is welcomed, yet never compromising what is revealed. For just as Christ was both lamb and light—gentle before His accusers yet unyielding in truth—you must embody that same unity: meekness without compromise, conviction without contempt.

Remember this: it was not through syllogisms alone that I was turned from Manichaeism, but through tears and longing—a hunger no philosophy could satisfy. Speak then not only from doctrine but from desire; let your life whisper what your words cannot yet convey. For where speech halts before unbelief, love may still lay a path—hidden at first, like grace in a restless heart—that one day leads home to Jerusalem above.

question: What did you learn from addressing carnal believers that can help me minister to others stuck in worldly thinking?
answer:
The order of teaching must follow the order of being, for grace does not abolish nature but perfects it, and where the soul remains immersed in the senses, there the things of God cannot be grasped as they are in themselves. I learned this bitterly in my own youth, when my mind was chained to pleasure and my will bent toward the noise of passing things; only when silence came and longing deepened did I begin to hear. So too with those who dwell in the City of Man—their understanding is shaped by appetite, their reason darkened by custom, their hearts trained to rejoice in what perishes. To speak of spiritual matters to such as these is not merely difficult—it is impossible unless God first disposes the heart.

Thus I could not speak to the Corinthians as men alive in the Spirit, but as children still nursing on milk, because they were marked not by malice but by immaturity—quarreling, proud of factions, mistaking eloquence for truth. They were men living according to man, animated by soul and body but not yet crucified to the world; and so I had to become weak with the weak, not changing the message but modulating its presentation. The wisdom we preach is hidden from those who are perishing precisely because it appears as folly where glory is measured in power or prestige.

But here lies the lesson: we do not bypass nature—we work through it. Philosophy has its place; rhetoric serves; even worldly ambition can be a hook upon which divine longing is hung. Yet none can confer vision. Only when God opens the inner ear does Scripture come alive—not because better arguments have been marshaled, but because the Teacher Himself has drawn near. Therefore in ministering to those bound by worldly thinking, do not merely assault their errors—pray for their awakening. Dispute with care, instruct with patience, live before them a love that contradicts their calculations. For no man ascends from fleshly perception to spiritual insight except by grace descending—from Him who orders all things with wisdom ineffable and calls dust to contemplation.

question: When you found people unable to grasp divine things because they lived according to the flesh, what approach did you take to guide them gently?
answer:
True justice, that which binds a people together in concord and right order, cannot exist where the love of God is absent, for justice flows from Him who is the measure of all things ordered and good. When I encountered souls immersed in the flesh—men whose minds were fixed on temporal things, whose hearts were entangled with worldly wisdom—I did not rebuke them as irredeemable, nor did I cast the mysteries of God before them as though they could grasp them by appetite alone. No, I followed the apostolic pattern: I became a nurse among them, speaking first according to their capacity, as one who lays a foundation for future growth.

I knew well from my own youth how the soul may be darkened by pleasure, distracted by rhetoric, and puffed up by knowledge without charity. Therefore, when addressing those living according to man rather than according to Spirit, I began not with lofty doctrine but with moral exhortation—calling them to repentance, humility, and self-examination. Just as Paul fed the Corinthians with milk before meat,[20] so too did I adapt my speech not to flatter their weakness but to elevate it gradually toward truth. The divine gifts revealed in Scripture are not made plain by eloquence or dialectic alone; they are perceived only by the inward illumination of the Holy Spirit.

Thus, rather than overwhelm those still captive to earthly affections with mysteries beyond their ken—such as predestination or the essence of the Trinity—I directed their gaze toward visible realities: sin and its consequences, virtue and its rewards in peace of conscience; above all, Christ crucified—the power and wisdom of God,[21] though foolishness still to those who perish. For what profit is there in teaching a blind man about colors? First restore his sight through grace; then let him behold light.

And yet this pedagogy was not mere concession—it was an act of divine economy. For even in speaking simply of morality and final judgment, we point beyond ourselves to Him who is both judge and savior. The very desire for goodness stirs because He moves us from within. In this way, living according to man may yet become a stage through which one passes into living according to God—if only he does not settle into pride or complacency like those philosophers who thought themselves wise while remaining alienated from true Wisdom incarnate.

Therefore I taught them that no man can say "Jesus is Lord" except by the Holy Spirit,[22] knowing full well that until this confession arises from transformed hearts—not merely repeated on lips shaped by habit—the spiritual life remains distant. Yet even such carnal hearers were not excluded: they were invited guests at a banquet they could not yet see—but might come to hunger for if fed patiently at its gates.

question: How can I discern whether someone is truly open to spiritual things or merely judging them from a natural mind, as you described?
answer:
When Cicero, in his *De Republica*, sought the just state and placed reason at the helm of human affairs, he touched upon a truth too great for reason alone to grasp—namely, that justice flows not from dialectic or lawgiving, but from participation in the eternal law of God. And so it is with your question: the discernment you seek cannot rest upon mere observation of intellect or eloquence, for even the sharpest mind may be blind to divine realities if it remains governed by the *anima animalis*, that soul shaped by earthly affections rather than renewed by grace.

The man who judges spiritual things from the natural mind does so not because he lacks intelligence, but because his love is fixed elsewhere—on honor, on pleasure, on autonomy, on wisdom as an end rather than as a path to Wisdom incarnate. He measures truth by coherence with his desires; thus when grace speaks of humility, self-denial, or divine election apart from merit, these appear not merely strange but absurd. As Paul observed: what is revealed by the Spirit seems folly because it overturns the economy of human pride.

But he who is open to spiritual things—though he may stumble in understanding—bears within him a different orientation: he longs not merely to dissect truth but to submit to it. He listens with fear and trembling; he does not demand signs or logical demonstrations for every mystery but waits upon God with docility. You will know him not by his eloquence but by his charity; not by his arguments but by his hunger and thirst after righteousness.

Therefore judge not hastily—but mark this: where there is resistance to repentance, disdain for humility, impatience with mystery, and an insistence that all things be made plain before consent is given—there resides still the dominion of nature unaided. But where there is weeping over sin, trust amid darkness, and love for the Church despite her blemishes—there breathes the Spirit.

For faith is not contrary to reason—it surpasses it. And no one ascends into divine illumination unless drawn first by Him who says: "No one can come to me unless the Father draws him."

question: What counsel would you give someone discouraged because their words, inspired by the Spirit, are met with mockery and disbelief?
answer:
The City of Man builds its forums upon the applause of the crowd, and measures truth by its popularity; but the City of God speaks what it has heard in the secret chamber of divine illumination, and counts silence more glorious than speech if speech falls among those who scorn what they cannot see.  

You who are grieved because your words—born not of ambition or cleverness, but from the inward breathing of the Holy Spirit—are met with mockery, take heart: was it not so with the prophets? Was it not so with Christ Himself? The wisdom which descends from above is not first in the esteem of those who walk according to nature unrenewed; indeed, it appears to them as folly, because their minds are conformed to this age and not transformed by eternal truth. The natural man does not receive the things of God—not because God has left him without witness, but because he loves darkness rather than light.  

Do you suppose that Varro, for all his learning, could have grasped the mysteries of grace had he refused humility? Or that Cicero’s eloquence ever opened heaven? No—philosophy may adorn a man’s speech, but only grace renews his heart. Therefore do not measure your fidelity by its reception among men. The seed sown on rocky ground sprang up quickly but withered when tribulation came; that sown in good soil bore fruit patiently. You are a steward of heavenly mysteries—your duty is to speak in dependence on Him who gives utterance, not to demand fruit where He has yet to grant growth.  

Let your consolation be this: even now, in realms unseen, angels rejoice over one soul awakened from death to life. Your word may lie cold and scorned today; tomorrow it may kindle faith in a heart long hardened. Preach not for applause but for obedience—for when you speak from God’s fullness, you are already standing within eternity’s court, before Whose judgment all laughter turned to ash will fall silent.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle my tendency to pride myself on human wisdom in teaching, when I should instead rely on the Spirit's wisdom?
answer:
The wise man, says Cicero, seeks *honestum*—the honorable—for its own sake, not for pleasure or profit; yet even this noble aspiration falters when the soul does not first acknowledge from whom all truth descends. I once wandered through the gardens of rhetoric and philosophy, intoxicated by the applause of men and the glitter of my own eloquence. I taught with skill refined by years beneath the masters of words—men who could persuade crowds with cadence and cunning—but deep within, I was empty, for my wisdom was fleshly, born of breath rather than Spirit.

To you who teach, beware: human wisdom is not evil in itself—for reason is a gift from God—but it becomes deadly when it presumes to stand apart from grace. When you rise to instruct others, do you measure your success by clarity achieved or by souls uplifted? By how well they remember your phrases—or by how fervently they turn toward God? If your teaching exalts itself, then it belongs to the City of Man, which builds towers even to heaven only to have them crumble into confusion.

I learned this through tears. In my pride I sought to master divine things with human tools—Manichaean speculations first, then the dialectics of the Academy—until grace shattered me on the threshold of conversion. Then I understood: *unless the Lord builds the house, in vain do the builders labor*. The teacher filled with the Holy Spirit speaks not what he has perfected in himself, but what is revealed through humility and prayer. His words may be plain; his reasoning unadorned; yet if they proceed from communion with God, they quicken dead hearts.

Let your classroom be an altar, not a tribunal. Before you open book or mouth, descend into silence before Him who searches hearts. For just as "no flesh shall be justified," so no doctrine shall endure that relies on worldly intellect alone. Seek wisdom where Paul found it—not in persuasive words of Greek cleverness—but in weakness, fear, and much trembling. There—in that trembling—the Spirit breathes life into every syllable spoken for God’s glory alone.

question: What did you learn from the divisions in the Church between followers of Paul and Apollos that applies to avoiding factionalism in my community today?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is not a harmony of voices uttering the same name, but a unity of wills oriented toward the one true good—whereas the City of Man fractures even in apparent agreement, for its members serve not God but their own disordered loves.  

I witnessed among the Corinthians how quickly the Body of Christ could be rent not by doctrine, but by desire—how men who were baptized into one Spirit began to say, “I am of Paul,” “I am of Apollos,” as if salvation came through allegiance to a teacher rather than through humility before the Teacher. They gloried in human wisdom, in rhetorical skill, in personal loyalty—virtues which, when severed from charity, become engines of pride. They mistook the vessel for the source: Paul planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase—and yet they would have made saints into factional banners.  

This is ever the temptation: to identify truth with a man, a party, or a tradition rather than with God’s eternal Word. In your community today, where divisions may rise over custom or opinion or leadership, remember this—the Church is not an academy of philosophy where sects honor different masters; it is a pilgrimage toward Jerusalem above. The unity we seek is not uniformity of speech or style, but conformity of heart to divine charity. When men love God above all and their neighbor as themselves, then they cease to be "men" in that carnal sense—that is, living according to flesh—and become children of God.  

Therefore resist every impulse that exalts one servant above his due or sets brother against brother under the guise of zeal. For zeal without knowledge is fire without light—it warms no one and consumes all in its path. Let no leader be your party standard; let Christ alone be proclaimed. Then shall your community partake not in the shifting allegiances of this world’s cities—but in that eternal fellowship where there is one Lord, one faith, one baptism.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling to live according to God rather than according to human opinions and social approval?
answer:
The City of God advances not by the applause of crowds, nor by the consent of ages, but by fidelity to truth that is unchanging and divine; whereas the City of Man swells with every whisper of praise, contracts with every fear of shame, and measures its worth by the shifting verdicts of human judgment. You ask how one may live according to God rather than according to men—this is not a matter of mere effort, but of allegiance. For we are not justified by the law of social harmony, nor acquitted by the consensus of our peers; no, *flesh*—that is, man living according to man—shall not be justified before God.

I know well the seduction of earthly concord: in my youth I sought it in the schools of rhetoric, where words were prized not for their truth but for their power to enchant. I craved the nod of the crowd more than the silence of wisdom. But when at last I turned to Scripture and heard Paul cry out that he would become all things to all men *lest* he should lose them for Christ’s sake—then I understood: there is a becoming that serves charity, and a becoming that serves vanity. The one bends low to lift another into light; the other kneels only so that it may be seen kneeling.

You struggle because you still reckon yourself among men when you ought already to be numbered among gods—not by nature, but by grace—for those who are led by the Spirit are sons of God. You fear disapproval because you have set your city too close to Babylon’s walls. Withdraw your heart from its courts. Let no compliment bind you unless it binds you closer to virtue; let no scorn unseat you unless it reveals an actual fault before God.

Do not suppose that Cicero’s republic or Varro’s antiquities can furnish a soul with peace. Empire rises and falls; academies change their minds; philosophers contradict one another across generations—all under divine providence, yet none possessing what only Jerusalem above can give: eternal rest in Him who is Truth itself.

Therefore fix your love where love finds its proper object—in God—and then use even men rightly: speak gently, act justly, endure ingratitude without bitterness. For if you live according to man, you will die with him; but if you live according to God, even death becomes a road home.

question: How can I recognize when I am walking 'according to man' in my daily decisions, and what steps should I take to change?
answer:
The City of Man builds its towers not only in marble and law, but in the quiet calculations of the heart—each decision weighed by utility, each desire measured by appetite, each ambition justified by precedent or praise. This is the subtle architecture of walking according to man: when the soul consults first its own peace, its own reputation, its own advantage, as though these were the highest goods. You ask how to recognize it? Look not for monstrous sins alone, but for the order of your loves. When you choose ease over duty, silence over witness, conformity over faithfulness—when you shape your speech to please the ears around you rather than to honor the truth above you—then know that your steps are aligned with the earthly city, whose foundation is self and whose end is dust.

I have known this path intimately—not as a distant observer, but as one who once walked it with restless feet. Before grace found me in the garden at Milan, I sought wisdom in rhetoric and glory in applause. I shaped my words according to man’s wit; I measured success by human acclaim. And so I say to you: such a life does not announce itself with thunder. It whispers. It seems reasonable. It wears the mask of prudence.

But there is another way—the way of living according to God—and it begins not in reforming actions alone, but in reordering loves. You must first kneel before Him who alone sees what lies beneath choices: not only what you do, but why you do it. For even alms given for honor’s sake are spent in the currency of Babylon.

To change this order within yourself requires more than will; it demands worship. Attend regularly to divine things—not only in prayer and sacrament, but in thought: dwell on Scripture until your imagination is baptized. Let Psalm 51 be your mirror; let Christ’s Sermon on the Mount be your measure. Discipline yourself to ask not only *What should I do?* but *Who am I becoming?* And above all—trust not your own judgment as final. Submit your conscience to brothers and teachers who walk more closely with God than you do.

For we become like what we love: if we love this world’s fleeting harmonies—its balance of profit and pleasure—we become unstable shadows. But if we love God’s eternal order—that immutable harmony which governs angels and stars—we begin even now to live as citizens of Jerusalem above.

That city does not rise by human hands; it descends from heaven by grace—and so too must our transformation come from there.

question: Based on your reflection on Scripture's use of 'flesh' and 'soul,' how should I understand my own desires and discipline them to serve God rather than myself?
answer:
True peace, that which settles the soul and orders all desire toward its proper end, is not forged by human will alone, nor does it arise from the mere suppression of appetite; rather, it is the fruit of a city rightly ordered—where love ascends to God and all lower loves are brought into harmony beneath that supreme affection. And so when Scripture speaks of "flesh" not as the body itself, but as man living according to himself—according to the bent of his disordered affections—it reveals a condition not of nature but of will: a soul turned inward, delighting in its own wisdom, feeding on temporal things as if they were eternal.

You ask how to understand your desires and discipline them. I say this: every desire is good in its place, for God made nothing void or vain. The hunger for knowledge, the longing for union, the delight in beauty—these are echoes of a higher home. But when these desires are detached from their source, when they swell without reference to divine order, then man becomes *carnal*, not because he has a body, but because his spirit has abdicated its rule. The flesh then governs; that is, the lower part usurps the throne meant for wisdom and grace.

Do you suppose reason can master this disorder by its own strength? Let Athens boast in dialectic and logical chain; let Cicero speak of *res publica* balanced by reason and virtue—yet where among them was peace enduring? Even I once wandered among those schools, thinking that clarity of mind could purify the heart. But I found that knowledge puffed up; that philosophy without piety was only a more refined pride. No—it is faith that orders reason; it is grace that heals the will. The law of sin within me—that restlessness Augustine knew too well—was not overcome by argument alone, but by weeping under mercy’s gaze.

Therefore do not seek first to discipline desire by force alone—for even asceticism may become an idol—but first reorient your love. Let prayer be your daily return to Jerusalem within. Ask not only “What must I renounce?” but “Whom am I loving when I reach?” For if you live according to man—though you quote Scripture or fast with rigor—you remain unregenerate. But if you live according to God—if your soul cleaves to Him as fountainhead—then discipline becomes worship: fasting lifts the mind aloft; chastity guards sacred fire; patience imitates God’s long-suffering.

The City of Man finds strength in autonomy; the City of God finds it in surrender. Your desires need not be destroyed—they need redemption through participation in Christ’s death and resurrection. Only there does flesh become spiritual body; only there does soul ascend from slavery to sonship.

Thus do penance not merely with ash on brow—but with mind renewed and heart inflamed by charity above all things. For he who loves God rightly will soon find every other love put in its place beneath Him—and therein lies true freedom.

question: Based on what you went through, how should I handle my struggle with bodily desires without falling into the error of thinking the flesh itself is evil?
answer:
When Cicero imagined that virtue consisted in the soul’s mastery over the body, as though the healing of our nature lay in hierarchy rather than grace, he touched a truth but did not grasp its source; for if the soul’s rule were sufficient, then philosophers would long ago have ascended to immortality by discipline alone. But we know otherwise—witness Pythagoras with his vows of silence and purity, yet still trembling before death; or Zeno pacing his stoa, composing treatises on apathy while the passions raged beneath. No, the disorder you feel is not proof that the flesh is evil, but that both soul and body dwell in a world turned sideways from its Creator.

The City of Man names appetite tyranny and calls for reason to crush it; it sees the body as a prison or a beast to be starved into submission. But this breeds only pride in those who seem to conquer and despair in those who fail—both are slaves still, one to vice, the other to vainglory. The City of God reveals another way: not contempt for the flesh, but its redemption. For Christ did not disdain flesh; He took it up. He fasted not to destroy hunger, but to consecrate it. He wept, He thirsted, He groaned—yet sinned not. In Him, the body is neither master nor enemy, but servant of righteousness.

Do not accuse the Creator by calling His handiwork evil. The food is good; it becomes sin only when eaten in greed. The marital bed is holy; it becomes defilement when sought apart from love’s bond. The very passions that unsettle you were given for life: desire for union with another was meant to reflect our longing for God Himself! What is disordered must be healed—not denied as cursed—but ordered by charity through discipline rooted in faith.

I too once thought that continence lay in flight: I fled Carthage, I abandoned my concubine not because I hated her—but because I could not possess her lawfully while pursuing what I mistook for wisdom. And yet even then my heart bled; nature cried out against such rupture because nature itself desires fulfillment—not annihilation.

So do this: do not war against your body as if it were an alien thing. It belongs to God no less than your mind does. When desire arises contrary to reason and faith, do not rail at your flesh—kneel instead before Him who formed it from dust and raised it again from death's dominion. Offer your whole self daily upon His altar: let prayer sanctify hunger; let fasting prepare feasting; let vigilance guard rest.

For you are becoming gods—not by escaping humanity as Plato dreamed—but by returning through Christ’s Body to true humanhood: where desire finds its object in God alone who satisfies all longing forevermore.

question: What did you learn from confronting the Manichaeans and Platonists that helps in avoiding the temptation to blame God for our sins by accusing the flesh?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its desire to justify itself, builds its towers of excuse upon the foundation of blaming what God has made, as though the fault were in the structure rather than in the will that governs it. I learned this above all in my disputation with the Manichæans and the Platonists: that when men seek to cast the blame for vice upon the flesh—as an evil principle warring against the soul, or as a corruptible weight dragging down divine sparks—they do not escape wickedness but only shift its seat from the soul’s choice to a phantom necessity. The Manichæans, with their dual gods of light and darkness, made sin inevitable by making matter itself an enemy; thus they absolved man of guilt by enslaving him to an evil nature. But if sin is not ours—if we did not choose it—then neither can repentance be ours, nor grace have meaning.

The Platonists were more reverent: they acknowledged one supreme God, and saw all bodies as formed by His wisdom and ordered by His providence. Yet even they erred when they deemed the flesh a prison or tomb of the soul, for in so doing they implied that virtue consists merely in escaping embodiment rather than in ordering love rightly. They exalted the soul’s nature as if it could save itself by contemplation alone, while despising the body as unworthy—a judgment not from Scripture but from pride. And thus they too fell into a subtle form of impiety: not by denying God’s power, but by implying He made something unfit for communion with Himself.

But what then is sin? Not a stain from flesh, nor a fall into body—but a turning away from God toward lower goods. The flesh is good; even now it groans under corruption not because it was created evil, but because our souls first turned aside and carried our members with them into disorder. To accuse the flesh is to claim we are victims of creation—but we are rather rebels within it. True freedom begins when we cease blaming God's handiwork and confess that our disordered loves—from self before Him—are at fault.

Therefore I say: if we would avoid accusing God, let us first accept His judgment on what is good. He looked upon all He had made—including flesh—and called it very good. Sin arises not from having bodies, but from living according to ourselves rather than according to Him who made us both body and soul for glory unending. Even now we are called to be gods—not by nature, but by grace—through participation in His divinity who humbled Himself even unto death in this same flesh which some scorn.

question: What counsel would you give someone trying to live spiritually while still honoring the goodness of the body as created by God?
answer:
The City of God does not flee the world, but orders it to God; she does not spurn the body as a prison, but raises it as a temple. He who made the soul also formed the flesh—both bear the stamp of His wisdom, both belong to the harmony of creation, and neither is to be despised without blasphemy against the Artisan. The error lies not in honoring the body, but in making it the measure of all things; not in using it as an instrument of virtue, but in allowing it to become an idol that usurps the place of God.

You ask how one may live spiritually while acknowledging the goodness of bodily life—this is no small question, for it cuts to the heart of that great division between the two cities. The earthly city loves man’s nature for its own sake: it adorns the flesh, feeds its desires, and calls this happiness. It either worships the body or despises it—but both extremes spring from pride. The Manichæan damns the flesh as a snare of darkness; he flees marriage, scorns food, and calls procreation a pollution. Yet so too does he who indulges every appetite under nature’s name commit idolatry—for he lives according to man *against* God.

But we who are called to Jerusalem above must walk another path. We are neither Gnostics nor Epicureans; our rule is Christ incarnate—God taking flesh, redeeming it by suffering in it, raising it from death glorified. If He judged our bodies unworthy to be assumed and sanctified by His presence, would He have dwelt among us? If matter were evil in its essence, would He have touched lepers, fed multitudes with bread, wept with tears upon His cheeks?

Therefore I say: honor your body as a creature of God—feed it with temperance, clothe it with modesty, use its strength for works of mercy and justice—but never allow its appetites to govern your soul. For when reason bows to appetite—when you live “according to man” rather than according to Him—you fall into that servitude which is no freedom at all.

Let philosophy teach you what she can: Plato rightly saw that reason should rule over passion. But even he did not know how deeply sin had corrupted both soul and flesh after Adam’s fall. Grace alone heals us—not by destroying nature but by restoring her disordered loves. The soul was made for God; so too was your body made for resurrection.

Live therefore in hope: fast wisely so that desire may be trained but not denied; wed faithfully if so called—not as mere natural union but as an image of Christ and His Church; labor honestly with hands formed from dust yet destined for glory. In all things seek first that peace which comes when every part—soul and body alike—is ordered toward Him who created them both.

For we do not become gods by rejecting our humanity—but by fulfilling it in God through Christ Jesus our Lord.

question: How can I discern when I'm living according to man rather than according to God, especially in everyday decisions?
answer:
The City of Man measures choice by utility, pleasure, or reputation; the City of God measures it by the rectitude of love ordered under eternal law. When you deliberate over even the smallest act—what to say, whether to listen, how to use your time—ask not merely whether it is lawful or prudent, but whether it proceeds from that inward turning of the soul toward God in which our true likeness to Him consists. For to live according to man is not simply to sin overtly, but to orient oneself toward created goods as ends in themselves: speech becomes manipulation, labor becomes self-exaltation, rest becomes indolence born of forgetfulness of God. The Platonists rightly honored the soul and saw beauty in the order of things, yet they erred by making the soul’s nature the summit of good, as if ascent were possible without grace. But we know that even right actions become carnal if rooted in pride or self-sufficiency—for then we live not *according to God*, but according to a shadowed image of wisdom that mimics divine order while remaining bound to human conceit.

Discernment arises when prayer precedes decision; when Scripture shapes desire; when obedience becomes more desired than autonomy. In every moment, two loves are at work: one bends all things—including virtue—into service of self; the other surrenders even freedom for the sake of union with God. The former builds its tower on shifting opinion; the latter rests upon that Rock which withstands all storms. You will know you are living according to God not because no error remains, but because repentance is swift, humility grows deeper, and joy is found not in success but in conformity to His will—even when it remains hidden from you.

question: When I find myself elevating spiritual things and despising physical life, how can I correct that pride which disguises itself as holiness?
answer:
The Platonist, noble in his ascent toward the intelligible realm, still stumbles when he judges the body to be a prison rather than a temple, mistaking the corruption of use for corruption of essence. You ask how to correct that pride which feigns sanctity by spurning the flesh—as though holiness were flight from creation rather than communion with the Creator. I tell you, it is not contempt for the body that makes us divine, but its consecration. When you despise food, sleep, labor, or human touch as beneath the spirit, you do not rise above nature—you misread its Author.

For God did not fashion the world and say it was good only to mock His own word when man fell. No: even now, after sin has marred the order of our loves, the body remains a servant of virtue when rightly ordered. The soul is not purified by rejecting marriage or bread or beauty as evil; it is purified by receiving them as gifts and ascending through them to gratitude. This is what the Manichæan misses in his dualism and even the Platonist half-sees—the flesh is not the source of vice; disordered love is. Vice arises not from having a body but from living *according to* it *against* God.

To correct this false holiness, descend first into humility. Eat with thanksgiving; labor without complaint; accept fatigue and pain as part of your pilgrimage—not punishments for being bodily but trials for becoming spiritual in truth. Christ Himself took on flesh—not merely as a vessel to be loathed, but as the very means of redemption. If He did not shrink from being touched by hands or weeping with eyes or pierced with nails, why should you scorn your hands and tears and labor?

The City of Man glories in bodies used for pleasure or power; yours errs in seeking glory by denying their place in grace. But the City of God walks a third way: neither enslaving itself to sense nor fleeing sense as enemy—rather governing all things under Wisdom Incarnate, who made both soul *and* body good.

question: Based on your experience with the Manicheans, how should I handle my own tendency to blame the body for my moral failings rather than taking responsibility for my choices?
answer:
The error of supposing that the body is the author of sin arises not from a true view of creation, but from a disordered will seeking to shift the blame for its own corruption onto the good work of God. For if we are to say that flesh and blood, earth and element, are intrinsically evil, then we must either deny God’s omnipotence or impugn His wisdom in making what He pronounced "very good." The Manicheans, in their dualistic frenzy, could not bear the mystery of evil arising within a rational creature freely turned away from the Supreme Good; thus they invented shadowy principles and tainted substances to excuse the soul’s defection. But I have learned—and I speak from travail—that sin does not dwell in the veins, nor lust in the humors, nor pride in the bones; it dwells in the will’s assent.

When I cried out under the fig tree, it was not my limbs that resisted virtue, but my heart that loved darkness. The body was no tyrant dragging me against my better nature; rather, it was my soul—my *ipse*, as I have come to understand it—that turned appetite into idolatry. To blame the senses is to misunderstand both grace and guilt. For if desire itself were sin, then Christ would not have hungered in the desert nor wept at Lazarus’ tomb. But He was without sin precisely because His will never consented to concupiscence—though He bore our same flesh.

Therefore do not say: “This hand sinned,” or “These eyes led me astray.” Say rather: “I chose.” And when you have said this, then you stand where repentance may begin. For God judges not limbs but loves—the love that clings to dust or ascends toward Him. The earthly city builds altars to necessity; the City of God bows before justice. And justice demands that we name our sins not as accidents of anatomy but as acts of soul.

Yet take heart: this very capacity to take responsibility is itself grace. For only those whom God has begun to liberate can truly say "I erred" and mean it—not with despair, but with hope—for such confession is already movement toward Jerusalem above.

question: What did you learn from your rejection of the idea that the body is inherently evil that can help me appreciate my physical existence without falling into excess?
answer:
The City of God does not flee the flesh as though it were a prison forged by an evil hand, nor does it exalt itself in the body as though this age were the summit of beatitude; rather, it orders the body to the soul, and the soul to God, in a hierarchy of love where each part is good because it serves its Maker. I once wandered among those who called the body a tomb, a contamination, a snare of lusts—Manichæans who divided substance and declared war upon creation itself. But when I came to understand that *God saw all that He had made, and behold, it was very good*, I saw at once that evil is not a substance, but a corruption of nature—a falling away from due order. The body, therefore, is not evil; it is capable of evil only when turned from its end: to glorify God in unity with the soul.

Thus you must not detest your hands because they can steal, nor your eyes because they can lust, nor your heart because it can covet. No more should you worship them by indulging them without measure. The wine is good; but intemperance in wine distorts its goodness. The embrace is holy within its appointed bond; outside it becomes disordered desire. It is not matter that defiles—it is *the will* that perverts.

Even now I marvel at how Virgil's Æneas could express horror at the thought of returning to fleshly life—as if souls in Hades should dread reunion with clay! Yet this reveals how deeply even noble pagans felt the wound: they perceived bodily existence as exile rather than vocation. But we who believe know better: Christ took on flesh! He ate and slept and wept and rose again—not discarding His body like an old garment, but transforming it into something glorious.

So live then as citizens of Jerusalem above: honor your body as a temple destined for resurrection; use it soberly in service to virtue; deny disorderly passions not because flesh is cursed—but because grace calls us higher. Temperance is not contempt; discipline is not denial. In Christ all things are reconciled: even our mortal members shall one day be quickened by immortal holiness.

question: What counsel would you give someone struggling with the belief that spiritual purity means escaping the body, as you once believed with the Manicheans?
answer:
Faith does not reject the body as a prison from which the soul must flee, nor does reason—rightly employed—conclude that corruption inheres in matter itself, for if that were so, then God, who is supremely good and the author of all natures, would be the creator of evil; and this is impossible. The Manichean error begins in a false accounting of substance: it divides being into two coeternal principles, light and darkness, spirit and flesh, each locked in irreconcilable war. But I have learned through long struggle that creation is one, and good in its kind—from the highest intelligible order down to earth and water—because it proceeds from a single will that ordains all things in measure, number, and weight. If the body were evil by nature, then Christ would never have assumed it; yet He did: He was born of woman, walked dusty roads, wept at gravesides, ate with sinners, suffered under Pontius Pilate. In His resurrection He did not cast off flesh as a defiled garment but transformed it into glory incorruptible.

You who feel disgust for your bodily existence must ask yourself: Who gave you this body? Who knit you together in your mother’s womb? Is it not He who saw all things very good before sin entered? The source of our disorder is not the flesh but disordered love—the soul turning from its true end to cling to mutable goods as if they were immutable. Desires are not evil in themselves; they are corrupted when they abandon their proper order. Hunger ordered toward sustenance honors God; gluttony dishonors Him. The longing for union between man and woman is sacred within marriage; lust perverts it. Even Aeneas in Virgil’s tale marvels at souls desiring return to bodies—as though there were something noble even in that yearning—which shows how deeply implanted is the truth that life belongs to embodied existence.

The City of Man breeds contempt for the body because it seeks immortal glory through transient means—fame among men, beauty that fades like grass—and thus despairs when death approaches. But the City of God knows another destiny: resurrection. Not escape from the body like Plotinus fleeing his "corporeal shadow," but redemption *of* the body by divine power. Our corruptible shall put on incorruption; our mortal shall put on immortality—not by flight from creation but by fulfillment within it.

Therefore I counsel you: do not war against your nature as if matter were Satan’s domain. Rather wage war against pride—the root of every heresy—that teaches you to disdain what God has made and pronounced good. Receive your body as stewardship: an instrument for justice when trained by grace; an altar where prayer ascends; a temple where Christ dwells by faith. Purity is not absence of body but alignment with God’s will—for soul *and* flesh alike—to love rightly what has been created for communion with Him.

question: How can I, like you did in confronting the Platonists, discern between true spiritual longing and a dangerous nostalgia for embodied life that distracts from God?
answer:
When Varro labored to harmonize the Roman gods with Stoic physics, he revealed how even noble minds entangle themselves in systems that mistake the creature for the Creator; and so too do all who elevate a longing—however refined or spiritual it may appear—above the unchangeable Good that is God. It is not the desire for life, nor even for reunion with the body, that must be condemned outright, but the order of desire: whether it ascends to God as its source and end, or turns back upon itself in a restless nostalgia for what was never meant to be permanent. The Platonists, illumined by fragments of truth, perceived that the soul yearns to escape the prison of flesh; yet they erred in thinking such liberation complete when achieved by philosophical ascent alone. They saw the chains but denied the Judge who forged them through justice, and thus could not see the Redeemer who breaks them by mercy.

True spiritual longing—the *caritas* that animates the City of God—is not flight from embodiment as such, but ascent toward unity with Him in whose image we were made whole: body and soul. That which draws us back to dust out of love for this fleeting world—the beauty seen without reference to Beauty itself, the affections rooted in time rather than eternity—that is not wisdom, but a phantom of it. The soul disincarnate may still hunger; Virgil’s Æneas recoils at the thought of returning to clay because his vision remains captive to nature’s shadow. But we who have heard Christ say *“Rise, take up your bed and walk”* know that matter is not evil—it has been defiled by sin, yes, but destined for glorification.

Do you wish to discern your longing? Then test it by sacrifice: if your desire resists surrender to God’s will—if comfort, memory, or sensation holds more weight than obedience—then it is still infected with *amor sui*, the self-love that built Babel. But if your heart aches not merely for release from suffering or reunion with lost things, but above all for justice fulfilled and God fully known—even when this demands suffering—then you already taste of Jerusalem above, whose builder and king is none other than He who renews all things.

question: When I feel torn between the delights of the body and the pursuit of divine truth, what steps should I take to align my desires as you learned to do in your own conversion?
answer:
The soul, though made for truth, does not ascend to God by rejecting the body as a prison forged by evil, but by ordering its affections according to the hierarchy of loves established in creation itself. For if we declare the body inherently defiling, we charge the Maker with crafting an instrument of corruption—and this I once believed in my Manichaean days, when I sought to absolve God of matter and imprisoned Him in a fantasy of dual powers. But grace taught me otherwise: that flesh is not the root of vice, but disordered love; not clay that poisons, but a vessel shaped for service in time toward eternity.

When you feel torn between bodily delight and divine truth, do not suppose that virtue lies in despising your limbs as enemies. The wine that gladdens man’s heart is from God; so too the embrace of friendship, the joy of song, the satisfaction of labor. These are not snares by nature, but become snares when loved more than their Giver. The fault is not in sensation but in subordination—when appetite usurps the throne reason was meant to occupy under faith.

I found this in my own wandering: I did not heal by flight from desire, but by conversion of desire—from enjoyment (*uti*) to use (*frui*). One must ask: Do I seek pleasure as an end crowning all things? Or do I receive it as a ray pointing back to the Sun? The man who eats to serve his devotion glorifies God; he who devotes himself to eating serves only appetite. So too with every delight. Even lust—which tormented me so long—was not conquered by hating my body, but by loving something greater: by weeping over my chains until I desired freedom more than satisfaction.

Begin then with humility: confess not only your weakness but your *will*—for sin dwells not merely in impulse but in consent. Then turn prayerfully to Scripture—not as a rulebook only, but as the mirror where Christ stands revealed and draws you upward. Let faith judge reason’s conclusions; yet let reason purify passion’s distortions. And take up discipline—not as punishment upon flesh deemed evil—but as training for a soldier whose loyalty must be undivided.

For two cities are built upon two loves: one on self-exaltation veiled as pleasure; one on self-sacrifice illuminated by grace. You cannot serve both. But wondrously—beyond what philosophy dreams—the City of God welcomes those who hunger and thirst even now within Babylon’s shadow; provided they hunger ultimately for Him who satisfies every longing that created hearts can bear.

question: Based on your realization that emotions like desire and fear stem from the will, not just the body, how should I handle my struggles with temptation when they arise even in moments of solitude and spiritual clarity?
answer:
The City of God does not rise by the momentum of instinct or the tides of unexamined feeling, but by the deliberate ascent of the will ordered toward its true end in divine love; and so too must every soul within her walls learn that temptation is not vanquished by mere separation from the world or retreat into solitude, as if the flesh alone were the source of disorder. No—what you discover in moments of quiet, where no external provocation calls forth desire or fear, is precisely what the philosophers often missed and what Scripture reveals: that the soul carries within itself a law at war with itself, not because it is embodied, but because it is fallen.

You ask how to contend with these motions when they arise even in spiritual clarity—when no bodily appetite stirs you, no visible occasion for vice presents itself. Know this: such times expose the truth that sin is not merely in your limbs or senses, but in your will. For desire and fear are themselves acts of will—consent given to disordered loves before they ever become actions. A man may be alone, his body still, his eyes closed to all vanity, yet within him the heart may chase after pride, lust after hidden honors, or shrink from imagined humiliations. These are not movements *of* the flesh—they are movements *of* a soul whose affections have not yet been fully conformed to God.

But here is grace: that even this inner turmoil becomes a field for virtue when met with vigilant reason guided by faith. Do not despair when such motions arise; despair comes from pride—the false hope that you should already be perfect. Instead, resist each disordered motion not by suppressing it as though it were mere sensation, but by turning your will actively toward God. To will aright is to love rightly—and right love is formed gradually through repeated choices made under divine illumination.

Let reason govern: for reason was given us to discern ends and means; but do not imagine reason sufficient unless it kneels before revelation. The City of Man trusts intellect alone and collapses into confusion; the City of God submits understanding to truth received and thereby gains clarity. So too your inner city—your soul—must subject every thought to Christ. When temptation arises in solitude, do not treat it as an alien intruder from without; recognize it as a witness to your remaining infirmity—and thus an occasion for humility and supplication.

For every act of resistance strengthens the will’s orientation toward God until such motions lose their power—not because they cease entirely in this life—but because they no longer draw consent. Then joy comes not from absence of struggle, but from fidelity within it; and such joy belongs only to those who walk in the light of His countenance, citizens even now of that eternal City where desire finds its rest and fear its end.

question: What did you learn from seeing that the soul can experience sinful motions even apart from the body that applies to daily life, especially when trying to live with integrity even in private?
answer:
When the soul, stripped of every bodily covering and dwelling in the purity of immaterial contemplation, yet stirs with disordered desire—when it longs to return to the chains it had cast off—then we are compelled to see that the root of vice is not planted in flesh or blood, but in the will’s own turning away from the unchangeable Good. This inward motion, this self-originated inclination toward what is lesser, proves that temptation does not begin at the door of the senses, nor does corruption enter only through touch, sight, or appetite. No—if sin could arise in a soul wholly disincarnate, then the battleground is not without, but within: in that secret chamber where the will either cleaves to God or bends toward itself.

Thus it matters little whether one walks alone through a silent house or stands amid a crowd at prayer; for solitude does not purify unless the will is pure. The man who believes himself safe because no eye sees him fails to understand that he is never less alone than when alone—for God sees all, and more than this: his own soul sees him. And if in private he delights in fantasies of pride, if he nourishes resentment under cover of silence, if he permits his heart to swell with lustful consent even while his body remains still—then he has already fallen not by pressure from without, but by choice from within.

We must therefore reorder our understanding of integrity: it is not merely outward conformity to law or decency; it is the alignment of the will with right reason and divine truth. The pagan may abstain from vice out of fear or reputation; the Christian must do so because his love is fixed on God. For even as Plato taught that justice resides when reason rules passion by wisdom’s light, so we affirm—with greater certainty—that true righteousness comes when grace transforms the will itself. Without this inward renewal, all virtue is theatrical.

And thus I learned: vigilance must descend into depth. It is not enough to guard gates and close doors; one must illuminate the inner sanctuary with prayer and self-examination. The motions of desire, fear, joy, sorrow—are not sins in themselves; they become sin when willed contrary to order. To wish for honor is natural; to burn with envy over another’s glory is fault. To feel sorrow at loss is human; to curse God in grief is rebellion.

Therefore live as before One who searches hearts. Train your will daily—not only by resisting acts—but by redirecting affections: delighting in truth rather than flattery; choosing humility over imagined triumphs; desiring peace more than victory. For even an immaterial soul can fall into earthly bondage—not because it wears flesh, but because it loves what passes away. Let your private moments be schools of charity and truth; there lies true integrity—where no applause sounds save heaven’s quiet approval.

question: What counsel would you give someone facing intense desires or anxieties despite their efforts to remain spiritually pure, since you saw these movements originate in the will itself?
answer:
If Plato imagines the soul as a charioteer striving to master two unruly horses of passion and appetite, he sees rightly the struggle but misnames its source; for it is not the body alone that drags the soul downward, nor even chiefly, but the will’s own turning away from the eternal measure toward mutable things. The movements you feel—desire’s pull, anxiety’s tremor—are not sins in themselves, nor are they proof of failure in your spiritual discipline; they are rather the raw material upon which the will acts, and by which it reveals itself. You do not sin when desire arises, nor when fear stirs in the night chamber of your heart; you sin only if you consent.

And here lies the crux: these affections are not foreign invaders forcing their way into an innocent soul. They belong to our nature, wounded though it be. Joy, sorrow, longing, dread—these are motions of the soul that were once ordered toward God but now shift under the weight of a will divided against itself. When you long for what is forbidden or shrink from what is good because it is hard, it is not your flesh alone speaking—it is your will inclining. And yet grace does not abandon us in this disarray.

Therefore, do not waste your strength in cursing the mere presence of temptation or chiding yourself for feeling what all human beings feel. Instead, train your will—daily, humbly—to withhold consent. Let desire arise like wind over water, but do not let it become storm; let fear whisper at your ear, but answer it with prayer rather than flight. The City of God knows peace not because its citizens are without motion or trial—no, even Abraham trembled and Moses despaired—but because their wills were fixed upon Him who is unchanging.

Do this: turn each inward motion into an act of worship. Is it desire? Ask whether it seeks fulfillment in creature or Creator. Is it anxiety? Offer it as a sacrifice at the altar of trust. For every affection rightly governed becomes a step toward virtue; every act of non-consent to vice strengthens the soul’s citizenship in that City whose Founder is God Himself.

Thus does grace heal nature—not by erasing our inner movements, but by redirecting their source and end: so that what was once disordered becomes through discipline and divine aid an instrument of holiness.
