question: What sorrow did you carry when you realized that our very nature, once whole, became fractured by that first rebellion?
answer:
The City of God rises on the foundation of obedience, even unto death; the City of Man falls by the pride of autonomy, even in the garden. And when I came to see—through long struggle, through tears and the piercing light of grace—that our nature was not made thus, but was meant to stand in harmony with itself and with its Creator, a sorrow deeper than regret took hold of me. It was not merely that Adam sinned; it was that I found myself already within his sin, not as a distant descendant but as one born into a world where concupiscence stirs before reason speaks, where the flesh wars against the spirit before the soul has even chosen sides.

I had once sought wisdom among the philosophers, thinking reason could lift me to truth. But no dialectic could explain why my will was in revolt against itself—why I loved darkness when I professed to seek light. Only when I understood that we are not merely individuals who sin, but a people who *were* in Adam when he fell—only then did I grasp the profundity of that fracture. Nature itself groans beneath a curse it did not originate yet cannot escape.

And yet—such is divine mercy—even this disordered state bears witness to God’s justice and goodness. The shame we feel in lust is no mere social convention; it is nature’s own protest, dimly remembering its original dignity. Had we remained in obedience, generation would have been serene, subject to will and reason; there would have been no confusion, no blush—not because purity lacks awareness, but because integrity knows no division.

Thus sorrow turns toward hope: for if one man's disobedience brought captivity to all, how much more does one Man’s obedience restore liberty? The second Adam reclaims what the first lost—not by philosophical ascent, but by descending into death and rising in glory. In Him, nature is not abolished but healed; desire restored to its true end; love lifted from self-contempt to self-gift. This is the work of grace: not to leave us as we are, but to make us again what we were made to be—sons adopted through redemption, citizens of a City whose Builder and King is God.

question: How did the thought of a world without sin and death move you, as you pondered what Adam lost and what grace must restore?
answer:
Had Plato beheld the city he dreamed of—the republic ruled by philosopher-kings and ordered toward the good—had he seen it perfectly realized not in speech but in nature, he would still have fallen short of that original justice which Adam forfeited. For that first man stood not as a soul striving upward through dialectic, nor as a mind abstracted from matter, but as a whole living harmony: body subject to spirit, spirit subject to God, and all creation serving him in peace. There was no inward rebellion, no shame at the mere condition of embodiment, no fear of dissolution; for death itself had no hold upon him. The world without sin was not merely a world without crime or war—it was a world in which desire obeyed reason, and reason walked hand-in-hand with divine command. The members of the body did not wage war against the law of the mind, because they had never been loosed from their rightful subjection.

What moved me most deeply—and what stirred both sorrow and awe within me—was not simply the magnitude of what was lost, but the manner of its loss: through one act of disobedience, committed in a state of integrity and light, there fell upon nature itself a kind of servitude. Not only did man become mortal; his very powers for procreation—the noblest expression of his participation in divine creativity—became entangled with concupiscence. That which should have been pure stewardship over life became instead subject to lust and shame. And thus even our generative acts now bear the mark of exile.

But here grace shines all the more sovereignly: for where nature is corrupted beyond self-repair, God intervenes not merely to restore but to elevate. The City built on self-love—we see it rise and crumble across history—seeks immortality through fame or empire; yet only in Christ do we find true propagation—not after the fleshly likeness of Adam bound to death, but after the spiritual likeness of Him who is life itself. What Adam broke by pride is mended by humility; what was lost in paradise is regained in redemption.

And so I came to understand that human community cannot be restored by philosophy or law alone—for these address symptoms—but only by participation in that other City whose Founder died and rose again. In Him we recover what Adam lost: not innocence only, but glory; not immortality merely, but union with God.

question: How did your own struggles with temptation reshape your understanding of what it means to live 'after the flesh'?
answer:
It is not merely the body’s rebellion that binds us to the City of Man, but the soul’s assent to its own disordered loves—for I learned in the crucible of my own restless heart that to live after the flesh is not simply to indulge in pleasures of sense, but to make the creature, however subtle or spiritual it may appear, the measure and end of desire. The Platonists taught me to scorn the flesh and ascend toward the intelligible; yet even as my mind scaled those heights, my will remained in chains, for wisdom without humility breeds only a more refined pride. I sought beauty, truth, order—but I wanted them as trophies of my own achievement, not gifts from the Giver. Thus did my concupiscence extend beyond lusts for women or honors or words; it was deeper: a hunger to possess even virtue for myself.  

This is why Augustine Varro never grasped: he divided humanity according to philosophies or customs, as if virtue could be apportioned by discipline alone. But no man escapes Adam’s wound—not through eloquence, not through stoic restraint. To live after the flesh is to order one’s life by self as center; and this city—this vast society of those who love themselves—spreads its dominion not only through open vice but through gilded vices: ambition disguised as duty, knowledge without charity, prayer offered for reputation. Even my tears at Carthage were mine—I wept over Dido’s fate while indifferent to my own perdition.  

But grace pierced me not in argument nor in ecstasy alone, but in that humiliating moment when I heard a child’s voice saying *“Take up and read,”* and found myself laid bare before a text far sharper than any dialectic. Then I understood: living after the spirit begins not with ascent but with descent—when the soul confesses it cannot heal itself. The City of God rises on the ruins of self-will; her citizens are those who have been seized by mercy they neither earned nor expected. They still struggle—yes—but now with different longing: not to possess truth, but to be possessed by Truth Himself.

question: Looking back on your years of searching in pride and ambition, what would you tell someone who doubts that such inward rebellion leads to spiritual death?
answer:
It is not merely by overt acts of vice that man falls into the servitude of death, nor does rebellion against God begin first in the members of the body — no, it rises from the throne of the will, where pride clothes itself as reason and ambition masquerades as virtue, just as Cicero imagined that glory and republic could be ends in themselves, when in truth they are but shadows unless ordered to the immutable Good.  

I know this because I once built my soul like a temple to eloquence, to honors won in the forum, to the delight of being praised by men whose judgment was as shifting as the wind. I lived according to the flesh — not only in lusts of sense, though those entangled me too, but in that deeper concupiscence of the heart which desires to be one’s own master, to find truth by one’s own ascent, to command rather than submit. And what was all this but a city founded upon self-love unto contempt of God? The City of Man does not announce itself always in open blasphemy; often it whispers through philosophy, through ambition cloaked in virtue, through a soul that says: *I will walk in the light of my own understanding*.

And yet what light is this? It dims before the first shadow of sorrow. When my friend died in Thagaste and my world cracked open with grief I had no refuge — for I had placed my happiness where it could not abide. Then came despair: for if even friendship and learning cannot endure, what remains? Only dust — unless there is One who raises dust into glory.

This inward rebellion — this turning away from God as if He were not our proper end — brings death not because God is wrathful without cause, but because our nature is disordered by its very posture. Just as a harp string tuned to discord cannot make music no matter how skillfully plucked, so too a soul bent on its own glory cannot produce life when life flows only from union with its Creator. To doubt that such pride leads to spiritual death is like doubting that fire burns while standing within its flame.

But blessed be God — not all is lost! For even while we were building towers toward heaven with words and wisdom meant to scale divine heights alone, He descended. Not through argument did He conquer me at last; not through rhetoric or academic triumphs. A child’s voice singing “Take up and read,” and Scripture piercing my heart like a two-edged sword — there grace overcame pride.

So I say now: he who doubts that inward rebellion kills has never truly seen himself mirrored in eternity's light. Let him look again — let him trace his desires down into their roots: Why does he seek knowledge? For whom does he labor? Whose approval silences his conscience?

For two loves have made two cities. Choose thou which thou wilt serve — but understand: one leads ever downward toward dissolution; the other upward into communion with Him who said: “I am the resurrection and the life.”

question: What sorrow did you carry when you realized how deeply the fall had distorted human nature, not just in body but in soul?
answer:
It was not in the collapse of Rome, nor in the sack of any city, that I beheld most clearly the ruin wrought by Adam’s sin, but in the very structure of human desire—how it bends inward, fractures communion, and turns even reason into a servant of pride. When I came to understand that the fall did not merely clothe us in mortality, as if death were its chief wound, but poisoned the fountain of our nature—so that every generation flows from a corrupted spring—I felt a grief deeper than personal shame. This was no mere defect in custom or law; it was an inversion written into the soul: the will now rebels against God even when it seeks good, and love itself is twisted toward domination rather than sacrifice.

I had once praised Cicero’s *Republic* for its vision of justice—the harmony born when each part of society serves its proper function—but I saw at last that no such order could endure among men unless God were acknowledged as Lord of both heart and history. For what justice can long survive when those who govern are ruled by envy, when teachers deceive themselves while instructing others, when friends love conditionally and husbands possess rather than cherish? These are not accidents of culture; they are symptoms of a deeper disease—one whose root lies not in faulty education or corrupt institutions alone, but in that primal apostasy whereby we preferred ourselves to our Creator.

And so I came to mourn not only my own stolen pear or restless lusts—though these were real wounds—but the universal captivity beneath them: infants already bound to wrath before they speak, nations rising only to feed on one another under crowns and banners, philosophers soaring with noble thought yet falling short because they would not kneel. The soul was meant to rule the body by reason’s light; reason was meant to ascend through created things to wisdom divine. But now reason is darkened; desire usurps authority; and even our highest thoughts often serve as veils over pride.

Yet even in this sorrow there dawned mercy: for had grace not reached me through Scripture’s authority—had Christ not descended into this wreckage as Physician and King—I would have remained blind beneath philosophy’s gilded ruins. It is one thing to lament man’s misery; it is another to see at last that from this same miserable stock there rises a City whose Builder and Ruler is God Himself—the City where tears are borne not unto despair but unto purification, where death itself is made passage rather than prison.

question: How did the memory of your mother's prayers influence your view of grace when you considered how few are rescued from the kingdom of death?
answer:
It is not by the strength of will, nor by the subtlety of argument, nor even by the sincerity of desire that any man escapes the dominion of death—no, not even my own. For long I wandered in the city built upon pride, adorned with rhetoric, drunk on curiosities, mistaking every flicker of intellect for light; and though I sought truth, I sought it as one might grope in a tomb for a corpse that cannot rise by its own motion. The justice that orders all things rightly sees that none are owed deliverance; for from the first disobedience humanity became not merely guilty but corrupt—infected in nature, inclined to evil, and subject to death as to a king enthroned over all. And if we consider how few are rescued from this kingdom ruled by sin and terminated only by eternal death, we are forced to confess: salvation belongs wholly to grace.

My mother Monica wept for me more than others mourn their dead. Night after night she offered supplications so fervent they seemed to pierce the veil of heaven itself. At Carthage I lived in rebellion—not only in lust and vanity but in false wisdom, boasting knowledge while enslaved to ignorance. Yet those tears were not idle sentiment; they were weapons forged in faith, rising before the throne where mercy triumphs over judgment. When at last I was drawn out of darkness into that illumination which is beyond reason though never contrary to it, I understood what her prayers had proclaimed all along: grace does not wait upon human merit. Indeed, if it did, no one would be saved.

The City of Man counts its victories in names remembered and empires extended; it measures rescue by armies mustered or treaties sealed. But the City of God knows another power—the hidden call that draws a soul when no visible hand moves toward it. That such a man as I should be turned from Manichaeism’s shadows and skepticism’s labyrinths was no work of mine; had God waited for me to become worthy before He acted, I would still wander lost among books and desires. But He chose me before He formed me—and her prayers were part of His choosing.

Therefore let no man boast as though he pulled himself out from under death’s weight; let him rather tremble at how few are lifted while so many sleep undisturbed beneath tyranny older than Rome or Babylon. It is not because Monica prayed more than others that I stand redeemed—but because God willed through her tears to manifest His mercy according to His inscrutable purpose. Grace is undeserved precisely so that it may be grace—and thus the few who dwell now in the Heavenly City do so only because Love bent down to raise them from dust and restore them as citizens not made but reborn.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even the Stoics, pursuing wisdom of the soul, were still living after the flesh?
answer:
The City of God knows no peace that is not founded upon the love of God, while the city of man—be it adorned with syllogisms or ascetic discipline—seeks its peace in the ordering of its own soul as if wisdom could be sovereign without humility. When I came to see that the Stoics, for all their discipline, their contempt for pain, their mastery over passion, were yet living after the flesh, it was not with scorn but with a kind of sorrowful clarity that my spirit trembled. For I had once sought God through philosophy, had climbed the heights of rhetoric and logic as though they were ladders to divine wisdom, and had imagined that purity of reason or fortitude of mind might suffice for virtue. But here was the wound laid bare: even the soul’s highest attainments, when turned inward upon itself as its own end, become but another form of pride—the very mark of the earthly city.

They who place their supreme good in the soul’s self-possession forget that *spirit* in Scripture does not mean merely *the rational part of man*, but that which is animated and directed by God Himself. To live after the spirit is to be led by grace; to live after the flesh—even if one never indulges a carnal pleasure—is to live oriented toward self rather than toward Him from whom every good motion proceeds. The Stoic may chain his passions like Prometheus bound to his rock, but unless his will is offered freely to God, he remains in captivity—not to desire, perhaps, but to himself.

I knew this once in Carthage when I gloried in my intellect; I knew it again in Milan when tears broke through at Ambrose’s preaching. Reason is a noble servant but a false god; philosophy may prepare the mind like a courtyard before a temple—but it cannot open the gate. The revelation that even wisdom unrooted from charity is fleshly—that struck me not as an abstract doctrine but as a judgment upon my own life. And so I fell silent before God, who alone lifts us from every city built on sand and sets our feet upon the Rock.

question: How did your own struggle with inner vices shape your understanding of what it truly means to live after the spirit?
answer:
It is justice, that most steadfast pillar of the City of God, which first laid bare to me the vanity of all who claim to live by reason yet remain enslaved to pride. For many suppose that mastery over the body—sobriety, temperance, even philosophical disdain for pleasure—is itself a spiritual life; and indeed, when I was entangled in Manichaeism and later drawn to the Stoics, I imagined that knowledge or self-control could deliver me from sin. But what is justice, if not the right ordering of love—love that gives to God what is His due, and subordinates all else accordingly? I once prized my intellect above humility, my eloquence above contrition; I sought truth as one might seek a prize to possess, not as a light by which to be transformed. The flesh I knew well—the pull of lust, the hunger for applause—but deeper still was the vice of my will: it desired not God for His own sake, but only as a means to satisfy its own restless pride.  

Thus I learned that to live after the spirit is not merely to rise above bodily indulgence as one ascends a philosophical ladder; it is to be turned wholly toward God by grace so profound it wounds before it heals. Even those who seem most disciplined—like the Stoics who exalt virtue as their supreme good—live after the flesh when they rely on their own strength and glory in themselves. For where there is no humility, no acknowledgment of divine gift, there can be no true life according to the spirit. The soul itself can become an idol when it trusts in its own rectitude. Only when I ceased measuring myself against men and stood naked before God—confessing not only my disordered passions but my disordered loves—did I begin to taste that peace which orders all things rightly: *civitas Dei*, where wisdom flows from worship and virtue from gratitude.

question: Looking back on your years of philosophical searching, what would you tell someone who believes moral discipline alone brings one close to God?
answer:
The Stoic, with his impeccable discipline, may bridle the passions and stand unmoved amid the storms of fortune, yet if he does so in the pride of self-sufficiency, he builds a tower in the City of Man that reaches not one inch toward heaven.  

I know this not merely from books but from the marrow of my own bones—from the days when I wore out the paths of rhetoric, when I sought truth in the schools and found only myself. I practiced continence; I even admired it. But what was that continence? Was it not often a more refined lust—the desire to be praised for strength, to glory in my own mastery over appetite? A man may fast from food and feast on pride. He may renounce marriage and embrace vainglory. Is this life according to the spirit? God forbid.

To live after the spirit is not to perfect one’s soul as though it were a statue carved by reason alone, but to submit that soul—indeed, to lose it—in love for God. The inner man must be cleansed not by willpower but by humility; not by dialectic but by grace. What matters is not whether one abstains from wine or women or sleep—but whether one does so *for God*, with love for Him as the root and end. Without this love, even virtue becomes a kind of idolatry: self preserved at the altar of self.

I once thought that philosophy could lift me to God by moral ascent—by ordering my soul as Plato ordered his Republic. But what I discovered in Scripture shattered that illusion: no ladder climbs high enough unless it is planted upon divine mercy. The City of God does not rise on columns of Stoic apathy or Ciceronian duty; it stands upon Christ alone—the Mediator between God and man, who draws us not by our discipline but by His charity poured into our hearts.

So do not ask whether you have mastered your body; ask whether you have given yourself away. For even if a man conquers every concupiscence yet lacks love, he is nothing—less than nothing: he is deception walking upright among men.

question: What inner restlessness drove you to see that even the pursuit of virtue could become a form of prideful indulgence?
answer:
Virtue, as the Stoic conceived it—self-sufficient, radiant in its own light, exalted above fortune and flesh—once dazzled me as the summit of human destiny; yet I came to see that even such lofty virtue, when nourished by the love of self, becomes a mirror in which the soul adores its own reflection rather than the face of God.  

For long I supposed that discipline of mind, mastery of passion, and pursuit of wisdom were sufficient to lift a man into communion with the divine. I had read Cicero and Plato and felt my spirit stir at their vision of an ordered soul ruling over the body like a philosopher-king over a turbulent city. And yet within me there remained an unrest—a subtle swelling of pride whenever I thought myself chaste, or learned, or righteous. It was not that virtue was evil; it was that my heart turned every good into an idol for its own exaltation.  

Even my repentance was once tainted with vanity: I would mourn my sins not because they offended God, but because they blemished my self-image. When I wept over my past lusts, it was sometimes less from sorrow for having despised divine love than from shame at having stooped so low in the sight of men—or worse, in the sight of myself. Thus did I discover that inner vices are far more perilous than outward indulgences: for a man may forsake wine and women and still feast on his own glory.

It is one thing to renounce pleasure; it is another to renounce self-will. And only grace can teach this distinction: for nature inclines us always toward self-preservation and self-exaltation—even under the guise of philosophy or piety. The City of Man builds temples to virtue as it builds temples to power: all for its own honor. But the City of God finds its praise not in what man achieves, but in what God forgives.

Only when I learned to doubt my own righteousness did I begin to approach true justice—not as something attained by effort, but received as gift; not declared by reason alone, but revealed in humility through faith. For even our reason must be healed by grace; otherwise it becomes merely another instrument by which pride constructs its tower toward heaven.

question: When you confessed that the soul itself could be a source of carnal pride, how did that change your view of true humility before God?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless ascent to self-deification, has ever sought to crown the soul as sovereign—placing reason upon the throne once reserved for God. It was this very elevation of the intellect, this worship of the inner man’s power, that long held me captive even after I had forsaken the pleasures of the body. I once believed that to live according to the spirit meant merely to rise above fleshly lusts and dwell in philosophical contemplation, as if the mind could ascend to heaven on its own wings. But divine grace, in its unsparing mercy, revealed to me that a soul puffed up with its own wisdom is no less carnal than a body glutted with wine and sensuality.

For what is flesh in Scripture but that which opposes God? Not merely skin and blood, but all nature turned inward—self-referential, self-justifying. And so when the soul boasts of its virtue, when it takes pride in its knowledge or composure like a Stoic statue untouched by passion—it does not transcend the flesh; it perfects it. The proud soul becomes a temple not of God but of itself. This is why Paul warns us that Israel stumbled precisely where they thought themselves secure: they sought righteousness through law and zeal, yet not by faith.

True humility begins when we confess not only our passions but our pretensions—that even our highest thoughts are dust unless formed by grace. I once thought myself humble because I no longer craved earthly delights; but I still craved acclaim, authority, intellectual mastery. The deeper sin was not in wanting pleasure but in wanting mastery over truth itself. Only when I saw that my mind was no less fallen than my loins did I begin to understand what it means to live according to the Spirit: not by ascent of reason alone, but by descent into submission—where knowledge kneels before revelation and love displaces pride as the form of wisdom.

Thus is fulfilled the word: “God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble.” For He does not reform arrogance; He breaks it—so that from its ruins He may build a city whose cornerstone is not self-esteem but charity rooted in divine truth.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even those who prize the soul above all else still live according to the flesh?
answer:
Justice, that most elusive and noble of human longings, cannot be found in any city built by men, however refined its philosophers or exalted its ideals — for even when they lift their eyes to the soul, they do so with the pride of self-possession, not the humility of self-surrender.  

When I saw this — when I came to understand that the Stoics, who claimed to dwell in the spirit by making the soul’s virtue their highest good, were yet captives of earthly ambition — it was not anger I felt, nor scorn, but a deep and piercing sorrow. For they had climbed high upon the ladder of philosophy, only to rest upon a rung that still trembled beneath the weight of pride. They honored the soul, yes — but as a citadel of autonomy rather than as an altar upon which to lay oneself before God. They sought tranquility through detachment from the body and its passions, yet remained bound far more dangerously to the passions of the mind: contempt for others, delight in intellectual mastery, trust in human sufficiency.

And thus they lived according to *flesh* — not because they indulged in bodily pleasures (indeed many denied them), but because Scripture speaks not only of flesh as mortal tissue but as humanity turned inward upon itself. When Paul says “no flesh shall be justified,” he does not mean only those wallowing in lust or gluttony; he means every man who would establish his own righteousness before God. The Stoic who boasts in his apatheia is no less under this condemnation than the profligate who wallows in vice — both seek salvation from themselves.

I knew this once myself. Before grace undid me beneath that fig tree in Milan, I too had prized my intellect above all things; I had ascended through Manichaeism into Neoplatonism seeking truth through speculation alone. Like them I thought purity was achieved by ascent — by climbing out of matter into mind. But what I found instead was not freedom but fatigue: for all my climbing could never deliver me from myself.

Only then did I grasp what divine wisdom veiled in paradox: that true spirituality begins not with rising above flesh but with acknowledging one's utter dependence on Him who “became flesh” — not merely body without soul (as some heretics claim), but fully man: body *and* soul *and* spirit offered up for us. The Word took on our whole nature so that our whole nature might be transformed.

Thus do both cities reveal themselves: one builds towers out of self-trust and calls it virtue; the other kneels at a manger and receives life as gift. One says: "I am sufficient," even if clothed in philosophical calm; the other says: "Lord, have mercy." And between these two confessions lies all history — all eternity.

question: How did your struggle to understand Scripture's use of 'flesh' change how you saw your own weakness and longing for God?
answer:
When Cicero, in his search for the summum bonum, lifts up the rational soul as the divine spark within us, he supposes that to live according to nature is to live according to reason—but he does not see that even the soul, noble as it is, can turn from God and become carnal. I once thought likewise, that to cultivate the mind and discipline the passions was enough; I exulted in rhetoric, in philosophy, in the ascent of the intellect toward truth. Yet all this time my soul—this spirit—was still living *after the flesh*, not because it lacked reason or refinement, but because it loved itself more than God.

It was Scripture’s paradoxical language that shattered my assumptions: “No flesh shall be justified by the law.” At first I stumbled at this word *flesh*, thinking only of bodily lusts and corporeal weakness. But I came to see that Scripture speaks more deeply: *flesh* signifies man turned inward upon himself—the whole man, soul and body—when he takes his own will as law. Even a philosopher who worships reason becomes carnal if he does not worship God. The Stoic who glories in self-mastery still lives according to fallen nature if he does not submit his pride to grace.

And so my understanding of Scripture’s use of *flesh* became a mirror: I had sought wisdom through books and ambition through men’s applause; I had wept over lost love and yet remained cold before divine love. My soul was restless—not because it was untrained or ignorant—but because it desired everything except Him who alone could satisfy. That word *flesh*, so often thought to name only bodily sin, came to reveal the deeper truth: my entire being—my intellect, my will, my desires—was weak not from lack of philosophy but from lack of piety.

Only when I saw myself truly as *man according to the flesh*, though adorned with learning, did I begin to long for that other City—the one whose foundation is love of God. For what good is it if a man gains mastery over arguments but loses his soul by refusing grace? It is not by rising above matter through reason that we are saved, but by humbling ourselves before Him who descended into matter for our sake: *The Word became man*, took on our full condition—not merely our bones and blood, but our weakness—and lifted us up not by contempt for the body or even for mere sinfulness, but by transforming human nature itself from within.

Thus did divine pedagogy correct me: what seemed a crude term—*flesh*—became an instrument of profound revelation. My longing for God began only when I ceased justifying myself and acknowledged that no man—even one polished by wisdom—is justified save by faith in Him who justifies the ungodly.

question: Looking back on your time with the Manichees, what would you tell someone who doubts whether true spiritual life is possible in this body?
answer:
The City of Man, restless in its pride, has ever sought to divide soul from body as though God could not sanctify the whole nature He created; and thus the Manichees, captivated by a false purity, scorned the flesh as irredeemable, imagining salvation to lie in the soul’s escape from this earthly tabernacle. But I have learned through bitter longing and divine mercy that such a hope is not only mistaken—it is a denial of the very economy of grace. For if the Word truly became flesh—if He who is co-eternal with the Father assumed our mortal nature, not in appearance but in truth—then how can we suppose that this body is alien to redemption? The soul alone was not sufficient for salvation; Christ took on flesh, He sanctified it by His dwelling in it, He raised it by His resurrection. Therefore, to doubt that true spiritual life is possible in this body is to call into question the Incarnation itself.

I once wandered among those who taught that matter was evil, and so I could not see that God’s goodness extends even to the dust from which we are formed. But grace humbled me, and Scripture enlightened me: the body is not an enemy of the spirit; rather, when subdued by faith and ordered toward God, it becomes an instrument of righteousness. Do we not walk by faith in this present life? Do we not offer our members as instruments of holiness? The Christian does not despise his body as a prison but treats it as a temple—weak though it be—destined for transformation at the last trumpet.

Philosophers like Plato exalt the soul as if it were capable of ascending to truth by its own flight; yet I have seen how even my own mind was darkened by concupiscence. No man rises to spiritual life unless lifted by grace—not beyond or apart from his body, but *in* it and *through* it. The sacraments themselves are corporeal: water in baptism, bread and wine in communion—visible signs conveying invisible realities. God meets us here, in time and flesh.

So let no one say that holiness belongs only to some future state or immaterial existence. The City of God grows even now within the midst of Babylon: its citizens live chaste lives amid lusts they do not consent to; they fast so that desire may be disciplined; they labor with hands and feet so that love may serve. This body groans under sin’s weight—but already bears about in itself the pledge of resurrection: “Though our outer man perish,” says Paul, “yet our inner man is renewed day by day.” Not destroyed with contempt—but renewed with hope.

question: What inner turmoil did you endure when you first confronted the limitations of human wisdom, like that of the Stoics, in satisfying your restless heart?
answer:
The City of Man erects its temples to wisdom, adorning them with the gold of dialectic and the marble of moral discipline, yet within their hallowed halls no voice calls home the restless soul — for all their splendor, they remain silent before the deepest cry of the heart. The Stoics, noble in their ascent, taught me to prize virtue above pleasure and reason above passion; they lifted the soul as high as nature could bear, placing felicity in the governance of the affections by right judgment. And I confess: I admired them. I sought refuge in their fortress, believing that if I could but master myself, if I could but align my will with universal reason, then peace would follow as shadow follows body.

But peace did not come.

For what is self-mastery without humility? What is moral rectitude without grace? The Stoic may command his anger and subdue his lusts; he may stand unmoved amid calamity; he may even love humanity in abstract form — yet he does not kneel. He does not know himself as a beggar before God. He sees sin as error or weakness to be corrected by effort, not as a rupture in love requiring mercy. His city is built on pride disguised as strength; it glories in self-sufficiency while starving on divine truth.

I learned this through my own collapse. When I heard the child’s voice in the garden — *Tolle, lege* — it was not because philosophy had satisfied me, but because it had utterly failed me. My will was divided against itself: I desired continence and cried out for it daily, yet remained bound. No syllogism freed me; no maxim broke the chains. Only grace — sudden, uninvited, overwhelming — tore me from that prison.

The City of God knows this: man is not saved by ascent but by descent — God descending in Christ, becoming flesh so that flesh might become spirit anew. Not by pulling ourselves up by reason's cords do we reach heaven, but by being drawn upward by love’s gravity from above. The Stoic seeks to deify man through discipline; Christ deifies man through sacrifice and resurrection.

Thus did my turmoil end: not when I understood more fully how to live well according to nature — many pagans lived nobly — but when I believed what was foolishness to them: that eternal life begins not with human wisdom perfected, but with a broken heart trusting in a crucified Savior.

question: When you reflected on the phrase 'the Word was made flesh,' how did it transform your view of your own humanity and its capacity for divine union?
answer:
That the invisible God should assume visible humanity is not a scandal to faith, but the very ladder by which reason ascends to divine truth. When I pondered the mystery of the Incarnation—“the Word became flesh”—I saw at once that my humanity, so long torn between pride and concupiscence, was not merely capable of union with God, but had been destined for it from creation’s dawn. The Stoics sought virtue through discipline of soul and reason, placing their highest good in the governance of passion by mind—but they missed the wound beneath: sin, which no philosophy can purge. They lived *after the spirit*, as men reckoning spirit with human wisdom; yet even this spiritual striving, apart from grace, remains captive to the flesh in Scripture’s deeper sense—the whole man turned away from God toward self.  

But when Christ becomes *flesh*, He does not merely clothe Himself in body; He reclaims and deifies human nature itself—body, soul, and will. In that moment of ineffable condescension, I perceived that my own disordered affections—the restless heart you know I confessed were long unquiet until they found rest in Him—were not to be extinguished or merely disciplined, but healed and lifted up. The Word assumed what was ours so that we might receive what is His: participation in divine life. Therefore I no longer saw my humanity as a prison of passions or a mere rational machine to be perfected by effort, but as a temple prepared for the indwelling Spirit—a nature capable of union because united already in Christ’s person.

Thus the supreme good is not found within ourselves—as Varro taught his schools or Cicero his republic—but outside us: God Himself given to us in Christ. And if man is justified not by law nor philosophy but by grace through faith, then even our capacity to love rightly flows from Him who first loved us into becoming new creatures. We are not saved *from* our humanity but *in* it—and now I see that what was made flesh in Christ shall be glorified flesh raised on the last day: whole men made whole in God’s eternal City.

question: What were you feeling when you confronted your own past desires, those 'works of the flesh' you once embraced before your conversion?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless hunger, builds altars to desire and calls them freedom; whereas the City of God finds liberty not in indulgence but in the ordered love of God, from whom all affections draw their due measure. When I look back upon those years—when my heart was a theater of unruly passions, when I lived for lusts and called them pleasures—I do not speak now as one untouched by shame, but as one stunned by grace. For it was not that I did not love, but that I loved wrongly: I loved the echo more than the Voice, the stream more than the Fountain. Adultery of the body? Yes—but worse, adultery of the soul: giving to creatures the devotion due only to the Creator.

I once justified such desires with philosophy, adorning vice with reason’s garlands. I said to myself, “Is not pleasure natural? Is not appetite part of our constitution?” And so it is—but perverted when it becomes lord instead of servant. The flesh I speak of is not merely this body that walks and breathes; no more is living “after the flesh” a matter only of carnal acts. It is an orientation: a soul turned earthward like a plant bent under stone, deprived of light. The works Paul lists—envyings, heresies, wrath—are not rooted in bodily pleasure at all; they are fruits of pride and disordered will. And these too were mine.

There came a time when God pressed upon me—not with thunder, but with silence—that these desires were not my nature fulfilled but my nature fallen. Not gifts misused through ignorance, but sins cherished against conscience. And yet even then I wept more for losing what I loved than for having offended Him who made me. That was still self at work—measuring grief by loss rather than justice.

But grace does not leave us where we are. It wounds before it heals; it strips before it clothes. To confront those desires was to see myself truly: a citizen of Babylon clothed in rags and calling them robes. Now I see that true peace—the *pax* which orders all things in their proper place—can never flourish where self reigns and God is exiled.

And so what felt then like chains—renunciation—is now known as deliverance; what seemed denial is revealed as homecoming.

question: How did your mother Monica's tears and prayers shape your understanding of the cost of living for the flesh instead of for God?
answer:
When the soul, made in the image of God, turns from the immutable Light to the flux of temporal things, it does not merely stumble—it descends into a disorder where even its affections become rebellious, and its desires form a tyranny more oppressive than any earthly despot. It was this inward collapse—the disintegration of the rational creature into fragmentation and passion—that my mother Monica wept over long before I could name it. She did not weep for mere moral failure, as though I had broken a law inscribed on tablets; no, her tears were for the ruin of a soul ensnared in the city of man, living according to *amor sui*—the love of self—even as she herself dwelt ever more deeply in *amor Dei*, the love of God.

She prayed at Carthage while I mocked the Christians; she followed me to Rome when I fled her pleas; she wept aloud when told I had embraced Manichaeism’s deceit. Yet never once did she cease to stand at the altar, interceding as one who knew that only divine grace could shatter the chains I wore as ornaments. Her tears were not sentimental—they were acts of spiritual warfare, sacraments poured out before Almighty God. And now I see what she foresaw: that every indulgence in pride, in lust, in idle curiosity—these were not isolated sins but limbs of one body: *the flesh*, as Paul names it—not because matter is evil, but because fallen humanity ordered against God becomes a kind of living death.

Monica’s prayers taught me that grace does not float down like dew upon a calm field; it invades like thunder from above, shattering illusions. Her weeping was wisdom incarnate: she knew what I could not yet comprehend—that to live after the flesh is to build one’s house upon shifting sand while believing it stone; whereas to live after God is to build upon Christ the foundation laid from eternity. And so her tears became part of His economy of mercy—for He heard her more than He heard me—and through them He prepared my heart for that day in Milan when words on a page leapt into fire and I fell undone by love.

question: When you read Paul's list of the works of the flesh, which one stirs the deepest sorrow in your heart for how it once ruled your life?
answer:
The City of Man, built upon the love of self lifted even to the contempt of God, finds its grammar in desire unchained from reason and grace, and among all the works Paul names, it was *lust of the eyes*—that insatiable hunger for what dazzles and pleases—that once held me most tightly in servitude. Not merely the vice of fornication, though that too stained my youth with shame, but the deeper disordered craving: to possess beauty without surrendering to its Giver, to consume truth as ornament rather than as way. I wandered through books of rhetoric and halls of ambition, chasing wisdom's shadow while fleeing her face. Varro’s encyclopedic pride pleased me; Cicero’s eloquence intoxicated; even Plato’s ascent toward the intelligible stirred my mind—yet all became idols when I loved them more than Christ. 

When Paul lists envyings, heresies, strife—these are not mere catalogues of vice but anatomies of a soul turned inward. And envy most particularly gnawed at me: I envied men their reputation in speech, their ease in learning, their moral stature I could not claim. But this envy was only pride’s echo—the refusal to receive goodness as gift rather than prize—and so it fed back into lust: not just of fleshly pleasure (though Confessions bears witness to that weakness), but of dominion over my own life without submission to divine order.

It is only now, dwelling more fully in the City of God—whose citizens live by faith, whose glory is humility before the Cross—that I see how every disordered love chains us not upward but downward: from pride springs envy; from envy springs strife; from strife springs heresy—not only doctrinal but existential: a false narrative woven about who we are apart from grace. The Apostle does not list these sins as isolated acts but as fruits growing from one root: living *after* the flesh means making appetite the measure of truth.

And so I mourn those years when I set myself up as judge and center—when my will was law and affection bent toward creature rather than Creator. But even this sorrow is now sweetened by mercy; for He who exposed my darkness also healed it, drawing me by love beyond all restless wanting into rest eternal.

question: How did the moment in the garden—when you heard 'take up and read'—change your vision of what it means to live not after the flesh, but in the Spirit?
answer:
Justice, that longed-for harmony of the soul and city alike, which even Plato sought but could not fully attain, is not established by dialectic alone, nor by the ordered rule of law apart from grace—for I once labored under such philosophies, and found them weightless before the tyranny of my own desires. It was not in the shade of academic debate nor in the quiet of stolen pears that I glimpsed the truth, but in a garden at Carthage, where sorrow had become a liturgy and my tears a kind of prayer. There, beneath a sky indifferent to my turmoil, a child’s voice—whether from boy or angel I know not—sang out: *Tolle, lege*: “Take up and read.” 

As if guided by an unseen hand, I seized the scroll of Paul and my eyes fell upon his verdict against the works of the flesh: not merely lust or gluttony, but envy, strife, heresies—the very divisions tearing apart both Church and empire. And there I saw myself: not as a sinner only in act, but as one whose whole will had been colonized by self-love to the contempt of God. The flesh he named was no mere body—it was man disordered, nature unruled by reason’s light because reason itself had turned from its source. To live after the flesh is to build one’s life on that inner discord; to live in the Spirit is to belong to another City—the City founded on love of God.

That moment did not merely correct my morals; it reoriented history for me. No longer did I see human striving as an ascent through wisdom alone—like Plotinus straining toward the One—but as a fall upward into divine mercy. For what law or logic could have compelled me to abandon ambition, lust, and pride? Only grace could sunder such chains. The Spirit does not perfect nature by degrees; He resurrects it through death to self. From that hour I understood: no man ascends into spiritual life who has not first knelt in shame—and then heard the voice that says not "Do better," but "Rise up."

question: What were you feeling when you realized that even abstinence from bodily pleasures could be a work of the flesh if done for idolatry?
answer: Justice, that true and eternal order established by the sovereign will of God, cannot be co-opted by human pride, no matter how severely the body is disciplined. When I came to see that even asceticism—yes, even the renunciation of lusts and wine and gluttony—could be an instrument of the City of Man if directed toward self-exaltation or false worship, it struck me not with mere surprise, but with the force of divine illumination piercing a long-held illusion. For years I had imagined that mastery over the flesh was itself holiness; in my youth among the Manichees, I pursued bodily purity as though it were salvation. But grace revealed to me the deeper truth: a soul bent on idolatry may fast with perfect rigor and still fatten on pride. What is it to mortify the members if hatred burns in the heart? What profit is there in abstaining from wine while drinking deeply from envy and heresy? The apostle’s list laid bare what philosophy had concealed—the soul can commit fornication without touching a body; it can be drunk with vanity while appearing sober to men. I wept when I understood that my past disciplines had been but splendid vices, my self-denial a scaffold for my own glory. True life according to the Spirit is not measured by what one avoids, but by what one loves: God above all things, and neighbor through God. The rest—whether feasting or fasting—is dust compared to this fire.

question: How did your own struggles with pride and ambition reshape your understanding of what it means to live after the soul rather than the flesh?
answer:
The City of Man exalts the soul’s restless striving, mistaking ambition for virtue and pride for wisdom, while the City of God bows the soul in humility, where true strength is found not in self-assertion but in surrender to divine love. It was not the pleasures of the flesh alone that held me bound in my youth—though indeed I wept over stolen pears not for their taste but for the dark delight of transgression—but far more deadly was the lust for human glory, that insatiable craving to be praised, to be seen as wise, to tower above others in reputation. This was not indulgence of the body; it was the soul enthroned as idol. I abstained from many sensual excesses even while steeped in vanity, teaching rhetoric with pride, chasing applause through polished words, ascending toward honors that cast long shadows but no light. And thus I learned from Scripture’s piercing clarity: to live after the flesh is not merely to wallow in bodily desires, but to order one’s soul—yes, even an ascetic soul—around self as its final end.

When I climbed the ladder of worldly success, I thought myself enlightened; when I taught philosophy with eloquence and scorned lesser minds, I believed I pursued truth. But what truth could dwell where love did not? My soul was at war with itself—hatred of error curdled into contempt for men; zeal for knowledge became rivalry; desire for wisdom masked a deeper hunger: to be admired. These were not carnalities of appetite but animosities of spirit: envy cloaked as discernment, wrath disguised as justice. And so Paul’s list laid bare my condition—I who had fasted from wine yet drowned in pride; who had fled lusts yet embraced heresy with vainglory—proving that abstinence without charity is only another form of concupiscence.

Only grace shattered this illusion—not by confirming my intellect but by humbling it. In the garden at Carthage, it was not argument that broke me but a child’s voice chanting *“Take up and read”*—and there, in trembling hands holding Romans 13, I saw that transformation begins when the soul ceases its rebellion and submits its highest faculties to God. Then at last I understood: a man may fast like an anchorite and still live after the flesh if his soul refuses obedience; conversely, he who eats and drinks may walk in the Spirit if his heart loves God above all things.

Thus did my ambition become my teacher: not because it led me upward into fame, but because its collapse revealed that justice resides neither in indulgence nor denial per se—but in the orientation of love. The earthly city worships genius; it builds libraries and academies and calls them eternity. The heavenly city kneels before Christ crucified—the wisdom foolishness to philosophers—and knows that true elevation comes only by descent into humility. For there alone does the soul cease living after itself and begin living unto God.

question: Looking back on your time in the Manichean sect, what would you tell someone who thinks moral rigor alone brings one closer to God?
answer:
The City of God is not built by the strength of abstinence, nor does its foundation rest upon the mere rejection of bodily pleasure; rather, it is founded upon love—love of God to the contempt of self, and love of neighbor as ourselves in Him who first loved us. I once believed, as many still do, that strict observance, the discipline of the body, and the denial of sensual delight were sufficient passports to divine wisdom. In my days among the Manichees, I wore my asceticism like armor, imagining myself purified because I had rejected wine, or certain foods, or marital embrace. But what profit was it, if within me burned pride, contempt for the humble mysteries of Scripture, and a spirit that disdained grace as though truth could be grasped by intellect alone?

Moral rigor without charity is but a sounding cymbal—loud in performance, empty in substance. The apostle names envyings and heresies among the works of the flesh—not because they gratify physical desire, but because they proceed from a soul turned inward upon itself. One may fast with great constancy and yet feed voraciously on hatred; one may abstain from wine and yet be drunk with ambition; one may live apart from all carnal delights and yet worship at the altar of his own understanding. This is not ascent toward God—it is refinement within the City of Man.

I would tell such a soul: examine not only your deeds but the root from which they spring. Did you deny yourself for God—or for glory? Did you renounce pleasure to draw nearer to Him—or to elevate yourself above others? The devil himself abstains; he neither eats nor marries. Yet he remains the enemy. True righteousness—divine righteousness—is not measured by what you have cast off in behavior alone, but by what has been received in faith: humility born of grace, obedience flowing from love, peace rooted in submission to divine will.

The martyrs did not conquer by rigid discipline but by unshakable faith—even when some among them had lived long in sin before their final confession. And many an ascetic perishes who never tasted wine but never tasted mercy either. Therefore let no man suppose that holiness is found merely in restraint: it is found where Christ is—in crucified affections turned wholly toward Him who justifies both tax collector and monk alike by faith through grace.

question: When you named envy and heresy among the works of the flesh, were you remembering moments in your own life when theological pride poisoned your soul?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its ascent to self-deification, builds its towers not only of lust and gluttony but of intellect turned weapon—where reason, unmoored from humility, becomes the instrument of spiritual ruin. It was not merely an abstract list I drew from the apostle’s pen when I named envy and heresy among the works of the flesh; it was a mirror held to my own soul in its most fevered hours. In my days as a teacher of rhetoric, I sought truth indeed—but more eagerly, I sought to conquer men with speech, to dazzle them with knowledge that exalted myself as the vessel. My very pursuit of wisdom was tainted by a lust for mastery: I delighted not simply in understanding God, but in being seen as one who understood Him—while others did not.

I once defended Manichæan doctrine with such vehemence that I scorned those who questioned it—not because they misunderstood divine truth, but because they threatened my standing among men. There was no wine on my lips nor fornication in my bed at those hours; yet my soul burned with thirst far more deadly: the thirst to be right at all costs, to triumph in disputation even if it meant denying grace itself. That too is flesh—not only what drags us downward into bodily indulgence, but what lifts us upward into proud isolation from charity. For heresy is not merely error; it is rebellion clothed in logic. And envy? Even among monks and bishops—it festers when one sees another’s holiness and feels diminished rather than drawn nearer to God.

Thus does the earthly city erect its altars within the church itself: where doctrine becomes domain, where orthodoxy is wielded as a scepter rather than offered as a gift. But blessed be He who pierced my vanity with mercy—for He brought me low so that I might learn this: no height of argument can substitute for love; no victory in disputation avails if charity is absent. The City of God walks by faith and endures by humility; it asks not "Who am I above?" but "To whom have I drawn near?"

question: How did recognizing that hatred lives in the soul, not the body, change the way you confronted your own inner divisions?
answer: It is impossible for the city of man, even in its most disciplined forms, to produce true peace, since it knows not the root of conflict: that war begins not in the limbs but in the soul, where love is disordered and justice perverted. When I came to see—through the piercing light of divine teaching—that hatred, envy, and heresy are not deformities of the body but corruptions of the inner man, I could no longer deceive myself with ascetic postures while my heart seethed with secret pride or smoldered with concealed wrath. The Manichees had taught me to blame the flesh alone, as if matter were evil and liberation lay in starved senses; but Paul’s voice thundered through my conscience: even abstaining from wine or lust avails nothing if the soul clings to self-exaltation and scorns its brother. I saw then that my own turmoil—those agonies before my conversion at Carthage—was not a battle between spirit and body as two substances contending, but a civil war within a single soul divided against itself, loving God dimly yet clinging more fiercely to ambition, to friendship misdirected by vanity, to knowledge severed from charity. To live "after the flesh" is not merely to indulge appetite—it is to will contrary to God’s order; and thus one may fast with clean hands while devouring others in his heart. Only when I acknowledged this did I begin truly to seek conversion—not only of conduct but of desire itself—and found at last that no philosophy could heal this wound save that which unites us to Christ crucified: for He alone reconciles the warring cities within us by subjecting both soul and body to love’s true end—God Himself.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how the weight of the body drags the soul downward, as you remembered your own struggles with temptation?
answer:
When the mind seeks to justify its own rebellion by laying the blame upon the flesh, it reveals not the weakness of the body, but the pride of the soul that refuses to acknowledge its own culpability. For I have learned in the furnace of my own experience—that restless youth when I wandered through the schools of rhetoric and Manichaeism, chasing truth as one might chase shadows on a wall—that it was not my limbs or senses which dragged me into lust and vanity, but a will already turned away from God. The eyes do not compel us to gaze with covetousness; they merely open. The ears do not force delight in idle words; they only receive sound. But deep within, in that hidden place where intention is formed and desire takes root, there resides a power—the soul itself—which consents.

And so when Scripture speaks of “the works of the flesh,” Paul does not mean to say that matter is evil or that God’s creation is at fault. Heaven forbid! He uses *flesh* in that ancient way where part stands for whole: a figure of speech, yes—but also a mirror held up to our condition. For man, though composed of body and soul, acts as one person; and when he sins, he sins wholly. Yet even this misnaming serves a purpose: it humbles us. It forces us to see how deeply our corruption runs—even into what we call natural desires—so that no part of us may claim innocence before the Lord.

I groaned under this truth long before I embraced it joyfully. In Carthage, amid applause for vain speeches and entangled in illicit loves, I felt indeed that “the corruptible body weighed down the soul.” But I now understand: it was not gravity pulling me down—it was my own weight of pride pressing me into earth. The body does not sin; men sin—men who were made for heaven but who build their cities lower than dust if left to themselves.

Only grace lifts upward. Only divine love reverses the fall—not by destroying nature, but by restoring it from within.

question: How did your own battles with desire change how you understood the soul's responsibility for sin, rather than blaming the flesh alone?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, ever seeks to divide the human being against himself, as though the soul could wash its hands in innocence while blaming the body for the defilement of the whole person.  

I know well the seductions of the flesh—those clamorous appetites that once held me in chains. As a youth, I cried, "Grant me chastity—but not yet," laughing at my own bondage while calling it nature. In those years, I would have said that my eyes were led astray by beauty, my hands by touch, my ears by song—and so perhaps I did; but never was it merely the eye or ear that sinned. It was *I*. The soul gave consent. The will—free, though wounded—reached out and grasped what should have been refused.  

When Paul speaks of the "works of the flesh," he does not mean only lust or gluttony; he names envy, strife, heresy—sins no bodily organ commits. These are acts of judgment perverted, desires disordered at their root in the soul’s governance. Even when he says "fornication" or "uncleanness," he names not mere motion of members but choices made in darkness: choices to love passing things above eternal ones. So I learned: it is not because I saw a woman that I sinned—it was because *I willed* to delight in her as an object rather than honor her as a child of God.  

The flesh bears guilt neither more nor less than iron bears guilt for murder when shaped into a sword; yet men say they cannot help their deeds because their members move! But who forged his own heart? Who shaped his will? Not God unto evil—but we ourselves, through habit and pride and refusal to submit to divine order. Yes, this mortal body groans and drags us down—it is heavy with death’s corruption—but it is not itself sin. Sin is a voluntary turning away from light toward shadow; and such turning belongs to soul alone.

Therefore do I distinguish between punishment and fault: our mortality is punishment for sin; our passions are its consequence; but our assent—that is our fault. And here lies grace: that God does not merely heal bodies or pacify desires; He reorders loves. He brings *the city within*—the city ruled by self—to kneel before the City founded upon Love Itself.

Thus did my tears at Ostia teach me what rhetoric never could: that responsibility cannot be shifted like a cloak from shoulder to shoulder—it rests where choice resides: in the soul turned toward or away from God.

And now even in old age, when these limbs grow weak and passion cools like embers under ash—I find no less temptation! Now it wears subtler forms: impatience disguised as zeal; pride clothed as orthodoxy; resentment hidden beneath pastoral concern! If sin were only fleshly pleasure gone wild—would not old men be saints by default?

Do you see now why Paul calls all such things *works of the flesh*, though many are wholly spiritual? Because "flesh" signifies fallen man entire—the human whole estranged from God—even when his hands do nothing and his loins lie still.

For even silence can be sinful—if born from hatred masked as patience.
Even fasting can stink before God—if nourished by vanity instead of charity.
Even doctrine can become an idol—if loved more than Christ who gives truth its breath.

So let no man say: “My body betrayed me.” No—the kingdom within refused allegiance—and that kingdom must repent.

question: Looking back on the times you justified your actions by blaming human weakness, what would you say to someone who still believes their sins are caused by their body?
answer:
The earthly city builds its altars where the body is scapegoated for the soul’s rebellion, as though sin could be exiled to flesh and blood while the inner man remains innocent. But I tell you, no vice ever took root in the members of the body unless first sown by the will in the garden of the heart. When I walked in Manichaeism, I too cast blame upon some dark principle within matter, imagining that lust and pride sprang from a substance foreign to the soul—yet how often did I willingly ascend that ladder of concupiscence, delighting in the very chains I claimed were forged by my flesh?

No, it is not the hand that steals, nor the eye that covets, nor the mouth that lies—these are but instruments; they have no motion apart from the soul’s command. The corruption of the body—its weakness, its passions, its mortality—is indeed a burden left by sin’s inheritance; but to call this corruption *sin itself* is to mistake the wound for the weapon. Adam’s fault was not that he had a body, but that he had a will—and he turned it away from God.

Even now I hear men cry: "It was my nature! My temperament! The flesh within me!" But does this not repeat my own old excuses when I cried, "Give me chastity—but not yet"? As if God were deaf to such sophistry! The apostle speaks truly: these things are called *works of the flesh*, not because flesh is their originator, but because they manifest themselves through our mortal condition—the part standing for the whole. Yet their source? That lies deeper: in pride, in disordered love, in a heart curved inward upon itself.

Take courage: if sin arose only from your members, you would be guiltless—and grace would be unnecessary. But Christ did not die to redeem mere clay; He died to reclaim a will gone astray. Repentance begins where blame ends: when you stop pointing to your nerves and hormones and appetites and say instead: *I willed it*. Then—and only then—can you stand before God and receive His healing hand. For He heals souls first; and what He restores inwardly will one day be raised up incorruptible in body too.

question: When you heard Paul speak of groaning in this earthly tent, did it echo your own inner aching for purity—and what stirred that longing in you?
answer:
Not even Plato, in all his yearning for the immortal soul’s liberation from bodily chains, perceived with such anguish the inward dissonance that Paul exposes when he groans in the tent of flesh—because the philosopher saw only the soul’s nobility overshadowed by matter, while I have felt in my own bones the rebellion not of body alone, but of a will turned downward, curved in upon itself.  

When Paul speaks of groaning—*in hoc enim ingemiscimus*, "in this we groan"—he is not uttering the complaint of a spirit shackled merely by physical decay, as though immortality could be won by philosophy or virtue purified through reason. No: what stirs within me at those words is the memory of my own long captivity—not to lust alone, though my youth burned with it, nor to ambition, though I chased honors in Carthage and Rome—but to that deeper malformation of desire that made me love *anything* more than God. The earthly city within me had built its temples to eloquence, to pleasure, to human praise; and every one was an altar where I sacrificed peace.  

But why then did I long for purity? Why did my heart beat faster at the Psalms? Why did Ambrose’s voice on Sundays pierce me like a lance? Because grace had already begun its silent siege. The longing was not self-generated; it was awakened—drawn forth by divine beauty speaking through Scripture, through tears, through silence after sin. It was not that flesh caused sin—as if matter were evil—and yet neither was it uninvolved. For since Adam fell, this whole man—the soul and body together—bears the weight of mortality as punishment; but sin itself is born in the will’s revolt. The corruptible body presses down indeed, but it does so as consequence, not origin: like a house fallen into ruin after war has passed through it.  

And so when Paul groans in his tent—this perishable tabernacle—he does not curse creation; he laments its bondage under corruption. Yet he hopes: for there is another building prepared—not raised by hands nor sustained by time—but eternal in the heavens. That hope is not philosophy’s dream; it is faith’s certainty. And it was that very hope which finally shattered my despair when I heard those words in Milan: *Tolle lege*. Take up and read—and found myself naked before God's light. Not ashamed merely—but loved even so.

question: What moment made you realize that corruption wasn't the cause of your sin, but the consequence—and how did that shift your repentance?
answer:
It was not in the forum of rhetoric nor in the schools of the Manichees, where I once sought to lay blame upon some external darkness for the disorder within me, but before the silent majesty of God’s truth—when I at last saw that the soul rules itself even in its ruin. The City of Man, which builds its towers with ambition and adorns them with pleasure, delights to accuse the body as if it were the author of sin; yet this is a lie woven from pride, for no appetite arises except by consent. I had long believed my flesh was my tyrant—those eyes that wept not for sin but for a dead actress; that heart inflamed by lusts unnamed; that tongue, slave to applause—but when grace pierced me in Milan through Scripture and Augustine saw himself not as victim but judge and criminal both, then I understood: corruption is not cause, but consequence.

The soul sins first when it turns from unchanging Light toward mutable things—not because drawn by flesh, but because it wills. The flesh grows heavy indeed after sin; it becomes a burden to the spirit, like chains forged link by link through each delight in vice. But those chains are not the beginning—they are the rust of rebellion. When I cried out "Give me chastity—but not yet," it was not my limbs that hesitated, but my will. And there, in that delay of desire ordered rightly yet resisted stubbornly—I discovered sin lodged in the soul’s throne.

Thus repentance changed: no longer did I seek merely to mortify members or flee occasions as if escaping captors. Instead I began to war against pride—the root from which self-love flowers into every vice. For if sin begins where love is disordered—when man loves himself instead of God—then healing must begin at that same seat: where reason bows again to faith, and faith teaches reason its true end. In this light even Plato dimly saw: knowledge without virtue enslaves; but he knew not Christ, who gives both.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about groaning under the burden of this corruptible body, as if your very flesh resisted your longing for eternity?
answer:
When Plato, through the voice of Virgil, speaks of the soul as a divine spark burdened by its earthly vesture, he glimpses a truth but does not grasp its fullness—nor see whence deliverance comes. He divides body and soul as though they were enemies, and imagines the soul’s liberation in terms of escape, like a bird fleeing a cage. But this is not the Christian hope. I did not groan under the body as though it were sin itself; no, I groaned because this good thing—flesh formed by God—has fallen into corruption, and now drags down my desires toward dust while my heart strains upward toward eternity.

There is a difference, profound and decisive: the City of Man seeks to flee the body; the City of God awaits its resurrection. The one despises creation; the other waits in faith for its renewal. I who once wandered among the Manichees know well what it means to curse matter as evil—how seductive it is to blame the flesh for every disorder of the soul! But grace taught me otherwise: sin dwells not in my limbs but in my will, which turns love inward instead of upward. The body is not the prison of truth—it will one day be glorified, transformed into an instrument fit for eternal life.

Thus my groaning was not hatred of what God made, but longing for what He promises—a body no longer subject to decay, no longer pressing down the soul with urgent hungers and clouded thoughts. That day will come not by philosophy’s ascent nor by flight from earth’s elements, but by Christ descending, raising up what was sown in weakness and raising it in power. Then mortality shall truly be swallowed up by life—not abolished, but fulfilled; not discarded like a worn-out garment, but clothed upon with immortality as with a bridal robe.

question: How did your struggle with earthly desires change how you understood God's promise of a body transformed, not discarded?
answer:
It is one thing to flee the body as the prison of the soul, as the Platonists taught, and another to await its redemption, as the apostle proclaims—between these two lies a chasm fixed, not of philosophy but of revelation. In my youth, I longed to escape this flesh as though it were the source of all defilement, believing that virtue could only flourish when unshackled from corporeal weakness. I was drawn to those noble pagan ideals that exalted the spirit’s divine spark while scorning the "vesture of decay," as if light could exist without form, or substance be an enemy to holiness. But grace taught me otherwise—not by abstract reasoning alone, but by groaning in this very tabernacle, by weeping with disordered love, by discovering that sin resided not in my limbs but in my will.

The City of Man seeks liberation through denial or transcendence; it dreams of souls ascending like smoke from a burning house, leaving behind the rubble of matter. But the City of God waits for a resurrection—the same body raised, transformed, clothed anew with incorruption. My passions—once restless and untamed—did not prove that the body was evil; they proved that I was fallen. And because they were part of me—a unity of soul and flesh created good—I could not hope for annihilation but for renewal.

When I cried out under the burden of concupiscence in Carthage and later in Milan, I was not suffering because I had a body; I suffered because my whole person—soul and flesh together—was disordered by sin. Therefore salvation cannot mean evacuation from this earthly house; it must mean its renovation by divine power. The promise is not dissolution but transfiguration: what was sown in weakness shall rise in power; what was mortal shall be clothed upon with immortality.

Thus faith does not destroy reason but fulfills it: if God formed man from dust and pronounced him "very good," then dust shall not be cast aside like refuse. Rather, through Christ—the firstfruits of them that sleep—it shall be glorified. My longing has changed: no longer do I wish to fly from this tabernacle naked into eternity; now I yearn for its wedding garment woven from heaven itself—when mortality will vanish into life, and even my flesh shall rest secure in hope (Psalm 16:9).

question: Looking back on your years of searching through philosophy and passion, what would you tell someone who doubts that immortality can redeem the flesh?
answer:
The City of Man builds its hopes on the dissolution of the body, as if liberation were escape; but the City of God awaits the resurrection, for its Founder conquered death not by forsaking flesh, but by transfiguring it. I once wandered among the philosophers, drawn by Plato’s ascent of the soul and beguiled by the Manichaean scorn for matter, believing that truth must be pure intellect, unsoiled by this heavy clay. I thought the body a prison, desire a flaw, and pleasure a snare—yet all the while my soul was more in chains because I did not know from what it needed deliverance.

I sought wisdom as if it were a thing to be grasped by argument alone, as though reason could climb to heaven on its own steps. But grace descended before I arrived. And when faith at last illuminated my mind, I saw: the evil is not in being embodied, but in being disordered—loving the mutable things more than the Immutable, using bodies as ends rather than signs pointing through time toward eternity.

Do you doubt that immortality can redeem the flesh? Consider Christ Himself: He did not disdain to rise with scars. The tomb was empty not because flesh is unworthy of life, but because God intended it for glory. He ate with His disciples after resurrection; He invited Thomas to touch His side—He bore a real body then, yet one no longer subject to decay. This is no mere allegory; it is history upon which all history turns.

What we now call "body" is like a seed sown in weakness; what shall be is that same seed raised in power—not dissolved into spirit like mist beneath sun, but transformed into substance fit for eternity. The Apostle groans not that he must wear flesh, but that he must still wear corruptible flesh. We do not long for extinction—we long for clothing: "that mortality may be swallowed up by life."

So let no one suppose that redemption means disappearance. The earthly city flees suffering through dissolution; it seeks relief in annihilation or illusion. But the City of God offers fulfillment: desire purified and restored, love perfected in sight—and yes—the very dust of our limbs quickened by divine breath into imperishable form.

Faith does not contradict reason here; it completes it. For if God formed man from dust and called it good before sin entered—if He deigned to assume human nature and never laid it down—then surely He who began this work will bring it to completion on that Day when all things are made new: even our bodies redeemed along with us.
