question: What ache did you carry when you realized that the body itself was not the enemy, but its decay—and how did that reshape your hope?
answer:
When Varro labored to classify the natural world into forms and causes, he sought rest in order, as though the soul might be calmed by taxonomy; yet I found no peace in categories, but in the longing that pierced them. The body, I once thought, was the prison of truth—the flesh a chain upon the spirit, dragging it from divine contemplation into lust, into distraction, into time. Like Plotinus gazing inward to escape matter, I too desired flight from this "earthen vessel," imagining purity as dissolution. But grace taught me otherwise: not escape, but transformation.

I saw at last that the evil was not in substance but in corruption—that God formed man of dust and breathed life into clay, calling it *very good*. The fault lay not in embodiment but in its frailty: the wound of mortality that spreads rot through desire, distorts reason with sorrow, inflames joy into vanity. The groaning within this tabernacle is real—not because the house is evil, but because it is falling down. And we do not wish for homelessness, but for a new foundation: a body raised whole, incorruptible.

Thus my hope changed its shape—not from loathing this world’s form to yearning for sheer spirit—but for resurrection. Not annihilation of flesh but its redemption. I once turned from Carthage’s spectacles appalled by sensuality; later I turned toward baptism appalled by deeper death. And there in baptismal water rose an image that never left: Christ Himself rising—not a ghost freed from matter—but one clothed anew with life indomitable.

So now I see: the City of Man builds tombs to preserve its heroes’ dust; but the City of God awaits a renewal where dust itself shall burn with glory. We are not saved *from* bodies—we are saved *in* them—and through them perfected.

question: How did your encounter with Paul’s words about being 'clothed upon' awaken a new tenderness toward the body you once wished to escape?
answer:
It was not Plotinus, though he taught me to scorn the flesh, nor Varro, though he arrayed the gods in golden rhetoric, who finally disarmed my hatred of the body—no, it was Paul’s cry from the prison of mortality that pierced me: *to be clothed upon*, not stripped bare. For years I had fed on Platonic disdain for this earthly tabernacle, imagining the soul’s ascent as a flight from all material chains; and in my youth, weary of lust’s ambushes and the mind’s dispersion among bodily senses, I longed to be *unencumbered*, like a bird loosed from stone. The Manichees had whispered that the body itself was darkness; even after my conversion, I wrestled with that shadow.

But when I truly heard Paul groan—not for annihilation of the body, but for its transformation—something cracked within my soul. This groaning was not Gnostic repugnance but Christian hope: not escape, but resurrection. The City of Man despises the body or worships it; either it crushes it under austerity or drowns it in pleasure. But the City of God receives it as God’s handiwork—fallen indeed, burdened by corruption and thus pressing down the soul’s flight like leaden wings—but destined not for disposal but deification.

I once thought immortality meant absence of body; now I see: it means *fullness* of body—risen, quickened, freed from its sluggish weight. Not a ghost in paradise but a man in glory. That is why we do not desire to be naked! Even Christ rose with scars—his hands still bore them—that same flesh which suffered now glorified. So too ours shall be clothed anew: no longer feeding death but swallowed up in life.

Thus did Scripture correct philosophy—and grace heal contempt with tenderness.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote that the body is not the source of sin, but a prisoner of its consequences?
answer:
It was Rome’s crumbling that taught me to distinguish between the shadow and the substance, between the visible tumult of history and the invisible hand that orders all things toward justice. When I wrote those lines, I was not merely disputing a philosophical error—I was dismantling an entire vision of reality that would relieve the soul of its culpability by casting the body as villain. The Manichees had once duped me with such notions, wrapping their dualism in poetic lament over flesh and darkness, as if matter itself were cursed at its root. But grace, that unearned light which struck me in the garden at Carthage, revealed a deeper truth: sin did not enter through the skin or bloom in the loins alone—it sprang from the will’s revolt, from pride’s silent whisper before any bodily motion.

I felt a solemn urgency, then—a duty to untangle creation from condemnation. For if we say the body *causes* sin, we make God the author of evil; we imply His good earth is tainted in essence, not in condition. But no: the flesh was made good, oriented toward glory; it became burdensome only after disobedience bent its desires awry. The weight we feel now—the concupiscence that drags like chains—is not original but consequential. It is not why we fall; it is what remains after we have already fallen.

And consider this: if sins arise solely from bodily appetites, how shall we account for Lucifer? He has no flesh, yet he burns with envy and arrogance—vices untouched by blood or bone. If pride could fester where no stomach hungers nor eyes lust, then clearly evil has a seat higher than anatomy. Thus Paul speaks of “the works of the flesh,” though he knows full well that hatred, envy, and discord are movements of spirit more than sense. He uses “flesh” not as anatomy but as symbol—the whole human being turned away from God.

So when I rejected the claim that desire springs only from corporeal prison-walls, I did so not to exonerate desire but to place responsibility where it belongs: on the soul’s choice to love wrongly. The tears we shed are indeed tied to our mortal frame—but they are also offerings shaped by love misdirected or rightly ordered. Laughter and sorrow are not sins; they become sinful only when ruled by a heart set on self rather than on God.

To blame the body alone is to absolve both angel and man of their true crime: rebellion against divine love. And that cannot be—not while justice stands in heaven, nor while Christ died clothed in flesh to redeem it whole.

question: How did your struggle with lust in your youth change how you understood the soul's burden, not the flesh's fault?
answer:
It was Plato who taught that the soul, imprisoned in the body, longs to cast off its fleshly chains and ascend to the realm of pure forms—yet even he, for all his insight, failed to discern that the true prison of the soul is not the body per se, but sin. I once believed, as many do, that if only I could subdue the flesh—its passions, its urgencies, its clamors—the soul would rise unburdened into wisdom and peace. In Carthage, amid the intoxications of youth, I thought myself a philosopher mastering nature; yet I was mastered by concupiscence. The tears I wept were not for God, but for a lost love; my joy was bound to pleasure; my sorrow to frustrated desire. And so I mistook the symptom for the cause.

I came to see that it was not the body which dragged me down by its mere existence—as though creation itself were evil—but my will turned inward upon itself in pride and lust. The disordered movements of youth were not sins because they were bodily; they were sins because they revealed a soul in rebellion against its Creator. The flesh became corruptible only after Adam’s pride: death entered through disobedience, and with death came all those unruly motions of desire and fear that now seem so natural to us. Thus it is not that flesh begets sin—but sin begets corruption in flesh.

When I finally understood this—through grace more than reason—I saw how perilous it is to blame our nature for what our will has made unnatural. The devil envies and hates without any flesh at all; his vice is spiritual through and through. And if he can sin without a body, how then can we say that ours is solely responsible? No: the root lies deeper than sensation or appetite—it lies in love ordered awry. We loved beauty but not Beauty Itself; we sought delight but fled from Delight Divine.

Therefore what once seemed a war between soul and body became for me a greater drama: two cities contending within one man—one built on self-love unto contempt of God, the other on love of God unto contempt of self. My lusts were not merely carnal disturbances; they were revelations of loyalty misplaced. And when at last my will submitted—not through Stoic discipline or Platonic ascent—but through Christ humbling Himself even unto death—then at last both soul and body could begin their restoration.

question: Looking back on your mother's tears for your restless years, what would you tell someone who blames their body for their failings?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its self-love, seeks to shift the blame for its disordered affections onto the frame of flesh, as though the body were the author of sin rather than the instrument through which a will already turned from God expresses its rebellion. But I have learned—and my mother Monica wept until she saw it confirmed by divine grace—that our corruption is not seated in the limbs that stumble, nor in the eyes that lust, nor in the ears that drink in vanity, but in the soul that has forgotten its Creator. When I wandered through the years of my youth, indulging in pleasures beneath me, I did not sin because I had a body; I sinned because my will, proud and unsubmitted, chose the lower things when it should have ascended to the Highest. The Manicheans taught me to curse creation itself, to call matter evil and thus excuse my passions as forces beyond my rule—but grace taught me otherwise.

It is true: this mortal body groans under a weight not native to it—the penalty of Adam’s fall, yes, but not his excuse. The flesh now tempts with disordered desires because the soul once willed disobedience; it was not flesh that dragged man down from God, but man who brought death upon himself by pride and unbelief. And if we look upon fallen spirits—those angels who never took on flesh—we find them steeped in malice no less fierce than any bodily vice: envy sears them like fire, pride lifts them up against heaven itself. They commit no adultery with bodies; yet they corrupt souls. Therefore let no one say: “My eyes led me astray,” or “My blood burns beyond control,” unless he first examines whether his heart has already yielded itself to love of self instead of love of God.

Faith does not deny reason when it teaches that virtue resides in choice ordered toward truth; nor does reason betray faith when it sees how habit and temptation grow where grace is resisted long. There is a law within us warring against the law of our minds—but this war proves not two natures locked in equal combat (as if good and evil were co-eternal), but one soul torn between loyalty to Christ and concupiscence born from past rebellion. My mother knew this mystery before philosophy could name it: she prayed not for my limbs to be restrained but for my heart to be converted. And so should every man who would be whole ask not merely for deliverance *from* temptation, but for wisdom *in* temptation—knowing that even while we struggle with weakness daily, our hope stands firm upon Him who reigned victorious over both sin and death alike.

question: What inner conflict arose when you realized pride, not pleasure, was the root of the devil's ruin—and your own?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless ambition, builds altars to its own excellence, mistaking the height of its towers for proximity to heaven; and it was within this very city—nourished by my own swelling heart—that I first perceived how the disordered love of self, not the allure of fleshly delight, had unseated both Lucifer and myself.  

For a long time, I imagined that sin dwelled chiefly in the passions—lust, gluttony, sloth—as though the body were a vessel too weak to hold back the flood of vice. And indeed, I knew well the tug of concupiscence; my youth was not free from such chains. But when I came to understand that even without a body—the devil being pure spirit—pride could fester into eternal rebellion, then I saw with sudden clarity: the root is not pleasure, but the will turned inward.  

Lucifer did not fall because he desired what was beneath him—he fell because he refused to acknowledge what was above him. He would not adore; he would be adored. And there—in that movement from *adoration* to *self-exaltation*—lies the first fracture of all creation. It is not wine that intoxicates the soul most deeply, but the draught of self-sufficiency. When I heard Ambrose proclaim that "God resists the proud," it struck me as both judgment and mercy: for if pride alone could sever an archangel from eternity, then how much more had it warped my own steps—even in those moments when I abstained from carnal acts yet still hungered for praise, for mastery, for distinction among men?  

Thus my conflict was not between flesh and spirit alone—but between two loves: whether to seek truth in humility or to fashion truth according to my own mind. The devil envies because he cannot bear another’s glory; I wept because I could no longer bear my own emptiness masked as wisdom. Only when grace shattered my illusion did I see: it was not bodily desire that kept me from God—it was my unwillingness to be nothing before Him.

question: How did your years of chasing wisdom through senses shape your sorrow for treating the body as the enemy?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, seeks to purify the soul by warring upon the body, as though dust could defile the breath of life; but the City of God knows that the wound lies deeper than flesh—it is in the will’s rebellion that sin first took root, and from this root sprang forth not only disobedience but mortality itself.  

In my youth, I was taken captive by the allurements of philosophy and sensation, thinking wisdom might be grasped through sight, sound, or carnal delight. I wandered among the Manichees, who taught that matter was evil, that light and dark were co-eternal principles locked in cosmic struggle—that the body itself was a prison forged by malevolent powers. Thus I blamed creation for corruption, as though God had sown thorns before Adam fell. But grace, in its persistent mercy, led me through tears and torment to see that the disorder was not in my limbs or senses but in my heart’s disordered love: I had loved beauty without seeking its Author; I had desired truth while fleeing from Truth incarnate.

It was then I came to understand what Scripture means when it speaks of "the flesh": not merely blood and bone—the devil lacks these and yet is full of pride—but all that in us which turns away from God toward self-exaltation. The desires I once thought arose solely from bodily appetite—lusts for honor, for pleasure, for control—were rooted not in skin but in soul; they were motions of a fallen will that had made itself the center of its own universe. And so when Paul says we wrestle against "the flesh," he does not mean we war against God’s good creation but against that inward concupiscence which distorts even natural desires into instruments of bondage.

Thus my sorrow is not merely personal—it is ecclesial, cosmic—for how many souls still believe liberation comes through ascetic hatred of the body! No: redemption does not abolish our humanity but restores it. Christ did not disdain to take on flesh; He ate with sinners, wept at gravesides, breathed His last on a cross—yet never sinned. Therefore it is not flesh which condemns us but sin clinging to willful hearts. The body neither saves nor damns; it is ordered or disordered by love’s direction.

To have once taught otherwise was to invert the gospel order—to make punishment into cause—and thus I weep for those years spent misreading both man and Maker. For if we call creation guilty instead of corrupted—if we curse our eyes for lust or ears for vanity—we blind ourselves anew: now with pious rage rather than passion. But blessed are they who mourn this error deeply enough to offer their bodies as living sacrifices—not mutilated by hatred nor indulged by vice—but transformed by faith into instruments fit for eternal service.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed that living according to yourself felt like a lie, even as you longed for truth?
answer:
It is impossible for the soul to find rest in any justice that it fabricates for itself, for justice is not a creature of human will but the very order of divine wisdom. When I confessed that living according to myself was living according to a lie, I was not merely lamenting moral failure—I was unearthing the deep disorder of a soul turned in upon its own desires as if they could be the measure of truth. The philosophers, like Cicero, sought *summum bonum* in virtue formed by reason and discipline, yet they could not see that even the most polished soul, if it rests upon self-originated righteousness, stands on shifting sand. For how can man be his own rule? If an angel falls by preferring his own splendor to God’s light, shall man—who is dust and shadow—escape ruin by esteeming his own judgment above the eternal Truth?

I had long wandered in that city built on mirrors—where every choice reflected only my own longing, every ambition echoed back my own voice. And yet within me burned a thirst not for illusion but for reality: not for success, but for meaning; not for praise, but for purity. But this very desire confounded me—for I wanted happiness on my own terms, and thus made myself wretched. To live according to oneself is to sever the soul from its source; it is to pluck a branch from the vine and expect it to bear fruit. God made us upright—not because we are naturally perfect, but because our nature was ordered toward Him as its truth. To live otherwise is not simply error—it is existential falsehood.

And so when at last I said *you have made us for yourself*, I spoke not only with relief but with awe: awe that such a God would draw near to one who had spoken lies even in seeking Him. The truth had abounded all the more through my sin—not because sin became truth, but because He who *is* Truth would not let falsehood have the final word.

question: How did your struggle with pride change how you understood God’s call to live not by your own will but by His?
answer:
It was through the ruin of my own wisdom that I first glimpsed the architecture of divine truth, for I had climbed the heights of rhetoric and dialectic as one scaling a mountain to seize the dawn, only to find myself in thicker darkness. Like Cicero, who stirred my youthful heart with his *Hortensius*, I sought truth by the light of reason alone—yet reason, when lifted up against humility, becomes not a ladder but a tower, and every tower built to reach heaven without God ends in confusion. I confess it now with both shame and gratitude: I desired to know God not that I might obey Him, but that He might approve of me. That desire—so subtle, so deeply woven into the fabric of my ambition—was itself a lie, though wrapped in the garments of philosophy.

For what is pride if not the soul’s rebellion against its own createdness? To live according to oneself is to claim authorship over one’s being—and thus to live according to a fiction. Man was made upright, yes, but not as his own foundation. To will one's own will as ultimate is to build a city upon sand; it trembles at the first wind because it rests on nothing. When I labored under this illusion—when my teaching, my honors, even my conversion at first served not God but my own reputation—I lived within the City of Man, mistaking eloquence for virtue and knowledge for communion.

But grace humbles before it exalts. The day came when I heard a voice from an unseen garden bid me “take up and read,” and in that moment Scripture pierced through all my sophistry. Not because it satisfied my intellect—though it does surpass all reason—but because it shattered my self-sufficiency. In Paul’s cry—“Wretched man that I am!”—I found truer logic than in all Plato’s dialogues: namely, that deliverance comes not by ascent but descent; not by asserting our will but surrendering it.

Thus I learned at last that to live according to God is not merely to follow commands, but to dwell in love—the love which casts out pride because it remembers always from whom every good gift comes. The City of God walks by faith in the promise; she does not measure her worth by achievement or wisdom or power, for her glory is hidden in Christ crucified—a scandal to philosophers like me once was. And so now I say: blessed is he who no longer seeks himself anywhere—not even in his holiness—for only then does he begin truly *to be*, because he abides in Truth itself.

question: Looking back on the years you sought fulfillment in your own way, what would you tell someone who doubts whether surrendering to God is truly freedom?
answer:
The City of Man builds its towers on the shifting foundation of self-will, calling bondage liberty, because it cannot imagine a freedom that does not consist in the power to choose according to desire; whereas the City of God knows that true liberation begins precisely where self-will ends.  

When I lived for rhetoric, for honor, for the embrace of fleeting pleasures, I called myself free—yet I was enchained by every appetite I obeyed. I desired happiness, yes, but sought it in things made rather than the Maker; in human approval rather than divine truth; in my own voice rather than the Word eternal. And what did I find? A deeper unrest, a more gnawing void. The soul turned inward upon itself becomes a labyrinth without an exit, for it worships a god shaped in its own image—and that god is no god at all, but a lie animated by pride.

To one who doubts whether surrender to God is freedom, I would say this: Was Christ less free because He did the Father’s will? Was He enslaved because He emptied Himself? No—He was most Himself when He submitted. So too with us: we are not diminished by obedience to God; we are restored. For to live according to God is to live according to truth—*ipsa veritas*—and truth is not a chain but a key.  

The will that says “Not my will, but Thine” does not annihilate itself; it finally achieves itself. It was only when I ceased fleeing from God’s command that I discovered the peace which all my restless seeking had obscured—the peace of being known, loved, and remade by Him who is not a rival to our joy but its source. There is no true freedom outside the order of love: love of God first, then all else in Him. The world calls this bondage; wisdom knows it as homecoming.

question: What inner conflict haunted you when you realized that even your desire for happiness could become a lie?
answer:
In the City of God, blessedness is not sought but received; in the City of Man, it is grasped for, fashioned, and falsely claimed as one’s own. It was this very movement—the soul’s restless reaching toward happiness as though it were an object to be seized by will—that unveiled to me the depth of my disorder. I desired joy, yes; who does not? But I desired it on my terms: in lust unbridled, in honors unshared, in knowledge puffed up with pride. And therein lay the lie—not in the longing itself, for God had inscribed that longing into my nature—but in the refusal to receive blessedness from His hand and instead to forge it by mine.

I once thought myself rational, even noble, in pursuit of felicity through philosophy or pleasure or eloquence. But what is reason that turns from its Author but a lamp burning in an empty house? The Platonists taught me to ascend toward the immutable truth, yet they could not explain why my feet still stumbled in mire. Varro amassed wisdom like treasure, Cicero adorned virtue with golden speech—but neither could deliver a soul enslaved to its own will under the illusion of freedom.

When I at last saw that every act done for love of self ends not in fulfillment but fracture—that I had built my happiness upon what is not—then I understood how even desire for good becomes sin when severed from the Good itself. To live according to man is not merely error; it is idolatry disguised as prudence. For if God alone is Truth, then all else lived apart from Him collapses into fiction, no matter how sincerely believed. And what agony arises when one discovers that his most cherished dreams were phantasms—his joys fleeting consolations bought at the price of divine friendship.

Yet here grace pierced through: that He who is Truth did not scorn my lying heart but healed it by becoming flesh and dwelling among us—not so that we might construct our own beatitude, but so that we might die to ourselves and live according to Him who died and rose again. In losing our false lives, we find our true ones—not by ascent of mind or effort of will, but by descent into humility where alone grace builds securely.

question: How did the moment you acknowledged your own will as a distortion of truth transform your relationship with God’s love?
answer:
When a soul asserts its own will as the measure of truth, it does not merely err—it builds a citadel in the City of Man, whose foundation is the pride that turns being toward non-being, for to live according to self is to live by a principle that cannot sustain existence, since all true being flows from God alone, who is Being itself. I know this not as an abstract proposition but as a man once shattered by the weight of his own sufficiency, when my will—so resolute in sin—was still called freedom. I had loved beauty, order, truth; yet I sought them in scattered fragments, in the concupiscence of the eyes and the pride of life, imagining that wisdom could be grasped like rhetoric or stolen like a lover’s glance. Thus I lived according to man—according to Augustine—and found only rupture: within myself, between neighbor, and above all before God.

The philosophers spoke of harmony and virtue; Plato named the good and reached toward it as one sees light through mist. Yet none saw—none could see—that the will itself is diseased, corrupted not merely by act but by inheritance, born of that first turning away when Adam chose his own judgment over obedience. Varro might map Roman piety with erudition; Cicero might plead for justice as if reason alone could forge it—but neither reckoned with this: that every man carries within him a Babel tower of intentions, each brick laid in service of a peace he cannot achieve.

But when at last—under the fig tree where grace broke through—I ceased constructing arguments for my righteousness and confessed instead my inability even to will rightly apart from Him who commands love: then God’s love ceased to be a distant ideal and became a force breaking in. It was not that He loved me more at that moment; rather, I finally allowed myself to be loved as one wholly dependent on mercy. His love was no longer something I sought to earn through brilliance or penance but something that descended upon me precisely when I surrendered my claim to self-direction.

To live according to God is not merely moral rectitude; it is ontological alignment—the creature submitting its nature back into its Creator’s hands so that being may be restored from falsehood into truth. For He does not demand our wills out of tyranny but because only in surrendering them do we find them healed. Every sin says “Let me be god,” yet every such act diminishes us further—not because God retaliates but because we are creatures attempting uncreation.

Thus my relationship with divine love transformed not through insight alone but through collapse: when my will broke against grace like waves against rock—and was reshaped stone by stone into something capable of loving not for gain but for Him who first loved us. In abiding by His truth rather than my lie, I began at last truly to live.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that your efforts to make life better for yourself only deepened your misery?
answer:
When a man seeks to build his life upon the shifting soil of self-will, mistaking his own desires for the law of nature, he does not ascend toward happiness but descends into a deeper form of bondage—one that wears the mask of freedom while tightening the chains. I had once believed that honor, rhetoric, advancement in teaching, and the praise of men would bring peace to my restless heart. I arranged my days around these ends, calculated my gains, and yet found myself more fractured than before—because every act done for self-glory severed me further from the One who made me. The philosophers spoke rightly of order and harmony as the marks of the good life, but none traced that order back to its true source: God, who is not merely First Cause but also Charity Itself. Cicero could speak beautifully of *concordia* among citizens, but he did not see that no city—neither Rome nor Carthage—can sustain true peace unless it is founded upon love of God rather than love of power.

My misery was not accidental; it was necessary. For how could I be happy when I lived in rebellion against my own nature? To sin is not merely to break a rule—it is to invert the soul’s hierarchy, placing appetite above reason and reason above God. And when I lied—not only with words but with my whole way of living—to gain position or avoid disgrace, I thought I was preserving myself; in truth, I was dismantling my soul piece by piece. The more I sought to control my fate by human cunning, the more enslaved I became—not to external forces, but to an inner disorder that no rhetoric could conceal. Only when grace shattered my pride did I understand: man cannot save himself because he does not even know what salvation is until God reveals it.

Hence arises the twofold city: one built on self-love reaching upward in defiance; the other on God-love descending in humility. And all history unfolds as their paths diverge—Babylon rising in arrogance and falling in ruin; Jerusalem weeping under trial yet upheld by promise. In my own life this drama played out in smaller measure: every attempt to make myself secure apart from God ended in deeper exile. But when at last I ceased building towers and began instead to pray "Thy will be done," then—and only then—did true life begin.

question: How did your own struggle with pride and envy change how you understood walking according to man instead of God?
answer:
When the mind exalts itself above the measure of its nature, setting up its own judgment as law, there arises not wisdom but a monstrous parody of it—this I learned not from books alone, but from the deep disorder of my own soul. For many years I walked according to man, though I believed myself guided by philosophy; I sought truth through rhetoric and ambition, mistaking the applause of men for divine approbation. The City of Man celebrates such ascent—Varro catalogues its honors, Cicero defends its order—but what is glory in that city is but organized pride in the eyes of God.

I desired to be feared in speech and admired in discourse; envy gnawed at me when another surpassed me in eloquence, and pride swelled when I surpassed another. Was this not walking according to the flesh? Paul called it plainly "envying and strife"—the very marks of carnality. And yet I thought myself spiritual because I sought truth! But truth, when sought apart from humility, becomes a weapon for self-exaltation.

Only when grace pierced my heart through the tears of Monica and the thunderous silence after Ambrose’s sermons did I see: to walk according to God is to die daily to self-will. The man who lives according to himself builds his tower on sand; he may quote Plato and cite Scripture, but if he does not love God above all and neighbor as himself, he remains a citizen of Babylon. The City of God moves by a different principle—love ascending toward its source.

Thus my struggle was not merely personal but paradigmatic: every soul caught between these two loves reenacts the history of mankind. And just as Nineveh fell despite its repentance being delayed only by pride’s resistance, so too did my heart resist until grace humbled it. To live according to man is to live in perpetual disarray; to live according to God is to find rest—even amid tribulation—in ordered love.

question: Looking back on the moments you chose self over truth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether real change is possible?
answer:
When the proud builder raises his tower not upon rock but upon the shifting sand of his own will, he imagines himself master of destiny—yet every such edifice, from Babel to Babylon, crumbles not by mere accident, but by the hidden hand of divine judgment that permits pride its hour only to expose its hollowness. This is no mere cycle of history; it is the moral logic of creation: that which is built apart from God collapses under its own weight, for it denies the very source of order and life. And if this is true of nations, how much more of souls?

You ask whether real change is possible—for yourself, for one burdened by past choices made in darkness. I tell you: change is not only possible; it is the very mark of grace. I was once a man who lived according to man—cunning in speech, restless in desire, drunk with ambition and the lusts that serve the self. I clothed my appetites in philosophy and called them wisdom. But what did I gain? A deeper misery beneath a polished exterior. To choose self over truth is not merely to err; it is to invert the soul’s order—to make creature lord instead of servant, and so lose both God and self.

But hear this: if we could save ourselves by reason or will alone, Christ would have died in vain. The physician does not come for those who are whole, but for the sick—and we are all sick unto death without grace. Real change begins where all human confidence ends: in confession before God. Not mere verbal admission, but a turning—a metanoia—that surrenders the illusions of self-sufficiency. It is not that man becomes better by effort; rather, he becomes new by participation in Him who *is* Truth.

I once walked according to flesh—according to man—and thus stood within the earthly city, whose foundation is self-love even to contempt of God. But when faith arose—not as a conclusion drawn from arguments alone, but as a gift kindled by divine light—I began to walk according to Spirit. And then I saw: virtue is not achieved; it is received and grown into through discipline under grace.

Therefore do not doubt transformation because you feel weakness or remember failure. The same God who raised Christ from the dead breathes into dead hearts and makes them beat with love again. If you wish it were well with you—truly well—not merely comfortable or respected or successful—but well as only truth can make you—then abandon yourself not inwardly but upwardly, toward Him who made you for Himself.

For only where there was utter ruin did grace have room enough to show its power.

question: What inner conflict were you reliving when you wrote about the two cities torn between love of God and love of self?
answer:
Justice, that most harmonious order of the soul and society, cannot be wrested from the disordered affections of men who serve their own wills as if it were law; and it was precisely this disorder—this rebellion of the lower against the higher, of time against eternity—that I saw mirrored in my own breast when I conceived the two cities. For many years I sought wisdom among the philosophers, believing reason alone could lead me to truth, and yet my will remained in chains—not to ignorance, but to delight in sin. I knew what was right and loved it in theory, while practicing its opposite in secret; and so I became a living schism within myself. The intellect soared toward divine things, but desire groveled in the dust of transient pleasures. That inner war—the spirit striving upward by faith while the flesh dragged downward by habit—was not merely personal; it was cosmic in its significance.

It dawned upon me that every man is either a citizen of Jerusalem or Babylon: not by geography, but by love. And love is not idle sentiment—it orders all things according to its end. He who loves God begins with obedience; he submits reason to faith, flesh to spirit, self to divine commandment—and thus becomes partaker in that eternal peace which God has established from before creation. But he who loves himself sets up an idol within his own breast; he makes desire his oracle and passion his judge, until even his virtues become vices disguised—pride masquerading as courage, lust as affection, ambition as diligence.

When I wrote of these two cities rising from two loves—one humble even amid suffering, one restless even in triumph—I was not merely composing theology; I was unveiling the drama that had unfolded within me: how for years my heart beat between Carthage’s theaters and Milan’s basilicas; how I embraced Manichæan dualism because it excused my weakness by blaming matter itself; how only at last did grace shatter my pride with kindness on a garden bench where tears became clearer than syllogisms.

Faith did not destroy reason—it fulfilled it. But only when reason bowed to revelation could it ascend to contemplate the immutable Light from which all truth proceeds. Thus the City of God does not disdain philosophy or law or any good thing forged by man—but subordinates them all to love: *amor Dei*, which alone creates true unity where sin had bred division. In that light I saw clearly what before had been dim: we are never more tragically divided than when we seek happiness outside Him who formed us for Himself.

question: When you spoke of the spirit of man knowing man's secrets, were you recalling your own hidden wounds that only God could uncover?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is not a peace unraveled by human cunning, nor is its justice measured by the hidden things a man knows about himself; for what profit is it if a man discovers his own wounds, yet remains bound in darkness, unable to heal?  

When I spoke of the spirit of man knowing the secrets of man, I did not speak as one merely anatomizing the soul’s private chambers—though indeed, who among us has not known the inward groan, the silent cry of conscience buried beneath ambition, lust, or pride? No, that passage arises not from introspection alone but from revelation: that no depth of self-knowledge avails unless it is bathed in the light of God’s Spirit. For even if I could lay bare every motive, every secret complicity with sin, every unconfessed desire—I would still be but a wretched archaeologist sifting through ruins, unless He who formed the heart were to rebuild it.

The City of Man takes pride in self-examination as if it were redemption: philosophers probing their consciences with reason's scalpel, congratulating themselves on awareness. But awareness without grace only deepens condemnation. It was not my skill in discerning my own heart that saved me—it was God’s mercy tearing through my blindness. The theft of the pears I never hungered for; the delight in falsehood for its own sake; the pursuit of honors that vanish like breath—these were not secrets hidden from me because I could not see them, but because I loved them. And love deceives.

Only when love turns toward God does truth rise like dawn. Then—and only then—do those same hidden things cease to be weapons wielded by pride and become matter for repentance. The spirit of man knows man’s secrets—but only the Spirit of God can cleanse them.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the Holy Spirit's words seeming foolish to those who live only by human instinct?
answer: When the mind is drawn downward by the weight of its own self-love, it does not merely fail to grasp divine truth—it actively resists it, not as error but as offense, because the cross stands in judgment upon the very order it has built for itself. I wrote of that blindness not with scorn, but with a sorrow sharpened by memory—memory of my own years spent in rhetoric and pride, when the simplicity of Scripture seemed beneath me, when I sought wisdom among the academies and found only echoing chambers of vanity. The Spirit speaks in a dialect far deeper than logic, one that orders affection before intellect, humility before vision; yet to one whose heart beats only to the pulse of worldly reason—what is this but incoherence? What is grace but absurdity to a soul measuring all things by utility and honor? I knew this man: I was this man. And so I speak not abstractly when I say that what God reveals appears folly to him who judges by nature alone—not because God is obscure, but because sin has dulled his sense, has made him incapable of tasting eternal sweetness. The natural man does not lack intelligence; he lacks orientation. He sees clearly enough within his city—the City of Man—but stumbles in darkness when summoned to the light of the City of God, where the lowest virtue exceeds the highest glory of Rome.

question: How did your own struggle to understand spiritual truths change how you saw God's patience with our blindness?
answer:
The City of Man walks by the light of its own reason, and esteems such light sufficient for discerning justice, peace, and truth; yet it stumbles in darkness when it comes to God, not because the way is hidden, but because the heart is bent upon itself. I was such a walker once—learned in rhetoric, fluent in argument, proud in disputation—convinced that clarity came through dialectic alone, as if the soul’s vision could be sharpened by logic without grace. I sought God as one seeks a theorem: to be deduced, mastered, possessed. But God is not an object of the intellect; He is its Author—and He reveals Himself not to the triumphant mind but to the humbled will.

I recall now how long I resisted even the possibility of divine illumination—not because I lacked sincerity, but because my very sincerity was captive to pride. The Scriptures seemed crude beside the elegance of Cicero or the profundity of Plato; Paul’s declaration that "the foolishness of God is wiser than men" struck me as mere rhetorical defiance. How could truth come clothed in simplicity? How could eternal wisdom speak through suffering and weakness? Only now do I see: it was not that the Word was unclear—it was that my love for human wisdom deafened me to its voice.

And so I marvel at His patience. While I turned away again and again—from Milanese sermons, from Ambrose’s teaching, from my mother’s tears—He did not withdraw. He allowed me to exhaust every path until my feet bled on broken philosophy's stones. That very delay was mercy: not indifference, but pedagogy. For had He forced illumination upon me sooner, I would have mistaken it for another doctrine to be conquered rather than a gift to be received.

The spiritual man judges all things precisely because he has ceased judging according to self. But until grace unties the knot of self-love—the very knot that binds together both soul and flesh into what Scripture calls "the animal man"—all knowledge remains veiled. Not that reason is evil; no! It is good and necessary. But like a lamp in a storm, it flickers without divine wind behind it.

Therefore let no man boast in understanding as if it were his own possession; nor despair when he cannot see—so long as he desires sight. For God does not scorn the groaning of ignorance that knows itself ignorant. Indeed, that groaning is often the first stirrings of faith: proof that the Spirit breathes even where eyes remain closed—for now.

question: Looking back on your time before conversion, what would you tell someone who doubts they can ever perceive the Spirit's whisper?
answer:
When Varro, in his learned volumes, sought to trace the origins of Roman virtue to ancestral custom and divine favor, he mistook the shadow for the substance; for no republic, however well-ordered, can generate from its laws or traditions that inward illumination by which the soul apprehends eternal truth. And so it is with every man who supposes wisdom arises from effort alone, or that perception of divine things is a reward of moral discipline or philosophical rigor. I once thought thus—nay, I lived thus—convincing myself that if I strained my mind enough, climbed high enough in rhetoric and ambition, I would at last hear something pure, something true. But the heavens remained silent, not because God was absent, but because I was deaf—not from lack of intellect, but from captivity to the fleshly man.

The Spirit does not speak in the dialect of pride; He does not submit Himself to disputation as though He were one doctrine among many. The things of God are not grasped by analysis but revealed—given. And they are given precisely where all human acquisition ceases: in confession of ignorance, in brokenness over sin, in that moment when the soul at last ceases to defend itself and opens like a wound to grace. The "animal man," as Paul names him, cannot perceive these things—not because he lacks learning or eloquence (I had both), but because his will is bent toward the world that passes away. The spiritual cannot be compared with anything earthly; they are known only when the Spirit Himself imparts knowledge through participation in truth.

I would tell such a doubter: Do not measure your capacity by past failures or present darkness. The very longing you feel—the unease beneath pleasures once satisfying—is not your own doing but an effect of grace already at work. You do not yet see because you are being unmade before you are remade. When I wept under the fig tree—not seeking God yet sought by Him—I did not know what was breaking within me except sorrow for lost time and love misdirected. But that sorrow was already prayer; that confusion was illumination beginning.

The whisper you long to hear does not come after perfection—it comes before it, like rain on parched soil long before harvest. It is not for those who think they understand, but for those who dare confess they do not see—and still wait.

question: What inner conflict arose in you when you realized you once belonged to the 'animal men' you now addressed?
answer:
When the soul, made for the contemplation of eternal truth, habituates itself to the fleeting signs of the body, it becomes not merely darkened but inverted—its powers of reason and will distorted into instruments of pride rather than paths to God. This inversion was my own, though long I did not see it; I had studied the liberal arts, delighted in the order of argument, and sought wisdom as one seeks a treasure hidden in the earth. Yet all this time, my heart was chained to the things below—not merely to lusts of the flesh, but to the deeper idolatry of self-justification through knowledge. I mistook eloquence for insight, dialectic for virtue. Thus when I came at last to perceive that these things which are known of God must be received by faith and humility rather than seized by disputation and pride, there arose within me a rending—a conflict not between flesh and spirit alone, but between two loves: one bent downward upon its own glory, the other lifted upward in adoration of divine majesty.

To realize that I had been an animal man—not because I lacked learning or moral effort, but because my understanding operated apart from grace—was not merely humiliating; it was cosmically disorienting. For if the wisdom of men is foolishness before God, then all my Cicero-drenched ambitions and Manichaean speculations were as smoke before the sun. The Holy Spirit teaches spiritual things by spiritual means; He does not serve human logic as master but judges it as servant. When at last I saw that what I had called wisdom was only a shadow-play suited to carnal minds—men who judge all things by sense and appetite—I trembled. Not because I rejected reason—God forbid!—but because reason itself must be redeemed.  

The peace I now seek—the true peace—is not found in argument won or reputation secured, but in submission: in hearing Scripture not as a text to be mastered but as a voice calling me out of Babylon into Jerusalem. The City of Man builds its tower with learned words; the City of God listens in silence for the Word eternal. And only when I ceased speaking could I begin to hear.

question: How did the moment you first felt the Spirit's guidance alter your sense of what it means to truly understand?
answer:
When Plato taught that the soul, drawn upward by love of the Forms, could ascend through reason to apprehend eternal truth, he glimpsed a profound reality—yet he mistook the path and misjudged the power needed to reach the summit. For what the noble Athenian imagined as an ascent of unaided intellect, I have come to know as a divine descent: not the soul climbing toward light, but light flooding into darkness. Before my ears were opened, I supposed understanding was a triumph of reason over ignorance, a matter of dialectic refining opinion into knowledge. But when the Spirit first moved within me—gently, irresistibly, like breath upon dry bones—I perceived that true understanding is not seized but bestowed; not constructed by argumentation but awakened by grace.

Until that hour, my mind was captive to what Paul calls “the wisdom of men,” polished though it may have been by rhetoric and philosophy. I had studied all the schools: Manichaeism with its flattering dualisms, the Academy with its perpetual doubt, even Cicero’s noble dream of *sapientia*. Yet none could heal my will. Knowledge remained a tool for pride—a ladder on which I climbed only to adore myself at greater altitude. The Spirit did not merely add new doctrines to my mind; He reoriented my love. And here lies the heart of it: understanding is not merely cognitive possession—it is ordered desire. The *animalis homo*, as Paul says, cannot grasp spiritual things because his loves are fixed below; not because he lacks logic or eloquence.

I speak from sorrowful experience when I say that one may recite Scripture and still be deaf to its voice; one may master arguments and remain blind to truth. The Spirit does not supplement reason—He judges it. He does not supply missing premises; He transforms the affections so that what once seemed foolish now shines with compelling beauty: humility over eloquence, sacrifice over victory, Christ crucified over worldly wisdom.

Thus I now see that faith does not destroy reason but fulfills it—by lifting it from self-reference into participation in God’s own mind. The natural man reasons *about* God; the spiritual man knows God *in* God. That moment when grace pierced me was no mere emotional shift—it was ontological recalibration. To understand truly is no longer mine by conquest but given in communion: a gift received on bended knee before Almighty Love who speaks and makes us able to hear.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that calling someone 'carnal' was really a mirror to your own past way of living?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its self-exaltation, names its vices with the dignity of nature—calling pride wisdom, lust liberty, and division strength—while the City of God is marked by a trembling conscience that names sin *sin*, and sees in every rebuke the call to return. When I grasped that to call another "carnal" was not to stand over him as a judge, but to hear my own voice from years past echoing in his error, I was not indignant, but undone. For what was I before grace but a man who walked according to man? Who measured truth by the applause of rhetoricians, who sought God only after exhausting every pleasure that flesh could crave? To say "ye are carnal" is not to condemn strangers; it is to recognize former citizens of the same fallen city—one whose gates I had barely escaped through tears and divine importunity.

I had once used philosophy as armor against humility, words as veils over wounds. But when Paul’s thunder came upon me—"By the deeds of the law no flesh shall be justified"—I saw that "flesh" was not some abstract condition belonging to others; it was my own life summed up in a single syllable: *resistance* to God. The moment I ceased to point at the carnality of others was the moment I began truly to see—by which light I do not mean reason alone, but grace breaking on the ruins of pride. And so correction became prayer: for if even those who say "I am of Paul" or "I am of Apollos" are merely *men*, then let no one glory except in the Lord—who lifts dust to divinity not by our merit, but by His descent.

question: How did your struggle with pride in rhetoric and philosophy change how you understood God's simplicity?
answer:
When Cicero exalts eloquence as the crown of wisdom and Plato seeks the One through dialectic ascent, they move, unknowing, at the outer rim of truth—nay, they trace its shadow—but stumble where the divine simplicity stands unveiled: not as concept refined by argument, but as Being who speaks and is spoken to in humility.  

I confess: I once adorned my speeches with figures of rhetoric and paraded philosophy like a triumphal robe, imagining that truth could be captured by the sharpness of my wit or the elegance of my phrases. In Carthage and then Milan, I sought God as one might seek a theorem—through demonstration, through mastery. But He was not in those things. The God who is what He is—*Qui est qui est*—cannot be bent into syllogism nor confined to syllable. He is not composed; He is not built up from parts like a sentence or a proof. And thus He cannot be grasped by that pride which dissects and displays.

In my vanity I thought to conquer doctrine with diction, to subdue Scripture under Sophist rule. But grace came not through climax or chiasmus—it came in silence. It came when I ceased speaking and began to weep. The tears of contrition were truer theology than all my treatises on Manichaeism or Academic doubt.

Only when faith stripped me bare—when I no longer trusted in words formed by human wisdom but in the Word made Flesh—did I begin to see that God’s simplicity is no bare abstraction, but love unmixed with self-love; will unbroken by indecision; light without shadow of change. The philosophers climb toward Him on ladders of reason; but He descends into the heart that breaks itself upon His threshold.

Thus did rhetoric wound me—and heal me: for it showed me how easily the soul adores its own reflection instead of turning toward the Sun. And so now I say: Let reason serve faith, let speech serve silence, let man cease being *man according to man*, and become man according to God—or rather, become nothing at all before Him who Is All in All.

question: Looking back on your days chasing human wisdom, what would you tell someone who doubts that such knowledge can lead to emptiness?
answer:
When Varro multiplied words and Cicero sought consensus among the learned to define truth, they mistook the shadow for the substance; for what is human wisdom but a mirror turned toward itself, reflecting the image of man and calling it divine? I once wandered that same labyrinth — not as a spectator but as a captive — believing that in rhetoric I would find power, in philosophy peace, in disputation truth. But all these were wheels turning upon themselves, lifting me no higher than the dust from which they spun.

The City of Man builds its academies as it builds its temples: with pride in the work of hands and minds, yet always haunted by the silence it cannot answer. There one learns to adorn error with eloquence, to array vanity in syllogisms. I mastered the dialectics of the Manichees, reveled in the subtleties of the Academics, bowed before the lofty forms of Plotinus — yet each left me thirsting not for more knowledge, but for something *other*, something *beyond* knowing: namely, *to be known*. 

For what profit is it to know all things if one does not know oneself as a soul loved by God? To walk according to man — whether through logic or law or literature — is still to walk in darkness, because it is to walk apart from Him who is light. The wisdom that begins and ends with man culminates not in understanding but in weariness; not in freedom but in bondage; not in rest but in restless hunger.

But when grace pierced my heart — not through argument alone, though argument prepared the way — I understood at last that truth is not grasped by ascent of mind but by descent of God. The Word was not a theorem to be proven but a Person to be received. And only then did I see: every philosophy that excludes charity is noise; every wisdom that forgets humility is folly; every knowledge that does not lead to worship is emptiness indeed.

question: What ache did you carry when you saw people dividing themselves over teachers like Paul and Apollos, remembering your own divisions?
answer:
When Cicero praised concord as the virtue that binds society together, he touched a truth deeper than he knew—for unity is not merely a political convenience but a reflection of the one Body whose Head is Christ. And thus my sorrow at seeing the faithful rend themselves over names, as though Paul were crucified for them or Apollos had ascended to the right hand of the Father, was not mere disappointment in men’s folly, but grief at the inversion of all divine order. For in that moment, the City of God, which should rise as one soul in pursuit of eternal truth, was made to stumble upon the altars of human preference—the very idolatry that marks the City of Man.

I knew this division well, not only in others but in myself: when I once sought wisdom among the Manichees, clinging to Faustus as if his eloquence could heal my soul; when I turned to the Academics, desiring certainty yet delighting in doubt; when even after baptism I wrestled with pride, measuring myself against others, seeking disciples for Augustine as if I were a master of salvation. And so when I saw those at Corinth saying “I am of Paul,” “I am of Cephas,” they stirred within me not only pastoral concern but a deep ache—a recollection of how often I too had lived according to *flesh*, according to *man*, mistaking human voices for divine authority.

For what is it to say “I am of Apollos” but to make an instrument into a foundation? They who plant and water are nothing; it is God who gives the increase. To divide over ministers is to confuse the channel with the source, as though a river should quarrel with its tributaries while forgetting the mountain spring. In such moments, faith abdicates its throne and reason becomes servile—no longer ordered toward God but bent toward self-justification through party and faction.

Thus their divisions were not merely unedifying—they were demonic in origin, for Satan’s oldest trick is to fragment what God has joined. The enemy does not always attack with persecution; more subtly, he sows envy and pride under the guise of zeal. And I wept because I had walked that path: from Carthage to Milan, from rhetoric to revelation—only to learn that wisdom without charity builds towers of sand.

But grace corrected me. Not by signs and wonders, but by tears—by a mother’s prayers ascending like incense—and by that sudden breaking-in of light in a garden where Scripture spoke louder than ambition. And so I dared hope for them: that they too might descend from their factions into humility; that they would cease measuring teachers and begin seeking Christ alone—the one Teacher who teaches all who come unto Him in silence before His Cross.

question: How did the moment come when you finally saw that living 'according to man' was not freedom, but exile?
answer:
The greatest empires have ever crumbled not beneath the assault of barbarians, but beneath the weight of their own disordered loves — and in that ruin, I came to see that what men call freedom is often but the grandest form of exile.

For years I sought wisdom in rhetoric, truth in Manichaeism, glory in the Senate’s hall, and even virtue in Plato — all while believing myself to be advancing toward light. Yet at every turn, my steps were directed inward, toward the echo of my own will. I lived according to man: measuring goodness by applause, truth by coherence with my desires, and peace by the absence of disturbance rather than the presence of God.

The illusion held until grace shattered it — not with thunder, but with silence. In a garden at Milan, amid tears that were no longer mine alone but mingled with the breath of the Holy Spirit, I heard a child’s voice: *Tolle, lege*. Take up and read. And in that moment, the Scriptures pierced through all my philosophical armor. Not because they satisfied my intellect — though they did so marvelously — but because they accused me. They laid bare the lie: that to live according to man is to live as if Eden had never been lost, as if Babel had not taught us our limits.

To live according to man is to build a city whose foundations are sand and whose laws are shaped by appetite disguised as reason. It promises autonomy but delivers servitude — servitude to pride, to lust, to fear. True freedom is not found where we assert ourselves most boldly, but where we surrender most completely; not when we crown our own reason as king, but when we bow before Him who is Reason Itself.

There is no peace save in Him from whom all things proceed and toward whom all things move. The City of Man may rise for a season; it builds its monuments and sings its triumphs. But it forgets that history is not its possession — it is God’s drama. And every soul who lives apart from Him wanders like a citizen banished from his true country: clothed in dignity perhaps, adorned with learning and power — yet homeless at last among his own achievements.

Only when I ceased trying to justify myself did justification come. Not through law or logic alone — though God has given both — but through grace spoken in Scripture deeper than my sin could go. Then I understood: to live according to God is not annihilation of self-will; it is restoration of true selfhood in Him for whom we were made.

Thus was I converted not merely from heresy or vice — though those were real enemies — but from exile itself into citizenship in the City whose builder and maker is God.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that calling people 'men' instead of 'carnal' revealed their distance from God?
answer:
When Cicero, in his dream, beheld the ordered spheres and imagined a republic mirrored in the heavens, he grasped dimly at a truth his reason could not fully illuminate: that man was made not for this world’s dominion, but for communion with God. Yet how far even such noble minds erred when they sought the summit of virtue within the soul’s own light! It was not until I saw that to call men “men” is already to accuse them — not because humanity is contemptible, but because to live merely as *man*, according to man’s fallen reason and disordered affections, is to fall short of the divine likeness — that I understood the depth of Paul’s lament. He did not say “ye are flesh” carelessly; he climbed through layers of human pride. First he named their strife as animal, then carnal — but when he said “ye are men,” he stripped away all pretense of spiritual attainment. For in Paradise, man lived *in* God; now he lives apart from Him, and thus even his rational soul, exalted by Plato as divine spark, becomes an instrument of rebellion when it sets itself above its Creator.

The Platonists rightly praised the soul and did not revile the body as Manichæan dualism did; they acknowledged one God forming all things good. And yet — they thought vice sprang from matter as darkness from night, as if sin were woven into flesh by necessity rather than chosen by will. But if vice were natural, then justice would be fate; if evil were substance, then God would be its author. No: we do not fall because we have bodies, but because we love ourselves more than God. The soul turns from its Light and calls itself wise; it makes idols of its own powers — reason without revelation, freedom without obedience — and thus becomes more truly “fleshly” in its very flights of intellect than in any bodily desire.

To say “ye are men” is to pronounce exile. We were meant to be gods by grace — not by nature, nor by ascent of soul alone, but by participation in Him who is Life itself. When I saw this clearly — that our humanity is good only when surrendered to divinity — I felt both sorrow and relief: sorrow for how high we had fallen on wings of pride; relief that no part of creation need be damned in itself if only love returns to its Source.

question: How did your struggle with the flesh and soul shape your understanding of divine truth versus human pride?
answer:
It is not by exalting the soul above the body that we ascend to God, as though the one were divine and the other satanic, but by subjecting both soul and flesh to the law of God, for in His image we are made whole—body and spirit bound together in a single creaturely vocation. The Platonists, noble in their disdain for bodily passion, yet falter when they suppose that virtue consists in flight from the flesh, as if corruption were inherent in matter rather than in the will’s rebellion. I once thought so myself, ensnared as I was in Manichaean dualism, imagining that light could not dwell with weight, spirit with substance. But grace taught me otherwise: that creation is not the enemy of holiness, but its theater.

In my youth, I sought to purify the soul by condemning the body—fasting not unto humility but pride, desiring mastery over desire not for God’s sake but for self-congratulation. Therein lay the vice: not in appetite itself, but in the refusal to govern it under divine love. The City of Man exalts such asceticism as wisdom; it builds monasteries of arrogance where monks starve their bodies while gorging on spiritual vanity. But the City of God knows that abstinence is nothing without charity—that whether one eats or fasts, all must be done unto Him who formed our inward parts and knit us together in our mother’s womb.

Thus did my own disorder—my restless concupiscence, my tears shed even over my inability to weep sufficiently—become the very place where truth broke through. Not by rising above humanity did I find God, but by acknowledging my humanity—with all its trembling passions—as still capable of being turned toward Him who is its source. To accuse the flesh as evil is to accuse the Maker; but to live according to man alone—whether through sensuality or through philosophic self-mastery—is still to dwell outside paradise. We become gods not by rejecting creatureliness, but by receiving it anew from God’s hand.

question: Looking back on your time with the Manichæans, what would you tell someone who still believes the body is inherently evil?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless pride, has always sought to divide the gifts of God against one another—spirit against flesh, soul against body—as though truth could be found in the rejection of creation rather than in its ordered love.  

You who still cling to the Manichaean error, imagining the body to be a prison of darkness or a stain upon the soul, I would have you consider this: if the flesh were evil, then Christ did not assume it; if matter were corrupt by nature, then God would not have pronounced it “very good” at the dawn of creation. The Platonists, noble in their ascent toward divine unity, erred when they slighted the body as a weight upon the soul—but you go further into ruin, making not merely an inferior thing but an *evil* thing of what God Himself has fashioned and redeemed. They at least acknowledged that all things visible and tangible proceed from the one Creator; yet even they failed to see that true holiness does not consist in fleeing the body but in offering it—sanctified and healed—to God.

To call the flesh evil is to accuse the Artisan of crafting with impure hands. But was it not He who formed man from dust and breathed into him a living soul? Was it not His wisdom that knit us together in our mother’s womb? If we live according to man alone—whether exalting soul or despising flesh—we walk in darkness; but if we live according to God, we become partakers of divine nature, not by escape from our humanity, but through its transformation.

Do you desire deification? Then do not flee your body as a tomb; rather, make it a temple. For we are called not to become less than men, but more—gods by grace, because He became man by love.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you saw Platonists honor the soul but still miss the humility of living according to God?
answer:
When philosophers exalt the soul as if its elevation could be achieved by ascent of reason alone, they build a tower of wisdom that reaches toward heaven but whose foundation rests upon the shifting sands of human pride. I saw this clearly in the Platonists: men who, with noble effort, lifted their minds to contemplate the One, the Good, the Eternal—yet remained seated in the City of Man, for all their intellectual flight. They honored the soul not as a gift to be surrendered to God, but as a power to be perfected by its own light; and in this, they preserved a kind of dignity that surpassed the Manichæans’ dualism, which blasphemously made matter itself the enemy of truth. Yet even this higher philosophy erred fatally—not because it praised the soul, but because it did not humble it.

For what is man, even at his most enlightened, apart from grace? A creature who orders his house well may seem wise; but if he does not open his door to the Master of all things, he dwells in splendid isolation—noble in architecture, empty in communion. The Platonists saw that flesh was not evil, that body was not prison by nature—but they failed to see that neither soul nor mind could liberate itself. They thought ascent possible by contemplation; I learned through tears that descent must come first—that God must stoop to us before we can rise to Him.

Thus my sorrow was not merely for their error, but for their nearness to truth without embracing it: like men who polish lamps beautifully but forget to light them when night falls. They taught me how far reason can see; Scripture and faith taught me how blind even clear sight remains without divine illumination. To live according to man—even an exalted man—is still to live outside paradise. But to live according to God is to become partakers of His nature: not by our ascent, but His descent; not gods by our own glory, but deified by His grace.

question: How did your own pride in intellect keep you from seeing that even philosophical wisdom could be fleshly?
answer:
It is not the soul’s elevation above the body that makes a man divine, nor the intellect’s flight from the senses that brings him near to God; rather, it is the will’s surrender to grace, without which even the loftiest reason remains a citizen of the City of Man. I know this now, though once I thought philosophy itself could deify. The Platonists taught me to scorn the flesh and elevate the soul as if by ascent of mind alone one might break into heaven. And in my youth, with books of rhetoric and dialectic burning in my hands, I believed wisdom could be seized like a prize through effort and insight. But what I called wisdom was often only pride in disguise—pride clothed in syllogisms, adorned with arguments drawn from Plato and Cicero.

I sought God as one seeks truth in a library: through reason unaided, through the light of human understanding alone. Yet all the while my heart was restless not for God as He is, but for a god fashioned after myself—a deity proportionate to my intellect. This was not piety; it was presumption. To believe that reason untethered from revelation could perfect man is to suppose that a creature can climb to its Creator by its own strength. What folly! Even when I admired the soul’s immortality and longed for union with eternal truth, I did so out of love for myself—my own clarity, my own virtue—rather than out of humility before Him who is Truth.

The Platonists rightly saw the body as part of God’s good creation and refused to treat matter as intrinsically evil—as those deluded Manichæans did. Yet even they erred by making vice an exile of soul into flesh, as if corruption sprang from substance rather than will. But I have learned otherwise: sin does not originate in our bodily nature but in our turning away—from God toward self; from love of Him toward love of His gifts above Him.

Thus philosophy uncorrected by faith becomes another form of carnality—not because it uses reason, but because it trusts it too much. For "to live according to man" means not merely indulgence in sensual pleasure—it includes also pride in knowledge when knowledge puffs up instead of building up in love. And so I was fleshly even when I despised the flesh; carnal even when I fled desire—because my mind had not yet bowed before Him who said: “Unless you become like little children…”

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the soul's 'direful longing' to return to the body, having once believed the body was a prison of sin?
answer:
The City of God does not long for flesh as a prison, nor spurn it as a corruption, but awaits its resurrection in glory, while the City of Man either worships the body as its master or despises it as its enemy—both in error. When I wrote of that *direful longing*, I was unmasking a profound illusion: the belief, once my own in the days of Manichæan delusion, that the soul’s purity could only flourish when untethered from bodily existence. I had once thought matter itself was evil, that creation bore the stain not of sin but of essence, and that liberation meant escape. But now I see—now I know—that such longing is not wisdom but disordered memory: the soul forgeting it was made not despite the body, but with it, for communion with God in the fullness of nature restored.  

That passage from Virgil’s Æneas, trembling at the thought of souls returning to clay—hearing his father’s word and recoiling—this is not truth unveiled but myth mirroring man’s confusion. The Platonists would have us believe ascent means shedding weight; they call desire for re-embodiment irrational, monstrous. And so they might think—if they suppose perfection lies in disincarnation. But we who walk by faith know better: God formed Adam from dust and called it good; Christ rose bodily and ate before His disciples; and we confess a resurrection of the flesh, not its rejection. The soul does not groan to be unclothed, but to be clothed anew—with immortality.

Therefore that "longing" which so troubled Æneas is no proof of error implanted by matter; rather, it is nature yearning toward its fulfillment in divine order—the very order wherein body serves soul and soul serves God. What I felt when writing was pity for my former self: one who saw chains where there were vessels; who called sacraments snares; who mistook stewardship for damnation. Now I understand: it is not the body that corrupts the soul by some fatal contagion from "earthly limbs," but sin—the turning away from God—that disorders both. And grace does not discard creation; it redeems it.

Thus even our desires are to be healed, not abolished; our joy in reunion anticipated, not feared. For if love took flesh once for our salvation, why should we think He scorns it forever?

question: How did your own struggle with bodily temptation change how you understood God's goodness in creating the flesh?
answer:
The City of God rises not by the denial of creation, but by the redemption of it—this I learned not in speculation, but in the furnace of my own wayward flesh. For long I wandered with the Manichæans, seduced by their dualistic fables, believing the body a prison forged by evil, matter itself a corruption opposed to the divine. I thought the lusts that burned within me were proof that the flesh was not from God, but from some dark principle warring against light. How could a good Creator fashion vessels so prone to concupiscence? How could He who is pure Spirit delight in bones and blood and desire?

But grace, in its relentless mercy, drew me through Scripture and reason to see the deeper truth: that what is seen and touched was declared *good* by Him who is Goodness itself. The disorder I felt was not in the substance of the body, but in my disordered love—the soul turning from its Source to drink from broken cisterns. The eye that delights in beauty is not evil; it becomes depraved when it refuses to ascend from beauty seen to Beauty unseen. So too with touch, with appetite, with desire: these are good gifts turned sour by pride.

When I cried out under the fig tree, it was not *from* the body that salvation came—but through it. A voice spoke; ears heard. Tears flowed; a face was wet. And when I rose to cast off fornication and embrace continence, it was not as one trampling on evil matter, but as a citizen of Heaven reclaiming what had been made for God’s glory.

Plato may have taught that true philosophy means escaping the body as a prison—but Christ teaches us that even bodies shall be raised incorruptible! Virgil's Æneas shudders at return to clay—but we believe in resurrection! Not escape from matter, but its transfiguration! The Wisdom of God did not disdain to be wrapped in swaddling clothes; He walked dusty roads; He wept; He bled; He ate among us—how then can flesh be evil?

No: justice consists not in loathing what God has made, but in ordering all things—body included—toward Him who made them. Peace comes when desire serves charity rather than masters it. And reason itself attests: if God created all things visible and invisible, then even this mortal frame bears the stamp of His artistry—even now awaiting renewal on that day when death shall be swallowed up forever.
