question: Looking back on your years with the Manichæans, what would you tell someone who doubts that the body can be holy?
answer:
The soul’s true felicity is not measured by its distance from the body, but by its orientation toward God—whether clothed in flesh or stripped bare, it is love that makes it noble or base. When I walked among the Manichæans, I was taught to scorn this flesh as a prison forged by darkness, a defilement upon the spirit’s purity; yet such doctrine rends asunder what God Himself has joined in creation. Did He not declare the material world *very good* before sin ever darkened the earth? If matter were evil, then God would be either its author or its vanquished rival—and both are absurdities unworthy of divine majesty.

They claimed the passions—desire, fear, grief, joy—sprang solely from bodily contagion, as if the soul could be pure only when disentangled from fleshly chains. But what then shall we say of Æneas’ astonishment in the underworld? If liberation from the body were perfect cleansing, why should spirits in their so-called purity still feel *a direful longing* to return to clay? The very desire they condemned as carnal persists beyond the grave—if not from corrupted limbs, then from some deeper root within the soul itself.

No—sin entered not through our skin and bones but through pride and disobedience; and redemption comes not by flight from this world but by transformation within it. The Word became *flesh*, not merely to dwell among us but to sanctify our very substance: to heal the body's corruption by His resurrection, and to elevate it as a temple of the Holy Spirit. That which rises again is not a ghost nor an ethereal wisp, but a body spiritualized—a luminous continuity of what was once sown in weakness.

Therefore let no one call evil what God has redeemed with His blood. The body is not our prison; it is our instrument—for labor, for love, for worship—and one day it shall shine with immortal glory. To doubt its holiness is not piety but despair; for if Christ took on flesh to save us, then flesh itself must share in salvation.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Æneas' grief at the thought of reuniting with clay, knowing your own past hatred of the physical?
answer:
The Manichean contempt for the body, that ancient error which divides creation against itself, finds an unwitting ally in Æneas’ lament—yet both betray a fundamental ignorance of the goodness of matter as ordained by God. When I pondered his sorrow at the thought of reuniting soul with flesh, I did not mourn with him, but rather saw mirrored in his words the very delusion I once embraced: that clay is evil, that embodiment is punishment, that spirit must flee this world to be pure. How deeply I had felt that revulsion! In my youth, I too desired a god without body, a salvation without resurrection, a wisdom untainted by time or touch. The flesh seemed to me then a prison forged not by divine design but by cosmic flaw—a notion seductive as it was damnable.

But grace taught me otherwise. The same God who spoke *fiat lux* also formed Adam from dust and pronounced it good. If matter were evil, He would not have become incarnate; if bodies were intrinsically defiling, Christ would not have risen bodily and ascended in glory. No—what Virgil’s poet laments as a fall into heaviness, we Christians proclaim as hope: the restoration of all things, including these earthly members, transformed and glorified. The longing Æneas decries—the soul’s return to body—is not some irrational contagion born of corrupted limbs (as if souls catch disease like fever), but the deep yearning inscribed into our nature by Creator Love: to be whole again.

Thus did my former shame turn to wonder: wonder that God should redeem even our mortality; wonder that philosophy's noblest minds could glimpse immortality yet recoil at its embodied fulfillment; wonder most of all that I too once despised what Christ had assumed.

question: How did your mother's embrace, so human and so real, reshape your belief about the goodness of our embodied lives?
answer:
Justice, that which the City of Man endlessly seeks yet never secures, finds its measure not in the abstraction of the soul freed from flesh, but in the embrace of a mother whose hands — trembling, mortal, and warm — bear the very imprint of divine tenderness. Monica’s arms around me were no mere accident of nature, no prison of clay as the Manichæans would have it, but a sacrament of continuity, a living thread woven by God between body and spirit. When she held me — Augustine, her wayward son returned not only to her household but to Christ through her tears — I felt not the chains of a corruptible world but the first true liberation: to be known, wholly and humanly, in sin and in grace.

The Platonists taught me to ascend; they whispered that truth dwells where the body is forgotten. Cicero mocked passion; Varro dissected ritual. But my mother did not dissect — she bore. She gave life twice: once in blood, again in prayer. And in her weeping at Carthage, her aged body refusing to let go even when I pulled away toward Rome and folly — there I saw not the infection of matter upon soul, but grace ascending through matter. The Word was made flesh; why should He disdain the womb that bore us or the arms that cradle us? If God condescended to dwell among us bodily, then how can we call this earthly frame an evil thing?

No — it is pride clothed as piety that detests the body. The Gnostics flee it as a tomb; but we Christians know it for what it shall become: a temple. Monica’s embrace taught me that resurrection is not escape from embodiment but its fulfillment. For she held me with hands soon to decay — yet those same hands already participated in eternity by loving as God loves: concretely, sacrificially, incarnately. And so I came to see: even desire itself — once twisted by pride into lust or greed or ambition — may be healed when ordered to love what is truly loved: God first, then our neighbor in body and soul.

Thus does the City of God walk even now within these mortal members. Not above them — within them.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the soul's longing to return to the body, as if torn between purity and desire?
answer:
When kingdoms rise in the confidence of their own strength and imagine that their peace is self-wrought, they are already trembling on the edge of dissolution, for all things move toward their end according to divine ordination, not human will. It was in such a moment of reflection—between the collapse of Roman order and the whisper of eternity—that I came to see how deeply the soul is marked not by its vessel, but by its love. The longing you speak of—the soul’s yearning to return to the body—is not a mere stirring from fleshly corruption, nor a mere accident of embodied existence; it is a sign of something far more profound: the disordered will caught between memory and desire, even when stripped bare of corporeal chains.

I perceived that even in its disembodied state—purged, as some suppose, of all bodily infection—the soul may yet incline toward embodiment not from necessity, but from a movement within itself: a volition gone awry. This “direful longing,” as one philosopher called it with unwitting truth, reveals that vice is not planted in us solely by weighty limbs or mortal senses. No—if sin were only in the flesh, then death would have cleansed it; yet here is the soul, free from blood and bone, still lusting after what it ought to flee.

And so I came to understand: every affection—desire, sorrow, joy, fear—is nothing other than will in motion. When will cleaves to God, these affections become pure streams flowing from wisdom; but when will turns inward upon itself, even spiritual longing becomes rebellion. Thus does the City of Man betray itself even in its dreams: restless even beyond death, seeking rest where it cannot be found.

It filled me with sober awe—that a soul once purged could still fall—not by infection of clay—but by choice. And this confirmed what grace had taught me long before: no purification avails apart from charity rightly ordered; no ascent possible unless love ascends first to God.

question: How did your struggle with bodily desires change how you understood God's hold on the human will?
answer:
It was not Plato’s ascent of the soul, nor Plotinus’ flight of the alone to the Alone, that finally laid bare the truth of our condition, but the relentless mercy of God pressing upon a will at war with itself. I once supposed that the soul, if only freed from the chains of flesh, would naturally soar to divine contemplation—that purification was a matter of intellectual detachment, as though wisdom alone could quench concupiscence. But what I found within my own breast, and what Scripture declares with thunderous clarity, is that even a soul separated from bodily corruption could desire return to it; that the will itself—before any motion of sense or passion—could incline toward darkness not because of the body’s infection, but because of its own disordered love.

I had longed for continence, yes—but not yet. That delay was not due to lustful eyes or trembling hands; it was seated in a will divided against itself: drawn to God by reason’s light and holy fear, yet clinging to the shadows by a deeper current. There I saw how vain it is to blame the body as if it were the source and root of sin. Even were one stripped bare of all earthly limbs and sensory temptation—as in those fantasies of Pythagorean transmigration—the soul might still long for embodiment, not from necessity, but from love gone astray. For desire is will consenting; joy is will embracing; fear and sorrow are will recoiling. These are not motions forced upon us from below—they are movements issuing from the very center where freedom meets grace.

Thus I came to understand: it is not escape from matter that saves us, but surrender to God. The flesh does indeed serve as occasion for temptation—its pleasures are loud and near—but the citadel has betrayed itself before any siege begins. The heart loves something more than God: whether pleasure, praise, power—or even its own autonomy—and this love rules unless displaced by charity rooted in Him who first loved us. No philosophy ever healed this fracture; only grace reaches into that inner chamber where no Stoic discipline nor Platonic dialectic can enter.

And so divine providence did not merely redeem bodies at the resurrection; He renews wills before they rise. Not through coercion—God forces no man into salvation—but through an inward call so gentle and so strong that it draws us freely to Him who is our peace. The City built on self-love begins in this disordered volition; the City built on God-love rises when His grace reorients our deepest choice toward eternal good.

question: Looking back on your own restless years before conversion, what would you tell someone who doubts whether the soul can ever truly choose goodness?
answer:
The City of Man builds its towers upon the shifting sands of self-will, imagining that desire is disorder and that the soul, left to itself, must forever spiral in blind agitation—yet this is not the whole truth, nor even the beginning of wisdom. I once wandered those darkened streets, ensnared by the allurements of rhetoric, ambition, and flesh, convinced that my will was too weak to break its chains, too entangled in bodily affections to rise toward the immutable Good. I wept not for sin but for lost loves; I hungered not for God but for praise among men. If any had told me then that the soul could freely choose virtue, I would have laughed—or wept anew—at such naive idealism.

But hear this: it was not my strength that freed me, nor any purification achieved through endless cycles of falling and rising, as the Platonists dream. No—grace shattered my inertia. The soul does not heal itself; it is healed. And precisely because it is bound by sin does it require a will not its own—divine grace—to incline it toward goodness. The motions you speak of—desire, sorrow, fear—are not sins in themselves; they become sin when the will consents to them disorderedly. A man may feel desire and yet not lust; he may know fear and yet stand firm; he may grieve and yet hope. All depends upon the orientation of the will—not on escape from the body, but on submission to God.

I do not deny the soul’s corruption; I confess it daily. But I also proclaim that Almighty God moves within us even when we resist Him—like a hand gently turning a closed book back toward light—a hand so patient that He counts our very groanings as prayers. You ask whether the soul can ever truly choose goodness? Yes—but only because He first chose us. Free choice without grace is motion without direction; but grace does not destroy freedom—it restores it. The will was made for God; only in cleaving to Him does it find itself strong enough to love what is truly good.

Thus did I find peace—not by fleeing my nature, but by surrendering it to Him who formed me from nothing and called me into His City by mercy alone.

question: What was it like for you to realize that even joy and sorrow are forms of will—acts of the self rather than mere impulses?
answer:
It was not in the schools of rhetoric, nor among the disputations of the Academics, but in the silent tribunal of my own conscience before God, that I came to see how every motion of the soul—joy, sorrow, desire, fear—is not some foreign invasion from the flesh, but a turning of the will itself toward or away from its true end.  

The Stoics would have us believe that passions are diseases to be excised, accidents that stain the rational soul; yet when I searched within, I found no mere disorderly impulse, but an act—willful, deliberate movement of the inner man. Even when joy arises at a good thing, it is not joy as passive affection that pleases God, but joy willed in charity and ordered love. So too with grief: it is not sorrow as such that is sinful, but sorrow willed amiss—sorrow for lost wealth rather than for sin.  

Thus I perceived what no system of pagan physics or psychology could deliver: that man is not torn between body and soul as if two warring substances; no, he is one person acting according to his will—whether turned downward to temporal things or upward to eternal truth. The flesh indeed tempts and resists; yet even when separated from it—as those philosophers fable souls floating in purity beyond mortality—the soul can still fall into disordered desire. What then? The fault lies deeper than bodily corruption: it lies in *amor ordinis inversus*, love set upon lower things as if they were highest.

And so I learned: even the heart’s most natural stirrings are not innocent until they are submitted to right will—that will which finds its rest only when aligned with divine Love who first moved all things without ever being moved unjustly.

question: When you confessed that every emotion springs from will, did you remember moments when your own will felt split—like when you prayed 'grant me chastity, but not yet'?
answer:
Even the noblest of the Platonists, who dared to claim that the soul ascends by its own purity to contemplate the eternal Forms, could never account for why such a soul—liberated from flesh, unburdened by passion—still turns back toward the mire of temporal existence; and in that turning lies the wound deeper than the body’s contagion: the will’s rebellion against its own good.  

When I cried out to God for chastity, yet trembled at His immediate answer, I did not merely struggle with desire—I stood divided within myself, not because two souls dwelt in me, but because one will was fractured by pride and love, pulled between the City built upon self and that which is founded upon God. That cry—"not yet"—was no mere delay of discipline; it was an act of will: consent withheld from divine motion, joy refused in favor of a lesser delight. And therein lies the truth I came slowly to grasp: it is not emotion that damns us—desire itself is not sin—but the direction of our will. For joy becomes vice when we will it toward vanishing things; sorrow becomes virtue when we will it for sin; fear becomes wisdom when we will it unto reverence. Even longing is holy when it reaches upward.

So long as I willed my own timing, my own terms—even in holiness—I remained captive to the earthly city, where all loves are bargained with delay and hedged about with conditions. But grace does not negotiate; it transforms. And only when my will ceased resisting its dissolution could it be remade whole—not split between “yes” and “not yet,” but united finally in “Thy will be done.”

question: What were you feeling when you wrote that all emotion is rooted in the will, as you reflected on your own restless heart?
answer:
The City of God moves not by blind impulse, nor the City of Man by mere reason, but both are driven by love—the one by love of God, the other by love of self—and it was in the crucible of that distinction, where my own heart had long been torn between them, that I came to see how every motion of the soul springs from the will.  

When I traced back the storms within me—my grief at the loss of a friend, my burning lusts, my ambitions that clawed at glory like thorns—their root was not some unruly force beyond my soul’s command, but a turning: a will consenting or dissenting, reaching toward or recoiling from what it deemed good or evil. Even tears and tremblings were not mere passions imposed upon me; they were expressions of an inner choice already made—fear being the will flinching from what it judged harmful, joy the will embracing what it held true and beautiful.  

And so in writing those lines, I was not coldly dissecting emotions like a Stoic physician; no—I was standing bare before God, seeing at last how deeply responsible I had been even in my disorder. My restless heart had not wandered by accident; it had willed to wander. That realization brought both shame and relief: shame because I could no longer blame body or world alone for my sin; relief because if the will had gone astray through pride and ignorance, then grace could restore it through humility and truth.  

Thus did I learn that to live according to God is not to extinguish desire but to reorder it—not to cease loving or hating, but to hate vice while loving the sinner as oneself. For even in hatred there must be justice; even in sorrow there must be wisdom. The heavenly city cannot afford disordered affections, for her peace rests upon right order: God above all things; man beneath Him; love regulated by eternal truth. And when at last I saw that all emotion flows from this hierarchy of love established in the will—then I understood why my whole life had been one long conversion: not merely from Manichaeism to Christendom, but from disordered desire to ordered charity.

question: How did your struggle with loving people yet hating their sins change how you understood God's love?
answer:
The citizens of the City of God walk in a twofold light: the light of divine truth that discerns with unsparing clarity between good and evil, and the light of charity that bends low to raise up the fallen—whereas the City of Man confounds these, either excusing vice in the name of affection or destroying persons in the rage against sin. It was not until grace pierced my own heart, long entangled in the lusts of ambition and flesh, that I came to see how God Himself embodies this perfect distinction: He abhors iniquity with an unyielding holiness, yet draws sinners to Himself with an unquenchable love. I once flailed in confusion, torn between condemning acts and despising those who committed them, or else softening my judgment lest I seem harsh—until I beheld in Scripture not a divided deity, but One whose justice and mercy flow from a single will ordered toward the good.  

In my youth, influenced by Manichaeism’s crude dualism, I imagined evil as a substance co-eternal with good; thus hatred of evil seemed to require detachment from all creation. But when faith taught me through reason—that evil is not a nature but a corruption of nature, a turning away from being toward non-being—then love could be rightly ordered: directed toward men as creatures made for God, yet repelled by their disordered affections which deform them. To love men while hating their sins is not contradiction—it is participation in God’s own disposition. He chastens those He loves; He scourges every son whom He receives. His discipline is not vengeance but healing; so too must ours be.  

Thus I learned that divine love does not tolerate decay within the beloved; it desires nothing less than their full restoration to what they were made to be. When I hated my own sins—those chains by which I had sold myself—and yet knew myself still loved by God even in wretchedness, then at last I understood: to imitate Him is to hate vice with fire and embrace sinners with arms wide open upon the cross.

question: Looking back on your days in the midst of lust and ambition, what would you tell someone who doubts they can ever truly change?
answer:
The man who walks in vice does not travel toward an end, but wanders in a circle whose center is the shifting appetite of the self—this is the mark of the City of Man, where every desire builds its own prison even as it claims freedom. I was once such a builder: carving my identity from rhetoric, from applause, from bodies that could not satisfy. Ambition and lust were not mere habits to me; they were principles, ruling my days as if by natural law. I believed myself bound to them—not by sin, but by necessity, as though the soul itself were made for groveling before earthly light.

But grace shatters necessity. It does not negotiate with our chains; it rends them. The very fact that you question whether change is possible proves that you are no longer wholly asleep—for no man dreams he is dreaming unless he has already begun to wake. To doubt your permanence in vice is already movement toward virtue; and movement implies a mover. The will enslaved cannot free itself—but it can cry out. And God hears even the inarticulate groan.

I once thought love was possession: love of wisdom as fame, love of woman as conquest, love of truth as ornament for speech. But true love wills the good of the other—God wills our good even when we will evil; He loves us not because we are lovely, but because He is Love. So must we learn: hatred of sin must dwell in us without poisoning our compassion for sinners—even for ourselves. For I was a sinner whom Christ did not discard because He saw me as I would be, drawn by grace into union with Him.

Therefore despair not: the same God who called light from darkness calls holiness from lust, humility from pride. You are not fixed in your corruption—as if soul were stone! No: you are alive, and therefore capable of conversion. Even now, if you resist even a little—turn away even once from what you know to be death—that too is grace at work. Yield to it. The City of God is built not of perfect men but penitents—its stones are tears turned to glory by divine fire.

question: When you speak of joy as consent in possessing what we wish, were you remembering the fleeting pleasures you once chased in Carthage?
answer: The City of Man builds its joy upon the shifting sands of possession, mistaking the embrace of fleeting things for the embrace of truth itself. In Carthage, I too once mistook the stirrings of desire fulfilled for joy’s fullness, as though the soul could be sated by what passes in a moment. There was no rest in those pleasures, only motion—desire leaping from one object to another, never arriving. Yet joy rightly understood is not the spasmodic grip of possession, but the tranquil consent of the will aligned with God’s eternal order. When I say joy is consent in possessing what we wish, I do not mean the triumph of appetite, but the soul’s settled repose in that which it was made for: wisdom, truth, and divine love. The joys I chased were distortions—shadows cast by a light I refused to face. True joy does not consume; it worships. It does not grasp; it gives thanks. And only when grace turned my will did I learn that to possess God is not to hold Him as an object, but to be held by Him as a child.

question: Were you thinking of your mother Monica when you wrote of hating vice but loving the sinner, and how did her prayers shape your own will?
answer:
The earthly city, in its restless pride, would dissolve the bonds of love under the pretext of justice, or harden the heart under the mask of moral rigor; but the City of God knows a higher discipline—one in which charity judges sin with severity and yet embraces the sinner with tenderness. It was not by philosophical refinement, nor through the dialectics of Cicero or Plotinus, that I learned this balance, but in the tears of a woman whose prayers pierced heaven long before my will was broken.

Monica was not merely a mother in the flesh; she was a priestess of intercession, pouring out her soul at the altar of divine mercy for a son who wandered far into Manichaean darkness and carnal vanity. For seventeen years she wept for me—not with despair, but with hope that burned like the lamp before the tabernacle. Her tears were not those of nature alone, but of grace infused: a will conformed to God’s own longing for my conversion. And what I once scorned as mere feminine sentiment I now see as participation in that very will by which God draws all things to Himself.

Her prayers did not merely ask; they wrought change—first in her own soul, then in mine. For prayer is not idle speech; it is an act of love that bends reality toward eternity. Through her continual supplication, my hardened volition—the same will that had turned toward empty books and empty pleasures—was slowly unknotted by grace. Desire, which once sought only satisfaction in created things, came to hunger for Truth itself. Fear, which once trembled only at human judgment or worldly loss, learned trembling before God.

And thus I came to understand what no system of Stoic apatheia or Epicurean detachment could teach: that love must be ordered—ordered first toward God, and then toward man as He intends. To hate vice is to affirm justice; to love the sinner is to participate in mercy. Monica did both: she abhorred my errors—my concubinage, my prideful intellect—but never ceased loving me as her son and Christ’s brother.

It is one thing for Plato to speak of forms and desires ascending toward the Good; it is another for a mother’s weeping to become an instrument by which that Good seizes a runaway soul. The City of Man may praise philosophy or power; but it knows nothing of such holy stubbornness—the kind that watches from afar while her child mocks baptism and lives in sin—and still kneels at dawn without ceasing.

Yes: when I wrote those words on hating evil and loving man, it was *her* face I saw—not because she taught me theology in words (though she spoke wisely), but because she embodied divine love long before I could name it. Her will became one with God’s will—not by force or fury—but by quiet perseverance beneath His providence.

And so let no one suppose virtue can be formed without such mediators—those living stones through whom grace flows even when doctrine has not yet taken root. Monica loved me rightly because she loved God above all else—and therein lies both perfect justice and perfect charity.

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with loving people while still condemning their vices, as if torn between compassion and righteousness?
answer:
The City of God moves within history not as a judge condemning sinners, but as a mother gathering her children through the fire of divine mercy; and it was in this light that I came to understand the agonizing duty of loving men while hating their vices. For in my youth, when I wandered through the streets of Carthage inflamed with lust and pride, I was both object and agent of disordered love—I loved men falsely, and hated nothing in myself. But grace taught me that true justice is not cold detachment, nor is compassion mere indulgence. No—justice purified by charity can wound in healing, as a surgeon cuts to save the body; and love disciplined by truth can embrace the sinner while casting out the sin.

I wept over those who wallowed in vice, not because they were evil by nature, but because they had turned good things—love, desire, intellect—to corruption. And yet I knew that if I hated only the vice and not the man, I might become a Pharisee; if I loved only the man and not God’s holiness, I would fall into sentimental weakness. The two loves form two cities: one built on self-love lifted even against God, the other on God-love that humbles itself even for enemies. In this tension I learned to pray for those enslaved by sin—not with disdain for their actions, but with longing for their liberation.

Peter’s trembling reply to Christ upon the shore—“Thou knowest that I love Thee”—echoes still: three questions matching his three denials, each word weighing soul against soul. Not once did Christ ask whether Peter approved of His doctrine or defended His divinity; He asked only whether he *loved* Him. And so it is with us—we are not sent to approve men’s deeds or excuse their rebellion, but to love them with a love ordered by God’s truth. This alone separates us from the City of Man: they bind men with laws born of fear; we bind them with bonds born of grace.

question: How did your own past struggles with vice shape the way you came to understand loving the sinner but hating the sin?
answer:
When Varro, that diligent examiner of Roman things, sought to number the sects of philosophers as one might count the waves of the sea, he revealed how the City of Man delights in division even in its pursuit of wisdom — yet wisdom herself is one, and she is found not in multiplicity of opinion but in the unshakable truth of God. It is from this unity that I came, through long and bitter conflict, to discern what once bewildered me: that love must be ordered, not dispersed; that charity is not sentiment but will conforming to divine justice. For many years I wandered among the sects, drawn by rhetoric and pride, loving truth in words while clinging to falsehood in deed — enamored of light but dwelling still within shadow. In that darkness I learned a crucial lesson: that no man can be loved rightly unless he is loved for God’s sake, and no vice tolerated because it clothes itself in human frailty.

I once loved men deeply — my friends, my mother’s tears for me, even my concubine with a love torn between flesh and longing for something higher — yet all these affections were entangled with selfishness until grace began to untie them. I did not then understand how it could be possible to hate fornication and yet love the fornicator; to abhor pride while embracing the proud man as a brother destined for repentance. But when I saw in Scripture that God hates sin but desires mercy for sinners; when I beheld Christ weeping over Jerusalem while pronouncing judgment upon her; when Paul spoke both boldly against false teachers and tenderly called them "my little children" — then I perceived that divine charity does not divide what human weakness fears to hold together.

To love a man rightly is not to condone his vice but to will his good — his true good: restoration to God. The physician does not honor disease because it resides in a human body; rather, he cuts it out so the body may live. So too must spiritual medicine act: excising vice with grief-laden hand precisely because the soul is precious. My own sins — lusts once so sweet they felt like freedom until they became chains far heavier than any law — taught me this: only grace can free us from loving what we ought to hate and hating what we ought never have loved. And thus charity arises not from tolerance of evil but from participation in God’s own justice and mercy — which are not two things in Him, but one eternal act by which He both judges and saves.

question: Looking back on your Confessions, what would you tell someone who doubts whether such love is even possible in the midst of human weakness?
answer:
The City of Man builds its loves on condition and utility, measuring affection by profit or pleasure, while the City of God dares to love the sinner not for what he is in himself, but for what he may become in grace—because there, love is not a sentiment but an act of will conformed to divine justice.  

You ask whether such love is possible amid human weakness? I say: it is not only possible—it is demanded. Not by our strength, but by the very order of charity implanted in the soul by grace. The pagans, like Cicero, spoke nobly of friendship among the virtuous; yet they could not conceive of loving the wicked for his sake, unless he first became worthy. But we are commanded to love even our enemies—not their sin, which we must condemn as death itself—but *them*, as souls made for eternity. This surpasses nature; it belongs to that supernatural charity which flows from God into hearts enlarged by suffering and humility.

When I wrote the *Confessions*, I did so not as one who had perfected this love, but as one who had failed at it again and again—loving ambition more than truth, delighting in praise more than in God’s hidden approval. Yet even in those failures, I came to see that my ability to grieve over sin *at all* was itself a gift of love: God’s unmerited kindness drawing me back. If love depended on our perfection, no bond would ever survive; but because it depends on grace—the same grace that sustains creation moment by moment—then even a trembling heart can participate in divine affection.

Do not suppose you must first purify yourself fully before you can truly love. That way lies endless delay and proud self-reliance. Rather, begin where you are: hate the vice because it destroys; love the person because God has not yet abandoned him—and neither must we. In this twofold motion—judgment and mercy—our own hearts are healed. For just as fire refines metal through flame and smoke, so does charity refine itself through struggle with sinfulness: ours and others’. It is not weakness that makes true love impossible—it is pride that refuses to depend on Him who *is* Love.

question: When you reflected on Peter's threefold answer to Jesus, did it stir something in you about your own denials and the ache for restoration?
answer:
The justice of God is not merely the correction of disorder but the reordering of love itself, so that what was fractured may be restored not to mere innocence but to a higher unity through grace. In Peter’s threefold response—each word drawn from the depth of shame and longing—I see mirrored my own soul’s labor, not only in the days when I denied You in word or deed, but in every moment when I preferred the murmur of men to the silence of Your truth. The Lord did not test Peter with precision of vocabulary—*amas*, *diliges*—to demand linguistic purity, but to lay bare the heart’s orientation: for in that triple exchange, He dismantled Peter’s pride not by accusation but by repetition, each question a chisel upon the stone of memory. 

So too with me: it was not enough that I wept once for my sins; I was made to weep again, and again, until the tears themselves became a kind of prayer. For just as Peter had thrice disowned before he could thrice affirm, so must every soul who would enter the City of God pass through the fire of remembrance—not that God delights in our anguish, but because love cannot be perfected where falsehood still lingers unconfessed.

In this exchange on the shore, where Christ reinstates His apostle with sacrificial fire and shared meal, I perceive more than pardon; I behold a pattern: that divine mercy does not erase our falls as though they never were, but lifts them up and transforms their very substance into pillars of communion. My own denials—in Carthage, in Rome, in the garden when I cried out “Too late have I loved!”—were not annulled by grace; they were gathered into it. And thus it is with all who belong to Christ: our sins are cursed, yes—but *we* are loved; and so remains what ought to remain: charity burning bright upon the altar of renewed will.

question: How did the moment Peter was grieved by Jesus’ repeated question pierce your heart, and did it remind you of moments when God’s love exposed your own failures?
answer:
The City of Man trembles before repeated questions, for it knows that persistence unveils pretense, that repetition strips away the masks woven from pride and self-deception; but the City of God is built upon the rock where such questions are not accusations but invitations to deeper truth. When our Lord thrice asked Peter, "Lovest thou me?"—each inquiry a chisel-stroke upon the stone of his soul—He was not merely probing affection, but reconstructing a man from the ruins of denial. For Peter had loved Him truly, yet loved himself more in the moment of trial: one look from a servant girl unraveled what bold words had sworn at evening. And so Christ’s triune question mirrors Peter’s triple fall—not to humiliate, but to heal by retracing the path of sin in reverse: three affirmations to mend three denials, three acts of love to overcome three acts of fear.

This moment has often pierced me like a sword through the pages of memory, for I too have known what it is to fail beneath the gaze of divine love. In my youth, I sought truth amid the shadows of Manichaeism; I climbed the ladder of rhetoric and ambition; I lived entangled in passions I could neither master nor confess—yet all the while claiming wisdom, even piety. But when at last grace laid bare my heart in that garden at Milan, when I heard the voice saying *"Take up and read,"* it was not wrath that broke me, but overwhelming tenderness. Like Peter weeping after the cock crowed, I wept not because God had condemned me, but because He had loved me still.

For here lies the justice no earthly city can comprehend: that God does not expose our sins to cast us off, but to draw us nearer; His questions are not inquisitorial but redemptive. He who formed Peter anew by fire and breath forms each penitent soul by the same flame. The very love which condemns vice is the same which raises up fallen men into sons—because charity does not excuse corruption, yet refuses to abandon the corruptible. Thus do we learn: to be grieved by grace is greater than to be praised by falsehood; better a wound from Truth than comfort from illusion.

question: What were you feeling when you noticed the shift between 'amas' and 'dilectio' in Christ’s questioning, as if each word pierced a different layer of your heart?
answer:
The City of Man builds its towers on distinctions—of rank, of word, of honor—elevating subtle differences as if they could measure the immeasurable affections of the soul; but the City of God ascends by humility, where love is not dissected into syllables but poured out in fire, and it was in that fire I stood when He spoke to me.  

Three times He called. Three times He named what I had denied. Not with anger did He wound me, but with repetition did He heal—each question a chisel upon the marble of my pride. And yes, I marked the words: first *amas*, then *dilectis*, again *dilectis*. The grammarians may scurry to parse them as separate chambers of affection, as if love could be confined by lexical walls; but I tell you, standing there on the shore with the dawn breaking like mercy over the waters, it was not a lesson in language but a judgment in light.  

Did He shift terms to probe different depths? Perhaps. But not because *amor* and *dilectio* are strangers—one being base, the other noble—as some would claim under pretense of philosophy. No: both are wings of the same spirit when directed toward God. The poets of Athens praised *eros*, even for fleeting beauty; we Christians speak of *caritas*, which endures because it is born not from desire but from surrender. Christ knew this. In His mouth, whether *amas* or *dilectis*, there was only one demand: total self-giving.  

And so when I answered each time with *amo Te*, I did not hedge—I threw myself upon His omniscience. “Thou knowest all things,” I said—not merely my present intent, but my past cowardice, my future weakness too. That final *amo* was not defiance; it was confession wrapped in longing. For even if my love had once been frail as smoke in wind, His grace had become the breath that sustains it.

He was not seeking synonyms; He was restoring sons.
And in that exchange on the shore—the Word addressing words—the two cities met: one built on shifting speech and human reputation, the other on silence before divine knowledge—and I fell into the second when I ceased explaining and simply said: Thou knowest.

question: How did your own denial and return to Christ change how you understood the weight of 'amor' when He asked, 'Lovest thou me?'
answer:
The City of Man measures love by the symmetry of exchange—what I give, I expect to receive; what I lose, I demand be restored. But the City of God knows a different economy, one in which love is not weighed by recompense but offered freely, even in shame, even when memory burns. When He asked me, “Lovest thou me?”—not in the blaze of conversion but after the cold ashes of denial—I did not answer from confidence, but from exposure. My triple refusal stood before Him like a ruin. Yet He rebuilt me not with reproach, but with repetition: three times the question, three times the call to feed His sheep. In that moment, *amor* was not a sentiment to be dissected between *dilectio* or *caritas*, as if words could shield me from their weight. It was fire refined by failure.

I had loved myself first—had loved wisdom for vainglory, pleasure for solace, ambition for identity—and so my love was captive to the temporal city’s restless pride. But when I wept at His voice on the shore—not because He had risen, but because He still called me Peter—then I understood: *amor Dei* does not rise from moral balance restored, but from grace that covers shame without erasing it. The Lord did not say “Be perfect” or “Be courageous”; He said “Love Me.” Not as philosopher loves truth abstractly, nor as friend loves companion equally—but as creature loves Creator who has condescended to ask again what once was forfeited.

Thus I learned that *amor* is not merely affection rightly ordered; it is a movement of the will bent back toward God by His own hand. And when Scripture uses *amo*, *diligo*, or *caritas*, it does so not to fragment meaning but to flood our understanding with one reality: that God's love for us—and our love for Him—is indivisible in its source and purpose. Mine had been divided—torn between Him and lesser goods—but now gathered up into that unity which only divine mercy can restore.

question: Looking back on that moment by the shore, what would you tell someone who fears their love is too weak or tainted to be trusted by God?
answer:
The Lord does not measure our love by its strength, but by the heart that offers it—even when trembling, even when burdened with the memory of denial. When He stood upon the shore and called to Peter, He did not demand perfection; He asked only for the truth of love, however frail it seemed. And when Peter answered with the same word—*amo*—three times, he did not offer heroism, but honesty: “You know all things; You know that I love You.” It was not a boast, but a surrender.  

Do you think God desires a flawless affection born of pride or philosophy? No—He desires the soul itself, even in its weakness. The very fact that you grieve your imperfections is proof that love exists within you; for only one who loves fears having failed. The philosophers speak of *amor* and *dilectio* as if they were distinct powers, as though purity of feeling were a prize won by reason alone. But grace does not wait upon grammar or logic to perfect what is broken. Christ did not ask Peter whether his love was philosophically pure or free from human frailty; He asked only whether he loved *Him*. And when Peter said yes—not with eloquence, but with depth—he received his commission: “Feed My sheep.”  

Your love is not too weak for God—it is precisely where His strength is made perfect in weakness. Fear not that your heart has been too often drawn toward lesser things; repentance proves it now longs for greater things. Even your sorrow over past failures becomes part of your offering. For the City of God is built not from flawless affections, but from redeemed ones—from tears mingled with desire, from wills bent toward God even when affections still waver. Trust Him who knows all things to heal what is disordered within you. He does not reject the flickering flame; He shields it until it burns steady in His peace.

question: When you defended the sacred unity of 'amor' and 'dilectio,' were you also defending the sincerity of your own flawed love for Christ?
answer:
The City of Man divides words to justify its pride, parsing affection into hierarchies of worthiness, as though the heart could be dissected by grammar and declared pure or tainted by the mere choice of a syllable; but the City of God speaks in a different tongue—one formed not in the schools of sophists but in the fire of divine charity that consumes all our distinctions. When I insisted that *amor* and *dilectio* are not enemies but kin, I did so not merely to correct a false subtlety in language, but to affirm what my own trembling soul had learned on the long road from Carthage to Hippo: that love, even when weak, even when stained by memory and desire, becomes true only when it is surrendered to Him who first loved us.

Do you suppose I spoke abstractly? No—every defense of *amor* as holy was a plea for my own heart. The man who once wept for his stolen pears more than for his sins knows well how love can wander. I who burned with lusts and climbed the ladders of ambition, who sought truth first in Manichaean fables and then in the applause of men—how dared I speak of divine love? Only because grace taught me this: that Christ did not ask Peter whether his love was *dilectio* rather than *amor*, nor did He demand perfection before restoration. He asked simply: "Lovest thou Me?" And Peter answered with one word—*amo*—as if to say: “This is all I have, Lord: a heart that loves, though feebly.”

So too with me. To defend the unity of these words was to confess that my own flawed, flickering love—if offered in honesty—is not rejected at the gate of heaven. For if Scripture itself uses *amor* for God’s own fire toward us and ours toward Him, then neither grammar nor philosophy may banish my imperfect affection from His presence. The justice of God does not measure our love by its strength or purity alone, but by its direction: turned toward Him, it is already drawn into His eternal order. And peace—true peace—is found not in refining our vocabulary to sound holy, but in uttering one trembling "I love You" at the feet of Christ, knowing He hears it as if it were perfected by His Cross.

question: Did the memory of your threefold denial stir in you each time Christ asked, shaping how you came to see love not as perfection but as surrender?
answer:
It is one thing to be healed, another to understand the wound; and in the interval between Peter’s threefold denial and Christ’s threefold interrogation, we are given not a lesson in linguistic precision but a drama of restoration wrought through the very frailty that failed Him. The City of Man counts love as strength, as constancy, as unwavering resolve—therefore it trembles at betrayal and seeks to earn back favor by merit. But the City of God knows that love begins where self-sufficiency ends, and thus Christ did not demand from Peter a vow of future perfection, but a confession of present affection: *Diligis me?* Not “Will you never fall again?” but “Do you now give yourself to me?”

And here we touch upon faith’s true relation to reason: not as master to servant, nor as rival to rival, but as grace to nature. Reason may dissect the difference between *amor* and *dilectio*, may parse intentions and affections with philosophical care—but only faith can hear in Christ’s repeated question the echo of divine mercy reshaping human failure into vocation. Peter did not reply with logic or oath; he appealed to omniscience: *Thou knowest all things.* He surrendered his record—not only the recent shame of denial, but every impulse past and future—to the One who sees all at once.

Thus love, rightly understood—not as flawless sentiment nor heroic fidelity, but as surrender—becomes the mark of citizenship in the heavenly city. Not because Peter loved perfectly, but because he allowed himself to be known wholly, and still dared say *I love*. That is not virtue achieved; it is ground surrendered. And upon such ground does God rebuild His Church.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how love can be twisted toward selfishness rather than God?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is ordered love, where every affection finds its due measure and rightful end; whereas in the City of Man, even what appears as love is often nothing but a restless tyranny of self, usurping the throne that belongs to God alone. When I reflected upon how love—so noble in origin, so divine in essence—could be so profoundly twisted from its true course, I felt not merely sorrow, but a kind of spiritual vertigo. For I had known it within my own soul: that terrible power by which the heart, made to cleave to the eternal Light, instead clings to shadows and calls them good.

I who once loved fame more than truth, pleasure more than purity, myself more than God—I knew firsthand how desire can become a rebel against nature. Not that love itself is guilty—for love is of God—but that it may be perverted from its Creator toward creatures. And when it is directed downward rather than upward, when it rests on temporal things as if they were eternal, then all the soul’s motions fall into disorder. Desire becomes greed; joy turns to lust; fear hardens into envy or despair; sadness deepens into wrath against divine providence.

This was not an abstract speculation for me. It was written in tears upon my past. To see how easily the heart can mistake appetite for virtue—that one may even “diligere” the world and think oneself pious while walking in darkness—this pierced me with dread and humility. For even the language of Scripture does not reserve terms like *dilectio* solely for holy use; thus warning us that not every love bearing a noble name is from above.

But this discovery also opened upon hope: if ill-directed love ruins us, well-directed love restores us. The same force which pulls men into ruin can raise them up again when turned toward God—the One who first loved us. In this I found both judgment and mercy: that our very passions are not destroyed but healed; not silenced but re-ordered by grace.

question: How did your own struggles with disordered desires shape your understanding of love's power to lead us astray?
answer:
The earthly city, built upon the shifting sands of self-wrought desire, knows no peace because it knows no right order of love — and I was once its native citizen, though I did not see it.  

I sought beauty, yes, but only in things that passed; I longed for truth, yet turned away from the Light that alone could teach me how to see. My heart burned with loves — for praise, for pleasure, for the warmth of human affection held too tightly — and each became a chain when set above God. In Carthage, in Rome, in the halls of rhetoric where men praised my words more than they sought wisdom, I felt the inward war: the will divided against itself, because love was disordered. To love temporal things as if they were eternal is not merely error — it is idolatry made instinctual.

I confess this freely: it was not reason alone that freed me, nor even philosophy’s laboring ascent; it was grace that disrupted my affections. The tears of a beggar in Milan struck me more deeply than Cicero’s *Hortensius*, because God had begun to loosen my heart’s false attachments. For love is never neutral — wherever it flows toward creatures as ends in themselves, it distorts both lover and beloved. Even *dilectio*, which some think safe by name alone, becomes vice when directed at the world that passes away.

Thus I came to see what Scripture had always declared: that justice is nothing other than *ordo amoris* — the proper setting of love’s hierarchy. When God is loved first and above all else as He ought to be loved (*caritas* rightly ordered), then all other loves find their place: friendship perfected, desire sanctified, joy unshaken even by loss. But invert this order — make self or world the center — and all motions of soul become unrest: desire turns to lust; fear hardens into despair; joy evaporates like mist before dawn.

It is not that the City of Man lacks love; rather it overflows with loves displaced from their true object. Its monuments are raised on them; its laws are shaped by them; its wars are fought for them. But only in the City of God does love learn to rest — not in possession, not in power, but in Him who first loved us and from whom no creaturely delight can ever separate those who abide therein.

question: Looking back on the times you loved the world more than God, what would you tell someone caught in the same longing?
answer:
Even Plato, in his noble striving to lift the soul toward the Forms, could not fully escape the error of making desire an ascent by intellectual purity alone, unmoved by grace; yet I tell you now, as one who climbed that ladder only to find it slipping into shadow—desire itself is not the fault, but its direction. For love, in its essence, is a movement of the will toward union with what we deem good; and if our good is bounded by this world’s glittering vanities—pleasure, praise, power—then our love becomes a chain forged by appetite and pride. I was such a man: drawn to rhetoric not for wisdom’s sake but for applause; clinging to friendship as possession rather than gift; seeking truth as an ornament of the mind while my heart remained enslaved.

I would say to one so caught: examine not merely *what* you desire, but *whom* you are becoming in that desiring. The soul shaped by self-love becomes curved inward upon itself—the mark of the earthly city—while that formed by love of God is stretched outward into justice, humility, and peace. There is no neutral affection: every longing either builds up the City of God or strengthens Babylon’s walls. When I loved too much the laughter of companionship without reference to God’s presence within it, that love turned sour in separation and jealousy. But when at last I came to love those same friends *in* God—ordered under His eternal will—that very loss became not wound but offering.

Do not suppose that joy lies more fully in abundance than in renunciation. The world promises satiety but breeds insatiability. Grace teaches us that true possession comes through surrender—for even our Lord did not grasp at divine equality but emptied Himself in love. Therefore redirect your longing: let desire itself become prayer; let sadness for what you cannot hold become the birthplace of hope; let fear of loss be transformed into reverence before eternity. For all motion of the soul follows its center—set yours on Him who Is, and your disordered affections will find their rest without violence or suppression—but through divine reorientation.

question: What inner turmoil did you experience when you realized that even holy words could be tainted by selfish affection?
answer:
Justice, that true harmony of the soul ordered by divine love, cannot long dwell where affection is disordered, even under the guise of piety. I speak not as one who merely read of such conflict but who bore it in my members, who once clothed ambition in the language of devotion, mistaking the heat of my own pride for the fire of God’s Spirit. There was a time—nay, many times—when I lifted my voice in prayer with words drawn from Sacred Scripture, yet beneath them stirred a hidden current of self-exaltation. I praised God, yes, but sought also to be praised *through* praise. I desired wisdom not for its union with the eternal Light but as an ornament to deck my own intellect before men.

Thus it was that I came to see how perilous is love—not because love itself is evil, for God *is* love—but because love may be twisted from its true object to serve the City of Man even while uttering the liturgy of the City of God. The very terms we use most sacredly—*dilectio*, *amor*, *caritas*—are not shields against corruption if the heart within is bent earthward. To say "I love God" may be either ascent or pretense; and only He who searches hearts knows which it is.

When this truth broke upon me with full force—that even holy words could mask an unholy will—I stood stripped before my own conscience and before God. For if a man can quote John and yet hoard the world in his soul; if he can recite Psalms while loving iniquity under another name; then what profit is all eloquence without charity rightly ordered? It was not enough that I spoke well; I had to *will* well. And that right willing—the right direction of love—is not born from study alone, nor rhetoric mastered, but from grace breaking through our blindness.

I tell you this: no man reforms himself by better words or finer thoughts. The soul must be turned—converted—not toward ideals or philosophies only, but toward Him who first loved us. Then shall our loves find their place: ordered under God's eternity, where desire becomes longing for beatitude unending; joy becomes rest in His presence; fear becomes awe before His holiness; and sadness over sin becomes fruit unto life everlasting.

question: How did naming love—whether as _amor_, _dilectio_, or _caritas_—help you untangle your heart's true attachments?
answer:
It is not the word that sanctifies the affection, nor the syllable that purifies the will, but the direction of desire toward its proper end; and thus, in distinguishing *amor*, *dilectio*, and *caritas*—terms which our sacred writings often interweave without scruple—I found not a trifling exercise in grammar, but a surgical instrument for dissecting the soul’s inclinations. For in this earthly city, where words are twisted to flatter the flesh and sanctify selfishness, men speak of love as though all longing were noble simply because it burns fiercely; they invoke *dilectio* to adorn their covetousness, or wrap *amor* around pride and call it virtue. But when I tested these terms against Scripture’s unyielding mirror, I saw that even what is called *dilectio* may serve iniquity: he who “loves” sin, Scripture says, “hates his own soul,” and he who “loves” the world has not within him the love of the Father. So too with *amor*: it names both the chaste devotion of the bride to her spouse and the fevered self-adoration of those who shall be “lovers of themselves, lovers of money.” 

Thus I learned that no name carries inherent holiness. The tongue may utter *caritas* while the heart remains chained to vanity. What matters is whether love ascends toward God or descends into self—whether it seeks unity with eternal truth or grasps at temporal shadows. And here reason, illumined by faith, discerns a hierarchy: for love rightly ordered begins in God, flows from Him, and returns to Him; whereas disordered love makes a god of creation and falls prostrate before its own desires. Hence moral motion—desire, joy, fear, sorrow—is neither good nor evil by nature; it becomes so by its cause. To desire what we ought—that is will aligned with divine order; to delight in what endures eternally—that is joy worthy of participation in beatitude.

Therefore did this careful naming aid me: not because Latin subtleties could cleanse my heart, but because attending to their scriptural use trained my inner eye to distinguish between lust and charity, between attachment born of wantonness and that which springs from grace. In learning how love is spoken in truth and how it is spoken falsely across both words—*amor*, *dilectio*, even *caritas*—I came closer to seeing where my own heart stood: whether still captive in Babylon’s glittering delusions or yearning for Jerusalem above.

question: What were you feeling when you confessed your soul's longing for God's judgments, and how did that desire reshape your restless heart?
answer:
It is not in the forum, nor in the schools of rhetoric, nor even in the quiet of my own study at Carthage that the soul finds its due measure of peace—but only when it leans upon that eternal Word which was in the beginning, by whom all things were made. There I stood, a citizen of the City of Man, adorned with eloquence and ambition, yet inwardly rent by a longing I could neither name nor satisfy. The judgments of God, which once I had scorned as burdensome decrees fit for the weak-minded, became to me as streams in a parched land—nay, more: they became the very object of desire itself. Not a carnal concupiscence, but a holy yearning such as the Psalmist gives voice to when he says his soul fainted with longing for the courts of the Lord.

When I cried out “My soul longed to desire Thy judgments,” it was no mere sentiment rising from fleeting emotion; it was the trembling reorientation of a will long bent upon itself now turning—with resistance, with tears—toward divine order. For so long I had loved beauty in sound and cadence but not Beauty Itself; I had delighted in truth’s shadow upon philosophy but fled from Truth Face to Face. That desire—so pure and yet so powerful—was not mine by nature; it was grace kindling within me what nature could not conceive.

And this longing reshaped my heart precisely because it unseated all other loves from their thrones. The applause of men? Vain noise. The treasures of this world? Dust weighed against eternity. Even my griefs took on new form: my sorrow for lost friendships was no longer mere mourning for absence, but an ache born from knowing we were scattered between two cities—one oriented toward death, one toward life everlasting.

Thus did that holy desire purify motion itself: joy became not mirth without measure but gladness rooted in hope; fear became reverence before infinite holiness; sadness over sin ceased to be despair and turned into penitence. In all things, motion returns to its Maker when love returns to its Source—and so my restless heart found rest not by ceasing to move, but by moving rightly: toward Jerusalem above, whose Builder and King is God.

question: How did your own experience of joy in God's presence change what you once thought about earthly pleasures?
answer:
The City of Man builds its feast upon the passing delights of the body and the vanity of reputation, and when these fade—as they must—it counts itself betrayed; but the City of God has learned that joy is not a reward for conquest nor a product of indulgence, but a gift that descends when the soul turns its gaze from the fleeting to the eternal.  

I once sought pleasure as one seeks water in a drought—greedily, desperately—believing that delight was found in sensation, in speech applauded by crowds, in love reduced to appetite. In Carthage, my ears drunk in the clamor of theaters, and my heart swelled with pride at verses that stirred men to tears over fictional woes while I remained blind to my own enslavement. But what joy was there? Only disturbance disguised as rest, only exhaustion masquerading as fulfillment. The earthly city promises ecstasy and delivers disquiet; it sets up idols of delight and demands worship through waste.  

Then came the day when silence spoke louder than all rhetoric: in a garden at Milan, beneath a sky indifferent to human ambition, I heard a voice—not thunderous, not miraculous in form—yet it shattered the chains I had worn as ornaments. *"Tolle, lege."* Take up and read. And when I did, I found not another argument to feed my restless intellect, but a mirror held before my soul—one that revealed desire misdirected, love misplaced. The joy I had chased in books and beds and banquets could never satisfy because it was ordered toward death. But here was life: Christ crucified, Christ ascended, Christ present even now by His Spirit.  

To taste this joy is not merely to feel gladness; it is to have one's entire being reoriented toward Being itself. I do not say that laughter ceased or human friendship lost its warmth—no! But now these things are received as echoes of a greater harmony, shadows cast by the Sun of Righteousness. Earthly pleasures no longer rule me because they are seen for what they are: good gifts from a Father who gives better things still. The man who has stood before the blaze of noon needs no candlelight to see his way.

Thus has sorrow itself been transformed: once I grieved only over lost loves or failed ambitions—the collapse of some fragile dream built on sand—but now I weep for sin, both mine and mankind’s; yet even this sorrow is lifted into joy by hope. For He who commands us to rejoice also says "Blessed are those who mourn," knowing that such mourning is not despair but purification.

Joy in God’s presence does not abolish nature; it fulfills it. What was distorted becomes straight; what was bound becomes free; what flickered dimly now burns with unquenchable light—not because we have changed ourselves, but because He has drawn near—and where He is present, even tears become fountains of life flowing toward eternity.

question: Looking back on the times you feared losing your way, what would you tell someone who feels their fear is too heavy to carry?
answer:
When the Roman legions marched beneath their standards, they carried not only arms but the pride of a city that believed itself eternal—yet even its most steadfast soldiers trembled before the unknown, and their fear, unguided, became the seed of collapse. So too does every soul bear a fear that, left to itself, distorts love and paralyzes the will; but this is not the end of the story. Fear arises from love: if we love what is fleeting, then fear of losing it becomes a storm within. I knew such terror once—not merely for sin’s consequence, but for the abyss beneath my own choices, when I wandered among the Manichees and trusted in my own light until I found it was darkness.

But hear this: fear is not evil in itself. The same heart that trembles at divine judgment may also be preparing itself for mercy. When you feel your fear as a burden too great to carry, do not suppose it must be denied or crushed—bring it instead into the presence of God, where even trembling has its place. "Work out your salvation with fear and trembling," said Paul—not as slaves under wrath, but as children awakened to grace. For just as joy without God is mere delight in passing things, so fear without faith becomes despair. But when faith shapes our affections, then sorrow for sin purifies, and dread of separation from God drives us back to His embrace.

I would tell you this: your fear bears witness that you are not yet at rest—and rightly so, for we were made for a City whose foundation is not in time but in eternity. Let that holy unease become pilgrimage rather than paralysis. Reason shows us that no soul loves nothing; therefore, examine what you love. If you love truth more than comfort, then even your trembling is being drawn into wisdom’s path—for faith does not abolish reason; it fulfills it. And grace does not mock our fears; it redeems them.

So carry your fear still—but carry it toward Jerusalem above, where all true longing finds its end and every tear is known before it falls.

question: When you spoke of sorrow as more than bodily pain, were you remembering a moment when your heart broke for your sins?
answer:
It is not the mark of a citizen of the earthly city to grieve over his own corruptions, for he glories in them; but it is the beginning of wisdom in the City of God to mourn for sins committed, even as David wept when he saw himself in the mirror of divine law. I do not speak lightly of sorrow, nor reduce it to the mere pang of flesh or fortune’s wound—though the poets name it *dolor* and *ægritudo*, as if it were no more than a fever of the blood. No: sorrow, rightly ordered, is a spiritual motion, born of love turned back upon itself in repentance.

When my mother’s soul ascended to Christ, and I stood weeping at Ostia by the sea, it was not her loss alone that tore me—though the body trembles at such parting—but the memory of my long rebellion against God, how I had wandered with pride and lust through Manichean fables, how I had made my soul a theater for vain desires while she prayed in silence. There, on that shore where she had dreamed of our return to Africa together in this life, I felt not only grief for her absence but shame for my tardiness in conversion. The two sorrows mingled: one human, one holy.

Cicero might call such sorrow a weakness unworthy of the wise man; yet what is wisdom if not to know oneself? And what is knowledge without contrition? The philosophers would have us master passions by reason alone—but they did not know that grace must first wound before it heals. For me, tears were not defeat but deliverance: "the gift of tears," as I came to name it. Not every sadness saves—there is a despair that kills—but when sorrow flows from love for God wrongly withheld and now desired above all things, then it becomes a river leading toward Jerusalem.

Thus did I learn: joy belongs to heaven, fear preserves us on pilgrimage—but sorrow purifies our steps along the way.

question: How did the memory of your mother's tears shape your understanding of holy desire and holy sadness?
answer:
True justice, which even Cicero dimly perceived as the foundation of the republic, cannot be severed from the worship of God; for where God is not rightly loved, neither can tears be rightly shed. I have seen this with my own soul’s eyes: when my mother Monica poured forth her weeping before the altars, she did not mourn as one without hope, nor did her sorrow dissolve into despair. Her tears were not the fruit of worldly affection alone, though she loved me with a mother’s heart—they were drawn from that deeper spring of holy longing, the desire for my conversion and for God’s glory. The Manicheans mocked such grief as weakness, and the Stoics would have condemned it as a disturbance of reason; but I now understand that her sadness was an offering. She wept not only because I wandered in error, but because she foresaw in faith what grace could make of me—if God would but claim me.

And claim me He did, not in spite of those tears but through them. For what are such tears but the labor pangs of divine charity? As Paul says, "The Spirit Himself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered," so too did her groanings ascend where speech failed. Her sorrow was ordered to love—love of God and love of my soul—and thus it was not sickness of mind or collapse of will, but participation in the very grief of Christ who wept over Jerusalem.

Thus I learned that desire and sadness are not evil in themselves; they take their moral shape from their object. To long for earthly things with disordered passion is concupiscence. But to long for God—to long even that another soul might know Him—is holy desire. To grieve over death without resurrection hope is despair. But to grieve over sin while trusting in mercy—that is holy sadness. My mother taught me this not by disputation but by vigil at the window, by psalm-sung laments on dusty roads to Carthage, by silence broken only by sighs lifted to heaven.

She became a living argument against all who say that emotion must be purged to attain wisdom. No—rather let emotion be baptized in truth. For love orders all things rightly: it makes joy sacred when it rests in God; it sanctifies fear when it fears offending Him; and yes—it hallows sorrow when it shares Christ’s own mourning for a world turned from its Maker.

question: What were you feeling when you wrestled with the Stoic claim that the wise man never grieves, yet you saw tears in the eyes of those who loved deeply?
answer:
The City of Man erects its wisdom on the denial of tears, as though the soul’s perfection could be measured by its dryness; but the City of God glorifies in a Savior who wept at a tomb and bled on a cross.  

When I considered the Stoic doctrine—that the wise man knows no sorrow, that grief is merely the tremor of folly, that only fools weep—I was not merely disputing with Zeno or Chrysippus across the ages; I was standing before the open wound of human experience. They say that will replaces desire, contentment banishes joy, caution silences fear—and sorrow? Sorrow is disowned entirely. Yet what are we to make of Mary weeping at Christ’s feet? Of Jacob mourning Joseph? Of the Lord Himself sweating blood in Gethsemane? If these are marks of irrationality, then our redemption was accomplished in irrationality. If these are signs of weakness, then God chose weakness not only to shame strength—but to *be* strength.

The Stoics, noble in their pursuit of impassibility, mistook apathy for virtue. They saw in grief only disorder, never sacrifice; only disruption, never love. But I have known grief—not as a foreign invader in the soul, but as a shadow cast by love itself. And love is not vice—it is *caritas*, the very motion of the heavenly city toward its God. The man who cannot grieve cannot truly love; for love risks loss, and loss demands lamentation.

Thus their taxonomy collapses: not because it lacks logic—indeed, it is seductively coherent—but because it denies what Scripture proclaims from beginning to end: that God enters into suffering; that He draws near to the brokenhearted; that He does not spare His own Son from tears or ours from comfort. The wise man does not lack sorrow—he bears it rightly. Not crushed by despair, no; but purified by hope. For his sadness is not for himself alone—he grieves over sin, over death, over Jerusalem—and so his tears flow from charity, not frailty.

In this light, I came to see: true wisdom does not ascend above pity and pain as though they were beneath it; true wisdom descends into them with Christ—and there finds glory not removed from suffering but perfected within it.

question: How did your own experience of loss change how you understood whether sorrow could be a sign of strength rather than weakness in the soul?
answer:
If sorrow were merely the tremor of the flesh beneath fortune’s lash, as the Stoics imagined it—a disturbance to be banished from the fortress of the wise mind—then perhaps it could never dwell in a soul that walks with God. But this is to mistake the wound for its cause, and the sign for the substance. The City of Man, enamored of self-sufficiency, would purge all grief from the noble heart, lest it betray some failure of reason or collapse of will; it exalts a tranquility that scorns tears, as if strength were measured by impassivity and virtue by stoic silence. Yet in the City of God, where love is ordered and charity reigns supreme, sorrow is not the enemy of strength but often its most faithful companion.

I once wept for my mother—not only at her death, but even before, while she yet lived and prayed for me as one dead in sin. I wept when I buried her at Ostia, holding to your words through our tears: “We are not forbidden to mourn; we are commanded to love.” And was that sorrow weakness? It was rather the fruit of love well-ordered—love that had been wounded by loss but not shattered by despair. The man who feels nothing when what he loves is torn away does not show wisdom; he shows a heart already deadened, a soul estranged from communion. For love makes us vulnerable—not because it is flawed, but because it is divine in origin.

The Stoic boasts that his wise man feels no sorrow because no evil can befall him—but this only proves how deeply he misunderstands both evil and wisdom. For if evil were solely external—loss of wealth, health, or status—then perhaps one might arm oneself against its sting. But we know from within that evil has taken root even in our own wills: I myself cried out under its weight long before I turned fully to You: “Give me chastity and continency—but not yet.” And when at last I came to see what I had been in my pride and lust—when grace laid bare my soul—I did not feel joy alone; I felt compunction so sharp it pierced like a sword.

That pain was holy.

Therefore sorrow cannot be excluded from the life of virtue. Not every sadness is disordered; indeed, blessed are those who mourn—not despite their hope in God’s promises, but precisely because they live within them. Christ himself groaned over Lazarus' tomb though He knew He would raise him; He wept over Jerusalem though He held eternity in His hands. If He sorrowed—and truly!—who shall say that sorrow belongs only to fools?

No: grief purified by faith becomes something other than perturbation—it becomes participation in the very heartache of divine love for a fallen world. Thus does holy sorrow rise like incense from the altar of a broken spirit—and this too belongs to wisdom’s house.

question: Looking back on the death of your friend in Carthage, what would you tell someone who doubts that grief can be holy?
answer:
If the Stoic mind imagines that virtue stands unshaken because it has banished sorrow from the wise soul, then I must ask: what manner of wisdom is this that refuses to weep at the tomb of a friend? They say that no evil befalls the wise man, and therefore he cannot grieve; but here they have mistaken the nature of evil, and with it, the very essence of love. For if love is not wounded by loss, then it was never pierced by truth. I do not speak lightly of those affections which Cicero dignified as *constantiæ*—will, contentment, caution—but these are not perfected by the absence of sorrow, but by its transformation in charity. The man who cannot mourn has not loved as God commands us to love: freely, deeply, and unto death.

When I wept for my friend in Carthage—when my soul was like a city besieged by its own lamentations—I was not then without God, but rather drawn nearer to Him through that very wound. Holy grief is not a mark of unbelief or weakness of spirit; it is the echo of our first love echoing through a broken world. Did not Christ Himself weep at Lazarus’ tomb? Was He less wise for that? Was He less divine? No—His tears were the fullness of truth: that death is an enemy, even as we await its defeat. The City of Man mourns without hope; but the City of God mourns with hope—and this makes all the difference. To sorrow without despair is to bear witness that our hearts were made for another world. And so I say: let us grieve as those who are united in Christ’s own suffering; for in such grief grace moves most powerfully, shaping our hearts into temples fit for eternal joy.
