question: What were you carrying in your heart when you questioned whether a soul without sorrow could still be fully human?
answer:
Justice, that true and invisible bond of human community, is not forged in the fire of unfeeling reason, nor in the Stoic denial of inward motion, but in the ordered love that bends toward God and neighbor alike. When I pondered whether a soul without sorrow could be fully human, it was not mere philosophical curiosity that stirred within me, but the lived tremor of a heart shaped by divine compassion and broken by the weight of this world’s disarray. The Stoics may divide the affections with precision, assigning to the wise man only will, contentment, and caution—cleaningse of grief as one might purge a house of smoke—but they have not reckoned with Abraham’s weeping for Isaac, nor David’s lament over his rebellious son, nor our Lord Himself weeping at the tomb of Lazarus. Can He who is Love incarnate sorrow and yet remain perfect? Indeed He can—and thus proves that sorrow is not sin.

I questioned whether a soul untouched by sorrow could love rightly because I had come to see that love itself opens the heart to suffering. To will the good is not enough; to possess it with contentment is not complete; even to exercise caution against evil falls short. For man was made for communion, not isolation; for participation in both grace and grief. If Christ had felt no pain at death’s dominion—if His soul had remained unmoved by Mary’s tears—would His victory over death have been ours? No: it is precisely because He entered into our mourning that we are lifted into His joy.

Thus I saw that the City of Man builds its wisdom on self-sufficiency and impassibility—its gods do not weep—but the City of God bows low in sympathy. There is no true justice without shared suffering; no peace without purification through tears. Sorrow, when ordered by charity, becomes participation in Christ’s own mourning—and so prepares us for beatitude: *Blessed are those who mourn*, He said—not because mourning is divine in itself, but because it belongs to those being drawn into divine life.

question: How did your struggle with the Stoics' perfect wise man shape your longing for a God who weeps with us?
answer:
When Varro, in his elegant fashion, sought to divide the soul’s motions into rational and irrational parts, as though wisdom could be measured by the exclusion of sorrow, he unknowingly revealed the poverty of a philosophy that would perfect man by excising grief. The Stoic wise man—so serene, so untouched by lamentation—became for me not an ideal but a mirror of pride, for he dwells in a city built upon self-sufficiency, where tears are signs of weakness rather than groanings too deep for words. If virtue consists in impassibility, then God Himself is not virtuous by their standard; yet Scripture declares He is grieved by sin, that Christ wept at a tomb, and that the Spirit intercedes with sighs. How then can man, made in His image, attain likeness to God by becoming less capable of sorrow?

I once sought truth among those who exalted reason as sovereign over all affections; but I found no door to mercy in their citadel. The Manichees taught me to despise the body and its passions—yet even there I felt within me contradictions they could not resolve: love that burned with lust, desire entangled with despair. It was only when I came to understand that the City of God embraces suffering as part of its pilgrimage through time—that Abraham’s faith was proved in trembling obedience, Jeremiah’s holiness voiced through lamentation, and Christ Himself "a man of sorrows"—that I saw how deeply the Incarnation reorders our affections.

To say no evil befalls the wise man may comfort one who fears loss; but what then shall we say of martyrdom? Of Job stripped bare? Of the Virgin at the Cross? If sorrow were foreign to sanctity, then Calvary would be a defeat rather than a triumph. But no—the Wisdom of God did not descend to abolish suffering from human life by philosophical fiat; He descended to transfigure it by participation. He wills not our joy apart from pain but through it: “Blessed are those who mourn.” Not because mourning is natural or easily borne—but because such mourning belongs already to the communion of saints.

Thus my longing was never for a god who stands above tears—but for *the* God who dies upon a cross and calls forth weeping from heaven itself. In His wounds I found refuge from all false wisdoms; in His weeping I learned that true strength lies not in detachment but in love so fierce it dares sorrow—and conquers death within it.

question: What were you feeling when you realized the prophet’s words revealed contentment as belonging only to the good, not the wicked?
answer:
It is one thing to name the affections, as the Stoics did with their precise distinctions, and quite another to weigh them in the balance of divine truth and find them wanting. When I encountered that prophetic utterance—“There is no contentment to the wicked, saith the Lord”—it was not merely an exegetical discovery, but a shaking of the soul’s foundations. Cicero had taught me to distinguish between *constantiæ* and *perturbationes*, as if virtue could be reduced to correct emotional taxonomy; yet here was Scripture piercing through all philosophical refinement with a single ray of light: contentment—true, settled repose of the heart—is not a faculty merely disciplined by reason, but a gift born of righteousness, flowing from communion with God.

I felt, then, not triumph over an intellectual puzzle solved, but awe before a moral cosmology laid bare. The wicked may rejoice in their desires fulfilled; they may exult in power, in pleasure, in revenge; but they cannot be *content*, because contentment is not motion toward appetite’s end—it is rest in what is good. It belongs to those whose wills are aligned with divine order, not those who orbit around the self as their own center. In that moment I saw more clearly how all things are disposed according to love: those who love God enjoy even suffering; those who love only themselves cannot enjoy even abundance.

And so I understood why Christ did not say “desire” but “will” when He gave His golden rule: “Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you…” To will is rational, ordered, directed by justice; desire wanders blindfolded through shadows. The one can be conformed to charity; the other must be purified by grace. Thus Scripture does not merely correct our language—it reorients our entire being toward the City where peace reigns because God is all in all.

question: How did wrestling with Cicero’s distinction between affections and passions change your understanding of what it means to will rightly before God?
answer:
When Cicero divides the movements of the soul into *constantiæ* and *perturbationes*, he discerns with acuity a hierarchy within human affections, yet he does not penetrate to their root in the orientation of the will toward its true end. He sees that contentment, wisdom, and caution belong to a higher order than mere rejoicing, fearing, or desiring; but he does not see that this distinction is not merely psychological, nor even moral in the abstract—it is *theological*. The man who is contented does not do so by philosophical discipline alone, but only insofar as his will rests in that Good which alone satisfies: God Himself. For what is contentment if not the soul’s repose in truth? And how can the soul rest where it does not belong?

I once sought such repose in rhetoric, in ambition, in the fleeting consolations of friendship and flesh—desiring endlessly, yet never willing rightly. Desire without direction breeds only turbulence; but will ordered by love ascends to participation in divine providence. When Scripture declares there is “no contentment to the wicked,” it reveals that peace of heart is not a virtue manufactured by reason’s governance over passion, but a gift granted when the will turns from self-love—*amor sui usque ad contemptum Dei*—to *amor Dei usque ad contemptum sui*. The fool may desire luxuriously, may rejoice at vanity, may fear death as if it were his master; but only the wise man *wills*, because only he sees all things under eternity.

Thus I came to understand that right willing before God consists not in suppressing passions as though they were enemies of reason—as if reason itself were sufficient—but in redirecting love to its proper object. From this reordering flows true contentment: not a stilling of waves by Stoic effort, but a calm born of being anchored in Him who Is. To will rightly is therefore not merely to choose well among goods, but to be drawn upward by grace into communion with the Highest Good—so that even our desires are transformed into longing for Jerusalem above.

question: Looking back on your study of Scripture to discern true contentment, what would you tell someone who still confuses joy with desire?
answer:
To distinguish rightly between joy and desire is not merely to refine one’s vocabulary, but to participate in the moral architecture of reality as God has ordered it—a task where reason, illumined by faith, must judge the affections of the soul. Cicero, that careful guardian of Roman virtue, observed that the wise man operates according to will, contentment, and caution—affections he deemed stable and worthy—while the fool is tossed by mere desires, rejoicings, fears, and sorrows, which he called disturbances. There is truth here: the soul rightly governed does not drift upon waves of impulse but stands fast in a well-ordered love. Yet philosophy alone cannot fix the root from which these motions spring; for it knows not that every affection is oriented toward a city—either that which is founded on self-love unto contempt of God, or that which is founded on the love of God unto contempt of self.

Desire, left to itself, is blind—it may reach even for poison if it mistakes pleasure for good. It belongs to our fallen condition to want what flatters the flesh or puffs up pride: luxuries, honors, vengeance. Such desires do not ascend; they drag downward. But joy—true joy—is never severed from will rightly ordered by charity. It does not leap at shadows; it rests in substance. When Scripture says there is “no contentment to the wicked,” it reveals that peace of heart is not a reward of appetite satisfied but a gift proper to communion with God. The wicked may rejoice wildly in their successes; they cannot be *content*, because their end is fragmentation.

And so I learned from Scripture what philosophy could only glimpse: desire aims at possession; joy already possesses its object in love—and when that object is God Himself, joy becomes participation in eternity. Desire asks: *What shall I gain?* Joy declares: *I have found all things in Him.* Therefore let no one say he rejoices in God if he still longs only for earthly consolations as his due. The fool desires happiness; the saint wills holiness—and finds delight therein as a foretaste of vision unending.

Thus faith completes reason: not abolishing natural distinctions among affections, but elevating them into their true form by grace. We do not suppress desire—we reorder it toward its true end—until we will only what we ought to will: goodness itself.

In this reformation of love lies deliverance from slavery to passion—and entry into the peace of the City whose Founder and Light is Christ.

question: What stirred in your heart when you noticed how easily desire could be misaligned—even for things like luxurious banquets—while the will ought to aim higher?
answer:
The citizen of the City of God walks ever in the light of a higher justice, not shaped by appetite but ordered by love—love of God first, and then love for neighbor as oneself; whereas the citizen of the City of Man builds his life on shifting sand, mistaking desire for will and calling pleasure wisdom. It was precisely this confusion that once held me captive long before my eyes were opened to divine truth: I desired banquets not merely of food but of praise, glory, and sensual delight, and called them goods because they stirred my soul. Yet desire unruled by right reason is not will—it is impulse; it is *perturbatio*, not *constantiæ*.  

When I observed how even something as seemingly neutral as desire—for food, for honor, for human affection—could become disordered when detached from the rule of God’s law, it struck me like a flash in the night: here lies the root of all earthly kingdoms’ decay. They are built upon what men *want*, not what they *ought to will*. The pagan moralists saw fragments of this truth—Cicero with his distinction between tranquil affections and turbulent passions—but they lacked the illumination that reveals their source: God Himself, who alone defines what is good because He is goodness.  

I came to understand that contentment (*contentio*, as Scripture implies) belongs only to those whose wills are aligned with divine will. "There is no contentment to the wicked," says the Lord—not because they feel no joy, but because their joy is unquiet, restless, self-consuming. They may feast lavishly, yet remain hungry; they may be praised universally, yet know no peace. For true contentment arises not from fulfillment of desire but from conformity to truth. And truth is personal: Christ Jesus, who said also—and here we touch mystery—"Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you"—not "desire" or "crave," mind you—but "would," meaning *willed* under justice and charity.

Thus did I see how dangerous it is to let language itself blur moral distinctions. If one interprets "would" as mere appetite, then indeed one might justify gluttony or vanity under cover of a Gospel precept! But such an error reveals deeper disorder: a heart accustomed to using others rather than loving them. The man who wishes others to serve him sumptuous meals so he may return them in kind has reduced virtue to barter; he dwells still in Babylon’s courts, where exchange rules over gift.

But in the City of God? There we do not trade luxuries—we hunger together for righteousness. There our wills are healed so that desiring becomes willing only when it ascends toward what is truly good. Grace does not abolish nature but restores it; thus does faith perfect reason: teaching us at last to want nothing more than what God wills—and in that very alignment discover perfect freedom.

question: How did the Gospel’s command to do what you would have others do to you challenge your own desires in daily life?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its cravings, mistakes desire for will and passion for virtue, supposing that to wish intensely is the same as to choose rightly; but the command of the Gospel pierces this confusion with divine precision. When Christ says, “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you,” He does not merely prescribe an ethic of reciprocity but exposes the disordered loves that govern our daily choices. I found in my own life—once given over to ambition, to lust for praise, to the soft tyranny of friendship—that I desired many things I would never will for another: honors unjustly gained, pleasures that enslave, silences born of cowardice rather than peace. This precept forced me into a judgment seat I could not escape: must I then bestow upon my brother the very vanities I clutch for myself? Must I serve him the banquet of my own concupiscence?  

Here reason and faith converge. Cicero distinguished *constantiæ* from *perturbationes*, and rightly so—yet even his noble soul did not see that only love ordered toward God can rectify the will. For if contentment belongs to the just alone—as Scripture declares when it denies such peace to the wicked—then our desires must be purified by charity before they can be made normative. To act toward others only as we wish them to act toward us is not license but crucible: it burns away what is selfish, inordinate, disordered. It compels us to ask not what pleases but what heals; not what flatters but what builds up in truth.  

Thus the command becomes a mirror held before the soul. In Hippo, among clergy and laity alike, I observed men eager to receive deference yet unwilling to give it; longing for forgiveness yet withholding it; craving food beyond necessity while their brethren starved. The Gospel’s simplicity unmasks these contradictions. It does not permit me to desire for another less than God’s justice, even if my flesh clamors for indulgence. And so by this rule—not custom, not appetite, but this law written in love—I was taught gradually to will rather than merely desire; to choose what is good because God is good; and thus to live as a citizen of that City where will and beatitude are one.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how people twist the Golden Rule to justify indulgence rather than love?
answer:
When the soul seeks to measure divine precepts by its own crooked line, reducing the law of love to a symmetry of selfish exchange, it betrays not only a failure of will but a corruption of reason itself—one which even the most refined pagan moralists might stumble over, though they prized proportion and equity in human affairs. Plato taught that justice consists in each part of the soul performing its proper function, and Cicero extolled the natural law that binds men in mutual duty; yet neither fully pierced the veil to see that true reciprocity is not mirrored desire, but ordered love. For if men delight in vanities, in sumptuous tables and carnal revelry, and then demand that their neighbors serve them the same—do they not make of the Golden Rule a covenant of concupiscence?  

I have watched such distortions with grief, not surprise, for I once wandered among them, flattering my vices with philosophical garb. But grace revealed what logic could not: that "whatsoever ye would" is not a license for mutual indulgence, but a test of what one truly loves. The wicked may wish to be feasted, flattered, fornicated with; but he who loves God knows that no man should be used as meat for appetite. To do unto others as you would be done by—this command stands only when the will itself is healed. Otherwise, it becomes an instrument of ruin: two blind men leading each other into the pit, exchanging poisons under the name of kindness.  

Thus Scripture omits "good" not because goodness is absent, but because it is presupposed—the very air in which Christian action breathes. What man *would* must first be purified by truth before it can form the basis of charity. Without this purification, even golden rules turn to leaden chains.

question: How did your own struggles with pleasure reshape your understanding of what it means to truly desire good for others?
answer:
The earthly city, as Varro might have observed in his antiquarian wanderings through Roman custom, builds its fellowship upon mutual gratification—reciprocity not as virtue, but as transaction, a commerce of favors that binds men together in chains of appetite. I once walked those streets willingly, parading my affections like trophies, offering friendship only insofar as it returned to me some pleasure or honor. But the City of God knows another law entirely: there, desire is not the servant of appetite but the echo of charity.

When I lusted after others—not with love but with consumption—I could not truly will their good; how could I? To desire another’s good means to will what God wills for them: holiness, repentance, union with Him. In my youth, had someone asked me to do unto others as I would have them do unto me, I would have invited them to feast on stolen apples and stolen kisses—banquets of vanity. But such desires are not acts of the will ordered toward truth; they are the spasms of disordered love.

It was only when grace pierced me—when I heard within the words “Take up and read”—that I saw how deeply my desires had been corrupted. To wish another well is not to mirror one’s own cravings onto them; it is to sacrifice oneself so that they may rise toward God. The Manichean in me once thought pleasure was knowledge; the Neoplatonist thought ascent was intellectual; but the Christian now knows: true desire for another’s good begins only when we die to self and live in Christ.

Thus, even natural affection must be transformed by grace. For if two sinners agree to fulfill each other’s lusts, they build nothing but a common ruin. But if one forgives another out of love for God—even when forgiveness brings pain—then at last we see that precept fulfilled: not because we exchange pleasures, but because we share in His holiness who desires our good more perfectly than we do ourselves.

question: Looking back on your youth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether desire can ever be purified?
answer:
Justice is not found in the equilibrium of desires, nor in their indulgence, but in their right ordering under the rule of love — a truth neither empire nor philosophy ever fully grasped, though Athens shimmered with its shadow and Rome collapsed from its neglect. You ask whether desire can be purified, as if it were a fire that must be quenched; but I tell you, it must rather be directed — lifted from the mud to the light, from the temporal to the eternal. In my youth, I did not seek God; I sought satisfaction — in rhetoric, in friendship that bordered on idolatry, in lust that left me empty even as it consumed me. I desired to be desired, and mistook pleasure for peace.

But what is desire if not a movement of the soul toward some good? The error lies not in desiring, but in what we deem good. The man who wishes others to serve his appetites perverts the Lord’s command; he confuses *voluntas* with *cupiditas*. He says, “I want this pleasure — therefore let others give it to me,” never pausing to ask whether what he wants is worthy of want. Yet Christ does not say “whatsoever ye lust after,” but “whatsoever ye would.” The word carries reason within it — discernment. A man may hunger for bread and rightly wish another would give it; but shall he hunger for another man’s wife and claim divine sanction? No — because such desire fractures both self and society.

I once believed my passions were too strong for grace to master. Then grace proved stronger than I imagined — not by crushing my will, but by healing it. Purification is not extinction. It is conversion: desire turned upward toward the One who first desired us. The City of Man builds altars to appetite; the City of God builds them to worship. And worship teaches us what we ought truly to desire.

So tell the doubter this: if nature itself groans for fulfillment beyond corruption, how much more does the soul cry out for its true object? Not all desire is base; only that which forgets its source and end in God. Let reason submit to faith — for faith does not destroy reason but completes it — and then you will see: desire can be purified because love has already been revealed.

question: What pain did you carry when you realized that even holy words could be used to mask unholy cravings?
answer:
The City of Man, ever restless in its imitation of divine forms, can twist even the law of love into a justification for concupiscence, while the City of God walks in the truth that charity must be measured not by desire but by right order. When I perceived how easily the commandment—so pure in its origin—could be distorted by those who wished to sanctify their lusts with Scripture, a deep sorrow seized me, not merely for the ignorance of others, but for my own past complicity in such deceptions. For had I not once sought to dress my unruly passions in philosophical garb, or cloak my pride in the language of wisdom? The pain was not sudden, like a wound, but slow and searching, like the dawning light that reveals how far one has wandered from home.

I saw men quote Christ’s words—“All things whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you”—as if every inclination, however disordered, were a warrant from heaven. They feasted on excess and called it reciprocity; they exchanged flatteries and named it righteousness. But what if what is desired is itself corrupt? What if what is willed is contrary to nature and to God? Then to obey such a commandment literally would be to build an altar to Moloch with stones taken from the temple of the Lord. No—what is meant is not “whatever ye desire,” for desire unruled by reason and grace is often rebellion against God’s eternal law.

In this I found confirmation: the Greek text omits “good,” yet goodness is implied precisely because man’s true will—his rational, divinely ordered will—is aligned with justice. But when appetite usurps reason’s throne, what seems willed is not will at all but slavery. Thus does Babylon mistake her chains for ornaments. Yet even here divine providence permits such confusion as a trial—for some to purify them through repentance, for others as judgment upon their pride.

And so I wept—not only because words could be bent, but because hearts so readily conspire with error when it flatters them. The wound lies deeper than misinterpretation; it reaches to that root where love of self displaces love of God. Therein resides the true pain: that we can speak holy words while our souls dwell far from holiness—and call it peace when there is no peace, saith the Lord.

question: How did the memory of your mother's tears influence the way you spoke about desire and holiness?
answer:
When Varro speaks of natural affection as the bond that holds society together, he sees only the form without perceiving the substance; for he knows not that love must be ordered by truth, nor that desire disordered is the very root of human misery. It was not merely a mother’s sorrow that shaped my understanding of desire and holiness, but the ceaseless stream of Monica’s tears—tears not of weakness, but of divine importunacy, like the widow before the unjust judge. Those tears were not shed for my success in rhetoric or my rise in the world, but for my soul’s deliverance from the snare of concupiscence. She wept while I laughed at sacred things; she prayed while I delighted in stolen fruit and vain philosophies. And yet, in God’s hidden economy, her grief became a mirror reflecting the groaning of the Spirit within her—for she loved me not as this world loves its own, with indulgence and flattery, but with that holy severity which desires only God and God alone.

Thus I came to see that to will what is truly good is not mere inclination, nor caprice disguised as choice; it is alignment with eternal reason. The man who says “I desire” must first ask whether his heart lies within the City of God or still grovels in the City of Man. For if his love is self-centered, then even his generosity becomes a transaction in vanity: banquets returned for banquets, honors for honors—what Scripture calls "filth" though men call it "virtue." But if his love is God-centered, then even abstinence from pleasure becomes an act of justice. My mother’s tears taught me this distinction—not by words alone, but by their persistence before an altar where no earthly reward was sought.

Therefore I speak sternly of desire because I have seen how it rules men more tyrannically than any emperor ever ruled Rome—and yet how easily it masquerades as liberty. True holiness does not consist in doing what others wish you to do simply because you wish them likewise to serve your pleasures; rather, it consists in referring all desire back to Him who is above all things pleasing—so that we neither demand nor render anything unworthy of sons adopted into divine sonship. In this light alone can one rightly interpret Christ’s command: “Do unto others,” not as license for mutual corruption under cover of reciprocity, but as a summons to bring every appetite into captivity under charity ordered toward eternity.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on how the same will can tilt toward shame or virtue, remembering your own restless desires in youth?
answer:
Justice, that most beautiful and disordered longing of the human soul, reveals itself not in the balance of scales but in the orientation of the will—this I came to see not through speculation alone, but through the fire of my own unruly years.  

When I look back upon that young man who wandered between Carthage and Rome, drunk with rhetoric and restless for pleasure, I do not scorn him, for he was myself—yet not my true self. The same will by which I later sought God with tears in a garden at Milan was once bent low with desire for vain glory and illicit delight. One and the same power of willing, capable of lifting the soul to wisdom and love divine, had been turned inward, curved upon itself—*curvatus in se*—until it found joy even in what it should have fled.  

I marveled then, as I still do now, how language itself bears witness to this duality: we say "I desire" and do not thereby confess virtue; Cicero could plead for mercy while Rome crushed nations beneath its eagles. The word remains the same; the heart behind it determines whether it ascends to God or sinks into mire. So too with that golden precept: “Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you…”—it holds only if the will directing it is cleansed, if it no longer takes pleasure in darkness because it has learned to love light.

For a will deformed by habit may delight in mutual corruption just as truly as holy wills delight in mutual charity. It is not enough that two sinners agree upon their sin; consent does not sanctify vice. The harmony of lovers matters little if they are harmonized by lust rather than by truth. Only when the will is healed—drawn upward by grace into friendship with God—does it become capable of genuine justice: doing good *not* because another commands or reciprocates, but because goodness itself is loved.

And so I learned: my youth was not lost to time’s oblivion; it became an argument written within me for the necessity of divine mercy. Without grace—that hidden motion of God stirring our dust—the will remains free only to wander; but under His hand, even a prodigal restlessness becomes the path home.

question: How did your struggle with lust and ambition change how you understood God's presence in the everyday movements of the heart?
answer:
The City of Man, in its restless striving, measures desire by the yardstick of mutual advantage, as if justice could be reduced to a bargain struck between two wills equally bent on self-gratification. I once walked that road, measuring all things—friendship, speech, even virtue—by how they might elevate me in the eyes of men. I desired praise as others desire wine: intoxicating, immediate, dissolving the shame of inadequacy. And in the fever of ambition and the clutch of lust, I mistook motion for life, passion for presence.

But what I learned in the long agony of conversion—what God taught me not in a single thunderclap but in the quiet erosion of my pride—is that every movement of the heart is either a turning toward Jerusalem or a descent into Babylon. The desires that once seemed neutral—the wish to be loved, to be known, to act freely—were revealed as altars already built: either to God or to self. When I cried out “Give me chastity—but not yet,” I exposed the heart’s duplicity: willing good in general but clinging to evil in particular.

This is why our Lord’s command—“Whatsoever ye would that men should do unto you”—is not a mere formula for social harmony. It is a fire that tests each desire at its root. For if my will is deformed by concupiscence or pride—if what I *will* includes domination masked as love or possession called intimacy—then reciprocation becomes not justice but shared corruption. The earthly city thrives on such exchanges: mutual flattery among lovers who use one another; alliances among rulers who trade power for silence. But the Heavenly City walks by another rule: not “as you will it done to you,” but “as charity would will it,” where love does not rejoice in wrong but rejoices with truth.

Thus did God begin His work—not by changing my circumstances but by unmasking my willing. In every restless pulse beneath pleasure, every throb of envy at another’s success, He showed me a soul disordered yet yearning for order. And there—in that very unrest—I found His presence: not as light overcomes darkness by abolishing it at once, but as dawn reclaims night slowly, making visible what was always seen by Him alone.

question: Looking back on the years you sought approval through eloquence, what would you tell someone who doubts their worth without acclaim?
answer:
The City of Man measures a man’s worth by the echo of his name in the ears of others, inflating the soul with wind while leaving it starved of truth. This is no mere accident of custom, but a principle woven into its very fabric: that honor, praise, acclaim—these are its highest currency, minted in the theater of human regard and spent on fleeting self-confirmation. But such coin will not pass in the City of God, where the only judgment that endures is that which proceeds from Him who sees not appearance but heart.

I know what it is to hunger for applause. In Carthage, among the schools and forums, I once shaped my words like an artisan crafting idols—every clause polished to dazzle, every period timed to stir wonder. My joy was not in truth spoken well, but in being *heard* as if I were its master. I mistook eloquence for virtue and believed myself wise because men nodded when I spoke. But what did their nodding profit me when my soul was at war with itself? When I delighted more in being admired than in being known?

Cicero himself prized persuasion above probity; he would have you believe that speech rightly ordered can bend opinion to justice—even as he flatters senators and courts favor. But we who serve Christ know a deeper logic: that no word rightly honors God unless it proceeds from a will rightly ordered toward Him. It is not how men receive you that confirms your worth, but how God sees you—naked, stripped of ornament, beloved not for your eloquence but for your humility.

Therefore let no one suppose that his value rises or falls with the acclamation of crowds. The angels do not sing over a packed hall; they rejoice over one sinner who repents. The Son of God was despised and rejected—and in His rejection He conquered death. If you seek approval as bread, you will starve; but if you seek righteousness—even in silence—it shall be given you.

Your worth lies not in being praised but in being His. And that cannot be lost—even if every tongue turned against you tomorrow.

question: When you confessed your theft of the pears, not for hunger but for twisted joy, how did that shame reshape your view of innocent desire?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is built upon ordered loves, where desire serves truth and justice; but in the City of Man, even the innocent faculties are turned into instruments of disorder by the pride that seeks not communion but conquest—even over one’s own soul. It was not hunger that moved me to steal those pears, nor any lack, nor any natural longing; rather, it was the delight in transgression itself—the dark pleasure of violating what I knew to be good. That act was not an aberration from my nature, but a revelation of it: that my will, left to itself, does not gravitate toward beauty and virtue as fire to the sky, but twists even harmless things—apples on a tree, laughter among friends—into occasions for sin.

I did not want the fruit—I wanted the breaking. And in that wanting, I saw how deeply corrupted my will had become: not because desire itself is evil—for God made it—but because it had been derailed from its end. The boy who climbed the tree at night thought he was defying orchard walls; he was really defying the law written in his heart, which even pagans glimpse when they speak of *do ut des*, or when Cicero pleads for mercy before the Senate. They know there is a right order—yet they cannot heal what is broken within.

But grace came later—not through reason alone, though I labored long in philosophy’s shadow—but through Christ crucified, who healed my will by first condemning it. For until I learned to hate that delight in evil—to see it not as freedom but as slavery—I could never love anything rightly. Only then did I begin to understand that true desire is not assertion but offering: to want for another what you would have for yourself only when your own heart has been cleansed by truth. The shame I bore was not meant to crush me; it was meant to unmask me—and so became the first stirrings of humility, without which no man sees God.

question: What inner conflict arose when you realized that even your finest intentions could be tainted by the desire to be praised rather than to love?
answer:
The City of God knows no applause, for its citizens are crowned not by the breath of human acclaim but by the silent benediction of divine approval; whereas the City of Man measures worth in glances, in murmurs, in the swelling echo of one’s own name repeated by others—this I came to see with a kind of shuddering clarity when even my prayers were haunted by the lust for honor.

For long I mistook eloquence for virtue, and the stillness of contemplation for mere prelude to a sermon that would stir the crowd. I desired to be seen as holy, even as I professed humility; I wanted my tears at the altar to be noticed, though I claimed to weep only for my sins. It was not enough that God knew—the wound lay deeper: that men should know *that* He knew, and thus confirm *my* sanctity in their eyes.

Cicero speaks nobly of mercy as something desired before it is done; yet he does not probe the springs from which such desire flows. Is it virtue when a man wishes to be merciful if his wish springs not from love of justice but from love of being called merciful? What difference does it make whether one openly commits injustice or subtly distorts righteousness into a mask for pride? The act may please men; but in the sight of Him who searches hearts, there is no peace where there is self-exaltation.

True justice—true peace—dwells where will and desire are purged of this duplicity. Not merely “I will” or “I desire,” but *what* I will, *for what end* I desire—it is here that charity must be tested. The Apostle says love rejoices not in iniquity; but how easily does our fallen nature rejoice even in apparent virtue when it serves as garment for vainglory!

Only grace uncovered this sickness within me—not philosophy, nor rhetoric, nor years spent among books and disputations. Grace laid bare the truth: that unless the will is ordered toward God alone, all deeds—even those aligned with moral law—are built upon sand. For even what appears upright may serve Babylon’s vanity while pretending to Jerusalem’s holiness.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote that both good and bad men 'desire,' yet their wills are shaped by such different loves?
answer:
It was not the diversity of desire that struck me, but its inescapable orientation—like arrows loosed from many bows, all tending toward some end, yet differing utterly in their aim. When I wrote of how both the righteous and the wicked *desire*, *fear*, *rejoice*, and *grieve*, I was not merely parsing grammar or borrowing turns of phrase from Virgil or Terence, though I had learned to read them not as idols but as signposts. No, I was dismantling the Stoic illusion—championed by Cicero and Varro—that peace resides in the suppression of passion, in a soul steeled against movement. They thought virtue lay in apathy; we know it lies in rightly ordered love.

For what is man but a pilgrim soul, bent either toward the City that rises on the love of God or that other, built on the love of self unto contempt of God? The profligate youth who cries “I will nothing else than Philumena” burns with a will no less real than that of Abraham when he bound Isaac upon the altar. Both *will*; both act from deep desire. But one is drawn by concupiscence chained to visible beauty, the other by obedience wedded to eternal truth. The motion is similar; the gravity differs utterly.

And sorrow? Even this—the Stoics would purge it from wisdom’s dwelling. Yet Paul rejoices when he sees Corinth weep with *godly sorrow*, a grief that kills death itself by crucifying pride. Here is reason enlightened by faith: not to deny the passions, but to baptize them. For if God Himself grieves over Jerusalem, who shall say grief belongs only to vice?

Thus do I hold: nature is not evil; appetite is not sin in itself. Sin lies in disordered love—the attachment of an immortal will to perishable things instead of to Him from whom all goods descend like rays from light.

question: How did your own struggles with lust, like that of the youth in Terence, change how you understood God's hold on your heart?
answer:
Justice, that most noble order of divine love, cannot be achieved by the mere correction of vice, nor by the suppression of passion through sheer will—but only when the disordered loves are reoriented toward their true end in God. I speak from the wounds of memory: when I burned with that same fire as Terence’s youth, crying out in my soul, “I desire nothing else,” not for virtue, not for wisdom, but for the fleeting warmth of illicit pleasure—then I knew neither peace nor justice within myself. The will, so praised by philosophers as the seat of moral strength, was in me no fortress but a battlefield where concupiscence held dominion. I desired; I feared losing what I loved; I rejoiced in sin; I grieved when it eluded me—thus fulfilling Virgil’s line: *Hence they fear and desire, grieve and are content.* But my contentment was death; my joy, a shadow.

The Stoics taught that the wise man must extinguish passion altogether and rise above all disturbance. Yet how could such a cure heal a sickness like mine? For what I needed was not detachment from feeling, but the transfiguration of it. Grace did not silence my heart—rather, it taught my heart to sing a new song. When at last tears flowed not for lost pleasure but for lost time spent away from God—when sorrow became godly sorrow—I saw that even grief could be holy. My will did not vanish; it was redirected. The same faculty that once clung to Philumena now clung to Christ. Not less passionate—but rightly ordered.

Thus I came to understand: God does not despise the affections He planted in our nature; He restores them. The City of Man loves itself unto contempt of God; but the City of God loves God even unto contempt of self—and therein finds itself restored on His eternal terms.

question: Looking back on the years you chased earthly affections, what would you tell someone who doubts whether joy can ever be pure or holy?
answer: When the soul, restless with the image of the eternal Good yet turned toward mutable things, mistakes its own movement for fulfillment, it breeds only a joy that consumes itself—this is the law not of man’s invention but of divine order, discerned even by Plato when he spoke of participation in the Forms, though he saw dimly what we now behold in Christ. For joy there must be in man’s heart; nature herself cries out for it, planted as she is by the Artisan of all things with longing for completion. But the Stoic who would purge all passion, and the Epicurean who would drown himself in pleasure, are both shipwrecked on the same rock: they know not that joy is not evil because it moves the soul, but becomes evil only when it rests where it ought not. I myself once sought joy in rhetoric’s applause, in friendship poisoned by pride, in lust disguised as love—and each time I found not joy but its shadow, a fleeting echo that left me emptier than before. Yet now I say with certainty: joy *can* be pure, because God Himself is joyful—not as we are, tossed between hope and loss, but eternally delighting in His own essence and inviting us into that delight. When love ascends to Him who is Unchangeable Beauty, then desire becomes worship, contentment becomes beatitude, and sorrow itself—like that which Paul calls *secundum Deum tristitia*—purifies rather than destroys. The City of Man rejoices in dominion over others; the City of God rejoices that God reigns. In this difference lies all distinction between a joy that passes and a joy that makes eternity present even now.

question: When you reflected on Virgil's line about fear, desire, grief, and contentment, did you see your own past reflected in those four wounds of the soul?
answer:
It is not the presence of desire, nor of fear, nor of grief, nor of joy that marks the citizen of the City of Man—he is known rather by the object to which his love cleaves and the end for which he lives. When I heard Virgil’s verse—not as revelation, but as a pagan echo of truth—I recognized at once the disordered motions that once ruled my own heart. Fourfold was the unrest within me long before grace illumined my will: I feared dishonor, I desired acclaim, I grieved over losses temporal, and took contentment in fleeting triumphs. These are not sins in their mere existence; indeed, even in the saints there remains a trembling at divine judgment, a longing for righteousness, sorrow for sin, and joy in grace. But in my youth—before I learned that all motion of the soul must be directed toward God—the affections were disordered: I loved what should have been used, and used what should have been loved.

The Stoics erred when they sought to excise grief entirely, as though wisdom required impassibility; they mistook apathy for virtue. But Paul teaches otherwise when he speaks of godly sorrow that works repentance unto life. So too with desire: Terence’s youth who cries “I will nothing else than Philumena” does not reveal the nature of will as evil per se, but shows how will becomes vice when untethered from right order. The philosopher may refine his passions with reason; only faith submits them to God. And thus it was with me: no dialectic could heal my inward division—only Christ, who reorients love toward its true center.

In this light we must judge all affections: not by their name or kind, but by their principle and end. The citizen of the City of God fears damnation not out of servile dread but because he loves salvation; he desires glory because it is divine; he grieves for sin because it offends holiness; he rejoices in truth because it is eternal. The same winds blow upon both cities—but only those built upon the Rock are lifted toward heaven by them.

question: What inner conflict were you reliving when you contrasted 'godly sorrow' with the sorrow the world calls wisdom?
answer:
True justice, which gives to God His due worship and to neighbor the love born of divine charity, cannot be found in any senate, however adorned with fasces and lictors, if the soul within that body politic loves itself more than the immutable Good.  

When I contrasted *godly sorrow* with that sorrow the world styles wisdom, I was retracing in my own soul the long pilgrimage from Manichaean dualism to the embrace of divine providence—a journey where tears themselves had to be judged not by their bitterness but by their source. The world calls wise a man who grieves only for lost fortune or failed ambition, whose sorrow is a measure of worldly calculation; and the Stoic would crown him wiser still if he felt no grief at all. But what is that but a deadness posed as strength? The apostle Paul rejoices that the Corinthians wept—not because grief had mastered them, but because it had cleansed them. That sorrow was fruitful because it flowed from love: love for God offended, love for holiness defiled.  

I knew such tears when I wept at Milan under Ambrose’s preaching, when the weight of stolen pears—so small to man, so monstrous before God—rose up in my conscience. That grief was not despair; it was compunction: *compunctio cordis*. It wounded in order to heal. The world’s “wise” mourning seeks self-preservation; godly sorrow seeks nothing less than transformation into another city—the City of God rising amid the ruins of this age. One sorrow clings to dust; the other ascends on wings of repentance toward that Light which alone makes all things new.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on the Corinthians' sorrow, knowing your own tears over sin had once seemed a sign of weakness?
answer:
The City of God knows tears not as marks of infirmity, but as rivers carved by the plow of divine mercy; whereas the city of man either drowns in its sorrows or damns them, fearing the flood they bring. When I read of the Corinthians’ sorrow—wounded by my letter, yet awakened to repentance—I saw not merely their grief, but a reflection of that same grace which once shattered my own proud heart in Milan. The weeping of the world collapses inward, a suffocating weight that leads to death; but godly sorrow lifts the soul upward, as contrition becomes the very ladder by which we descend into God and ascend to truth.

I had long disdained tears, trained as I was in the schools where wisdom wore a stony face, where Alcibiades wept at Socrates’ words only because his folly was exposed—yet even then, no one taught him to weep *for* his sin, only *over* his shame. So too I thought myself strong until I was broken; and in that breaking found strength. The apostle rejoices not because sorrow came, but because it came with purpose: it worked carefulness, zeal, fear of God—steps toward salvation unrepented. And so I rejoiced over Corinth, for there I saw not weakness yielding to emotion, but wills being reforged in fire.

The Stoic may say the wise man feels no sorrow—but what is wisdom apart from God? Was not my own wisdom foolishness until it died? If philosophy scorns grief as disturbance of reason’s order, then let it answer why Christ wept at Lazarus’ tomb though He held life in His hands. Grief is not always rebellion against providence; sometimes it is its most faithful echo—the soul harmonizing with divine judgment and divine love alike. My tears were not weakness: they were the first obedience of a will too long rebellious. And theirs? They were marks that grace had reached them too—not sparing them pain, but sanctifying it.

question: How did your struggle with tears of repentance change how you understood God's closeness in sorrow?
answer:
The Stoic, in his pursuit of impassibility, would banish sorrow from the wise man’s soul as a defilement of reason; yet he fails to see that the heart broken by godly sorrow is not ruled by passion, but ordered by love — love that ascends through contrition to communion with God.  

I once wept not because I was wise, but because I was blind — and in that blindness, I wept for lost time, for stolen affections squandered on lesser things. The tears I shed at the death of my friend, which tormented me with guilt and longing, were not yet godly sorrow; they were the cries of a soul entangled in the City of Man, mourning as one without hope. But when at last I came to grieve not merely for loss, but for sin — for the pride that held me from You, O God; for the lusts that made me deaf to Your voice — then my tears became something other than mere grief: they became a baptismal stream flowing from humility.

For the wise man of the Stoics stands dry-eyed upon a mountain of self-possession, untouched by suffering or shame; but the citizen of the City of God kneels in dust and ashes, and from that low place cries out: *Miserere mei*. That cry moves heaven. That sorrow draws God near. It is not weakness; it is the very work of grace disassembling pride. The world's sorrow collapses inward and kills; but godly sorrow stretches upward and opens the soul to divine infusion.  

Thus did my tears change meaning: once signs of disordered love, they became instruments of purification — not because emotion itself is holy, but because God condescends to meet us in our brokenness. He does not dwell only with the serene; He inhabits those who are crushed in spirit. In this way, His closeness is revealed not despite our weeping, but often through it — for where there is true repentance, there also dwells His mercy without distance.

question: Looking back on your weeping in the garden, what would you tell someone who fears their sadness is a failure of faith?
answer:
Sorrow, when it arises from the recognition of our estrangement from God, is not the mark of a failing faith but the first stirrings of faith restored. For what is faith, if not the soul’s assent to truth after long resistance? The philosophers, noble in their striving, would purge all disturbance from the wise man’s heart; they call sorrow a disease to be excised by reason’s scalpel. And yet they cannot account for Alcibiades weeping under Socrates’ gaze—not because he was rebuked with cruelty, but because he saw himself as he truly was: clothed in ignorance, adorned with illusions. Was that sorrow vain? No—it was the breaking open of a soul too long sealed by pride.

So too in divine things: when grace begins its silent work, it does not first bring joy—as though salvation were a reward for levity—but it brings sorrow, holy and purifying. I wept in that garden at Tagaste not because I had lost a friend or suffered injustice at human hands, but because I saw how long I had squandered love on shadows while the Light Himself stood knocking. That grief was no enemy of faith—it was faith’s awakening. The Apostle does not say “blessed are the joyful,” but “blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted.” He knows that tears water the ground where hope takes root.

Do not fear your sadness if it draws you toward God rather than into despair. Ask rather whether your sorrow is worldly—rooted in loss of reputation, failure of appetite, collapse of ambition—or whether it is godly: born from seeing how often you have turned aside from Him who made you for Himself. The City of Man flees grief as weakness; the City of God bends beneath it like grain before harvest wind, knowing that only what dies bears fruit.

question: What made you finally accept your tears not as a disturbance but as a gift from God?
answer:
The City of God knows no fortress more impregnable than the humility of a contrite heart, for it is there, and only there, that the light of divine wisdom descends to purge the blindness of pride. For many years I mistook tears for weakness, a failing of the flesh to be disciplined into silence by reason—like the Stoics, who imagined the sage untouched by sorrow, dwelling in a citadel of impassivity. But what is such a city built upon? Not upon love, but upon self-exaltation; not upon grace, but upon human sufficiency. And I had long dwelt in that city.

When I wept over my mother’s death, I reproached myself for mourning as if I had no faith; when I groaned under the burden of my sins, I thought such sorrow unworthy of a philosopher. Yet how could philosophy heal what it refused even to name? The tears I shed at Milan beneath the fig tree were not the spasms of nature unbridled—they were rivers sent from heaven to dissolve the stone within my chest. In weeping, I ceased arguing with God; in weeping, I stopped trying to justify myself; in weeping, I finally listened.

It was then that I understood: godly sorrow does not belong to the wise man because he has never sinned—that is the Stoic dream—but because he *has* sinned and knows it. True wisdom begins not in impassivity but in confession. The apostle rejoices not because sorrow exists, but because through sorrow God breaks open our rebellion and draws us back. The world’s sorrow collapses under guilt into death; but *godly* sorrow—the grief that arises from seeing oneself truly before an infinite holiness—that sorrow is wrought by grace and leads to life.

I no longer feared my tears when I saw them for what they were: not signs of nature unruly, but whispers of grace humbling me so that love might rise where pride once stood. The City of God builds its foundations on such brokenness; for He who is Truth himself said: "Blessed are those who mourn."

question: When you read of Alcibiades weeping at his folly, did you see yourself, and how did that moment stir your own memory of brokenness?
answer:
The City of Man builds its wisdom upon the polished columns of reason, yet trembles before the mirror that reveals its nakedness; whereas the City of God finds its strength not in unshaken composure, but in the trembling heart that sees itself as it is and falls silent before the judgment of God. When I read of Alcibiades weeping at the voice of Socrates—his tears drawn not from wounds inflicted by others, but from the sudden unveiling of his own disorder—I did not merely see a pagan stirred by philosophy; I saw a shadow of my own soul before it was seized by grace.

For what is folly, if not love misplaced? Alcibiades wept because he had adored himself, adorned his life with honors and desires like gilded ornaments upon a tomb, and only then perceived that within was emptiness and death. So too with me: my tears were long withheld not because I lacked sorrow’s occasion, but because I lacked truth. In Carthage, in Rome, in the halls where I taught rhetoric with pride, I gloried in voices that praised me—while within me grew a silence louder than all clamor. The Manichees offered me explanations; the Academy taught me to doubt all things—but neither could make me *weep* for my sin.

And then Scripture pierced me: *"They are darkened in their understanding, alienated from the life of God because of the ignorance that is in them due to hardness of heart."* There—I was laid bare. Not as one merely mistaken, but as one *loving* darkness. That knowledge did not come through dialectic alone, nor through any syllogism Stoic or Platonic; it came when God opened my inward ear. Then I wept—not as Alcibiades wept for lost honor or missed ambition—but because I had squandered years loving creation more than Creator.

The Stoic boasts that the wise man feels no sorrow; yet what is such wisdom if it cannot mourn its own blindness? What triumph is there in impassivity when one sits contentedly in chains? No—godly sorrow is no defect of reason; it is reason restored by grace, seeing rightly at last both sin and Savior. And so my tears became waters for cleansing, not merely signs of pain. They were gifts—not because sorrow saves, but because they marked the moment love began to displace pride within me.

Thus do two cities differ even in their tears: one sorrows only for loss of power or reputation—the other sorrows for having loved power more than God. One dries her eyes quickly lest she seem weak—the other kneels and lets them flow until divine compassion washes her clean.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about Alcibiades weeping at Socrates' words, remembering your own tears in the garden?
answer:
When the soul, darkened by misplaced loves, is pierced by the light of truth, its trembling is not weakness but the first motion of return — as when Alcibiades, swollen with Athenian pride and intoxicated by his own brilliance, was undone by Socrates’ dialectic not through insult but through revelation. He wept not because he was rebuked, but because he saw himself: a man built on sand, calling himself happy while rooted in folly. And in writing of that moment, my hand did not merely transcribe an ancient anecdote; it traced the contour of my own collapse beneath the weight of divine mercy.  

In Carthage, in Milan, and at last in that garden where the voice from the unseen lips said *Tolle, lege*, I too was Alcibiades — not schooled by Socrates but shattered by Scripture. My tears were not philosophical regret but penitence wrung from depths no argument could reach. The Stoic would have scorned such sorrow as a disturbance to be expelled; yet I found in it the very pathway to peace. For what is grief rightly ordered if not love corrected? The City of Man mourns only its defeats; the City of God mourns its sins. One grieves that it has lost power; the other that it has loved unwisely.  

Thus do we who walk toward Jerusalem weep and rejoice — fear death and desire life eternal — not because our reason fails us, but because our love is fixed upon God Himself. Our perturbations are no disorder of soul but signs that grace has begun its work: for only those who once were blind know how to cry out when they see.

question: How did your mother's prayers during your years of wandering change how you understood God's nearness in sorrow?
answer:
The order of love determines the disorder we mourn, and thus the sorrow that heals; for where affection is rightly placed, grief itself becomes a path to God. When I wandered in the labyrinth of my own pride, chasing shadows of wisdom and delighting in the noise of words while deaf to divine silence, my mother Monica wept before the altar with groans that no rhetoric could articulate. Her tears were not those of despair but of hope—hope grounded not in philosophical speculation, but in faith that clings to the unseen. At the time, I saw her sorrow as excessive, even irrational, a weakness unworthy of a mind disciplined by logic and eloquence. But now I see that her grief was wiser than my so-called wisdom.

She knew what I had not yet learned: that God is nearer to us in contrition than in speculation, present not only in arguments won but in hearts broken by longing for Him. The Manichees offered me explanations for evil; they did not teach me to repent. The Academy taught me to doubt; it did not teach me to desire truth with my whole soul. But Monica’s prayers rose like incense because they sprang from charity—love fixed on God and therefore love truly ordered. And such love cannot be indifferent to disorder within.

When Socrates made Alcibiades weep by showing him his own folly beneath his pride, he struck a chord close to grace—but still outside it. For Socrates knew only that virtue is knowledge; he did not know that humility is union. My mother’s tears taught me more than any dialogue ever could: that God draws us through those who love us faithfully, and often speaks most clearly through their grief. In her weeping was fulfilled what Scripture declares—that godly sorrow produces repentance leading to life.

Thus I came at last to understand that divine providence does not stand aloof from our afflictions; rather, He bends near when we are lowly of heart. It was not argument alone that brought me home—it was prayer poured out without ceasing, night and day, from one whose city was already fixed above while she lived as a pilgrim here below.

question: Looking back on your theft of the pears, what would you tell someone who doubts that foolishness can lead to holy grief?
answer:
The City of Man rejoices in secret sins as though they were triumphs, while the City of God mourns its transgressions as though they were wounds—and therein lies the dividing line between death and life.  

When I stole those pears, not for hunger, not for need, but merely because the act of theft pleased me in its very wrongness, I was not exercising freedom; I was enslaved to a disordered will, a soul turned inward upon itself like a wound that festers unseen. That night in the orchard was no victory of spirit, but a collapse into foolishness—a folly so deep it could not even recognize itself as such. And yet, it was precisely this folly that became the ground for holy grief. For had I thought myself righteous in that moment, had pride clothed my sin in dignity, how could sorrow have found entrance? It is only when the mirror of divine truth shatters our self-deception that we weep—not because we are punished, but because we see what we have become.

Socrates made Alcibiades weep by showing him that wisdom was absent where he presumed it present; but grace makes the sinner weep far more deeply, by revealing not mere ignorance, but love betrayed—God’s and one’s own. The Stoic may claim sorrow unbefitting to the wise; but we who walk by faith know that true wisdom begins in tears shed over sins once loved. Such grief is no disease of the soul—it is its healing. The man who doubts this has never stood before God in honesty; he still dwells in the city where shame is hidden and sin is adorned. But let him come into the light where all things are laid bare—even his own folly—and he shall find that holy sorrow is not punishment inflicted from outside, but mercy awakening within.

question: What were you feeling when you contrasted the Stoic ideal with the weeping faith of the psalmist?
answer:
Socrates, in his piercing way, once exposed the hollowness of Alcibiades’ pride by showing that no man can be happy who is not wise—and that no man can be wise who does not love what is truly good. But the Stoics, even as they admired such moments of self-awareness, turned sorrow itself into a sign of weakness, a disturbance to be purged from the soul of the sage. How far this is from the truth revealed in Scripture! When I contrasted their impassive ideal with the weeping psalmist—when I saw David pour out his soul before God in grief, in longing, in trembling hope—I felt a profound recognition: here was a man whose heart beat in rhythm with divine justice, not because he was free from passion, but because his passions had been healed.

The Stoic would have us believe that peace lies in apathy, that true wisdom banishes sorrow as though it were sin. But what then shall we say of contrition? Of Jeremiah mourning over Jerusalem? Of Christ himself, groaning over Lazarus' tomb? These are not the spasms of an unruly soul—they are movements of charity too deep for silence. The citizens of the City of God do not flee sorrow; they sanctify it. They grieve—not because they lack faith, but because they love rightly. And in that grief there is more virtue than in all the dry-eyed composure of Zeno’s followers.

For our affections are not enemies to be subdued but servants to be ordered: fear that leads to reverence; desire that reaches for eternal things; sorrow that unbuilds pride; joy that anticipates glory. This alone is true human restoration—when every motion of the soul submits to Him who first stirred it into being.

question: How did your friendship with Alypius shape your belief that love bends even our grief into something righteous?
answer: It is not by the Stoic’s impassive reason, nor by the self-contained wisdom of the Academy, but through the furnace of shared affliction between friends that divine love refines the very nature of sorrow. The City of Man, in its pride, would have us believe that grief is either a disease to be purged or a weakness unworthy of the wise—witness how the philosophers praised impassivity even as they denied the groanings of the soul. But in the City of God, where Alypius walked beside me through darkness and conversion, I learned that love does not abolish grief; it redirects its course. When we grieved together over sin—not merely our own, but each other’s—as men bound not by blood alone but by baptism and longing for holiness, our tears became offerings. What Socrates may have stirred in Alcibiades—shame for folly—is but a shadow compared to what grace wrought in us: a sorrow that did not crush, because it arose from love fixed on God. Thus our friendship was no mere human consolation; it was an instrument by which charity shaped our passions, so that even mourning could serve righteousness—for where two abide in Him, no affection remains untransformed.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about fearing eternal punishment while still clinging to hope in God's mercy?
answer: When Cicero speaks of the republic as a people bound by shared justice and common interest, he glimpses but does not grasp the deeper truth: that no society, private or public, can order its loves rightly unless it is founded upon the fear of the Lord, for *there* alone is wisdom rooted and justice made whole. It was out of this conviction—wrestled from years in the City of Man, where I once sought glory in rhetoric and solace in error—that I came to write of fear not as a tormenting specter, but as the beginning of right affection. To fear eternal punishment is not to cower before an arbitrary tyrant; it is to stand at last in the presence of truth, aware that sin is not a trivial habit but a mortal wound, and that every unchecked desire carries us further from the light. Yet this fear does not paralyze—it purifies. For just as fire tempers metal, so divine judgment refines love. I wrote under the tension of this dual awareness: that I had been a man who loved darkness because my deeds were evil, yet had also tasted the unspeakable kindness of God who bent low to reclaim me. Therefore my fear was not despairing—it was filial—because joined inseparably with hope: hope not in myself, but in Him who first called me out of nothingness into grace. The groaning within me—for my disordered desires still lingered even as I wrote—was real; yet it was enveloped by joy unshakable: joy that death would be conquered, that my body would be redeemed, and that love itself would be perfected in vision. Thus both fear and hope arose from one root: charity rightly ordered toward God alone.

question: How did your own struggle with sin change how you understood God's presence in weakness?
answer:
The earthly city builds its towers with the mortar of self-assurance, imagining strength where there is only pride, and calling peace what is merely the silence of unchallenged vice; but I learned, in the trembling of my own soul, that true strength is forged not in resistance to temptation alone, but in the humility that acknowledges defeat—and turns, again and again, to God.

When I was young in spirit—though not in years—I sought mastery over myself through will alone, as if virtue could be seized like a prize in rhetoric or philosophy. I thought to tame my desires by reason’s light, as though Plato’s ascent of the soul could carry me past the weight of concupiscence. But I found that knowledge inflamed my pride even as it exposed my weakness. To know the good and yet choose the lesser—this was not mere ignorance; this was bondage. And it was precisely in that bondage that I began to see God not as a distant First Principle, but as a near and piercing Light, who did not wait for me to become worthy before He loved me.

My fear of sin was once only fear of punishment—the slave cowering before the lash. But when grace pierced me, I feared sin because it separated me from *Him*, because every disordered love built another brick into the wall between my heart and the City whose Founder is God. And so too my desires changed: I no longer longed merely for tranquility or intellectual repose, but for perseverance—for grace to endure, because I knew how easily even noble desires collapse under the burden of habit.

I rejoiced not because I had conquered vice—how could I?—but because in confession there was cleansing; because Christ had descended into the pit even after me. In weakness, then, I discovered that divine strength is made perfect where human effort fails. For if we could save ourselves by wisdom or willpower alone, what need of a Savior? The groanings within—the body still subject to vanity—are not signs that God has abandoned us; they are signs we are pilgrims still. But He who hears Isaac’s laughter also hears our sighs under the burden—and numbers them as part of His own redemptive work.

Thus do both fear and grief belong to the citizen of Heaven—but purified by love. Not servile fear like those who dread punishment without loving justice; nor despairing grief like those who mourn sin without hope of pardon. We fear lest we fall; yet desire to stand firm—not by our own power, but by His gift. We grieve for sin—not as pagans do over missed pleasure—but because it offends Him whom we long to please.

And so it is: God does not dwell only with those who have triumphed; He dwells with those who struggle toward Him through tears and repeated failure. Therein lies His mercy: He draws us *through* our weakness into His strength—not around it.

question: Looking back on the times you groaned within yourself, what would you tell someone who doubts their worth because of their failures?
answer:
When Varro sought to number the sects of philosophy and found them torn by endless discord, he exposed the helplessness of human wisdom when left to its own devices—yet even he did not see that the true measure of a soul’s worth is not its freedom from weakness, but its orientation toward God amid the struggle.  

You who doubt your worth because of your failures—I too have borne the weight of wasted years, of affections misdirected, of tears shed not yet in true repentance but only in regret for being caught. I lived long in that twilight where desire and shame war without resolution. But now I know: it is not perfection that proves you belong to the City of God, but longing for it; not freedom from sin, but hatred of it; not absence of fear, but fear rightly placed—the fear that keeps you from offending Him whose grace alone sustains you.  

The groanings within are not signs of exclusion—they are birth pangs. The very grief you feel over your failings is evidence that the Spirit breathes in you, for no man mourns what he does not love. The earthly city mocks weakness and builds monuments to pride; but our City counts tears sacred, because they wash the path to Zion. Your failures do not cancel your calling—they press you toward the Physician whose hands alone can mend. Even now, your desire to be better is itself His gift. Do not despise His work within you because it is incomplete. The Builder measures His temple not by present symmetry, but by final design—and He finishes what He begins.

question: What was going through your heart when you spoke of rejoicing in hope, even as death loomed?
answer:
The earthly city trembles at the shadow of death, for it knows no peace beyond the breath in the lungs and the pulse of power in its veins; but the City of God, though clothed in mortal flesh and passing through the valley of weeping, lifts up its eyes to a resurrection not made with hands. When I spoke of rejoicing in hope as death loomed, I did not speak as one untouched by dread, but as one whose heart had been seized by a greater reality—the promise inscribed in the very fabric of creation that decay is not the end. I knew too well the groaning within, that inward sighing which St. Paul named when he wrote of our bodies awaiting redemption; yet even in that groan was a note of anticipation, like dawn felt before it is seen.

Death, to the city shaped by love of self, is the final silence, the extinguishing of light. But to those whose love is fixed upon God—though fear may flicker at its approach—it is both enemy and servant: enemy by nature, for it came through sin; servant by divine will, for it delivers us into life. To rejoice in hope was not to mock suffering nor feign Stoic indifference; rather, it was to stand firm upon that rock where reason and faith meet—the truth that He who raised Christ from the dead does not abandon His children to corruption.

I have known desire turned inward until it became despair; I once sought glory in words and fame among men while my soul starved unseen. But when grace pierced me—when I heard those words spoken from heaven though uttered by a child: "Take up and read"—I learned at last what it means to desire rightly. And so even at death’s door, joy arises not from denial but from vision: we see dimly now, yes—but we *do* see: Christ risen, Christ returning, Christ holding all things together even as our breath fails.

Thus we grieve *and* rejoice—not confusion in one heart, but harmony born of charity ordered aright beneath God’s eternal gaze.

question: When you wrote about fearing temptation, were you remembering your own past desires—how did that shape your trust in God's strength?
answer:
It is not by our victory over temptation that we are numbered among the citizens of the City of God, but by our dependence on Him who has already conquered. The Manichean in me once believed the soul was so corrupted by matter that it could never withstand evil unless delivered entirely from the body; and thus I sought not grace, but escape—escape through secret knowledge, through philosophical aloofness, through a purity I imagined attainable by intellect alone. But the truth confessed in weakness is far greater: I did not overcome lust, ambition, or pride by strength of will—I was overcome *by* grace.  

To fear temptation rightly is not the mark of one who doubts God, but of one who knows himself. The Stoic may boast that the wise man feels no passion, that he stands unmoved by trial; but we who walk this pilgrimage feel deeply—because love moves us. We fear temptation not as pagans fear fate, nor as cowards dread suffering, but because we have tasted how sweet is the yoke of Christ and shudder at the thought of returning to chains. When I recall those years when my heart was divided—when I cried out "Give me chastity, but not yet"—I see now how God allowed me to feel both the weight of desire and the shame of delay, not to condemn me, but to break me open for mercy.

Thus my trust in God’s strength was forged not in triumph, but in repeated failure. Each fall taught me that vigilance without grace is vanity. To desire freedom from sin and yet tremble at its nearness—is this not wisdom? For such trembling keeps the soul watchful at the door of the heart. And when temptation comes—and come it will—we do not rejoice in our resistance, but in His faithfulness: He resists the proud and gives grace to the humble. It is His victory inscribed upon our wounds.
