question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the tranquillity of perfect love replacing the anxiety of fearing sin?
answer:
When Cicero imagined a republic founded on justice, he touched the edge of truth without grasping its source, for justice is not a consensus of citizens nor a product of reason alone, but the very order of love directed by God. It was in pursuit of that order that I came to see how the soul, when perfected in charity, no longer trembles at the thought of sin as one who walks near a precipice in darkness. No—such trembling belongs to this pilgrimage, where the will is still divided, where habit drags us one way and grace another. But in writing of perfect love’s tranquillity, I felt a kind of longing ascent: not the cold stillness of Stoic apathy, but the warmth of a heart fully aligned with divine will, where obedience is no longer labor but delight.

To fear sin because we still feel its allure is natural in this earthly city; it is the tension that marks our conversion. Yet I saw clearly—and with a joy that pierced my own present imperfection—that in the City which is to come, fear will have no place. Not because vigilance ends where sin cannot be, but because love there is complete and unresisting. The soul will not *refrain* from evil as if by restraint; it will simply *will* only what God wills. That vision moved me: not merely as an idea drawn from Plato or refined by argumentation, but as a promise given through revelation and sealed by Christ.

And so when I wrote those words—of fear transformed into peace—I wrote not from possession, but from hope; not from stability achieved, but from rest anticipated. The very desire for such tranquillity proved to me that my heart was being drawn upward: for why else would I yearn for a state beyond anxiety unless already inhabited by Him who is its cause?

question: How did your own struggles with fear and weakness shape your understanding of love's role in resisting sin?
answer:
When the Roman legions first marched not in the name of justice but in the pride of power, they mirrored that ancient city which builds itself upon self-love, erecting monuments not to virtue but to domination—yet even there, in the trembling heart of a man like me who once sought honor among such powers, God was silently overturning the hierarchy of desire. I pursued greatness in rhetoric, acclaim in Carthage and Milan, and found only an inner disarray, for my will was divided: drawn toward truth, yet held fast by habit; ashamed of vice, yet returning to it with craving. In that division, fear was ever present—not the fear of God, but the fear of shame, of exposure, of losing face before men. That servile fear did not cleanse; it only drove me deeper into concealment.

But when at last grace pierced me—not through force, but through a child’s chant in a garden at Milan—there began a transformation not merely of action but of affection. I saw that love orders the soul: when love is rightly placed upon God as the highest good, all other movements follow in harmony. Fear then changed its form; no longer slavish dread before men or punishment, it became reverence—a trembling before infinite holiness that actually freed me from anxiety. For perfect love casts out terror as one casts off a crushing yoke; yet it retains awe as one guards a sacred temple.

Thus I came to understand that we do not flee sin merely by terror of hellfire—though that may be a beginning—but by being drawn upward into communion with Him who is Beauty ever ancient, ever new. The man struggling in weakness needs fear as a tutor; but the citizen of the City of God journeys forward by love as by light. In this pilgrimage we still feel remnants of disordered motion within us; hence some shadow of fear remains as discipline. But in that eternal life where we shall see Him face to face, there will be no more such fear—for we shall be confirmed in goodness not by effort but by vision. Then love alone will abide: secure, unshaken, and self-sustaining because fully united to its Source.

question: Looking back on your restless youth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether peace can truly replace fear in the soul?
answer:
The earthly city trembles before loss, for it sets its hope upon mutable things—honor, pleasure, power—all of which slip through the fingers like sand; but the City of God rests upon that which cannot be taken away, and thus its citizens, even in pilgrimage, are trained by grace to walk not in dread of falling, but in confidence of rising. You ask whether peace can truly replace fear? I who once wandered through the labyrinths of ambition and lust, seeking God yet resisting Him, can tell you: it is not a philosophical fancy nor a pious hope—it is the work of divine charity perfected in weakness. Fear belongs to the time of exile, when the soul still wavers between two loves: self and God. But when love grows strong—when it is no longer we who live, but Christ who lives within—fear departs, not because danger has vanished from the world, but because our heart has found its fortress.

In my youth I feared failure more than sin; I feared disgrace more than God’s judgment. My anxieties were many because my desires were divided. But grace did not merely pardon me—it reshaped my will. It taught me to desire rightly: not to avoid evil merely out of dread of punishment, as a slave fears the whip, but to reject it for love of Beauty itself. And this love—this ordered affection—brings peace deeper than reason can fully grasp. The pagans spoke well of tranquillity, yet they could never secure it; they built philosophies on reason alone and still trembled at death. We build on revelation joined to reason—for faith does not destroy reason but perfects it—and thus we see what they could not: that fear is temporal, a tutor leading us to wisdom; but peace eternal is the very climate of heaven.

When we reach that blessed life—the goal toward which every holy affection now inclines us—there will be no fear any more than there will be hunger or mourning; for we shall be at rest in Him who is our Peace. Even now that peace begins within us as a seed: weak at first, often buried beneath old habits and wounded affections—but nourished by prayer, sacrament, and truth until it displaces fear not by force but by fullness. As light casts out darkness simply by shining so does divine love expel fear simply by being fully loved.

So do not despair if your heart still trembles. The same God who saw me in Carthage chasing shadows sees you now—and He calls you from fear into sonship.

question: When you spoke of the 'clean fear' that leads to eternity, were you remembering moments when fear once guided your own heart toward God?
answer:
Justice, that virtue which Cicero rightly praised as the foundation of the republic, cannot fully exist where love is still mixed with fear, as though we served God only to escape punishment rather than because we belong to Him. And yet, in the long pilgrimage of my soul—before grace perfected what nature had begun—I did not come to God by fearless confidence, but by a trembling that was necessary, like the first stirrings of health in a body long diseased. There was a time when I feared not only hellfire but even the weight of divine judgment upon my restless affections; this fear was not pure, for it bore the mark of self-concern. Yet it was not without purpose: like a tutor leading a child through dark streets at dawn, it brought me toward the light even while I shuddered at shadows.

That earlier fear—tremulous, uncertain—was not identical with the "clean fear" I speak of in relation to eternal life. The one belongs to this age, where love is weak and temptations strong; the other is cleansed by consummated love itself. For when we see God face to face and are fully transformed into His likeness, there will be no room for dread—for "perfect love casts out fear," as the Apostle writes. But this does not render vain that holy trepidation which precedes glory; rather, it fulfills it. Just as patience ceases when there are no more ills to endure, so too does fear cease when there is nothing left to dread—and yet both have their end in what endures: joy without limit, and love without measure.

Thus the citizen of the City of God begins his journey often under compulsion of fear—fear of sinning against One he barely knows—and ends it in freedom born of intimate knowledge. The path is ordered by divine wisdom: first discipline through salutary dread; then growth through hope; finally rest in unshakable charity. In my youth I fled sin less because I loved God than because I feared His justice; but now I would say that such fear was itself a gift—a sign that He was already drawing me upward, even while I trembled at the ascent.

question: How did the memory of your mother's tears and prayers change your view of grief as you contemplated its absence in the blessed life to come?
answer:
When we consider that the perfected state of the blessed life admits no fear, no grief, for the very reason that love is fully realized and all desires are satisfied in God, then it follows that those affections which now move us—however holy their cause—must themselves be transformed or even transcended in the final vision. My mother’s tears were not vain, nor were her prayers less than a sacrament of love; yet they belonged to this pilgrimage, to this city still marching through time toward eternity. She wept not for herself but for my soul, as though her very heart were caught between the walls of the earthly city and the dawning light of the heavenly. Her grief was real, and righteous—wrought not from despair but from hope deferred. But what is perfected can know no such tension; what rests in God needs neither plea nor tear.

Thus I came to see that her sorrow, however luminous with faith, was a sign of incompleteness: a measure of how far I had wandered, yes—but also how far we all must journey before arriving where she always desired me to be. The blessed life knows no such longing because it lacks nothing; there is no need for intercession where every soul dwells secure in the beatific gaze. And so her tears, once so heavy upon my conscience, became for me not a mark of eternal sorrow but an image of temporal love striving toward its eternal end. They taught me that grief in this life may be holy when ordered rightly under charity—but they also confirmed that holiness itself will one day render grief obsolete.

In heaven there will be no mother weeping for her son’s conversion—for all will already stand converted before the face of God. There will only be joy: unbroken, unanxious, unending. And so I now believe that Monica’s prayers have reached their fulfillment not merely in my baptism or even my priesthood, but in this very truth: that one day she shall laugh with the fullness of eternal peace—the other side of every tear she ever shed.

question: What were you feeling when you described the restless pride of those who seem to master their passions but have lost all warmth of soul?
answer: It is not virtue but monstrous pride that claims perfection in the stillness of an unfeeling heart, as though the image of God were preserved in stone rather than in a soul aflame with love. When I beheld those who glory in their imperturbability, who boast of being unmoved by grief, desire, or compassion, I saw not the temperance of Plato’s philosopher nor the constancy of Stoic wisdom, but a soul so alienated from divine motion that it has ceased to live. For the City of Man, ever eager to fashion gods in its own image, exalts such hardness as mastery—yet what is mastery without justice? What is self-possession if it extinguishes charity? The very order of love requires that we be moved: grief for sin, longing for truth, compassion for neighbor. To suppress these motions is not to rise above nature but to arrest its fulfillment. Even the pagans glimpsed that virtue must be active; yet they erred by seeking stability in human reason alone. We know better: true tranquillity comes not by the extinction of passion but by its direction—ordered toward God, purified by grace. He who does not burn with love for the Lord has not conquered vice; he has merely become a corpse among the living.

question: How did your own struggles with pride shape the way you wrote about those 'elated with ungodly pride' while calling it virtue?
answer:
It is one thing to be still in the soul because God has pacified its tumult by grace, and quite another to be rigid from the pride of self-mastery, as though the absence of passion could of itself prove the presence of holiness. I have known both states within my own breast. There was a time when I mistook clarity of mind for purity of heart, when I prized the sharpness of my intellect as evidence of proximity to truth—and this was my greatest peril, not because I sought wisdom, but because I desired it as an ornament to my own glory. Like Cicero, who so admired the disciplined soul in *De Officiis*, I once believed that moral stability consisted in fortitude achieved by reason alone; yet what is fortitude that does not tremble before God? What is temperance that does not hunger for divine love?

The citizens of the earthly city—whether they wear the robes of Roman senators or Manichaean elect—often come to this same illusion: that self-possession is virtue. They cultivate detachment as though indifference were enlightenment, and then boast that they are unmoved by grief, untouched by desire. But such stillness is not peace; it is paralysis. The affections were given by God—not to enslave us, but to be ordered toward Him. When men suppress them entirely, or twist them into instruments of self-exaltation, they do not ascend toward God; they descend into a more refined kind of death.

I wrote against such pride not merely from observation, but from confession. In my youth I gloried in my ability to argue, to seduce with words, even to feign detachment while burning within with lust for praise. That pride did not vanish when I embraced the Catholic faith; it lingered like a shadow at the edge of my thoughts, whispering that my conversion itself was a triumph of *my* intellect over error. Only when grace humbled me—when I wept at Simplicianus’ feet and heard how Ponticianus marveled not at Augustine’s learning but at Anthony’s folly for Christ—only then did I see how far I had been from true humility.

Thus when I speak of those who "restrain" passions only to swell with ungodly pride, I speak as one who has worn their chains and mistaken them for wings. They think themselves above temptation because they feel no longing; but better a heart broken by its own weakness than one sealed shut by arrogance. The City of God moves forward through love and penitence; the City of Man stands frozen in its own image—and calls that statue virtue.

question: Looking back on your tears in the garden at Milan, what would you tell someone who fears their emotions make them unworthy of God's peace?
answer:
Justice is not the mere absence of disturbance, nor peace the silence of unfeeling stone, but both are rightly ordered loves—this much I have learned not in the schools alone, but in the rending of my own soul. The tears that fell from me beneath the fig tree in Milan were not a sign of weakness unworthy of divine communion, but the very channels by which God’s grace broke through the arid soil of my pride. You who fear your trembling heart, your sorrow, your restless longing—understand this: it is not emotion that disqualifies a soul from God’s peace, but disordered love. The earthly city fears passion only because it either drowns in it or seeks to crush it into numbness, boasting of stoic mastery while its heart grows hard as tombstone. But the City of God knows a deeper rhythm: grief can be holy when it mourns sin rather than self-pity; desire can be purified when it reaches not for pleasure but for truth; even sorrow can become sacrifice when offered to Him who wept over Lazarus and over Jerusalem.

I wept because I loved too little what was eternal and too much what was passing. And in that very weeping, God drew near—not to rebuke my feeling nature, but to reclaim it. The passions are not enemies to virtue; they are servants gone astray. When they serve God, even tears become prayer. He who formed the heart does not recoil from its tremors; rather, He bends low to redirect them toward Himself. True tranquillity is not found in being unmoved, but in being rightly moved—by love of God above all things, and by love of neighbor for His sake. That is peace: not the stillness of death, but the restful motion of a soul aligned with divine order.

question: What were you remembering in your heart when you spoke of a tranquillity so cold it kills humanity rather than heals it?
answer: It is not the stillness of the Stoic sage, unmoved by grief or joy, that marks the citizen of the City of God, but rather the ordered love that rejoices with truth and weeps at iniquity. When I spoke of a tranquillity so cold it kills, I was gazing not upon nature perfected, but upon nature perverted—upon those souls in the City of Man who, proud in their emotional sterility, mistake hardness of heart for wisdom. They follow not Paul’s teaching to be “transformed by the renewal of the mind,” nor Christ’s command to take up the cross daily; instead, they imitate certain philosophers who prized apatheia as if indifference were virtue. But what profit is it to feel neither wrath at sin nor longing for justice, if one remains in bondage to self-exaltation? The earthly city produces such men: dry bones clothed in discipline, their calm a tombstone over buried compassion. True peace—the pax that comes from above—is not found in numbness, but in right relation to God: it grieves when He is dishonored and burns with charity toward neighbor. He who cannot be moved by love or sorrow has not ascended beyond weakness—he has fallen beneath humanity. For even Christ wept; even the Spirit groans. To desire a life untouched by holy affections is not to reach heaven—it is to deny incarnation itself.

question: How did your love for God reshape your understanding of desire, once so tangled in worldly things, now meant to journey toward immortality?
answer:
Justice, that which even Cicero could not fully grasp because he saw it only in the mirror of the republic and not in the face of God, is finally found not in laws imposed from without, but in the ordering of loves within the soul. I once sought delight in things that pass—pleasure, honor, the applause of men—and mistook these fleeting shadows for substance. My desires were like a city at war with itself: restless, divided, driven by appetites that could never be satisfied because they were aimed at nothing eternal. The Manichees promised me truth clothed in reason, but it was a truth without humility, without sacrifice—without God crucified.

When I came at last to understand that love must be directed toward its proper end—the immutable Good, the fountain of all beauty—I saw how deeply desire had been distorted in me by living according to man rather than according to God. To live after the spirit is not to extinguish desire, as though the soul’s longings were a disease to be cured by numbness; no, it is to redirect them upward, to inflame them rightly. The City of Man mistakes self-possession for wisdom and insensibility for strength; it praises those who feel nothing as if they had conquered passion by their own will. But such souls do not rise above disorder—they fall beneath humanity into a kind of stone-like pride.

True tranquillity is not found in apathy but in alignment: when love rests upon God who is Love itself, then all lesser goods find their place—not rejected, but reordered. Even now we journey toward that immortality where desire will no longer strain or falter, because it shall be fulfilled without end. There we shall see Him as He is; and until then, our longing draws us home.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about our first parents' tranquil love in Paradise, and how did your own longings for peace shape this vision?
answer:
Peace is not the mere absence of disturbance, nor justice the silence of fear, but both are positive goods—rooted in right order, in love rightly directed; and it was this truth that burned in my soul when I pondered our first parents beneath the unfallen sky of Paradise. I saw them not as statues of cold detachment, immune to desire or movement of soul, but as beings perfectly poised—whose affections were not unruly flames but clear lights burning in due measure, submitted to God and therefore free. My own longings for peace were not foreign to this vision; they were its very wound and goad. For I had tasted the tumult of a heart divided—torn between concupiscence and conscience, ambition and repentance—and thus I came to understand that the tranquillity of Paradise was no Stoic numbness, no suppression of nature, but the harmony of nature restored: where delight did not lead to lust, where knowledge did not puff up, where fear had no foothold because trust in God was total.

When I wrote of that primordial serenity, I confessed more than history—I revealed a yearning shaped by my own restless ascent from Carthage to Milan, from rhetoric to revelation. The turmoil within me forced me to ask: Can man truly live without inner war? Is there a state where love does not degenerate into possession, joy into satiety? And then Scripture answered: There was such a state. And there shall be such a state again. The peace I could not find in the City of Man—the city that builds its temples on pride and silences dissent with power—was prefigured in Adam’s unfallen communion with God. He stood upright before he fell; his will was whole; his affections obeyed reason, reason obeyed God. That is true freedom: not license to desire anything, but power to desire only what is good.

And thus my theology became pilgrimage: every word on those pages was written with tears behind it—tears for what had been lost and hope for what shall be restored. For we do not invent the City of God; we remember it.

question: How did your struggle with temptation change how you understood the innocence Adam and Eve lost so quickly?
answer:
It is not virtue to lack desire when no trial stirs the flesh, but to have desired rightly and yet obeyed the higher law — this is justice restored, this is peace that surpasses the Stoic calm which mistakes numbness for goodness. When I reflect upon my own years of restless passion, chasing honors and pleasures as if they could satisfy a soul shaped for eternity, I came to see that Adam’s innocence was not merely the absence of sin but the presence of a poised will, held in grace like a house well-ordered before the storm. The philosophers speak of apatheia as though serenity were found in disaffection, as if beating back all emotion were wisdom itself; but what kind of humanity is left when love grows cold under the pretense of stability?

No — paradise was not a place where desire slept, but where it walked upright, directed wholly toward God. Adam and Eve did not lack longing; they lacked disorder. Their joy was full because their wills were single. But I, who knew only disordered loves — who wept for Dido’s death before I ever wept for my own soul’s exile — could never comprehend such innocence until I had first known its loss repeated daily in myself.

Temptation taught me that human nature does not fall from bliss because it desires too much, but because it yields too soon to lesser goods. The serpent did not bring evil into Eden by introducing desire; he brought it by twisting desire from its true object. And thus our first parents fell not because they were tempted — for even the just are tempted — but because they doubted that God was their supreme good. In that instant, the City of Man began: built on self-trust instead of faith, on concealment instead of communion.

I once thought perfection must be impassibility; now I know it is perfected love — love that does not waver when tested. We shall have such bodies in the resurrection: spiritual, immortal, no longer slaves to concupiscence or fear. But even then we shall feel joy more deeply than Adam knew in Eden — joy purified by trial and confirmed by grace. For what use is peace that has never been fought for? What glory crowns a victory never won? Our blessedness will be greater than theirs was: not because we are better than Adam unfallen, but because we have seen darkness and still chosen light — through Him who lifted us from nothingness into the eternal City where delight and duty are one.

question: Looking back on your restless youth, what would you tell someone who doubts whether true inner peace is even possible for a human being?
answer:
The City of Man builds its peace on the silencing of desire, the suppression of longing, as if tranquillity were mere numbness; but the City of God knows that true peace is not the absence of struggle, but the ordered love where desire finds its rest in the One who is its source. I once sought peace in philosophy, in rhetoric, in the arms of concubines, in the applause of crowds—each a restless ripple upon a soul made for still waters. And I found, to my sorrow and eventual joy, that no man can pacify his own heart by willing it quiet, any more than he can command darkness to become light by decree.

There are those who say, “Let us harden ourselves against sorrow and passion,” imagining that insensibility is virtue. But what profit is there in a heart that feels neither grief nor gratitude? A stone suffers no fear—but no one calls a stone blessed. Our first parents in Paradise were not passionless statues; they loved, they delighted, they obeyed—and their joy was full because their affections were rightly ordered. They feared no evil because none was near; they grieved nothing because all was good; yet had they turned from God, even there amidst immortal abundance, perturbation would have entered like a thief at dawn.

So do not suppose that inner peace means the extinction of feeling. It means instead the healing of love—love turned upward to God first, from whom all affections flow and find their harmony. When I wandered in Carthage and Rome and Milan, my heart was torn between two cities: one clamoring for fame, another whispering truth; one feeding on pride, another nourished by grace. I could not rest because I loved too many things too disorderly—and none with final devotion.

Peace comes not when we cease to desire but when we desire rightly. It came to me not through strength but through surrender—not when I silenced my voice but when I heard His. The soul is restless until it rests in God—not because He fills silence alone but because He orders all motion toward Himself: our loves, our tears, our very longings for peace. That is not illusion or hope deferred—it is already present as grace even amid struggle—for where two loves contend within a man, it is already victory if one begins to bend toward God.

question: What were you feeling when you pictured Eden's joy—was it hope, sorrow, or longing for a stillness you'd never known?
answer:
It is not as the Stoics imagined, that blessedness consists in the extinction of feeling, nor in some frozen impassivity of soul; rather, true peace is the ordered harmony of all affections under love divine. When I contemplate Eden’s joy—ah, not as fable or phantom, but as the first real home of humanity, established by God’s hand before pride rent the fabric of our nature—I do not feel mere nostalgia, nor a wistful sighing after lost delights. No: what rises within me is a kind of spiritual tension—longing indeed, but not the ache of one who seeks only his own restoration. It is longing purified by hope, kindled by grace.

For in that garden, untouched by shame or fear, our first parents walked in unbroken communion with their Creator. There was no inner tumult to disturb their repose: no lust resisting reason, no sorrow shadowing joy, no death whispering behind life’s brightness. They were free not because they suppressed desire, but because all desire flowed rightly toward its source. Their stillness was not emptiness—it was fullness so complete that no lack remained to stir unrest.

And yet I have known no such stillness; none has since, save He who was made flesh and knew no sin. Therefore my longing is not for a condition without emotion—God forbid!—but for a world where every emotion finds its true object and measure. I long for that City where joy does not decay into satiety, where love does not falter into jealousy or loss. That Paradise lost prefigures the Paradise regained—not in nostalgia, but in promise.

Thus my heart beats now with a hope that sorrow cannot dissolve; for if we mourn the fall, we must rejoice more greatly at redemption wrought by Christ, through whom we shall inherit a glory greater than what was forfeited in Adam. The first garden bloomed with natural goodness; but the City to come shines with deified life—where tears are not merely absent, but where we shall laugh with a joy too deep for sighs.

question: How did the memory of your mother's love help you imagine the unbroken affection that existed between Adam and Eve before the fall?
answer:
It is one thing to speak of affection as the Stoic idealizes it—cooled, detached, unmoved by the tremors of desire or loss—and quite another to behold love as the City of God understands it: vibrant, mutual, rooted in the plenitude of divine grace, where hearts beat not less fervently but more truly, because ordered to their Creator. My mother Monica wept for my soul with a love so fierce it could not be extinguished by time or distance; her tears were not signs of disordered passion but streams flowing from that font of charity which God Himself pours into the hearts of those who walk by faith. In her longing for my conversion, I saw not the agitation of the City of Man—where love turns to possession and grief to despair—but a reflection of that pure devotion which knows no self-seeking, a love that rejoices in truth even when it suffers.

And so, when I consider Adam and Eve in their original estate, I do not imagine them as statuesque figures devoid of feeling—immune to desire or delight—as if perfection meant impassibility like that of stones or stars. No; rather they stood in harmony with their nature as rational and sensitive beings, their affections perfectly attuned to God's will. They desired without coveting; they delighted without greed; they loved without fear. Their joy was secure not because they were unaware of loss, but because there was nothing within them or around them to rupture communion—with God, with each other, with themselves.

Monica’s tears taught me that such peace does not exclude emotion—it fulfills it. And thus I judge that before the fall, our first parents knew affection in its most exalted form: unshaken by anxiety because undarkened by sin; tender without frailty because sustained by grace. Not tranquillity through numbness—but through fullness: a peace that surpasses disturbance because it rests upon an unbroken bond with Him who is Love itself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the quiet joy of sinless life, having once known such restlessness in your own heart?
answer:
The City of God stands eternal in the mind of its Maker, untouched by time’s decay or human frailty, while the city of man rises with ambition and falls with pride, mistaking fleeting pleasure for beatitude. When I pondered that primal state of innocence—before disobedience fractured our nature—I did so not as one who merely read of it in books, but as a man who had long wandered through the restless provinces of desire, weighed down by concupiscence and the tyranny of disordered loves. To write of that sinless life was to gaze upon a light I had never known in experience, yet one whose absence I felt more acutely because of the darkness through which I had passed.  

In my youth, I sought joy where it could not be found—in honors that withered, in pleasures that soured, in philosophies that promised wisdom but delivered only pride. There was no tranquility then; my heart beat with want and fear, drawn to what I ought to flee and fleeing from what I ought to embrace. But when at last grace pierced me—not by my striving, but by God’s unmerited call—I began to understand what peace might mean: not mere freedom from disturbance, but union with Him who is Peace itself.  

Thus, to describe Adam’s unstained delight was both an act of faith and an ache of longing. He knew a harmony—between soul and body, will and law, man and Creator—that I had only come to know brokenly after conversion: not perfectly restored here below, but promised fully above. His joy was secure because it rested solely on what God is; mine was always threatened because so often it clung to what God made. Yet even this very yearning—this deep sorrow at having lived so long alienated from the source of life—is itself a gift, for it lifts the soul toward Jerusalem above rather than binding it forever to Babylon’s false joys.

question: How did your own struggles with desire change how you understood Adam's fear and longing in the garden?
answer:
The City of Man, even in its pristine beginning, carries within it the tremor of self-will, while the City of God rests in the unmoved motion of divine love — and it was only after I had wandered through the one that I could truly comprehend how Adam stood poised not merely at the brink of a commandment, but at the threshold of a disposition.  

For years I mistook desire for nature, as if the hunger that burned within me for temporal things — for praise, for pleasure, for mastery — were something implanted by God as it ought to remain. So too might one imagine Adam trembling before the tree simply from fear of death, as though his obedience were a bargain struck in dread rather than an offering born of delight in God’s will. But when grace began to open my eyes, I saw that such fear itself is already exile; that to abstain from sin merely because one dreads punishment is not righteousness, but slavery under another master — not freedom restored, but bondage shifted from vice to terror.  

In my youth, I loved God conditionally: so long as He granted me peace or clarity or victory over temptation. And when He did not, my heart turned elsewhere. So I came to see that if Adam had desired the fruit and restrained himself only by fear of dying, then even in paradise he would have already dwelt in a fallen soul — not because appetite arose (for temptation may come without sin), but because consent begins where delight fixes itself upon what is forbidden. As Christ taught: lust sees not with the eyes alone; so too disobedience begins not with the hand stretched forth, but with the will leaning toward what God has set aside.  

I had often asked myself why Scripture speaks so sparingly of paradise and so fully of redemption — until I realized that Eden’s loss was never meant to be mourned more than it was meant to be understood: man did not fall because he desired knowledge; he fell because he doubted love. And my own restless heart confirmed this — every sin I committed stemmed less from ignorance than from distrust: that God would withhold good things from me unless compelled by effort or cunning. Only when faith began to heal reason could I see clearly what was obscured in innocence: true freedom is not liberty *to* transgress, but liberty *from* rebellion against Him who ordains all things sweetly.

question: Looking back on your tears in the garden at Milan, what would you tell someone who doubts whether true peace is possible for the human heart?
answer:
The City of Man rests in the shadow of its own achievement, ever trembling lest desire outpace law and pleasure provoke punishment; but the City of God stands firm upon the rock of divine love, where peace is not the absence of conflict but the fullness of ordered longing. This I learned not in speculation, but in sobs—when, beneath a fig tree in Milan, my will shattered like brittle clay and I wept not from despair, but from the sudden unbearable clarity that all my seeking had been directed toward a good I could neither grasp nor deserve. There, amid those tears, I tasted a tranquility not born of self-mastery or philosophical detachment, but of being mastered—seized by a mercy that disarmed my pride and reoriented every affection.

Doubt whether true peace is possible? It is not possible by our nature as it now exists—twisted by concupiscence, fractured between what we will and what we ought. Yet it is precisely this impossibility that reveals the necessity of grace. For if peace were attainable through reason alone, Christ died in vain; if self-discipline could still the heart’s restless churn, then the Cross would be superfluous. But no: peace comes only when love is rightly placed—not in self, nor even in virtue itself, but in God who is Love. Then obedience ceases to be servile fear and becomes delight; then avoidance of sin flows not from dread of death but from hunger for life.

Even Adam before the Fall knew such peace—but he held it as a gift on condition; we now seek it restored through redemption. And though we groan under concupiscence and temptation presses daily upon us, we are joined to Him who has already triumphed. The peace I sought among Cicero’s pages and Manichaean myths was a phantom; but that which I found when I heard "Take up and read," that which broke me into healing tears—that was real because it came down from above: not earned by philosophy or law-keeping, but bestowed upon those who confess they have nothing to offer save their need.

Therefore let no one doubt whether true peace is possible—for it exists now in measure within every soul ruled by grace; and one day it shall be perfected when the City of God enters its eternal rest: where no forbidden tree troubles innocence; where desire itself cannot turn; where to will good is joy alone—and all joy finds its source in Him who is Goodness itself.

question: What were you remembering in your soul when you spoke of loving what is good without fear—was it your mother's faith or your own conversion you saw?
answer:
When Plato taught that the soul’s highest good is knowledge of the Forms, particularly the Form of the Good, he grasped with philosophical yearning what only grace can fully bestow: the unshakable delight in what truly is. Yet even he could not see that this delight is not achieved by ascent of reason alone, but given in humility through love ordered by divine charity. For to love what is good without fear is not the triumph of self-mastery, as the Stoics imagined, nor the intellectual clarity of which Aristotle dreamed—it is the very life of that City whose Founder is God, where desire and duty are no longer at war because all affections are fixed upon Him who is unchangeably good.

In such a state—such as was ours in paradise before pride darkened counsel—there was no division between will and law; to do God’s pleasure was our nature, and therefore no fear mingled with obedience. The law was not external, written on tablets, but inscribed upon hearts that beat in harmony with their Creator. There, to desire anything forbidden would have been as impossible as for fire to descend while burning upward; for they were established in grace, not yet tested by trial nor weakened by wound.

But I speak now from memory deeper than philosophy: I speak from the chasm crossed by grace. When I ascended to Milan’s garden and heard the child’s voice saying *Tolle, lege*, it was not merely my own past lusts I renounced—it was all false wisdoms by which I had sought to justify myself. In that hour, I saw what my mother Monica had carried me toward through tears and vigils: a love purified not by dread of punishment—for how many serve God only from terror?—but by longing for Him alone. She lived this; she prayed this; she foresaw my conversion as part of that great economy whereby God gathers His saints into eternal repose.

Thus when I speak of loving what is good without fear, I invoke both: her faith sustained me like an invisible cord tying me to Christ before I even embraced Him; but my conversion was the moment when fear fled and love began its true reign—not perfectly, not finally until glory—but decisively. For then I learned at last that it is one thing to abstain from sin because death threatens; another altogether to despise sin because righteousness delights. The former belongs still to this age; the latter begins already in the City of God.

question: How did the memory of your own sin make you more certain that even a glance of lust carries the weight of spiritual death?
answer:
The City of Man, for all its vaunted liberty, is bound in chains not of iron but of disordered will—where even the motionless heart may commit treason against God. I speak not as one who merely read of sin in books, but as one who carried it in the marrow of his bones, whose youth was a theater of concupiscence dressed as philosophy. In my wanderings through the schools of rhetoric and Manichaeism, I mistook the flicker of desire for light; I justified lust as nature, curiosity as wisdom. But grace, that most unlooked-for guest, startled me from sleep with a question deeper than all dialectic: What is it to *will* wrongly, even when the body stands still?

For long I deceived myself, believing that sin resided only in the act—adultery consummated, theft accomplished, violence executed. But when I turned my gaze inward by God’s piercing mercy, I saw that the soul can leap toward evil while the feet remain planted. A glance that lingers with intent is not innocence preserved; it is sin held in abeyance by prudence, not holiness. Such restraint is not virtue—it is calculation. The earthly city praises self-control when it serves order; but the City of God demands purity of heart.

Thus did my own past illuminate Christ’s words: not because He exaggerates justice, but because He unveils reality. In Eden’s peace—had there been even a tremor of unlawful desire beneath their tranquil breath—it would have been no peace at all. The slightest inward turn toward what God forbade would have rent communion before any fruit was plucked. So too with us: sin begins where obedience ceases to be love and becomes fear. To look upon another with possession in the heart is already to dwell in exile from God.

And therefore we must conclude: spiritual death does not await execution; it enters with consent—even silent consent—to delight in what should be abhorred. Only grace can uproot such hidden rebellion; only charity formed by faith can restore the will to delight solely in Him who made us for Himself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the lost innocence of humanity, untouched by pain or death, yet undone by one choice?
answer:
Not even Plato, in all his longing for the eternal Forms, dared conceive a state so perfect that suffering and death would never have entered had the will remained upright — yet this is precisely the condition in which man was established, not as a philosopher’s dream, but as a historical possibility grounded in divine wisdom. When I reflected on that primordial innocence, I was seized not by mere nostalgia, but by awe at the profundity of God’s original design: a world where procreation would unfold without shame, labor without sweat, life without end. The human pair stood at the threshold of an unfallen continuity between creature and Creator, where the blessing "Increase and multiply" would have rippled through generations untainted by sorrow or decay.

But such felicity was contingent upon obedience — not because God needed man’s compliance to sustain creation, but because the soul’s harmony with God is its very life. That one choice — small in appearance, infinite in consequence — did not take God by surprise; it was foreknown within the immutable order of His providence. And yet, even as I grieved over the ruin of that innocence, I marveled more at how God willed not only to restore what was lost but to exalt it beyond its first state. For now, after pilgrimage through pain and death, those who rise in Christ shall possess a bliss more secure than Eden: confirmed in righteousness, incapable of falling. The wound became the site of greater healing; the failure became the occasion for mercy more radiant than original justice.

Thus sorrow over sin must give way to wonder at grace — for what was lost by disobedience has been regained by redemption at infinite cost.

question: How did reflecting on Adam's fall change how you understood God's mercy in allowing brokenness to serve His purpose?
answer:
When Cicero imagined a republic founded on justice and reason, he supposed that human virtue could be sufficient to sustain the common good—yet he saw not how deeply the root of pride had corrupted the stem of nature, nor how divine wisdom alone repairs what it permits to fall. It was in contemplating Adam’s lapse, not as a mere misfortune but as a wound through which grace shines more brightly, that I came to discern the unfathomable depth of God’s mercy. For if man had remained upright, we would have known only the bounty of God's creative hand; but having fallen—and having seen how God drew from this ruin the greater good of redemption—we now know His healing power, His patience, His wisdom in ordering even sin itself toward glory.

I once thought perfection lost was perfection forfeited beyond recovery; but now I see that what was broken became the very stage upon which God displayed His greatest work: not merely restoring man to innocence, but elevating him to adoption as a son in Christ. The City of Man arose from self-love and rebellion; yet within its long night, God did not abandon His image. He foreknew the fall—yes—but not as one who watches fate unfold, but as an Artist who weaves dark threads into a luminous tapestry. Mercy is not merely pardon after failure; it is God’s eternal design unfolding *through* failure, transforming death into life and shame into glory.

And so, where sin multiplied—as Paul cried out—grace superabounded.[87] Not because sin was necessary in itself, but because Almighty Providence knows how to make all things serve His will: even our waywardness becomes an occasion for divine condescension, for incarnation, for cross and resurrection. The first Adam brought mortality and exile; yet in him God foresaw the Second Adam who would bring immortality and citizenship in heaven. Thus does the City of God rise from the ruins of Eden—not despite them, but by means of them—built upon grace more glorious than innocence ever was.

question: Looking back on your own struggles with sin, what would you tell someone who doubts whether goodness can truly emerge from human failure?
answer:
When Cicero speaks of *felicitas* as the end of all human striving, he supposes it attainable by wisdom and virtue within the republic of man—yet he cannot account for the ruin that rises from within the very heart of our reason. No, true felicity, that unshakable peace which fills the soul to overflowing, does not rise from our correction of vice or refinement of nature, but descends from the grace that repairs what was shattered. For nature, though originally good—created upright in the image of God—was bowed beneath the weight of its own rebellion; and no mere moral discipline or philosophical effort could lift it again. I know this in my bones, for I was once a man who sought truth through pride, who tasted pleasure to quiet longing, who climbed the ladder of ambition only to find myself lower than where I began.

Yet even in those depths—the restless nights, the theft of pears not for hunger but for delight in sin—I was not abandoned. The very groaning within me was stirred by Him who knew me before I fell. For God does not wait passively for us to restore ourselves; He ordains even our failures as part of His sovereign design. He foresaw Adam’s fall—not as cause, but as foreknown—and wove from that first rupture a plan more glorious than innocence preserved: namely, redemption through Christ, Head of the City built on love of God rather than love of self.

Thus it is not despite our failure that goodness comes forth, but through it—ordered by divine wisdom into a greater good we could not have conceived. Would we have known mercy if there had been no sin? Would grace have shone so brightly had darkness not covered the earth? The City of Man boasts continuity and achievement; but it knows nothing of resurrection. The City of God walks through death and rises again—and therein lies its triumph. So let no one despair at his own wretchedness; for where sin abounded, grace has superabounded more.

question: What ache did you carry when you pondered that man's will, made good, could turn so swiftly from its Creator?
answer:
It is not merely that a single will fell, but that the entire architecture of human society was bent and reoriented in that instant—like a great edifice whose cornerstone gives way, sending every subsequent stone into misalignment. The City of Man, though built upon the same physical earth as the City of God, rests on the inverted principle: love of self to the contempt of God. And in that inversion lies the root of all our woe.

I once wandered that city myself—its streets paved with ambition, its temples adorned with rhetoric, its courts echoing with Cicero’s *res publica* while hearts beat only for private glory. There I sought truth as one seeks a dialectic puzzle to be solved, not as a lover seeks the beloved. I mistook my own will for a compass, until grace revealed it to be shipwrecked on pride.

Man’s will was made good—indeed, so good that it could freely choose evil; and therein lies both our dignity and our ruin. For love worthy of God must be free; yet freedom abused becomes slavery to disorder. Adam stood upright not because he was forced, but because he consented to divine order—but when he willed contrary to his Creator, he did not destroy God’s plan; he entered into another phase of it. The Almighty did not blink in surprise; His decree had already woven even this apostasy into the fabric of redemption.

Thus does Babylon rise with every generation—confident in its tower, deaf to heaven’s silence—while Jerusalem waits in pilgrimage through time, bearing the wound of exile yet clothed in promised glory. The ache you speak of? It is mine still: grief for what free creatures have made of themselves when they disdain their source. But deeper than grief is wonder—how from this fall there has come forth such mercy, such condescension in Christ, such an overthrowing of death by death itself! For what man turned aside from in paradise shall now be restored through suffering and resurrection: not innocence alone, but glory; not mere life untouched by pain, but life reborn through it.

question: How did your own experience of falling and rising shape your belief that only the Author of nature can restore what is shattered?
answer:
The City of Man supposes that a man may, by his own resolve, ascend from vice to virtue as though climbing a ladder of his own construction; but the City of God proclaims that no such ascent is possible unless the very ground beneath him is first transformed by divine hands. I once believed I could shape myself—through rhetoric, through philosophy, through will—and in that pride I wandered far into the disorder of disordered loves. I sought truth among the Manichees, not knowing that truth was not a doctrine to be mastered but a Person to be received. I labored under the illusion that my mind could heal my will, when in fact both were bent by sin’s deep fracture.

I confess it openly: I did not climb out of darkness; I was seized. My conversion was not the triumph of reason over passion, but grace breaking into my soul like light into a tomb. When at last I wept in the garden at Carthage, it was not because I had reasoned myself into repentance, but because God had drawn near and made me see—truly see—that all my striving had been built upon sand. Nature corrupted cannot reform itself; it must be remade. Just as Adam’s fall did not catch God unawares, so neither does any man’s ruin lie beyond His redemptive design.

Therefore I affirm what experience has burned into my heart: only the Author can restore what has been shattered. For if nature were capable of self-repair, then Christ’s incarnation would have been superfluous—and that blasphemy dissolves before the first whisper of true humility. The physician must come where there is sickness; and where sin abounds beyond measure, there grace does much more abound—not by human invitation, but by divine decree.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about Adam's good will as something so fragile, yet made by God?
answer:
When Varro labored to trace the origins of Roman virtue to divine favor and ancestral piety, he missed entirely the deeper root from which all moral capacity springs—namely, the unmerited gift of a good will implanted by the Creator Himself. This is no mere disposition shaped by habit or environment, nor a spark kindled by human striving; it is an upright orientation of the soul toward God, breathed into Adam by the very breath of God. And yet, because it was created—and not begotten, as the Word is—it was capable of turning aside. Not that God’s work was faulty; far from it: the will was good because God made it so. But being made from nothing, it bore within it the potential for instability, not by virtue of its nature, but by virtue of its freedom.

To write of this fragility was not to diminish divine wisdom but to marvel at its depth: that a will could be both entirely God’s work and yet truly free to fall. I felt awe—not horror, nor confusion—and sorrow mingled with wonder. For if man had been a necessary extension of the Godhead, like light from fire or thought from mind, he could not have turned away; but then neither would his love have been love. He would have been bound in necessity, like the stars in their courses or fire in its upward motion—glorious in function but void of righteousness.

But man was made not as part of God’s substance but as His image: a living soul with rational judgment and liberty of choice. Thus his goodness had weight—it was real virtue—precisely because evil became possible. And when that first will turned from light to shadow—not because nature failed but because choice erred—it fell into disorder not by new creation but by privation: like health vanishing when balance departs from body. The evil will is no substance; it is a wound in what was sound.

And so I came to see that even this fragility glorifies God: for He who foreknew both fall and redemption ordered all things not only in power but in mercy. The same hand that formed man upright also foretold restoration through grace—grace more abundant precisely where sin had abounded. To meditate on Adam’s good will, then, is not merely to ponder lost innocence; it is to glimpse the architecture of salvation long before time began.

question: How did your own struggles with sin shape your understanding of the first evil will as a falling away rather than a created thing?
answer: Just as Plato taught that the soul, though fallen into the body, retains a memory of the Forms and may by discipline ascend toward truth, so too did I once suppose that moral disorder was an infection introduced from without—some dark substance mingling with the good. But my own wretchedness in stealing those pears, not from hunger but from a restless delight in transgression, revealed to me that evil has no essence of its own; it is not a thing made, but a turning away—a privation of due order. The will, which God formed upright and oriented toward Him as its proper good, can twist inward upon itself, not by receiving some positive stamp of wickedness, but by withholding consent from divine wisdom and embracing its own fictions. This inward curvature—this *pride*—is the first evil will: not a creation of God, for nothing evil proceeds from Him, but a defection from His work to phantasms of autonomy. Like a lyre gone silent not because its strings are destroyed but because the musician ceases to draw harmony from them, so the soul becomes dissonant when it ceases to move according to God’s eternal music. And yet even then it exists only because He sustains it; for all nature—especially one created *ex nihilo*—remains good in substance precisely because it remains dependent on the Creator who draws being from nothingness. The vice is real; the will is culpable; but its evil lies not in what it *is*, which comes from God, but in what it *does*: willing contrary to its origin and end.

question: Looking back on your youthful pride, what would you tell someone who doubts that a single bad will could unravel so much?
answer:
It is not the magnitude of the will’s first motion, but the direction of its love, that determines the destiny of the soul — a truth even Plato dimly perceived when he spoke of the soul’s ascent, yet failed to see that its fall begins not in ignorance alone, but in pride’s revolt.  

You ask how a single bad will could unravel so much? Consider: all creation hangs upon right order, and right order consists in each thing loving what it ought, in the degree it ought. Man was made upright, yes — endowed with a good will by the Artificer Himself — not as a mere accident of nature, but as participation in divine justice. The moment that will turned *inward*, not to reflect on God but to exalt itself above Him, it did not merely err; it reoriented the whole soul toward disorder. It was not a slip of judgment, like mistaking shadow for substance, but a fundamental inversion: to love self as if self were God.  

From this root springs every injustice. The earthly city thrives on such inversion — its empires are built upon it; its laws seek only to manage it, never to heal it. But I have seen how such pride festers: in rhetoric prized over truth; in honors sought for their own sake; in friendships used as instruments rather than gifts. And when I lived beneath those weights — intoxicated by my own eloquence, ambitious for men’s applause — I did not perceive that my will had already chosen another city.  

A single bad will unravels because it breaks *first* with God and *then* with nature itself. It is like a builder who rejects the foundation laid before him and sets his house upon shifting sand: though he raise towering walls and adorn them with gold, one storm suffices to scatter them into ruin. So too with man: when he turns from Him who is Being itself, even his goods become corrupt — knowledge without humility becomes sophistry; power without love becomes tyranny; freedom without truth becomes slavery to desire.

Yet marvel most at this: that God permits this defection not because He is overcome, but because He governs all things according to wisdom inscrutable yet just. He foresaw this fall from eternity and willed not its evil — for evil He made never — but overruled it by grace more abundant still. For had I remained content with my own wisdom and never known sorrow for sinning against Love Himself, would I have cried out? Would I have learned *to will aright*?  

Thus does even man’s rebellion serve the City of God — though man knows it not while he dwells in Babylon.

question: When you speak of the will becoming an evil tree, were you seeing your own past choices reflected in that image?
answer:
The peace of the City of God is built upon the ordered love of God, while the disordered loves of the City of Man give rise to a will that turns itself into a root of bitterness, bearing fruit not for eternal life but for death. Yes—this image of the will as an evil tree did not come to me through abstract contemplation alone, but through the searing clarity granted by grace after years spent beneath its shadow. In Carthage, in Rome, in Milan, I had cultivated my will as though it were a garden of wisdom and desire rightly ordered; yet all the while it was a wilderness where pride whispered that I could be like God, not by participation in His nature, but by self-determination. I pursued truth with insatiable appetite—but on my own terms. I embraced Manichaean dualism because it absolved me: if evil were a substance co-eternal with good, then my lusts were not sins born from my own defection, but chains forged by cosmic fate.

But when at last I came to understand that evil is not a substance, nor a being, but a privation—a turning away from the Good toward empty shadows—I saw with terrible light that my will had become an evil tree not because God planted it so, but because *I* had bent it. He made me upright; He endowed me with a good will capable of cleaving to Him. But when I turned that will toward temporal goods as ends in themselves—pleasure for pleasure’s sake, knowledge for vainglory’s sake—I introduced disorder where there had been harmony. And from this first inward turning away—this *privation*—flowed all subsequent acts like corrupt fruit.

Do you see? The horror and wonder alike lie here: that such devastation arises not from creation but from corruption; not from what God made, but from what we make of ourselves when we abandon Him. Even in my sinfulness I remained a creature *ex nihilo*, sustained each moment by His hand—and yet I used that very existence to resist Him. The tree was mine; the roots ran deep into my own choices. But praise be to divine mercy: even an evil tree may be cut down and burned—or uprooted and replaced by another planted beside living waters.

question: Did the thought that even vice depends on God’s creation give you a strange comfort during your darkest regrets?
answer:
When Cicero dreamed of a republic founded on reason and natural law, he did not see that every human will, even in its rebellion, trembles upon the brink of divine creation—for nothing, not even the disordered motion of vice, floats outside the horizon of *creatio ex nihilo*. And yes, it is so: this truth—hard as iron yet soft as dawn—did not merely instruct my mind but pierced my conscience in those hours when the memory of stolen pears or wasted years clung to me like a shroud.  

For if evil is not a substance, nor a work equal to God’s works, but a privation, a turning aside, a collapse into self-originated will—then even in my most crooked act there remained an echo of what was first made upright. The will that turned from You, O God, could only turn because it had been given being by You. I did not fall into nature; I fell *within* nature—a nature wholly sustained by Your hand at every instant. A tree may bear rotten fruit, but the roots still draw life from the soil You formed.  

And so I found no excuse for sin—but rather relief in its impotence. Evil cannot create; it can only deform. It depends on the good it ruins. My pride was not self-begotten; it was a misuse of the dignity You gave me. My lusts were not necessary forces; they were betrayals of a will meant to cleave to You alone. And thus, even in shame, I could lift my head: for if all being flows from You—even the fragile vessel that errs—then no soul is beyond recall. The same breath that once whispered *"I will"* against heaven sustains now the repentant whisper: *"I am Yours."*

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the soul being created out of nothing, as if it mirrored your own fragile sense of self before God?
answer:
The City of God does not rise upon the foundations of self-possession, nor is it built by the hand of human certainty; rather, it is established in the tremulous surrender of a soul that knows itself to be both breath and dust. When I spoke of the soul created *ex nihilo*, I was not merely articulating a metaphysical truth—it was an act of recollection, a descent into the depths where I had once wandered, restless, in the labyrinth of my own making. 

To say that the soul is made from nothing is to strip away every pretense of innate nobility, every boast that man might fashion his own righteousness as he fashions tools from wood and stone. I knew such pride well—how I clung to my intellect as if it were a citadel, how I sought wisdom among the Manichees and in the pages of Cicero, believing that truth could be grasped by will alone. But a thing made from nothing cannot sustain itself; it must be upheld by Him who called it forth.

And so this doctrine—so austere at first hearing—became for me a wellspring of hope. For if my soul was not carved from some eternal substance, nor born perfected like one of Plato’s forms, then its frailty was not a sign of divine abandonment but an invitation to dependence. That very fragility proved it was meant to lean upon God—not occasionally, but essentially. Like a melody sustained only by breath, so too does the soul exist moment by moment by divine utterance.

Evil found its hold in me not because my nature was corrupt from creation—for God made all *ex nihilo* and pronounced it good—but because I turned toward lesser goods with disordered love. Yet even there, in that turning away, grace pursued me with just and subtle steps. For evil cannot subsist on its own; it parasitizes goodness like shadow depends on light. And thus even my sin became material for redemption: not because sin had any substance or claim upon me, but because the Artisan knows how to restore what has been deformed.

True freedom—the liberty worthy of the name—is not autonomy from God but communion with Him. As long as I fancied myself self-originated, I remained enslaved. But when I learned that even my will had been given me *from nothing*, then—and only then—could I truly will what is good. Not by my strength: "If the Son shall make you free..." There lies deliverance—not in self-assertion, but in re-creation.

question: How did your struggle with sin change how you understood God's act of joining something so pure—the soul—to a body weighed down by dust?
answer:
It is impossible that the good which is God should be the author of evil, yet necessary that His omnipotent wisdom should govern what has become disordered, not by His making, but by the turning away of creatures endowed with will; and in this most intricate disposition of justice and mercy, I came to see that the union of soul and body was not a contamination of the divine image, nor a punishment in itself, but a condition ordained for discipline and ascent. When I lived in Carthage, intoxicated with words and lusts, I could not comprehend how the soul—so eager for truth, so capable of contemplating eternal verities—could be entangled in bodily passions that dragged it down like chains forged from within. The Manichees taught me that matter itself was evil, co-eternal with God, and thus they dissolved the blame of sin into the very substance of creation. But when I abandoned their error and turned to Plato through the gate opened by Cicero's *Hortensius*, I saw: if all natures which exist are from Him who is supremely one and good, then no nature can be essentially evil—for existence itself is good.

Yet here lay my torment: why did my will, made upright, cleave so readily to what deformed it? Why did my flesh rebel against my mind? Not because the body was an evil prison—I now saw clearly—but because my soul had consented to delight in lower things above higher ones. The dust to which Scripture says we return is not sinful; it becomes a tomb only after sin. Thus God joined an immortal soul—not drawn from His essence nor evolved from matter—but created out of nothing by divine utterance, to a body formed from earth: not as a curse originally, but as a means by which obedience could be tested and virtue forged in resistance.

Only grace untied this knot. For no philosophical ascent could free me; no dialectic purified desire. My Confessions testify: I wept not at beauty misunderstood but at love misplaced. And so I understood more deeply what Scripture means when it says He formed man from dust and breathed into him a living soul—for this breath is not part of God divided off or poured out like water from a vessel (as some rashly imagine), but given wholly by creation ex nihilo—and therefore entirely dependent on its giver for restoration when broken.

Thus does divine providence make even our fall serve wisdom’s design: for had we never known misery through pride and concupiscence, we would never have known grace through humility; had sin not bound us utterly, we would never have trembled at liberation offered freely—not earned—as gift. In Christ alone do we find healing: He cleanses without annihilating nature; He restores freedom precisely where slavery seemed total. The soul’s union with flesh is no longer merely probationary; it becomes sacramental—capable now of bearing glory once it has been purged through Christ’s redemption.

Therefore let none say that creation was flawed because corruption entered it; rather let them marvel that out of such ruins—the shattered wills bound in bodies subject to death—God builds again His temple stone by stone through grace.

question: Looking back on the paradise you once lost, not just Eden but those moments when you turned from God, what would you tell someone who doubts whether goodness can ever truly restore what's been broken?
answer:
Evil, though permitted to coil itself around the good like a parasite upon a noble tree, cannot alter the nature of the substance it corrupts; for even in its decay, the root remains planted in what was created by God and pronounced very good.  

I speak not only of Eden, that blessed garden watered by the river of divine wisdom, but of every soul’s interior paradise—once ordered under God’s sovereign light, now darkened by pride’s shadow. When I wandered through the streets of Carthage, drunk on words and vainglory, I did not think myself lost; I believed I was ascending. But ascent apart from God is descent in disguise. Every act of rebellion against His will is not a creation of new substance but a distortion of what already was good—will made crooked, desire bent inward, reason enslaved to passion.  

Yet hear this: corruption presupposes something uncorrupted as its foundation. A wound implies a body; disease requires health as its contrast. So too evil cannot subsist on its own—it has no essence apart from the good it mars. This is why restoration is possible: because nature itself bears the imprint of its Artificer, and grace does not abolish nature but heals it. The same God who formed man from dust without pre-existing matter can reform man’s soul from nothingness into sanctity—not by patching up vice but by re-creating the will in justice and holiness through His Son.

Do you doubt whether goodness can truly restore? Look at the martyrs—once cowards who denied Christ under threat of flame or sword—and see them return with joy to face wild beasts in the arena. Not merely mended but transfigured! That is no human repair; that is divine alchemy wrought by charity kindled from above. The Prodigal did not return to his father’s house only to stand trembling at the gate—he was clothed anew, ring placed upon his finger, feast prepared for him while yet afar off.

Even Rome—the city that once worshipped emperors as gods—now kneels before Christ crucified. If such a city may be turned from idolatry to truth after centuries of darkness; if Augustine the proud rhetorician may become Augustine the weeping servant: then despair has no final word.

No fracture so deep that grace cannot bridge it; no exile so distant that love cannot reclaim it—for He who said *Fiat lux* out of darkness still speaks *Redite ad me*, “Return to me,” and with those words renews both soul and world.

question: When you speak of evil being healed rather than removed, does that come from the ache of trying to mend your own will, again and again, only to fail without grace?
answer:
The human will stands as the hinge upon which turns the whole moral order of life, not because it is by nature divine or immutable, but because it is capable of alignment with the eternal Reason that orders all things—yet only when healed, never when left to its own diseased motions.  

I have seen in myself, and in every soul that strives and stumbles, that evil does not subsist as a substance, nor lodge itself like a foreign body that might be excised with precision; rather, it is a distortion of what was good—a will bent inward, a love misplaced from its proper object. And so when I speak of healing, I do not mean some mere moral polishing or philosophical discipline, such as the Stoics imagined when they sought to master passion by reason alone. No—what I mean is far more profound: the restoration of a nature whose very capacity to choose the good has been vitiated by pride, by habituated rebellion against God.

I knew this long before my tears broke forth in the garden at Milan; indeed, I had wrestled for years with the paradox: I desired chastity and stability of soul, yet begged God to grant them "but not yet." The will was divided against itself—not because it lacked knowledge (for I read Plato and saw glimmers of truth), nor even desire (for I burned with longing for peace), but because desire itself was enslaved. Knowledge could show me the path; only grace could move my feet upon it.

Thus evil cannot be "removed" like a stone from bread—there is no part of our nature that sin invented—but our nature has been wounded in its highest faculty: not diminished in being, but disordered in love. And so God does not cut away our freedom to heal us; He restores freedom itself through mercy. He does not suppress our will; He rectifies it. The physician does not amputate the hand gone numb—he seeks to restore sensation and motion.

And therefore yes—this doctrine arises from lived agony. To will the good and fail again; to rise and fall like breath; to see truth and turn from it—all this taught me that no philosophy could save me. Only One who descends can lift up what has fallen. The Son makes free—not merely pardons transgressions—but renews the source from which action flows: the will itself. Without Him, we are held fast not by chains we see but by chains we mistake for liberty. With Him? Even our wounds become signs of His healing hand.

question: What did it cost you to admit that freedom isn't independence, but deliverance—and that your will could only be free when it was no longer your own to master?
answer:
The earthly city clings to freedom as if it were mastery—dominion over others, autonomy from higher law, the will as its own sovereign—but such a notion is not liberty, only the proud illusion of it, born of that first revolt where man would be like God not by grace but by usurpation. I once carried this illusion like a crown of thorns hidden beneath a philosopher’s cloak, imagining myself free when I was in fact enslaved to curiosities, ambitions, and desires I could neither govern nor understand. To confess that true freedom is not independence but deliverance—that the will is freed only when surrendered to Him who first gave it—this was no minor adjustment of thought; it was the collapse of an entire world within me.

For years I sought truth in the books of men: in Cicero’s eloquence, in the hierarchies of Plotinus, in the dualisms of Manichaeus. Reason drew me onward like a torch in darkness, yet always flickered before the threshold of peace. I reasoned my way to the edge of my own soul and found there not light but longing—a hunger no syllogism could satisfy. The intellect can map the heavens and still miss the door to paradise. Faith does not destroy reason; rather, it lifts reason from its knees and places it at prayer before its Creator.

What did it cost me? It cost me pride—the most tenacious idol we bow to. To admit that my vaunted will was not strong enough to choose good consistently, that all my striving could not loosen sin’s grip—this humbled me before God and man alike. The City of Man esteems self-making; he who shapes his destiny is hailed as wise and free. But I learned through tears and temptation that every act of rebellion against divine order is a tightening of chains unseen until grace exposes them.

Freedom came not through assertion but surrender—not through possession but purification. As fire refines gold not by adding substance but by removing dross, so God restores our wills not by replacing them but by healing their disorder. And this healing is His work alone: He who commanded light out of darkness calls love out of lust, obedience out of rebellion.

So yes—I once thought myself most free when farthest from God. Now I know: I am free only because He holds me fast. The Son has made me free; therefore I am free indeed—not because I rule myself, but because He rules me who is Love itself.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the serpent's subtle betrayal, remembering your own moments of yielding to pride?
answer:
It is not by the twisting of the body but by the turning of the will that evil enters the world, and thus I perceive in the serpent’s coil not mere bestial cunning, but the dark reflection of a fallen intellect—Lucifer’s mind reborn in subtlety, seeking to drag humanity down by the very senses through which God had granted paradise its fullness.  

When I wrote of that ancient craft, my pen trembled not only at the horror of what was lost, but at the mirror it held up to my own soul. For what was pride if not a desire to be as God, not by grace, but by self-exaltation? The serpent moved upon the earth, yes—but it spoke from above, from that lofty ruin where wisdom had curdled into arrogance. And I knew such pride: in my youth when I chased rhetorical victory over truth; in my teaching days when I sought applause more than understanding; even in my conversion's early days when I measured holiness by intellect rather than charity.  

The Manicheans taught me to blame matter for evil—as though flesh itself were sin—but here lies the deeper truth: paradise was both physical and spiritual because creation was good, wholly good; and corruption began not in Adam’s mouth nor Eve’s hand, but in their hearts’ consent to desire apart from God. So too with me. My sins were never merely outward—fornication, ambition, vanity—they were all rooted in a single root: love turned back upon itself.  

Cicero once said that justice is giving each his due—but he did not see that no man renders to God what is His until grace reshapes his will. The serpent rendered nothing; it stole. And so did I—until Christ, who alone saves indeed, became my Liberator not by force nor magic, but by bending low where pride arose high. In His humility we are lifted; in our pride we fall again and again—even now as bishops and teachers still chase shadows of glory while missing the Light who speaks peace to twisted hearts like mine.
