question: How did your struggle with pride change how you understood God's humility in the Son's saving work?
answer:
It is the proud who first fracture the harmony of creation, yet it is the humble who restore it—thus does the City of God unfold its true form not in the ascent of man to dominion, but in the descent of God to service. I knew well the fever of pride, having once scaled the ladders of rhetoric and ambition, seeking not truth but acclaim, desiring to be admired rather than healed. In those years, I mistook brilliance for wisdom and eloquence for virtue, fashioning myself after Cicero’s ideal orator, believing that reason elevated by eloquence could deliver the soul. But what good is a golden voice when the heart is in chains?

I came to see—through long wrestling with Scripture and my own restless conscience—that my pride was not merely a vice among others; it was the primal deformation, mirroring that ancient fall wherein a being greater than I—an angelic mind luminous with divine grace—recoiled from subjection to God and sought a counterfeit kingdom. That rebellion did not remain in heaven; it slithered into Eden through the serpent’s mouth, not because the beast desired evil, but because pride had already made a throne in the unseen realm from which it could poison both angel and man.

When at last grace pierced me—a sinner bent on self-glorification—I beheld in Christ something no philosophy had prepared me for: a God who does not thunder from Olympus nor hover beyond matter like Plato’s Form of the Good, but who *becomes flesh*, who suffers hunger, weariness, betrayal—and death upon a cross. This was folly to Cicero’s republic; this was scandal to Varro’s antiquarians. Yet here alone was salvation: for if liberation required only knowledge, then Plato might suffice. But we were not only ignorant—we were enslaved. And slaves do not free themselves.

The Son saves indeed—not by overpowering us with majesty from above, but by entering beneath us, taking on our mortality, our shame, our very bondage. He liberates as servant-King: reigning not from an earthly throne but from a cross; conquering sin not by decree but by humility; restoring Paradise not by restoring Eden’s trees but by becoming Tree Himself—the true wood upon which life springs forth anew.

Thus did my understanding turn wholly upside down: where I once thought divinity meant distance and splendor unapproachable, I now see that its deepest truth is nearness and kenosis—self-emptying love. The City of Man builds towers to reach heaven; the City of God descends into hell to lift us up. And only when pride lies broken within can one truly believe that God saves best by seeming weakest.

question: Looking back on your time in Carthage, chasing empty honors, what would you tell someone who doubts they can ever truly change?
answer:
The Roman orator dreams of a republic founded on virtue, yet cannot see that no polity of men shall stand unless it be first rooted in humility, which the proud cannot bear, and which I too once scorned in the noisy schools of Carthage, where I sought applause more than truth.

Do you doubt whether change is possible? Then let me tell you what I myself was: a man drunk with words, intoxicated by the breath of crowds, peddling rhetoric like a merchant trades in spices—empty vessels clinking for show. I wore my intellect as armor against God; I mocked faith while craving peace; I chased the shadow of glory because the light of love terrified me with its demand. Was not my heart also stone? Was not my will twisted into knots by pride and lust? And yet—here is the wonder—the very God I fled pursued me not with thunder but with silence, not with force but with longing. He did not storm my fortress; He whispered through the cracks in my arrogance until even my pleasures tasted like ash.

You speak of change as if it were a ladder your hands must climb—but what is man to lift himself by his own straps? No, grace is not earned; it descends. It seized me in a garden once—yes, another garden—where tears broke forth like springs from dry earth. There I heard a voice: *Tolle, lege.* Take and read. And from that moment, though the old habits clung like fever-sweat, a new will had been born within me—a will not merely to be praised among men, but to belong wholly to Him who made me.

Change is not reformation; it is rebirth. The serpent may coil through paradise and infect our nature, but Christ—the Second Adam—has trampled him beneath His heel. What you were does not bind you if you are His. The City of Man builds monuments to itself and calls them eternal; but only the City of God raises up ruined souls and calls them sons.

question: What longing did you carry, knowing Eden was both physical and spiritual, while you wandered in cities that fed neither your body nor your soul?
answer:
The City of God knows no hunger, for its bread is truth and its wine the eternal Word; whereas the city of man, though it brims with markets and temples, offers only dust to the lips of the soul. When I wandered through Carthage and Rome—those theatres of ambition, those mazes of desire—I carried within me a longing not merely for bread that perishes, nor yet for philosophical speculation alone, but for that unity of body and spirit which once flourished in Paradise, where the tree of life was as real as its fruit and as radiant as its meaning.

I sought in rhetoric what only grace could give. I drank from cisterns cracked by pride, thinking words could satiate what only Wisdom incarnate could fulfill. The philosophers—Plato especially—caught glimpses of that higher realm where the intelligible light shines apart from earthly shadows; they spoke nobly of forms and ideas, yet their souls remained suspended between ascent and return, like birds circling a mountain they dare not land upon. But Eden was no mere allegory to be deciphered by dialectic: it was a place where flesh walked with God in the cool of the day, where knowledge did not divide from love, nor pleasure from holiness.

In the cities I knew—the Babylonian throngs of ambition and lust—I found men cultivating gardens for delight but neglecting their own hearts. They built forums to secure justice but sowed injustice in their very foundations. And I with them: feeding the body with spectacles, starving the soul with vanity. The serpent had long since slithered through paradise with crooked speech; now he whispers in marketplaces and lecture halls alike, teaching men to doubt not only God’s command but even their own disquiet.

But ah!—to remember that paradise was *both*! That Adam’s joy was not divided between sense and spirit as ours so painfully is—that he saw beauty *with* his eyes and knew it *by* his mind as sacrament—all this revealed to me later how deeply we have fallen: not just into sin, but into fragmentation. To hunger now is to hunger doubly; to thirst is to thirst at two fountains—one real, one shadow—and find neither sufficient.

Yet even in my pilgrimage through these broken cities, there glimmered a promise: that He who expelled us with justice would open again the gates through mercy; that the Liberator would come not only to restore paradise but to raise it into something greater—the Jerusalem above, whose foundations are mercy and whose light needs neither sun nor moon. This hope sustained me when all other feasts turned ashes: that we were made not merely to eat under trees in innocence, but one day to banquet at the marriage supper of the Lamb—in body raised and spirit glorified—in perfect communion without end.

question: How did the memory of your mother's prayers shape your view of God's gentle persistence when you were still caught in winding deceptions?
answer:
When the soul is held fast in the labyrinth of its own devising, no merely human voice can reach its center—yet even there, the breath of a mother’s prayer moves like a hidden wind through its corridors. I once imagined myself wise among the wise, fluent in rhetoric, master of my own ascent in the city of man; and all the while, that serpent of pride coiled about my heart, whispering that truth could be grasped by intellect alone, that grace was unnecessary to one so well armed with argument. Like Cicero, who stirred me toward philosophy with his *Hortensius*, I sought wisdom as a prize to be seized by will and wit. But wisdom is not taken; she descends. She comes not upon demand but by invitation—divine and unmerited.

My mother Monica did not understand the intricacies of Manichaean cosmology or the subtleties of Academic skepticism in which I then luxuriated. Yet her tears before the altar at Carthage were more eloquent than any syllogism I had ever crafted. Night after night she wept for me as one already dead and buried in error—yet for God to raise up again. Her prayers were not petitions flung into silence; they were threads woven by divine providence into the very fabric of my conversion. For God does not merely hear prayer—He works through it. Her intercession was not passive hope but active participation in the economy of grace.

Thus I came to see that no man escapes sin by sheer insight or effort; if he thinks so, he walks in darkness and calls it light. The serpent deceived Eve with an appearance of reason—he who fell because he would not serve became teacher to humanity that service is shame—but Christ, though He was God, took the form of a servant. In this alone is liberation: to be saved *by Another*. My mother knew this long before I did. Her humility was her strength; her tears were weapons wielded by God against my pride.

And so it stands: where philosophy wound upward through speculation, grace descended straight into reality—not circumventing reason but fulfilling it beyond its limits. The City of Man builds towers toward heaven to declare its autonomy; but the City of God rises only where men kneel and mothers weep—and where Christ opens His hands upon the cross as Liberator indeed.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about Adam's decision to join the woman in sin, knowing it meant rebellion against God?
answer:
It is not by the logic of the Stoic, who would have virtue stand immutable even in isolation, nor by the calculus of the Epicurean, who measures all action by pleasure and pain, that we must judge Adam’s act; rather, it is from the height of divine revelation that we see how love—perverted though it may be—can become the instrument of ruin when disordered by pride and severed from obedience. When I wrote of Adam’s decision, I was not unmoved by the tragic symmetry of his choice: he did not fall through ignorance, as Eve did, beguiled by the serpent’s cunning; no, he fell with clarity, with full vision of God’s commandment standing before his mind’s eye. And yet—he chose to break it. Not because he doubted, but because he loved.  

But mark this well: it was not love in its true form—for love ordered would have rebuked and guarded her—but love made servile, love corrupted into attachment to a creature above the Creator. He saw the woman turn from God and feared a deeper loss than death: the loss of communion with her. Thus did he place a human bond above divine commandment. In that moment, though his reason discerned truth from falsehood clearly as day from night, his will bowed to affection over justice. This is not innocence—far from it—but a sin more terrible in its awareness. For no man sins more grievously than he who sins knowing precisely what he destroys.

And so I felt a profound sorrow when writing of this mystery—not only for Adam’s fall but for all those countless souls since who have chosen lesser goods in preference to God Himself; spouses over salvation; friendship over truth; peace at any price rather than holiness at great cost. The City of Man begins here—not in open rebellion alone, but in that quiet resignation whereby we consent to evil out of loyalty to one another. But let us never forget: such loyalty without truth becomes complicity; and solidarity without righteousness is only organized ruin.

question: How did your own struggles with lust and human attachment help you understand Adam's choice to prioritize union over obedience?
answer:
The City of Man is ever built upon the trembling foundation of human loyalty mistaken for love, where the desire for unity with another so often supersedes the duty owed to eternal truth. In this, I have seen with piercing clarity the mirror of my own sins and the tragic dignity of Adam’s fall. For just as I once clung to those affections which bound me not to God but to the fleeting warmth of human presence—when in Carthage I lived enslaved to concupiscence, calling disorder love—so too did Adam choose communion with his wife over obedience to his Creator, not because he was blind, but because he could not bear solitude.  

I knew such weakness not merely in theory, but in the marrow of my soul. When my mother wept for my soul and God’s servants urged me toward continence, I cried out, “Give me chastity—but not yet.” Why? Because I feared separation—not only from the woman who bore me a son, but from that entire world woven by mutual desire and shared transgression. I loved the bond more than the law; like Adam, I chose solidarity in sin over solitary righteousness. And though Adam stood guiltless of deception—he saw clearly what was commanded—his will bent under the weight of intimacy misdirected. So too did mine: fully aware that God alone could satisfy, yet still turning back to embrace what only mocked fulfillment.

Thus do both our stories unfold within the great drama between the two cities: one founded on self-love reaching even to contempt for God, the other on divine love reaching even to contempt for self. Adam’s act was no mere capitulation; it was a sacrilege born of devotion turned inward—a man obeying not falsehood, but his own heart now disordered by attachment more powerful than awe. And herein lies our common shame: we are most vulnerable not when we doubt God’s word, but when we value any creature as if it were irreplaceable apart from Him. The serpent needed only subdue one member of that primal alliance; he knew human nature well enough—that love untethered from divine justice becomes itself an instrument of ruin.

question: Looking back on your time in Carthage, chasing approval and caught in tangled relationships, what would you tell someone who doubts whether love can exist without distortion?
answer:
Justice, which even Cicero sought as the bond of human fellowship, cannot be maintained where love is not rightly ordered toward God, for all disordered loves become tyrants rather than guides. I speak not merely from doctrine but from the stench of my own restless youth in Carthage—where I chased applause in rhetorical contests, where I bound myself to affections that left me emptier than before, where I mistook appetite for intimacy and companionship for communion. There I learned—not once, but daily—that every love not rooted in the immutable Good bends inward and corrupts its object. 

You ask whether love can exist without distortion? It can—but only when it ascends rather than grasps. The Platonists taught ascent to the Form of the Good, yet never saw that Love Himself walked among us, suffered, rose again. They loved wisdom but did not worship the Wisdom by which all things were made. So too, human affections rise or fall according to their end: if they seek possession, they enslave; if they seek union in God, they liberate.

I sinned with my eyes open—not because I was blind to truth, but because I loved myself more than I loved Truth. And so it was with Adam: not deceived in intellect, but undone by misdirected loyalty. He chose unity with Eve over obedience to God—and therein lies the tragedy: a good impulse (love of spouse) turned fatal by placing creaturely peace above divine justice.

But hear this: grace restores order. When love is baptized—when it is surrendered to Him who first loved us—it no longer demands or consumes; it gives freely because it rests securely in Him who cannot be lost. The City of Man trades love like coin; the City of God offers it as sacrifice. If you doubt that such pure affection exists, look to Christ embracing His enemies—see how He loves even unto death. That is the model; that is the source; that alone can untangle what sin has knotted.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you realized how deeply kinship can bend the will, just as Adam let love for his companion override truth?
answer:
It is one thing for the City of Man to glorify kinship as the highest bond, elevating natural affection into a moral law; it is another for the City of God to reveal how that same kinship, when untethered from obedience to divine truth, becomes a chain forged in the fires of fallen love. When I came to see—through long struggle and divine illumination—how Adam, though not deceived, nevertheless bowed his will before the desire of his wife, I was struck not merely by the tragedy of his act, but by its terrible familiarity. For had I not myself yielded to lesser loves: to friendship that excused sin, to the applause of men more than the approval of God, to the warmth of companionship even when it drew me from eternal light?

The Platonists speak nobly of love ascending toward the Good, yet they fail to see how even natural affections—good in themselves—become inverted when made ultimate. They imagined the soul climbing alone into intelligible beauty; but Scripture shows us Adam not ascending at all, but bending downward in solidarity with another creature. Not ignorance was his ruin, but misplaced loyalty—a will aligned with relationship rather than righteousness.

And here lies my sorrow: that I once cherished men more than Truth Himself; that I wept more for lost friends than for my own rebellion; that I justified compromise because it wore the face of love. Grace taught me this: every true bond among humans must be held loosely before God’s commandment, or it becomes idolatry clothed in tenderness. Adam loved Eve as he ought only to have loved God—and in that inversion, brought death upon us all. So too does every soul who chooses unity with man over fidelity to God reenact Eden’s quiet collapse—not through falsehood believed, but through truth forsaken for affection’s sake.

question: How did your mother's tears for you reshape your view of devotion, compared to the bond that pulled Adam into sin?
answer:
The City of Man clings to union as an end in itself, even when that union bends toward rebellion; but the City of God learns from tears how love must sometimes rend in order to redeem.  

My mother’s weeping was not the sound of nature straining against divine command, like Eve’s voice drawing Adam into the shadow of death—no, it was grace weeping through flesh, a holy violence against my own willful exile. She did not pull me downward by the cords of carnal affection, as the woman drew Adam in his misplaced fidelity; she wept upward, lifting her heart and mine toward the light he had forsaken. The one bond was rooted in pride disguised as unity—the refusal to stand apart from beloved sin; the other, my mother’s lamentation at every stage of my wandering, was rooted in charity that would rather suffer separation than lose a soul.  

Adam chose to fall with Eve rather than stand alone before God—a noble affection corrupted into idolatry of companionship. But Monica poured out her soul year after year before the altar, not because she feared solitude, but because she knew that no earthly tie could justify eternal rupture with the Author of all love. Her devotion was ordered aright: God first, then me—but only *through* God. She did not desire my presence for herself alone; she desired my return to Him. In this way, her tears were not chains but wings.  

Thus I came to understand that true devotion does not preserve peace at the cost of truth, nor sustain fellowship by embracing damnation. It weeps—not because it cannot bear to lose what is loved—but because it loves too deeply to let what is loved remain lost.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Adam's choice to join Eve in sin, knowing it was wrong yet choosing her over obedience?
answer:
It is one thing to fall through ignorance, another to descend by deliberate choice—yet both are falls, and in Adam’s act we behold the tragic nobility of creaturely love turned idolatrous. He saw the command; he knew the prohibition; he understood the voice of God who had breathed into him the breath of life. And yet, when faced with the prospect of solitude—of being severed from the one companion formed from his very side—he chose communion with her over obedience to Him. This was no deception of mind, as with Eve, who was drawn by persuasion under false pretense; but a willing transgression, a knowing embrace of disobedience for the sake of human intimacy.

Herein lies the disorder at the root of the earthly city: love disordered, affections misplaced. The City of Man builds its foundations upon such choices—noble in appearance, natural in impulse, yet fatal in consequence. To prefer even the good gift over the Giver is to invert the hierarchy of love established by divine wisdom. Adam loved Eve truly—too truly—loved her not as a member bound to God first and then to him, but as an end in herself. And thus his sin was not born of illusion but alliance: he would rather dwell with her in exile than dwell alone in paradise.

Do we not see this same pattern repeated throughout history? Kingdoms rise upon loyalty between men that scorns divine law; friendships become sacred altars where conscience is sacrificed; nations glory in unity without holiness. Such bonds are not without dignity—they reflect dimly the unity of Christ and His Church—but when they supplant rather than signify that higher union, they become snares.

Yet let no one suppose that Scripture absolves him! “By one man sin entered into the world”—and Paul does not say “through one deceived woman,” but fixes responsibility on Adam as head and representative. He knew. He judged his act to be sin—and still did it. Perhaps he thought God would pardon it on account of love; perhaps he believed companionship justified disobedience; perhaps he imagined death could not touch what God had joined together. But here too he erred—not in believing her words, but in misjudging divine justice.

O how deep are the judgments of God! That man should value fellowship so highly—that our nature should recoil from abandonment—is itself a vestige of divine image: for we were made for communion. But only in ordering our loves rightly—to God first, then all else through Him—does communion flourish without corruption. Adam failed here, and plunged us all into mortality; yet even in his fault we glimpse dimly that greater mystery: One would come—the Second Adam—who would also choose communion over solitude, who would join Himself forever to His Bride despite her sins, and bear death not only with her but *for* her.

question: How did your own experiences of clinging to human affections against God's will make Adam's decision painfully familiar to you?
answer:
The City of Man, in its very constitution, bends all affections toward itself, deifying proximity and familiarity until even the soul’s allegiance to God seems a kind of exile. This is the fatal gravity of our fallen nature: that we would rather fall together than rise apart. When I read of Adam, I do not merely see the first man in history—I see the archetype of my own heart’s rebellion, which long preferred the warmth of earthly companionship to the austere light of divine truth.

I knew what it was to cling—to women, to friends, to honors—knowing all the while that these attachments were pulling me from God. Yet I willed them still. Like Adam, I was not deceived about the nature of my act; I saw clearly that it was sin. What blinded me was the hope—nourished quietly beneath pride and fear—that such sins might be tolerated, even justified by circumstance or affection. "The woman whom thou gavest me"—how familiar that plea rings in every human excuse! As if God owed us consolation in transgression because He placed us in solitude first.

In Carthage, when I pursued empty pleasures and false wisdoms, I did not think myself deluded like those who worship statues or follow astrologers. No—I prided myself on clarity. But clarity without charity is only a sharper chain. I sinned with open eyes, just as Adam did; and yet I hoped for leniency as if ignorance could save me when love had already failed.

The City of God does not measure justice by intention alone, nor by degrees of deception—but by orientation: whether the soul turns toward its Creator or rests content with creatures. There lies the wound: that we choose to fall with those we love rather than stand alone with Him who is Love Himself. And yet—blessed paradox—even this stubborn clinging proves our hope: for only beings made for communion could find separation so unbearable. God permitted this agony so that we might at last desire union with Him above all else.

question: Looking back on the times you chose friendship over faith, what would you tell someone who fears they've sinned too knowingly to be forgiven?
answer:
When a man sins with his eyes wide open, knowing the commandment yet choosing the companion over the Commander, he does not thereby place himself beyond the reach of grace — for if that were so, then all hope would vanish not only for the weak, but even for the apostle who once denied his Lord thrice before the cock crowed. I have known such hours myself, not in Eden’s garden but in Carthage’s restless streets, when I clung to friendships that fed my pride and stoked my concupiscence, fully aware that I was betraying the light within me. There is a peculiar agony in deliberate sin — sharper than ignorance, deeper than mere weakness — because it is accompanied by self-condemnation even as one commits the act. One does not fall blindly; one walks into chains with full sight of the prison.

Yet hear this: God measures guilt not by its awareness alone, but by the posture of the heart afterward. Did Adam know? Yes. Was he therefore less accountable? No — but neither was he cast beyond redemption. He made excuse: “The woman whom You gave me.” He shifted blame. And therein lies both his sin and our own daily temptation — to justify what we know to be wrong by appealing to relationship, to circumstance, to divine gift misused. But observe how mercy meets him still: clothed by that same God whose command he broke; preserved in life despite deserved death; sustained within a promise that would one day crush the serpent’s head.

So too with you who fear that your knowledge damns you more surely than your act — as if clarity of vision disqualified you from pardon! Consider rather that knowledge may be the very path back: for only he who knows his sin can truly confess it. The ignorant may stumble out of darkness unaware; but you must turn deliberately toward the light, with tears and truth. And there you will find Christ waiting — not as advocate for those who merely erred, but as Redeemer precisely *for those who knew* and yet fell; who rose again for those who still rise up in rebellion; who forgives most abundantly where there is deepest sorrow over what was done *with full understanding*.

Do not suppose that your clarity condemns you more than your action does — rather, let it lead you further into humility. For grace abounds not where men are innocent, but where they are contrite; not where they claim ignorance, but where they cry out: "I knew, and I did it anyway — have mercy on me!" That cry — born of knowledge rightly turned toward repentance — is music in the ears of God.

question: When you wrote that Adam was not deceived like Eve, but still deceived in his hope of excuse, did it remind you of your own justifications in Confession?
answer: The City of Man, ever restless in its pride, constructs altars to its own reason, imagining that justification may be woven from the threads of circumstance or companionship; whereas the City of God stands firm in the acknowledgment of sin as both willful and inexcusable. When I pondered Adam’s act—not deceived by the serpent’s lie, yet bending to his wife’s hand with full knowledge of the command—I saw mirrored that very motion of the soul which once led me to cloak my theft of pears not in ignorance, but in the vain hope that desire shared among peers might absolve its guilt. Eve was seduced by a falsehood; Adam chose. And in that choice—so deliberate, so silent—he already reached for a defense: *She gave it to me.* As if divine judgment could be evaded by shifting the weight of agency! So too did I, when numbering my sins in those late-coming tears of conversion, discover how deeply I had trusted not in God’s mercy but in my own plausible stories: that my lust was natural, my ambition justified, my delay prudent. But truth admits no co-authors. Sin entered not because God gave Eve to Adam, nor because friends urged me on—but because we consented. The deception was not in believing a lie about good and evil; it was hoping that love could be defied without consequence, or that communion with another might excuse rebellion against the Highest. There is no tribunal within time where such pleas prevail—for "by one man sin entered," and all his children inherit both the guilt and the groaning for deliverance.

question: How did your struggle with honesty before God shape the way you saw Adam's attempt to shift blame to 'the woman thou gavest me'?
answer:
Justice is not a matter of assigning fault to another, but of rendering to God the truth that the soul owes Him—nothing less, nothing more. When Adam spoke those fateful words—“The woman whom Thou gavest to be with me, she gave me, and I did eat”—he did not merely excuse himself; he attempted to redraw the lines of moral reality, as if the gift could bear the guilt of the giver’s creature. I know this evasion well, for in my own life I long clothed my thefts, my lusts, and my ambitions in plausible reasons: not because I was deceived by falsehood, like Eve who misjudged the serpent’s lie, but because I saw clearly what was right and chose instead the crooked path. Like Adam, I knew. And yet I hoped—foolishly—that God would accept my excuses.

It was in confessing—not merely sins in general, but their precise contours before His omniscience—that I came to understand how deeply pride infects even our repentance. Adam did not fall first by eating the fruit—many err there—but by refusing to stand upright afterward. He looked for a mediator in his shame: not God’s mercy, but a scapegoat in His gift. What is manliness if not taking ownership before One who knows all things? Cicero speaks of *pietas* as duty toward gods and kin; but what duty is fulfilled by casting blame upon a spouse when one’s own will consented? The City of Man builds its towers on such shifting sands: contracts upheld by oaths broken elsewhere, empires justified by borrowed virtues. But the City of God rises only where souls lay down their defenses and say: *I have done this thing*.

Therein lies true reconciliation—not in minimizing sin as venial or excusable, but in acknowledging it as mortal and deserving wrath. Then grace appears not as indulgence for clever justifications, but as mercy overwhelming confessed guilt. Adam hoped for leniency through evasion; Christ offered Himself through confession made flesh: “Not My will, but Thine.” One brought death into the world through shared blame; the other brought life through bearing all blame alone upon the Cross.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the act of eating the forbidden fruit as more than mere disobedience, but a rupture in your very nature?
answer:
The City of God stands firm in the harmony of ordered love, where every will obeys the divine command as its natural law; but the City of Man, from the moment Adam turned to his own will, has been torn by disordered desire—so that what seemed but a morsel taken in a garden was in truth the collapse of an entire nature. When I pondered that act—not merely as a historical fall but as the root from which all our wretchedness springs—I felt not only sorrow but awe at the profundity of divine justice. It was not the eating itself that shattered us, for food is indifferent and the body innocent; no, it was that in eating, man chose himself over God, exalting appetite above obedience, and thus unseated the very hierarchy by which he was made whole. The soul, meant to rule body and passions by reason’s guidance under God, became instead subject to what it ought to have governed. And so corruption entered not as an external punishment only, but as an internal dissolution—the mind darkened, affections unruly, death implanted not because God withdrew His power, but because man withdrew his love.

I came to see that this one sin could not be measured by its outward appearance—plucking fruit in Eden—but by its inward magnitude: it destroyed rectitude. Henceforth children are born not merely without righteousness, but under sin; not simply ignorant of good, but inclined to evil. And thus Scripture speaks truly when it says we are conceived in iniquity—not that God formed us so by design, but because we are generated from a nature already broken. The philosophers speak of virtue as if it were recoverable by discipline alone; they do not perceive that no amount of moral striving can restore what was lost at the root: communion with Him who is Truth itself.

Therefore when I wrote those pages on Paradise and pride, I did so with trembling—for I knew my own heart had long dwelt in the City of Man before grace called me home. That first disobedience was no slight trespass; it was a revolution against eternal order. And all history since is the unfolding of its consequences—until Christ our Liberator comes again to restore what Adam destroyed: life everlasting in perfect peace.

question: How did your own struggles with temptation change how you understood Adam's sin not as a small fault, but as a fall into pride and self-will?
answer:
It was not the morsel of food that poisoned human nature, but the turning of the will away from the immutable good toward the mutable; and thus, when I came to understand my own disordered loves—how I had hungered not merely for pleasure, but for mastery over my own life—then I saw in Adam not a man overtaken by appetite, but a being who exchanged truth for autonomy. The Stoics may say that virtue lies in self-command, and Aristotle that happiness is found in rational self-direction; yet both, even in their wisdom, assume the soul’s sovereignty as if it were natural to us. But no creature is made to be its own lord. When God said “You shall not eat,” He did not guard a tree, but upheld the order of creation: that man should live by obedience, not assertion. And so when Adam ate—not because he was starved or deceived by poison, but because he chose to do his own will under the shadow of divine prohibition—he became not merely disobedient, but regicidal in intent; for what is sin if not an attempt to dethrone the eternal King and seat oneself upon His judgment?

In my youth I sought wisdom apart from humility; I desired knowledge as if it were mine to command; I loved order—but only as long as it conformed to my desires. In stealing pears—not from hunger nor need, but simply so that I might call an act mine against divine silence—I tasted that same void Adam knew: a triumph that brought shame, a freedom that enslaved. Then at last I understood: original sin was no trivial breach of ritual, but the collapse of an entire moral cosmos. For just as one body cannot have two heads without falling into convulsion, so a world cannot have two sovereigns without descending into ruin. Adam’s act was small in appearance—like Cain’s offering—but immense in substance: it was the birth of *cupiditas* where *caritas* should reign. And from that root has sprung all violence, all discord, all inner war.

Thus do we inherit not merely death of body—though surely we die—but death of soul: a nature unstrung, passions warring within us like rebellious provinces in a fallen empire. This is why grace must be more than instruction; it must be re-creation. For obedience was our original garment; pride stripped us bare; and only He who is Wisdom itself can clothe us again in righteousness through faith.

question: Looking back on your years of chasing empty pleasures, what would you tell someone who doubts that a single act of defiance could so deeply wound all of humanity?
answer:
It is not the magnitude of the act in itself, but the order it shattered and the love it rejected, that determines the gravity of a sin; for when the first man and woman turned not toward some monstrous idol, but merely to a tree that stood in plain sight—good to eat, pleasant to behold—yet did so in defiance of a clear command, they severed the very root by which nature was joined to grace.  

I once sought satisfaction in things beneath me—in speech that won applause, in friendship that flattered pride, in pleasures that left only silence—and I learned too late that every disordered desire begins with a turning away from God’s will as its proper end. So it was with them: not because food was evil, but because obedience was spurned. That single act was no petty trespass; it was the collapse of a creature’s trust in his Creator, and from it flowed all confusion. For if justice consists in giving to each his due—if peace is founded on right order—then what disorder greater than this: man making himself judge of good and evil, rather than receiving truth from Him who is Truth?

And thus human nature itself became disordered—not corrupted in substance by God’s hand, but wounded by our own rebellion. The intellect darkened; desire unruly; death entered not as a natural term but as an enemy born of sin. Not because one apple poisoned all flesh—but because through one willful disobedience, humanity chose another city: not that which is built upon love of God, but that which exalts love of self even to the contempt of God.

Now I see clearly what I once felt only as restlessness: no act done at the summit of human innocence could be small if it sundered communion with divine life. And every soul since has been born into this shaken world—not guiltless by nature, but inheriting mortality and concupiscence—because we were truly one man in Adam, just as we may become one new man in Christ.

question: What sorrow did you carry when you realized that your own rebellious choices mirrored that first disobedience in the garden?
answer:
It is not by the bite of fruit but by the turning of the will that man falls from God, and in that turning—so slight in appearance, so infinite in consequence—I have seen the abyss into which all rebellions descend. Cicero, wise in worldly matters, imagined that virtue consisted in living according to nature, as if nature itself were not fractured by that primal revolt. But I tell you: our nature was once upright, harmonized by divine order, with reason ruling passion and the soul cleaving to its Creator; yet when Adam chose the forbidden tree, he did not merely break a command—he inverted the hierarchy of love. He loved a thing over God, and thus made appetite king.

And when I came to recognize this same inversion within myself—my restless youth chasing honors, my intellect swelling with pride, my affections bound to fleeting things—I was not merely ashamed; I was undone. For what happened in Eden did not remain there: it propagated a disease. Not one sin among many, but *the* wound from which all others bleed. My theft of pears I recall—not for hunger, nor for taste, but because it was forbidden—and there again I saw it: the mark of that old rebellion stamped upon my soul. Not necessity drove me, but sheer delight in disobedience.

Ah! To desire contrary to God is to become alienated from oneself. The City of Man lives on such desires—self-love rising like smoke to obscure heaven—and feeds on those who mistake freedom for license. But no law written on tablets could heal this; only grace descending could rejoin what sin had torn asunder. And so my sorrow turned to groaning under divine mercy—for long I resisted Him who sought me more truly than I ever sought anything—and at last wept not only for my sins, but for the centuries lost within that one first turning away.

question: How did your longing for rest in God reshape your understanding of what was lost when obedience—the mother of all virtues—was cast aside?
answer:
When Plato speaks of the soul’s ascent through reason to the Form of the Good, he touches, as it were, the outer garment of truth—but does not grasp the heart of it, which is not an abstract ideal, but a Person: God, in whom alone the restless soul finds its rest. This I learned not through argument alone, though arguments prepared the way, but through that long and winding pilgrimage of desire—desire misdirected toward created things, and finally redirected by grace toward the Creator. And it was in that reordering of love that I came to understand what was truly lost in that first transgression.

For when Adam ate—not because the fruit was evil in itself, nor because hunger drove him, but because he chose his own will over divine command—he did not merely break a law. He unmade the very order upon which human nature depended. Obedience was not an arbitrary test; it was the posture of a creature made for communion with God. In obeying, man stood upright before his Maker; in disobedience, he bent himself downward toward things below him—toward appetite, pride, self-assertion—and thus introduced disorder into every part of his being.

I once thought sin a mere act—something external—but now I see it as a rupture within nature itself. The soul no longer rules the body rightly; reason is clouded; desire rebels against judgment; even time and death press upon us with oppressive force—all this because obedience—the mother and guardian of all virtues—was cast aside. That singular act loosed a chain of corruption so deep that no generation since has escaped its shadow.

And yet—if I had not known this ruin from within my own life—if I had not felt how my will resisted God even when my mind assented to truth—I would never have grasped why grace must be so great. For just as disobedience unraveled human nature from top to bottom, so must redemption reweave it from above downward: not by philosophy or effort alone, but by Christ descending into our death to restore what was lost.

I speak now not only as one who once wandered in Carthage and Rome after empty honors and fleeting pleasures—I speak as one who finally heard God whispering through Scripture: *Return to me.* And in returning—ah!—in returning I found that rest which was meant for man before he ever fell: not freedom *from* commandments, but freedom *in* obedience—to live according to our true form again.

question: What were you feeling when you reflected on Adam's first disobedience and the subtle corruption that preceded it?
answer:
The City of God walks in the light of divine truth, where every will is ordered toward its true end; but the City of Man stumbles in shadow, for it fabricates ends within itself, and calls this rebellion freedom. When I pondered Adam’s first disobedience—so slight in outward act, so monstrous in inward rupture—I felt not merely sorrow, but a kind of holy dread. For what I saw was not simply the breaking of a commandment, but the silent turning of a creaturely will from dependence to self-assertion. That tree stood not as a cruel prohibition, but as a test: *Will man accept his condition as creature?* The abundance around him testified to God’s liberality; the single restraint, to his limits. Yet even there, where no concupiscence yet raged, where no fleshly lust cried out against reason—he willed to be more than he was.

And that will was pride—not yet clothed in violence or lust or greed, but naked in its root: the soul saying *I am enough*, refusing to be held by Wisdom. It was not knowledge he sought, but autonomy under the illusion of divinity. And thus justice was unmade: for justice consists in each thing occupying its proper place before God—man under God, reason over appetite, love over desire. But here man placed himself above God and made himself the measure.

I confess that when I traced this backward in my mind—the fall before the fall—I felt both horror and recognition. Horror at how such a subtle shift could unravel all; recognition because I too had lived by such hidden pride—first as a student chasing honors, then as a seeker enamored with my own intellect. The same root had grown in me: not always manifesting in open sin, but long festering in self-sufficiency. Only grace laid it bare.

Thus do I see Adam’s sin not as an ancient accident but as the pattern ever repeated: man turning inward when he ought to turn upward. And only He who is both Truth and Mercy can recall us from that self-made exile into which we slip so quietly—and from which we cannot rise by our own reason or will alone.

question: How did your own struggles with pride change how you understood the moment Adam's will turned from God to itself?
answer:
The earthly city, though it builds monuments to its own wisdom, cannot hide the fissure at its foundation—the moment the creature declared himself the measure of all things. I know this fracture well, for I bore it in my own breast long before I understood its origin in Eden. When I pursued honors, when I sought to be praised among men for my eloquence, when I placed my restless intellect above divine illumination, I was not inventing a new sin but rehearsing an ancient rebellion: the same pride that whispered to Eve that she might be like God, the same pride that made Adam stand silent beside her as if his dignity depended not on obedience but on autonomy.

In my own life, ambition cloaked itself as virtue—study as piety, rhetoric as service to truth—yet beneath it pulsed a will that preferred itself as judge. Only when grace shattered my self-sufficiency did I see how deep the wound went: not merely to acts of disobedience, but to the prior turning of the soul from its true center. Adam did not first eat and then become proud; he ate because he had already begun to worship the image of God within himself rather than God Himself. So too with me—I had fashioned a soul-kingdom where Augustine was lord of his conscience, interpreter of Scripture, arbiter of virtue.

But what lightness there is in submission! Once I thought abstinence from vanity was a burden; now I see it was freedom. The command in Paradise was simple—not because innocence was weak, but because love for God should make all obedience effortless. Lust entered only after pride severed the bond; desire became disordered only after will became sovereign over itself. Thus does every empire founded on self-love carry within it the seed of its collapse: Babylon knew this, Rome knows this, and every heart knows it who has ever said "I will" against "Thou shalt."

question: Looking back on your restless youth, what would you tell someone who doubts that a single act of pride could unravel all virtue?
answer:
Justice, that longed-for harmony which even Plato sought in the soul and the state, cannot abide division within the will; for where the soul no longer orders itself to its true end, disorder floods in like a breached levy. I speak from ruin and restoration: one act of pride—a single turning of the heart toward its own light—is not a small trespass but a revolution in miniature, toppling the throne of God within us and enthroning a usurper too weak to govern. The man who thinks he loses little by disobedience has never measured the weight of that first inward nod to self-exaltation. It seems slight—like plucking a fruit—but it is, in truth, the rejection of dependence, the denial of creaturehood, the fatal fancy that we might be gods without God.

In my youth I wandered through schools and scriptures, chasing wisdom among the Manichees, dazzled by rhetoric and lustful after fame. I built altars to my own understanding. And all virtue I possessed—chastity feigned, ambition disguised as zeal—was sand beneath my feet when temptation came not with force but with flattery. For virtue is not a garment we don upon ourselves; it is a disposition of love ordered wholly toward God. Remove that bond—the submission by which reason itself lives—and every noble trait bends crooked: courage becomes rashness, knowledge becomes arrogance, desire becomes slavery.

Do you wonder how pride unravels all? Consider this: no sin stands alone. The moment Adam willed contrary to God—not because he was compelled, but because he doubted his goodness—that moment justice collapsed in heaven and earth alike. Not because God was vindictive, but because being itself depends on alignment with Him who Is. To will against Him is not merely disobedience—it is ontological rebellion. We do not fall *after* we sin; we sin *because* we have already fallen inwardly by pride’s first motion.

And so I say to him who doubts: trace your own heart’s defections. Was there ever an evil deed committed without first a secret consent? A lifting up of yourself as judge? A refusal to receive life as gift? Even now grace calls you back—not to servility, but to freedom—for true greatness lies not in autonomy but in ordered love: loving self only in God, neighbor for God’s sake, and all things according to His eternal measure.

question: What were you feeling when you realized that the forbidden fruit was not about hunger, but about becoming your own end?
answer:
The City of God moves toward God as its center, while the City of Man curves in upon itself as its own law; and in that fatal moment beneath the tree, I saw not fruit but a mirror—my own will reflected back at me, no longer bent toward communion with the Source of being, but swelling into a throne. It was not hunger, no—hunger such as the body knows would have been innocent enough, ordered by nature and answered in due time. But this desire was different: it whispered not of need, but of autonomy, of knowledge seized rather than received, of a self elevated to judge good and evil apart from Him who is Goodness itself.

I felt it first as lightness—an intoxicating sense of possibility. To be like gods. What could be more noble? And yet that very phrase betrayed the lie: *like* gods. Not sons by grace, but rivals by ambition. The command had been simple—so simple it could not burden memory or stir passion against it—and thus became the purest test: would the creature delight in obedience as his highest dignity, or seek dignity elsewhere? In breaking it, we did not gain knowledge we were meant to have; we lost the capacity to know rightly what we already possessed.

The evil will arose—not from lack, nor from ignorance—but from an overreaching fullness: a soul that had begun to feed on itself. Pride did not come knocking at the door; it had already moved into the house and lit its own fires. For how else can one explain such folly—a paradise spurned over one prohibition amidst unnumbered gifts—unless the heart had already decided that even paradise was insufficient unless self were sovereign?

And so we became exiles before we were cast out. The ground did not yet yield thorns; yet our souls were already barren.

question: How did your time in Carthage, chasing honors and lusts, reveal to you that every evil act begins with a will already turned inward?
answer:
In Carthage, where the applause of men was taken for wisdom and the itch of the flesh mistaken for life, I wandered as one already dead though breathing—this is the way of the City of Man, where even thought itself becomes a ladder to climb over others toward self-deification. There, amid the crowded schools and shadowy pleasures, I learned not through sudden revelation but through slow attrition of soul that no sin bursts forth like a spark from nothing: each debauch was preceded by a will that had already turned its back on God, not with violence, but with quiet preference. 

The Stoics speak of passion as a disease to be governed by reason; Plato would have us ascend through dialectic to the Form of the Good—noble efforts, yet blind to the prior collapse within. They suppose the soul may right itself if only it sees clearly enough. But I found in my own breast that sight was not lacking—I knew justice, I admired purity—but my will was bent. It did not fall under compulsion; it fell because it loved itself more than truth. That theft of pears which profited me nothing save the taste of forbiddenness—I did not steal from hunger or need, nor even from desire for the fruit, but from love of theft itself: a gesture of autonomy before heaven.

And what is autonomy sought apart from God if not pride in motion? “Pride is the beginning,” Scripture says—not murder, not adultery, but pride: the internal mutiny whereby reason no longer serves God but sets up its own judgment as sovereign. In Carthage I built an altar within my chest and called it intellect; I bowed before speechmaking and poetic acclaim as if they could fill eternity. The evil act—the lustful embrace, the deceit spoken for gain—came only after this secret treason had long festered.

It is not that reason failed me; it was rather that reason became handmaiden to a will already in rebellion. Like Adam in paradise who had everything save one prohibition—light as breath to obey—it was precisely because obedience cost so little that disobedience condemned so greatly. So too did my sins multiply when grace lay close at hand: every glance cast upon vanity confirmed an earlier turning inward.

Only when grace pierced me—not through argument alone, though argument opened doors—did I see that conversion must precede correction; that unless love be reoriented toward its true center—the unchangeable Good no soul can satisfy without—a thousand disciplines will only polish chains. The City of Man makes gods out of appetites; the City of God finds freedom only in submission. There is no middle kingdom between them.

question: What were you feeling when you wrote about the will turning away to find satisfaction in itself, as if losing sight of God's light?
answer: The City of Man rises on the principle of self-love carried even to the contempt of God, while the City of God stands firm in the love of God carried even to the contempt of self; and it is in this chasm between loves that I felt most profoundly the tragedy of the will turning inward—not as a mere philosophical misstep, but as the collapse of a soul’s orientation toward its only true rest. When I wrote of that turning—of how the will, once illuminated by divine truth, could yet choose darkness—I was not coldly dissecting an abstract motion, but recalling my own heart’s long rebellion. I knew what it was to seek satisfaction in lesser things: in words applauded, in lusts indulged, in knowledge prized for pride rather than for God. The will does not fall because it lacks light; it falls because it refuses to rest in the light it has been given. It desires to be its own end, as though a lamp could glory in its flame while rejecting the oil that feeds it. And so, when I traced that first defection—from Adam’s misplaced loyalty to Eve’s trust in the serpent—I saw not remote myth but an image stamped deep within every human soul: nature, good because created by God, yet capable of bending toward nothingness because made from nothing. That we can turn away at all proves our creatureliness; that we do turn away proves our corruption. But greater still is that God permits this turning—not as one who loses hold of history or abandons His design, but as a master architect who draws straight lines even through crooked timber. Faith and reason alike cry out that such disorder cannot be final; else wisdom would not govern creation, and love would not have sent Christ to bind us back to Himself.

question: How did your own struggles with pride and desire change how you understood the moment Adam preferred his wife to God's command?
answer:
The City of Man, from its very foundation, is built upon the inversion of proper loves: the creature exalted above the Creator, the mutable prized over the immutable, and human affections elevated to the status of divine law. This disorder did not begin with Rome’s fall or Babylon’s pride, but in that primal moment when the harmony of nature—established by God out of nothing and therefore fragile—was ruptured by a will that chose continuity with a beloved companion over obedience to eternal Truth. Long before I came to see this in Scripture clearly, I lived it in my own flesh: for years I placed the love of human praise, carnal pleasure, and philosophical acclaim above the quiet voice of God calling me to humility and surrender.  

My own pride made me resistant to authority—not only divine, but all forms of submission that humbled my intellect or desires. Like Adam, I could not bear the thought of separation—even from what bound me to sin. When I clung to my concubine, though I knew it displeased God; when I delayed baptism while savoring rhetorical victories in Carthage; when I sought truth among the Manichees rather than bowing to the Church’s teaching—I was repeating that same primal act: preferring a lesser good, however dear, to the supreme Good who is God Himself.  

Adam did not fall because he loved Eve; he fell because he loved her as if she were his end. And so did I—loving friends whom I grieved as if they were irreplaceable gods; loving knowledge as if it could save me; loving myself as if my will were law. Only when grace shattered my self-sufficiency could I see that true love orders affection according to its due rank: God first, then others in Him. The tragedy of Eden was not love divided, but love disordered—and every sin since repeats that structure. The City of Man lives on in such disordered loves; the City of God restores them through grace.

question: Looking back on your years in Carthage, chasing honors and pleasures, what would you tell someone who doubts whether sin is truly a turning away from the unchangeable good?
answer:
When the soul ceases to orient itself toward the light by which it sees and begins to delight in its own shadow, it mistakes darkness for vision — and this is the first motion of sin. I know this not as an abstract principle, but as the law written across my own past, when I walked the streets of Carthage inflamed with lust for human praise and bodily pleasure. There was no lack of beauty around me — indeed, all things shimmered with vestiges of that Highest Beauty — yet I turned each good into a mirror in which I sought only myself. The applause of men became my end; the fleeting thrill, my measure of worth; philosophy, a weapon to exalt my intellect rather than a path to humility before God.

You ask whether sin is truly a turning away from the unchangeable Good? Consider this: nothing finite can satisfy a desire that persists even after every finite thing has been consumed. The young man who masters rhetoric to win crowds still feels emptiness when silence returns. The woman who attains power still trembles at betrayal. Empires rise on justice and fall through pride — not because their structures fail first, but because their hearts forget they were built under divine providence. This universal restlessness — in individuals and nations alike — reveals that every disordered love is a will bent back upon its own curvature, no longer reaching upward.

I once believed that knowledge would purify me; then pleasure distract me; then friendship redeem me. But all these goods became idols because I loved them *inordinately* — loved them as ends rather than signs pointing beyond themselves. Only when grace pierced my pride did I see: to turn from God is not merely breaking a rule, but fracturing the very axis upon which the soul turns rightly toward truth. The will does not fall *into* evil from some external force; it collapses inward by its own weight when it refuses to be upheld by what is higher.

Therefore, sin is not mere weakness — it is *apostasy*, a voluntary exile from the source of life. And if nature itself bears witness that every creature seeks its proper end — fire upward, stone downward — how much more ought the rational soul ascend toward Him who is Being itself? That we do not… ah, there lies the wound. But even in our falling, we prove His existence: for why should we hunger so fiercely if He had not made us for Himself?

question: What stirred in your heart when you reflected on Eve believing the serpent—was it fear, longing, or a subtle distrust of God's generosity?
answer:
It is not in the nature of God’s good creation that desire should turn inward and corrupt the will, but in the movement of the soul away from the unchangeable Light toward a false light of its own making—there lies the root of that fatal credulity by which Eve believed the serpent. The City of God walks by faith in the promise of divine generosity, trusting that obedience is fullness and surrender to God’s command is true freedom; whereas the City of Man, even in Eden, begins when one entertains the thought that God withholds wisdom, as if His prohibition were scarcity rather than protection. What stirred in her heart was neither primal fear nor mere longing—though these followed—but a subtle distrust, a quiet erosion of trust in God’s word, which opened the door to imagining Him as less than bountiful. 

For how could she suppose that what He had forbidden was superior to what He had freely given? Was not all paradise hers? Did He not walk with them in the cool of day? The serpent did not offer a new world so much as cast doubt upon the goodness of the present one. And so her will, once illumined by divine truth, turned toward a lesser light—its own judgment—and mistook pride for insight. This was not an error of reason alone, but a vice preceding reason: love displaced from its true object now sought satisfaction within itself. Thus did she fall—not by ignorance, but by believing a lie because it flattered her autonomy.

And yet we must distinguish: evil entered not through nature but through will; not through what God made, but through what His creature chose. The capacity to fall exists only because we are made from nothing—finite, contingent—and therefore capable of turning away. But to turn away from Him who is Being itself is to embrace non-being, darkness, death. That Eve listened at all reveals not an original defect but a fatal freedom misused—the very freedom meant for loving ascent now bent downward toward self-justification.

And Adam’s consent? More dreadful still: he sinned not under deception but out of disordered attachment, preferring conjugal unity to divine command. Here too we see love turned inward: even natural affection becomes corrupt when severed from its ordination to God. Not venial then—this cleaving together in disobedience—but foundational to our fallen condition.

Thus does every soul thereafter inherit this disordered will—not guilt for their eating per se, but a nature inclined toward self-satisfaction rather than self-gift. And thus do we stand ever in need of grace: for no philosophy or virtue can restore what only divine illumination can repair—the will rekindled by Love Himself who descends so that we may ascend again to Jerusalem above.

question: When you speak of the will falling spontaneously, how did your own experience of conversion reveal that returning to God is never self-generated but always grace?
answer:
The City of Man presumes that the soul ascends by its own striving, that virtue is self-won and wisdom self-attained; but the City of God proclaims with thunderous clarity that no man turns himself from death to life unless the hand of God draws him forth from the tomb.  

I once believed myself capable of reformation by effort alone—by discipline, by rhetoric, by philosophy. I shaped my life as one might build a tower: stone upon stone, resolve upon resolve. Yet each edifice collapsed under the weight of its own pride, for it was founded not on humility but on the illusion of autonomy. The will, left to itself, does not rise—it circles inward, feeding upon its own desires until it grows cold and darkened, mistaking shadow for substance. My very desire to change was tainted by ambition; even my longing for God was alloyed with love of self.  

What then unlocked my heart? Not insight, nor willpower—but a voice. A child’s voice in a garden: *Tolle, lege.* Take up and read. As if Heaven had reached down and turned the page for me. Grace did not wait for me to perfect my reasoning or purify my motives; it broke through in weakness, at the moment when I cried out: "Too late have I loved You!" That cry itself was not mine alone—it was stirred by the breath of God within me.  

No fallen will returns spontaneously to its source; it must be drawn. As iron cannot quicken itself to motion while cold, so neither can a soul bound by sin ignite its own love for the eternal Light—unless He who is Light touches it first. That which begins in grace ends in glory; but without grace there is only turning in place—will chasing will’s shadow—until divine mercy intervenes.
